# THE BOOK OF MARK{.heading_16}

# Introduction {.heading_74}

This is the second Gospel account of the life of Christ, as recorded by John Mark. Mark first appears, in the historical narrative, in Acts 12:12, where his mother's house was used as one of the meeting places of the church in Jerusalem. Some commentators believe the ["young man"]{.class_3054} of Mark 14:51--52, who fled naked from Christ's captors in the Garden of Gethsemane, was Mark referring to himself anonymously in the third person. That is just "scholarly conjecture" though (i.e., an educated [guess]{.class_3055}), and Biblical scholarship doesn't exactly have the best of "batting averages" (we will discuss the identity of the ["young man"]{.class_3054} later in our comments on 14:51--52).

John Mark's mother, Mary, was the sister of Paul's first missionary companion, Barnabas, so John Mark was Barnabas' nephew (Col. 4:10). Because Mark was "family," Barnabas took him with him when he and Paul (then Saul) returned from Jerusalem to Antioch (Acts 12:25). From there, Mark accompanied the two apostles (Acts 14:14) on their first missionary journey as their servant (Acts 13:5). It is obvious, though, from Scripture, that Mark was not ready to "jump in" that deeply into the Lord's service that quickly, for half way into the trip, he quit and went home to momma (Acts 13:13).

This became a source of contention between Paul and Barnabas when they got ready for their second missionary journey (read Acts 15:36--40) and resulted in a "church split." Barnabas, as ["The son of consolation"]{.class_3054} (Acts 4:36), wanted to give Mark another chance and take him along, but Paul would have none of it because of Mark's previous defection. As a result, ["Barnabas took Mark, and sailed unto Cyprus"]{.class_3054} (Acts 15:39), and Paul took Silas and ended up going to Macedonia and Greece (Acts 16--18).

While Paul may have been absolutely right in his assessment of Mark's original spiritual condition, it is important to note that Mark "made good" on his second chance and was reconciled with Paul long before the apostle recorded the words of 2 Timothy 4:11 (see our comments under Phile. 24 in the [Bible Believer's Commentary on 1 & 2 Thessalonians and Philemon]{.class_3055}).

Mark was Simon Peter's "Timothy." That is, he was Peter's []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw68.xhtml_page_4}["son in the faith"]{.class_3054} (1 Tim. 1:2 cf. 1 Pet. 5:13): Peter led him to Christ. Because of Peter's statement in 1 Peter 5:13, tradition (coming through Irenaeus, Papias, Justin Martyr, Clement of Alexandria, et al.) says Mark was the "disciple interpreter" of Peter. In other words, Mark got all the information for his Gospel from the Apostle Peter. Apostate scholarship, in agreement with the Roman Catholic Church (via apostates like Clement of Alexandria, Origen, Eusebius, and Jerome), have Mark in Rome recording Peter's words after Peter's death in Rome. You can consign such malarkey to "File 13," given the fact that there is no Scriptural or historical evidence that either Peter or Mark were ever within 500 miles of Rome (see our comments on 1 Pet. 5:13 in the [Bible Believer's Commentary on the General Epistles,]{.class_3055} Vol. I).

Moreover, though Mark may not have been an eyewitness to all the events recorded in his Gospel, as a contemporary of the apostles during the earthly ministry of Jesus, he certainly would have been familiar with the reports of Jesus' teachings and miracles (see Luke's comments on the eyewitness accounts in Luke 1:1--2). With the prominent place his mother had with her house being a meeting place for the early church, there is no reason to limit Mark to just Simon Peter; he probably had contact with all of "the Twelve." The only reason for making Mark the "disciple interpreter of Peter" is to elevate Peter to the level of "Prince of the Apostles" so the Pope can usurp Peter's authority. In the end, Mark's source for his Gospel was the same as that for the writings of Peter and Paul (or for that matter, the same as Moses, Isaiah, Jeremiah, David, Daniel, et al.)---the [Holy Ghost]{.class_3055} (2 Pet. 1:20--21 cf. 2 Tim. 3:16).

Of course, the higher critics (Kant, Astruc, Graf, Wellhausen, Schleiermacher, Weisse, Holtzmann, Streeter, Parker, Robinson, Koester, et al.) don't like the Holy Spirit getting any credit for His own Book, so they came up with the "Two-Source" Document Theory (also known as the "Q" Document Theory). The idea is that [no]{.class_3055} Gospel writer was "original" in his material. As the shortest Gospel (and therefore the most likely to be a "real" Gospel---the shorter reading is the more accurate), the higher critics have Mark being the first Gospel written. To supplement Mark's lack of material, Matthew and []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw68.xhtml_page_5}Luke not only used Mark as a source, they also used an unknown "Gospel," designated by the higher critics as the "Q" document. Therefore you can dismiss the divine origin of the "synoptic Gospels" (Matthew, Mark, and Luke) due to speculation on some unknown source, or sources, no one has ever found or seen. SOP for the Scholars' Union.

Who didn't know, who could read sixth-grade English, that there were ["many"]{.class_3054} sources consulted by a Gospel writer who was not actually there as an eyewitness to the events in the life of Christ? If you don't believe me, read Luke 1:1--3. Even if Mark had been the first Gospel writer (which, given the track record of infidelity by the higher critics, you can safely say he [wasn't]{.class_3055}), that wouldn't mean that Matthew or Luke had to copy him, much less even consult his Gospel. There were all kinds of eyewitness accounts to the life of Christ---NOT INSPIRED BY THE HOLY GHOST!---to which Matthew and Luke had access. "Q document" your dog's hind leg! All these infidels want to do is find some extracanonical writing [so they can reject the Scriptures God gave them.]{.class_3055} We shall dismiss their opinions as so much "offal" (a "five-dollar word" for [garbage]{.class_3055}).

All the early church fathers agreed that [Matthew]{.class_3055} was the first Gospel written. Clement of Alexandria goes so far as to say that the Gospels with genealogies (Matthew and Luke) were written before Mark. Be that as it may, one may infer from Luke 1:1--3 that Luke wrote after Matthew and Mark. We shall accept the order given us by the Holy Spirit in the Scriptures He gave us: the [Authorized King James Version.]{.class_3055} Matthew wrote first around A.D. 37. Mark wrote second, approximately A.D. 57--63. Luke wrote after Mark, sometime during the same time period. And you can be sure that whatever the "source" material Mark used to write his Gospel, he was precisely led by the Holy Ghost to write what he did, and [that]{.class_3055} is the canon; not any mysterious "Q" document ("logia," lambda, quelle, whatever; one designation is just as apostate and infidelic as the others).

"The Gospel according to St. Mark" has 16 chapters, 678 verses, and 15,166 words in the God-honored English text. Most expositors agree that Mark's "target audience" were Romans. There's not a lot of teaching in Mark. It moves right along with a lot of action. That sort []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw68.xhtml_page_6}of thing appealed to Romans.

While Matthew presents Jesus Christ as the King of the Jews, Mark presents Him as the Servant of God. That is why there is no genealogy in Mark. Matthew traces the genealogy of the King of the Jews back to their first King (David) and to the first "Jew" (Abraham). Luke traces Christ's genealogy back to the first man (Adam) because Luke presents Him as the Son of man. John takes His ancestry all the way back before Genesis 1:1 because John presents Him as the Son of God. But Mark gives no genealogy because nobody cares about the lineage of a slave.

As we are wont to do in all our writings in the [Bible Believer's Commentary Series,]{.class_3055} we will give no quarter to the opinions of any scholar, commentator, or expositor where those opinions cross the [words]{.class_3055} God gave us in the Scriptures in English. We certainly will not correct the words of that text with any "originals" no one has ever seen or read. Where we do have to refer to the original language in which Mark wrote, we will always do so with the intent of justifying what God gave us in the [Authorized Version]{.class_3055} of the Holy Bible.

We approach the words of Mark's Gospel as the words of God and not just the words of men (1 Thess. 2:13). We will not give you ours, or anyone else's (e.g., scholar, church, or ecclesiastical megalomaniac masquerading as the "Vicar of Christ"), ["private interpretation"]{.class_3054} on any passage in the book (2 Pet. 1:20); choosing rather to let the Author Himself interpret the text for us from His own Book (1 Cor. 2:13). In that spirit, we look to God's Servant---the One He chose not only to save us from our sins, but to rule over the entire earth---and we will delight in Him as His Father does.

["Behold my servant, whom I uphold; mine elect, in whom my soul delighteth; I have put my spirit upon him: he shall bring forth judgment to the Gentiles"]{.class_3054} (Isa. 42:1[).]{.class_3089}

[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw91.xhtml}

# Chapter 1 {.heading_97}

1:1 The beginning of the gospel of Jesus Christ, the Son of God;

Notice that the emphasis of the Gospel of Mark is on ["the gospel"]{.class_3054} as the message of ["Jesus Christ"]{.class_3054} as ["the Son of God."]{.class_3054} Although Jesus Christ's favorite designation of Himself during His earthly ministry was the [Son of man]{.class_3055} (used fifteen times by Jesus in the Gospel of Mark alone), "the intent of the author" is to identify Jesus to his readers as ["the Son of God."]{.class_3054}

Now you take that term ["the gospel."]{.class_3054} The Gospel of Mark begins with it (vs. 1) and ends with it (16:15). It means "the good news." The good news of what? Well, it [ends up]{.class_3055} with the good news as defined by the Apostle Paul in 1 Corinthians 15:1--4. ["Christ died for our sins according to the scriptures; And that he was buried, and that he rose again the third day according to the scriptures"]{.class_3054}---[that's ]{.class_3055}the Gospel. Paul calls it ["the gospel of the grace of God"]{.class_3054} (Acts 20:24). It is the good news that a sinner can be saved by grace through faith in the finished work of Jesus Christ WITHOUT WORKS (Eph. 2:8--9). Paul calls it ["my gospel"]{.class_3054} (Rom. 2:16, 16:25; 2 Tim. 2:8) because he received it through a [direct revelation of Jesus Christ]{.class_3055} (Gal. 1:11--12). It did [not]{.class_3055} come through Peter or any of "the Twelve." In fact, even though it was preached before the conversion of Saul of Tarsus in Acts 9 (see Acts 8:26--35 and note that Isa. 53:10 contains a reference to the blood atonement [for sin,]{.class_3055} which is missing from the preaching of the apostles in Acts 2--7), it was [not]{.class_3055} preached by any of "the Twelve"; it was preached by a deacon of the church (Acts 6:5). Moreover, it is evident that works are still a part of the Jews' salvation in the Gospel of Mark (see 10:17--21).

But you see, Mark is careful to tell you that what you are about to read is ["the BEGINNING of the gospel of Jesus Christ."]{.class_3054} The Holy Spirit is using what Mark recorded to lay an historical foundation for the Gospel. The four Gospels record the historical facts of the death, burial, and resurrection of Jesus Christ. That is the []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw91.xhtml_page_8}reason they are included in the New Testament.

Technically speaking, the New Testament doesn't begin until ["the death of the testator"]{.class_3054} (Heb. 9:15--17). That means, here in Mark, the New Testament doesn't show up until chapter 15. But Mark, like Matthew, Luke, and John, is included in the New Testament because it gives you the historical background behind ["the death of the testator."]{.class_3054}

So ["the gospel"]{.class_3054} begins in the Gospels (specifically here a reference to what Mark wrote) with the historical facts, and ends with the revelation to Paul. However that ["gospel"]{.class_3054} was applied to the Jews in the Gospels and in Acts 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, and 8, it was finalized in Acts 9 (cf. Gal. 1) to both Jew and Gentile, and affirmed by all the apostles and elders in Acts 15. Peter, the "apostle of the circumcision" (Gal. 2:8), said, ["But we believe that through the GRACE OF THE LORD JESUS CHRIST we ]{.class_3054}\[the Jews\][ shall be saved, even as they]{.class_3054} \[the Gentiles\]["]{.class_3054} (Acts 15:11). When he backslid from that position (Gal. 2:11--13), he was rebuked by the Apostle Paul and had to repent (Gal. 2:14--16).

[Today,]{.class_3055} salvation is by grace through faith in the message Paul preached in 1 Corinthians 15:3--4. If anybody tries to go back to the Gospels and force the message Christ preached to the Jews then onto Jews or Gentiles now, in order to be saved, that person is [cursed by God,]{.class_3055} according to the apostle whom you are to follow this side of the Resurrection (1 Cor. 11:1 cf. Gal. 1:8--9).

2 As it is written in the prophets, Behold, I send my messenger before thy face, which shall prepare thy way before thee.

3 The voice of one crying in the wilderness, Prepare ye the way of the Lord, make his paths straight.

The grossly corrupt Alexandrian Greek, and all the corrupt translations that follow it, changes ["the prophets"]{.class_3054} in verse 2 to ["Isaiah,"]{.class_3055} and then it quotes something that Isaiah never wrote. You see, the quote in verse 2 isn't from Isaiah; it's from [Malachi]{.class_3055} (Mal. 3:1). The quote from Isaiah is in verse 3 (Isa. 40:3). That's why the God-honored text in English says ["the prophets"]{.class_3054}; otherwise you []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw91.xhtml_page_9}have what all the Liberals, Modernists, and agnostics keep talking about being in the Bible: a [contradiction.]{.class_3055}

Count 'em: a one, a two. Malachi---one prophet; Isaiah---a second prophet. Hence the correct reading: ["in the PROPHETS"]{.class_3054}---[plural.]{.class_3055}

This change marks the beginning of the end for American Christianity. In 1901, the professors and scholars in the Seminaries, Christian Universities, and Bible Colleges dumped the [King James]{.class_3055} for the Alexandrian text (the [ASV]{.class_3055}). Nearly every modern version since then follows that text to one degree or another, and the majority of the new translations (with [very few]{.class_3055} exceptions) read with the [ASV]{.class_3055} here in Mark 1:2. That is, they all follow a [bald-face lie]{.class_3055} and call it "scripture."

Do you know what is so damning about changing ["the prophets"]{.class_3054} to ["Isaiah"]{.class_3055}---I mean, other than stating something that is [not so]{.class_3055} as Holy Ghost-inspired Scripture? It is the fact that Malachi deals with the [Deity of Christ.]{.class_3055}

Do you know who is speaking in Malachi 3:1 (the verse that the new "Bibles" deny is being quoted in Mark 1:2)? It is [Jehovah]{.class_3055} (["the LORD of hosts"]{.class_3054}). Read it!

["Behold, I will send my messenger . . . ."]{.class_3054} Who is that? Why, it's John the Baptist, the forerunner of Jesus Christ---Mark 1:4. Keep on reading in Malachi 3:1.

[". . . and he shall prepare the way before ME ]{.class_3054}\
[. . . ."]{.class_3054} Who is the ["me"]{.class_3054}? It's the speaker---["the LORD of hosts." ]{.class_3054}But look at the quote in Mark 1:2.

["Behold, I]{.class_3054} \[the Jehovah of Mal. 3:1\][ send my messenger before THY FACE . . . ."]{.class_3054} Whose ["face"]{.class_3054}? The face of ["Jesus Christ, the Son of God"]{.class_3054} (vs. 1).

[". . . which shall prepare THY way before THEE."]{.class_3054} Did you see the Holy Spirit switch the ["me"]{.class_3054} of Malachi 3:1---in reference to ["the LORD of hosts"]{.class_3054}---to ["thee"]{.class_3054}---in reference to [Jesus Christ?]{.class_3055} That is the Holy Ghost telling you, through the Gospel writer (Mark), that the Jesus Christ of the New Testament is the [Jehovah]{.class_3055} of the Old Testament.

Don't you find it peculiar that the new "Bibles" manage to change the text right where it deals with some aspect of the Deity of Christ? Like in 1 Timothy 3:16 and 1 John 5:7 and Acts 4:27, 30 and Luke []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw91.xhtml_page_10}23:42 and Luke 2:33 and John 1:18 and Daniel 3:25 and Acts 3:13, for example. The translators and revisers of the modern versions are being led---they are being led by the same spirit that usurped the title of Jesus Christ (the ["morning star"]{.class_3054}---Rev. 22:16) in Isaiah 14:12 in an [NIV.]{.class_3055}

Back to Mark 1. Verse 3 is a quote from Isaiah 40:3, and it illustrates a great phenomenon in the book of Isaiah in regards to the Scriptures. There are 66 chapters in the book of Isaiah---the same number of chapters as the books in your [King James]{.class_3055} Bible. There is such a distinction in theme and style between chapters 1--39 (the number of books in the Old Testament) and chapters 40--66 (the number of books in the New Testament) that the higher critics strike again and say a "Deutero-Isaiah" wrote the second section (another writer [pretending]{.class_3055} to be Isaiah wrote chapters 40--66).

Do you know [why]{.class_3055} they did that? Isaiah 40:3---up pops John the Baptist, like he does in the first book of the New Testament (book [forty]{.class_3055}---Matthew). Do you know who pops up in chapter 41 of Isaiah? Why, it's God's [Servant]{.class_3055} (Isa. 41:8--9), just like He does in book 41 of the New Testament---Mark. And the last chapter in Isaiah (chap. 66) ends just like the last book of the Bible (book 66---Revelation): with ["new heavens"]{.class_3054} and a ["new earth"]{.class_3054} (Isa. 66:22).

The scholars say, "The chapter and verse numberings in a [King James]{.class_3055} Bible aren't inspired." Then how do you explain what you just saw there in Isaiah? [You can't]{.class_3055} from a human standpoint. That Book was laid out by Somebody sitting up in the future who knows ["the end from the beginning"]{.class_3054} (Isa. 46:10).

God delights in messing up the scholars who mess with His word, did you know that (Matt. 21:16; Luke 10:2)? If you don't believe me, look at Isaiah 40:3 again. There is John the Baptist at the First Advent sandwiched right in the middle of two Second Advent passages (Isa. 40:1--2 and Isa. 40:4--6). [Read the verses!]{.class_3055} Jerusalem's ["warfare"]{.class_3054} isn't ["accomplished"]{.class_3054} yet, and it won't be until after Zechariah 14:1--3. Her ["iniquity"]{.class_3054} isn't "pardoned" yet, and it won't be until Romans 11:26. Those Jews are [still]{.class_3055} receiving ["double"]{.class_3054} from the Lord's hand for the sin of rejecting their Messiah. No valley has been ["exalted"]{.class_3054} yet, and no mountain or hill has been ["made low"]{.class_3054} either; and they won't be until Revelation 6:14, 16:20. And ["all flesh"]{.class_3054} ["together"]{.class_3054} hasn't seen ["the glory of the LORD"]{.class_3054}[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw91.xhtml_page_11} yet either, and they won't until Revelation 1:7.

You see, people don't pay attention to what they read. They figure because they recognize a First Advent prophecy, that the rest of the passage has to be spiritualized to fit the First Advent. That is not the case at all. The Lord will put the First and Second Advents together in a passage---sometimes in the same verse---separated by nothing but a comma or semicolon (see our comments on Luke 4:18--20 in that Commentary). And if you think that's confusing, what do you think it is like when the Holy Spirit gives the two Advents [backwards]{.class_3055} (see our comments on Psalm 89 in that Commentary)?

You say, "Why does the Lord do that?" To mess up a bunch of proud, egotistical stuff-shirts educated beyond their intelligence---that's why. He will do it to [make you study]{.class_3055} and rightly divide that Book like He told you in 2 Timothy 2:15 in a [King James]{.class_3055} Bible and in no other modern version ([King James]{.class_3055} ONLY!).

Now the fact of the matter is that in the Gospels, both Advents are right together, and they aren't separated by an intervening "Church Age" until Acts 7. In Acts 2--3, Peter is telling those Jews they had better repent of crucifying their Messiah because He's coming back in judgment, and Israel's sins won't be blotted out until that takes place (Acts 3:19--20). In Acts 7, Stephen preaches that message to the leaders of the nation one last time, and they reject the message and kill the messenger. When they do that, Jesus stands up---not to receive Stephen's spirit like you hear preached. If Christ had to stand up every time a saint died, He would be like a yo yo, man---up and down all day and all night.

When Jesus Christ went back to Heaven, He sat down at God the Father's right hand, and He doesn't get up until He's ready to come back. The Lord was ready to come back there in Acts 7, and His Father told Him, "Sit down; this is going to take a while." At that point, the blood atonement for sin was preached to the Gentiles (Acts 8), the Apostle to the Gentiles was saved and called to preach (Acts 9), the Gospel was preached to Gentiles without them having to be Jewish proselytes (Acts 10), and God gave the Gentiles 2,000-plus years to get saved. But up until Acts 7, the whole thing is still "in []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw91.xhtml_page_12}flux."

4 John did baptize in the wilderness, and preach the baptism of repentance for the remission of sins.

5 And there went out unto him all the land of Judaea, and they of Jerusalem, and were all baptized of him in the river of Jordan, confessing their sins.

6 And John was clothed with camel's hair, and with a girdle of a skin about his loins; and he did eat locusts and wild honey;

This is John the Baptist, the forerunner of Jesus Christ, according to the prophecies of Malachi and Isaiah in verses 2--3. Notice that he is described in three ways: a ["messenger"]{.class_3054} (vs. 2), a ["voice"]{.class_3054} (vs. 3), and a [preacher]{.class_3055} (vss. 4, 7).

John's purpose was not to promote or push himself. He was the herald of the King. The Apostle John told you in his Gospel that John the Baptist came to bear witness to the Light of the world---Jesus Christ (John 1:6--8). Jesus said John bore ["witness unto the truth"]{.class_3054} (John 5:33). What truth? That he himself was not the Messiah but that Jesus was (John 1:19--20, 30--34), and that He came to take ["away the sin of the world"]{.class_3054} (John 1:29). When John was informed that Jesus' ministry was taking off and eclipsing his own, John said, ["He ]{.class_3054}\[Jesus\][ must increase, but I must decrease"]{.class_3054} (John 3:30). Why? Because John's whole purpose was to speak up for the King, not himself.

Second, he was a ["voice."]{.class_3054} That is not only how Mark described him, that is how John described himself (John 1:23). The important thing about John was not who he was (the son of a priest) or what he did (baptize those who repented), it was the words he [spoke.]{.class_3071} John's father, at his baby's birth, said his son would ["give knowledge of salvation unto his people"]{.class_3054} by what he said (Luke 1:77). John followed the admonition given by God to Isaiah:

["Cry aloud, spare not, lift up thy voice like a trumpet, and shew my people their transgression, and the house of Jacob their sins"]{.class_3054} (Isa. 58:1).

And that brings us to the fact that John was a [preacher;]{.class_3055} specifically a [street preacher.]{.class_3055}[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw91.xhtml_page_13} John's entire ministry was conducted [outdoors.]{.class_3055} As far as the record goes, he never spoke in a synagogue a day in his life. Whenever one of these holier-than-thou brethren, who don't have the courage of a bunny rabbit, tries to tell you that street preaching doesn't do any good and "turns off" people, just remember that John the Baptist, the Lord Jesus, and all the apostles (including the Apostle to the Gentiles) [preached on the street.]{.class_3055} The purpose of street preaching is not to "get results" (although God does indeed give results); it is to proclaim the truth in no uncertain terms so that the lost heathen who go to Walmart, the mall, McDonald's, the grocery store, the beach, the rock concert, the movies, etc., but would never darken the door of a church, can [hear the truth]{.class_3055} and have no excuse in the day of judgment (Ezek. 2:1--7).

John was a [preacher,]{.class_3055} not a [teacher.]{.class_3055} There is nothing wrong with good Bible teaching; it is necessary to impart knowledge to the believer so he can ["grow in grace"]{.class_3054} (2 Pet. 3:18). But preaching demands [action]{.class_3055} on the part of the hearer; it requires the congregation to [do]{.class_3055} something about what they hear.

Lukewarm Laodiceans don't mind teaching; in fact, Paul says, ["they shall HEAP to themselves teachers"]{.class_3054} (2 Tim. 4:3). They will get so many teachers that they are "Piled Higher and Deeper"( Ph.D.s!). But that preaching is something else. They don't like that preaching because it [reproves]{.class_3055} them, [rebukes]{.class_3055} them, and [exhorts]{.class_3055} them to act on those reproofs and rebukes (2 Tim. 4:2).

When it comes to the Gospel and saving sinners, God doesn't want teachers who speak in ["good words and fair speeches"]{.class_3054} (Rom. 16:18) to convince the lost by the wisdom of the world (1 Cor. 1:17--20). He wants [preachers]{.class_3055} who, though they may be [rude]{.class_3055} and [crude]{.class_3055} in their language (2 Cor. 11:6, 10:10), will "tell it like it is" when it comes to sin, Hell, Jesus Christ, and the Gospel.

["For after that in the wisdom of God the world by wisdom knew not God, it pleased God BY THE FOOLISHNESS OF PREACHING to save them that believe"]{.class_3054} (1 Cor. 1:21).

Now notice what John preaches: ["John did . . . preach the baptism of repentance for the remission of sins"]{.class_3054} (vs. 4). And here the Campbellites, Catholics, Lutherans, Episcopalians, []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw91.xhtml_page_14}Methodists, and Pentecostals have a field day. Every one of them, somewhere in its "statement of faith," has baptism necessary to get your sins forgiven. Ironically, the one denomination with baptism in its name doesn't believe or teach that baptism has anything to do with getting your sins forgiven.

The key word in verse 4 is ["for."]{.class_3054} The standard understanding in the phrase ["for the remission of sins"]{.class_3054} is "IN ORDER TO get your sins remitted," but it can't mean that at all. How do you know that to be so? Look at Romans 3:25.

"Whom God hath set forth to be a propitiation through faith in his blood, to declare his righteousness FOR THE REMISSION OF SINS THAT ARE PAST, through the forbearance of God."

Well, if the sins were in the past (the Old Testament), then God's righteousness through the blood atonement of Christ wasn't declared [in order to ]{.class_3055}remit them, for they were [already]{.class_3055} remitted. You see, when the writer of Hebrews said, ["without shedding of blood is no remission"]{.class_3054} (Heb. 9:22), that is [not]{.class_3055} a reference to [the blood of Christ.]{.class_3055} Read the context: it's ["the blood of bulls and of goats"]{.class_3054} that sanctified the people and the Tabernacle (Heb. 9:13, 19--21). That blood brought [remission]{.class_3055} but could not ["take away sins"]{.class_3054} (Heb. 10:4). God forgave ["thousands"]{.class_3054} in the Old Testament (remission) without ["clearing the guilty"]{.class_3054} (redemption) because He had no basis for taking sins away until the Lamb of God came (John 1:29) and shed His blood (Heb. 9:14).

The Old Testament sins were remitted, even though they weren't redeemed (see Heb. 9:15 and comments in that Commentary). That means that the declaration of Christ's righteousness ["for the remission of sins that are past"]{.class_3054} was God declaring the Old Testament saints righteous (i.e., their sins were completely taken away) BECAUSE their sins had been remitted in the Old Testament.

So ["the baptism of repentance for the remission of sins" ]{.class_3054}was baptism BECAUSE a Jew had repented and God had forgiven his sins. If a criminal goes to prison [for]{.class_3055} murder, he is not going to prison [in order to]{.class_3055} murder, but [because]{.class_3055} he [had]{.class_3055} murdered (past tense) someone. In the sense of being a [testimony]{.class_3055} of something God has [already accomplished]{.class_3055} for or in a person, the baptism of John and []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw91.xhtml_page_15}believer's baptism in the New Testament are similar. John's baptism showed that that Jew had [already]{.class_3055} repented and had received ["remission of sins"]{.class_3054}; the baptism of the New Testament believer shows that he has [already]{.class_3055} trusted Christ and ["received the Holy Ghost"]{.class_3054} (Acts 10:47).

Back to Mark 1, verse 5: ["And there went out unto him all the land of Judaea."]{.class_3054} That is all without [distinction,]{.class_3055} not all without [exception.]{.class_3055} Not everyone in Judea and Jerusalem repented and was baptized of John. Luke tells you, ["the Pharisees and lawyers rejected the counsel of God against themselves, being not baptized of him"]{.class_3054} (Luke 7:30). ["All the land of Judaea"]{.class_3054} came out to be baptized of John in the sense that there were people from every part of the land of Judea.

["And were all baptized of him in the river Jordan."]{.class_3054} The Apostle John tells you where at the Jordan River John was baptizing:[ "Bethabara"]{.class_3054} (John 1:28). The name means "house of the ford"; it is spelled ["Bethbarah"]{.class_3054} in the Old Testament (Judg. 7:24). It is the fords where the two spies crossed when they came into the land to spy out Jericho (cf. Josh. 2:7). That is where Joshua and the Israelites crossed into Canaan on dry ground (Josh. 3). It is where the Lord Jesus was baptized (John 1). And it is where He will cross at His Second Advent (see our work on [The Path of the Second Advent]{.class_3055}).

["Confessing their sins."]{.class_3054} This is [not]{.class_3055} Roman Catholic "auricular confession." John is operating in the office of a [prophet,]{.class_3055} not a priest. He is not "absolving" anybody of his sins. The confession is made according to the formula of Proverbs 28:13---confessing and forsaking sin. The forsaking was the repentance and bringing forth ["fruits meet]{.class_3054} \[i.e., fit\][ for repentance"]{.class_3054} (Matt. 3:8). It had nothing to do with saying fifty "Hail Marys" or forty "Our Fathers" and they were OK until next Sunday at Mass. This was confessing their devilment, stopping it, and doing right (see Luke 3:10--14 and comments on that passage in the [Bible Believer's Commentary on Luke]{.class_3055}). And while salvation under "grace through faith" (Eph. 2:8--9) is dependent on [confessing Jesus Christ as your Saviour]{.class_3055}, NOT on confessing your sins (Rom. 10:9--10), still confession of sin is necessary for fellowship [after]{.class_3055} salvation (1 John 1:9), and Paul spends []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw91.xhtml_page_16}two whole chapters in the same book where he talks about being saved by grace through faith, telling his converts how to ["do works meet for repentance"]{.class_3054} (Acts 26:20 cf. Eph. 4--5).

Verse 6: ["And John was clothed with camel's hair, and with a girdle of a skin about his loins."]{.class_3054} John dressed exactly the same as the prophet in whose ["spirit and power"]{.class_3054} he came---Elijah (2 Kings 1:8 cf. Luke 1:17). His diet was ["locusts and wild honey"]{.class_3054} because that was what was available to him in the desert (Luke 1:80). The locusts, of course, were large grasshoppers, and were permitted to be eaten under the Mosaic Law (Lev. 11:22). They were certainly [not]{.class_3055} some kind of "locust plant" like you hear from some "good, godly" commentators who don't have the sense God gave a brass monkey. There isn't one place in that Book where a locust is "the top of a plant" (Adam Clarke's commentary).

7 And preached, saying, There cometh one mightier than I after me, the latchet of whose shoes I am not worthy to stoop down and unloose.

8 I indeed have baptized you with water: but he shall baptize you with the Holy Ghost.

The ["one mightier"]{.class_3054} than John is obviously Jesus because Jesus was ["the Christ"]{.class_3054} and John wasn't (John 3:28). Jesus Christ was sinless; John wasn't (Matt. 3:14). Jesus Christ was the Bridegroom; John was just the "best man" (John 3:29). John was ["of the earth"]{.class_3054}; Jesus Christ was ["from heaven"]{.class_3054} and therefore ["above all"]{.class_3054} (John 3:31). Jesus Christ is ["worthy,"]{.class_3054} not only because He is the Creator in human flesh (Rev. 4:11 cf. John 1:1--3, 14), but because ["he humbled himself, and became obedient unto death"]{.class_3054} to redeem lost sinners (Phil. 2:8--9 cf. Rev. 5:9).

Notice what is [missing]{.class_3055} from verse 8. Mark records John as saying, ["he shall baptize you with the Holy Ghost."]{.class_3054} But if you look at the same quote in Matthew 3:11 and Luke 3:16, the full statement is:[ "he shall baptize you with the Holy Ghost AND WITH FIRE."]{.class_3054} So the canon of "textual criticism" that the shorter reading is to be preferred is [wrong.]{.class_3055} In Mark's Gospel, the shorter reading is [incomplete.]{.class_3055} That is important because in Biblical hermeneutics (how to [interpret]{.class_3055}[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw91.xhtml_page_17} the Bible properly), you should never interpret a [complete]{.class_3055} statement in Scripture (like Matt. 3:11; Luke 3:16) by an [incomplete]{.class_3055} statement (like Mark 1:8); the [complete]{.class_3055} statement always takes precedence. That principle will be important when we get to Mark 10:11--12 and deal with the subject of marriage, divorce, and remarriage.

Now the Pentecostal and Holiness folks have a heyday with John's statement about Jesus baptizing with the Holy Ghost and fire. They make the two the same based on a misreading of Acts 2:3. [No fire]{.class_3055} appeared over anybody's head at Pentecost; it was ["cloven tongues LIKE AS of fire."]{.class_3054} Do you know what ["like as"]{.class_3054} means? It means it's [not the actual thing.]{.class_3055}

The baptism of fire has nothing to do with the baptism of the Holy Ghost, and that is why the Holy Ghost had Mark leave it out of his Gospel: to show you [the two are not the same.]{.class_3055} The baptism of fire turns out to be [literal fire]{.class_3055} (see comments on Luke 3:16--17 in that Commentary). The cross-references are not only the parallel passages in Luke 3:16--17 and Matthew 3:11--12, they are Matthew 13:40--42; 2 Thessalonians 1:7--9; Habakkuk 3:5; Zephaniah 3:8; Deuteronomy 32:22; Psalm 97:3, 21:8--9; Isaiah 66:15--16; and about a dozen more places. The baptism of fire is Hell-fire now for the unsaved and a fire that goes before Christ and burns up His enemies at the Second Advent. That fire turns the land of Edom into a lake of fire (Isa. 34), and that is where the Beast and the False Prophet are cast (Rev. 19:20). If any stupid Charismatic brags about having the baptism of fire, tell him that means he's [headed for Hell.]{.class_3055}

9 And it came to pass in those days, that Jesus came from Nazareth of Galilee, and was baptized of John in Jordan.

10 And straightway coming up out of the water, he saw the heavens opened, and the Spirit like a dove descending upon him:

11 And there came a voice from heaven, saying, Thou art my beloved Son, in whom I am well pleased.

Now up to this point, Jesus had been in Galilee, at His hometown []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw91.xhtml_page_18}of Nazareth, being in subjection to His parents (Luke 2:51). Over in Mark 6:3, He is identified by those who knew Him in Nazareth as ["the carpenter,"]{.class_3054} so He took up His foster father's profession (Matt. 13:55) up until the ministry of John. But now He comes to be baptized and begin His earthly ministry.

Matthew tells you in his account that ["John forbad him, saying, I have need to be baptized of thee, and comest thou to me"]{.class_3054} (Matt. 3:14). Why? Because baptism by John meant the "candidate" had repented of his sins. Jesus Christ was sinless: He had committed nothing of which He needed to repent. John, on the other hand, was a sinner. He figured that [he]{.class_3055} needed to be baptized by the One who [didn't need]{.class_3055} to be baptized.

Jesus submitted to John's baptism, even though He didn't need to, because ["thus it becometh us to fulfil all righteousness"]{.class_3054} (Matt. 3:15). The ["us"]{.class_3054} is a reference to the Trinity, as is evident from what follows when Jesus is baptized (John 1:33). The ["all righteousness"]{.class_3054} is not only Jesus confirming the divine origin of John's baptism (cf. 11:30--31), it speaks also to the real purpose of John's baptism, which was to manifest Christ to Israel (John 1:31). The Father told John he would know the identity of the Messiah for sure when he saw the Holy Spirit descending and remaining on Christ (John 1:33).

Now there are several things we need to get out of verse 10. The first is the [mode of baptism.]{.class_3055} Note that Jesus came ["UP OUT of the water."]{.class_3054} Acts 8:38--39 tells you that the Ethiopian eunuch ["went DOWN . . . INTO the water"]{.class_3054} and came ["UP OUT of the water."]{.class_3054} Baptism is by [immersion,]{.class_3055} not by sprinkling or pouring, because it is a picture of death, burial, and resurrection (Rom. 6:3). You don't bury a corpse by propping it up against a tree and sprinkling or pouring a cup of dirt on it; you plant it under the ground, man. First Corinthians 15:42--44 says, ["It is SOWN"]{.class_3054} like seed.

The second thing to notice is the heavens opening. The first time the heavens were opened in the Bible was in Genesis 7:11 when God brought judgment on the earth by the Flood. God promised to ["open . . . the windows of heaven"]{.class_3054} to those Jews when they paid tithes as they were commanded (Mal. 3:10). That was a reference to God sending rain on the land for the crops to grow (Deut. 28:12). []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw91.xhtml_page_19}Prophetically, that's a reference to the early and latter rains the Lord sends just before the Second Advent (Joel 2:23) after Elijah stops the rain (again!) for three-and-a-half years (Rev. 11:3--6). In the future, the Lord opens heaven to call up the New Testament saints at the Rapture (Rev. 4:1), and again at the Second Advent when Christ returns in power (Rev. 19:11). At Christ's baptism, God opens the heavens to send the Holy Spirit down in a visible way to John in order to identify and affirm His Son.

Notice, thirdly, the Holy Spirit is likened to a clean bird---a [dove.]{.class_3055} The dove first shows up in Genesis, where Noah sends it out to see if the waters are abating on earth. It is contrasted with a [raven,]{.class_3055} an unclean bird (Lev. 11:15; Deut. 14:14). In the Bible, unclean birds are likened to [unclean spirits]{.class_3055} (Isa. 34:6--11 cf. Rev. 18:2). When we get to the Parable of the Sower, here in Mark 4, Christ will liken ["the fowls of the air"]{.class_3054} that steal the good seed to [Satan]{.class_3055} (4:4, 15). We will comment on this more in our comments on "devils," especially ["Beelzebub"]{.class_3054} in Mark 3:22.

Verses 10--11 are one of the classic proof texts for the doctrine of the Trinity. This is not one God playing three parts; neither is this three separate Gods, like the Moslems accuse Christians of believing. These are three Persons, with different responsibilities, who are all [one God]{.class_3055} (a trinity is a tri-unity: three in one). God the Son is in human form being baptized, God the Father is speaking from Heaven, and God the Holy Spirit is descending on God the Son in the form of a dove. The Trinity isn't a [Catholic]{.class_3055} doctrine; it's a [Bible]{.class_3055} doctrine: Matthew 28:19; John 10:27--33, 15:26; 2 Corinthians 13:14, 3:17--18; Romans 8:9; 1 Peter 1:11; 1 John 5:7.

Verse 11:[ "Thou art my beloved Son"]{.class_3054}---a fulfillment of Psalm 2:7. The ["this day"]{.class_3054} in that passage is not eternity past before Genesis 1:1 like the Calvinists teach (see comments on Psa. 2:7 in that Commentary), nor is it a reference to the Resurrection as John R. Rice and many Fundamentalists teach (see our comments on Acts 13:33 in that Commentary). It is a reference to the virgin birth, exactly as you find it in Hebrews 1:4--5.

According to Mohammed, God has no Son (["Holy" Koran,]{.class_3055} Sura 25:2, 2:116, 112:1--4, 17:11, 19:34, 4:171), and those who say He does (e.g., [Jehovah!]{.class_3055}) are [liars]{.class_3055} (Sura 23:89, 37:150). But in the Holy []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw91.xhtml_page_20}Bible, God has all kinds of sons (see our comments on Luke 3:38 in that Commentary). The thing that sets Jesus Christ apart from the angels, Adam, Israel, and Christians is that Jesus Christ was the only Son [physically begotten]{.class_3055} by God. So when pieces of pulp trash like the [NIV]{.class_3055} refuse to correctly translate [monogene]{.class_3055} in John 3:16 (["only begotten"]{.class_3054}) and instead tell you that Jesus is God's ["one and only Son,"]{.class_3055} they are giving you a [bald-faced lie!]{.class_3055} A bald-faced lie by "good, godly, dedicated" Evangelicals and Conservatives, but a bald-faced lie nonetheless.

Notice that Jesus is the Father's ["beloved Son."]{.class_3054} The first time love shows up in your Bible, it is in reference to the love a father has toward his son (Gen. 22:2), and that son just happens to be a type of Jesus Christ (Heb. 11:17--19). God the Father loves His Son (John 5:20), and the Son is ["dear"]{.class_3054} to Him (Col. 1:13).

Brethren, the only reason we Christians are ["beloved of God"]{.class_3054} (Rom. 1:7) is because we are ["accepted in the beloved"]{.class_3054} (Eph. 1:6), and ["the beloved"]{.class_3054} is [His Son. ]{.class_3055}Outside of His Son, God wouldn't even know us (Matt. 7:23 and comments in that Commentary). All this modern baloney about ["God is love," "God is love."]{.class_3054} First John 4:8  is quoted by a bunch of sentimental, thin-skinned Scriptural ignoramuses with no reference to its context at all (1 John 4:7--19). In that passage, the love of God is founded on the sacrifice of God's Son for sinners---READ IT!

God loved this sin-cursed world by sending His ["beloved Son"]{.class_3054} to die for it (John 3:16), and outside of that, God has no love for a Christ-rejecting sinner at all (John 3:36). Don't believe me? Read 2 Thessalonians 1:8--9 and Matthew 25:41, and then tell me with a straight face that that is God loving the sinner but hating the sin. Brethren, God may be good to a lost sinner in order to bring him to repentance (Rom. 2:4). He may be merciful and longsuffering to the sinner in that He puts up with him rejecting Christ over and over again while extending chance after chance to him to be saved. But love? Don't kid yourself. The only way a sinner can have access to the love of God is to go to the one place where that love is manifested---in Jesus Christ (Rom. 8:39). You take Him and God will take you (Eph. 1:6). If you don't, don't expect anything from God but wrath and vengeance.

[". . . in whom I am well pleased."]{.class_3054}[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw91.xhtml_page_21} Now is that statement so or is it not? God has never said that about any other man, woman, or child who ever lived on this earth. You can go through all 66 books of the Bible, from cover to cover, and you will never read that said of anyone else but Jesus Christ. He didn't say it about His friend, Abraham (James 2:23). He didn't say it of the only man in his generation to find grace enough for God not to drown him with the rest of the population of the earth---Noah (Gen. 6:8). He didn't say it of a man so ["perfect and upright"]{.class_3054} that the Lord wasn't afraid to sic the Devil on him---Job (Job 1:1). He didn't say it of the two men to whom He listened when they interceded for Israel (Moses and Samuel---Jer. 15:1). He didn't say it of the King whose throne He established forever (David---2 Sam. 7). He didn't say it of Mary, Peter, Paul, or the best person you know; and He didn't say it of [you or me,]{.class_3055} either (Rom. 3:23).

Modern Christians have been spoiled so much by this "love" mess that they forget they are dealing with a [holy, righteous God]{.class_3055} in Jesus Christ. Don't misunderstand me, I know He loves me because I'm saved. But this idea that when you get to Heaven, you're going to run up to Him right away and hug Him, and you're automatically going to hear ["Well done, thou good and faithful servant"]{.class_3054} (Matt. 25:21) just because you ended up in the right place by grace through faith is for the birds, man. If you are honest with yourself, can't you think of some times in your Christian life when you haven't been ["good"]{.class_3054} or ["faithful"]{.class_3054}? I sure can.

Brethren, when someone in that Bible---people whom you would consider to be [good]{.class_3055} people---encounter that holy God, they don't react the way this modern "love" bunch thinks they will when they see Him. As far as the written record goes, the prophet Isaiah probably had more revealed to him about Jesus Christ than any other writer back there in the Old Testament. Do you know how he reacted when he saw God ["high and lifted up"]{.class_3054} in the Temple (Isa. 6:1)? He said, ["WOE IS ME! for I am undone; because I am a man of unclean lips, and I dwell in the midst of a people of unclean lips: for mine eyes have seen the King, the LORD of hosts"]{.class_3054} (Isa. 6:5).

You say, "Ruckman, that was Old Testament; it was before God []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw91.xhtml_page_22}showed His love by sending His Son." Then let's try the New Testament, shall we. Here is a saved member of the Body of Christ. In fact, he is an apostle; but not just any apostle---the [beloved]{.class_3071} apostle. This is John on the isle of Patmos. How did he react when he saw his Saviour? Well, he didn't run up to Him and throw his arms around Him. Revelation 1:17---["And when I saw him, I FELL AT HIS FEET AS DEAD."]{.class_3054}

How do you "lovey-dovey" Christians account for that? That was the guy who wrote ["God so loved the world."]{.class_3054} He was the one who told you ["God is love"]{.class_3054} and said ["perfect love casteth out fear"]{.class_3054} (1 John 4:18). Evidently he had a more realistic and sober view of the One with whom you are dealing than you do.

Brethren, I will give you some sage advice, and you can do with it whatever you want. In the Army, I learned three things: 1. Get down. 2. Stay down. 3. Don't get up. When I am called "front and center" at the Judgment Seat of Christ, I plan on crawling in with my face to the ground, and I don't plan on looking up until I'm told. That's the only way to treat Deity. You aren't dealing with some sinner like the President of the United States or the Queen of England or the Secretary-General of the United Nations. You are dealing with the sinless Son of God who spoke galaxies into existence and who could raise dead people out of the grave. He's not your "buddy" or "pal"; He's [God Almighty.]{.class_3055}

["Thou art my beloved Son, in whom I am well  pleased."]{.class_3054} That being the case, what does that make Mohammed? He's a dirty, filthy, rotten, [Bible-rejecting liar]{.class_3055} of the worst sort. Mohammed would have you [killed]{.class_3055} for believing that Jesus is God's Son (Sura 9:29--30). In fact, if a Moslem trusts Jesus Christ as Saviour, other Moslems are obligated to kill him (Sura 4:91; [Hadith,]{.class_3055} Bukhari, Vol. VI, Book 61, No. 577). That's why when a Moslem in Niger, India, Indonesia, Pakistan, Afghanistan, Egypt, Iran, etc., converts to Christianity, he is usually [poisoned to death]{.class_3055} by his own family.

Tell me about it, man. Years ago, a couple of our young men brought four Moslems to church from a freighter docked here in Pensacola. Back then, we had a lady in our congregation who could act as an interpreter in Arabic for us, and after the service, three of those men ended up getting saved. Before leaving, they asked me, []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw91.xhtml_page_23}"What do we do when we get back to the ship? When our shipmates find out what we did, they'll kill us." That's what a [Moslem]{.class_3055} has to say about it; not some idiot used by the Marxists, like Barack Obama, John Kerry, Hillary Clinton, or George W. Bush. That is not just something practiced by "radical terrorists"; it is a common occurrence among [normal, average, everyday]{.class_3055} Moslems.

Those Moslem sailors knew what would happen, even if your President doesn't. I don't know whatever happened to those three men. They knew they should be witnesses for Christ, but they knew what would happen if they spoke up for their Saviour: [they would be killed.]{.class_3055}

God is ["well pleased"]{.class_3054} with His Son. The Father was pleased with Christ's obedience and atonement for sin (Phil. 2:8--9). If you want to satisfy God's demand for absolute righteousness, you had better accept the only righteousness with which God is pleased: [the righteousness of His Son]{.class_3055} (Rom. 3:25--26).

12 And immediately the Spirit driveth him into the wilderness.

13 And he was there in the wilderness forty days, tempted of Satan; and was with the wild beasts; and the angels ministered unto him.

This is Mark's very abbreviated account of the temptation of Christ. You can read the full accounts in Matthew 4:1--11 and Luke 4:1--13, and see our comments on those passages in their respective Commentaries.

Notice that Jesus isn't just ["led"]{.class_3054} by the Holy Spirit, as you read in Matthew and Luke; He is [driven.]{.class_3055} The picture here isn't of someone being [directed]{.class_3055} (led); it is someone being [forcefully put]{.class_3055} into a situation (driven). The word ["driveth"]{.class_3054} speaks to the [intensity]{.class_3055} of the Holy Spirit's leadership in the matter. This is not an incidental skirmish in the course of events, as Christ often had with the Pharisees and scribes throughout His ministry. This is direct, intentional confrontation. The Holy Spirit is forcing the Lord's "face-off" with the Devil.

The ["forty days"]{.class_3054} of verse 13 should remind the student of the []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw91.xhtml_page_24}Scriptures of the two men, at the end of the Old Testament, who are connected with Christ's Advent: Moses and Elijah (Mal. 4:4--5). Like Christ, both Moses and Elijah are in the wilderness fasting for forty days and nights (Exod. 34:28; 1 Kings 19:8). When they do, they both end up at Mt. Sinai (Horeb). So without a doubt, ["the wilderness"]{.class_3054} where Christ is tempted is ["the wilderness of SIN"]{.class_3054} (Exod. 16:1). Coincidental, of course, of course (don't you know!).

Now Mark simply says Jesus was ["tempted of Satan"]{.class_3054} (vs. 13), but he gives no details. We won't go into the details or prophetic implications of Christ's temptations here. You can see our comments in the [Bible Believer's Commentary on Matthew ]{.class_3055}(Matt. 4) and the [Bible Believer's Commentary on Luke]{.class_3055} (Luke 4). We will say this: Jesus ["was in all points tempted like as we are, yet without sin"]{.class_3054} (Heb. 4:15). The ["points"]{.class_3054} are given in 1 John 2:16; they are the same ["points"]{.class_3054} in which Eve was tempted in Genesis 3:6.

I had someone ask me one time in an open Bible forum, "How could Jesus know the temptations of a married man if He were never married?" Well, the text doesn't say He faced every particular temptation you and I may face. It says: ["in all POINTS."]{.class_3054} Every temptation falls into one of three categories: it is a temptation to ["the lust of the flesh"]{.class_3054} or to ["the lust of the eyes"]{.class_3054} or to ["the pride of life."]{.class_3054} Every temptation you face will be one of those three.

Eve[ "saw that the tree was good for FOOD"]{.class_3054} (["lust of the flesh"]{.class_3054}), ["and that it was pleasant to the EYES"]{.class_3054} (["lust of the eyes"]{.class_3054}), and that it was ["to be desired to make one WISE"]{.class_3054} (["pride of life"]{.class_3054}). Christ's temptations were: 1) "You're hungry; turn the stones into bread" (["lust of the flesh"]{.class_3054}), 2) "Jump off the highest point of the Temple and let everyone see the angels catch you" (["pride of life"]{.class_3054}), 3) "Look at all the kingdoms this world has to offer you; just fall down and worship me, and they're yours" (["lust of the eyes"]{.class_3054}).

Now Christ did what no one else could: He "faced-off" with the Devil, and [He beat him.]{.class_3055} Up to this point, Satan was the all-time grand champion of the universe. He took on Adam and knocked him out. He took on Noah and got him drunk. He took on Abraham and got him to lie. He took on Moses and got him to strike the rock when he should have spoken to it. He got Joshua not to pray. He got Gideon []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw91.xhtml_page_25}to set up an ephod as an idol. He got Samson whore-hopping. He got David to commit adultery and murder. He got Solomon involved with ["outlandish women"]{.class_3054} who caused him ["to sin"]{.class_3054} (Neh. 13:26). The Devil never had any problem taking down any descendant of Adam and Eve (Rom. 3:23, 5:12), but he had a problem with Jesus Christ. Why was that?[ Because Jesus Christ had no human father from whom to inherit a sin nature.]{.class_3055} There was no propensity in Jesus Christ to sin.

Don't misunderstand me: the temptations were real temptations. Those who teach the so-called "impeccability of Christ"---that Jesus [could not]{.class_3055} have sinned---don't know about what they are talking. The weakness of Christ's earthly body certainly made a temptation like turning the stones into bread appealing. But Jesus was in the same position Adam was before the Fall. With no "original sin," He was completely free to [turn down sin.]{.class_3055} So when it comes to the "impeccability of Christ," I teach exactly what the Scriptures say about the matter: Christ [did not ]{.class_3055}sin---in thought, word, or deed.

If you want to know one of the reasons God the Father was ["well pleased"]{.class_3054} with His Son, [that's]{.class_3055} why. The writer of Hebrews says Jesus Christ ["is holy, harmless, undefiled, separate from sinners"]{.class_3054} (Heb. 7:26).

Now the reason for Christ's temptation, [when it comes to us,]{.class_3055} is that, having gone through temptations Himself and overcome them, He is able to assist and support us when we are tempted.

["For in that he himself hath suffered being tempted, he is able to succour them that are tempted"]{.class_3054} (Heb. 2:18).

Christian, you [can]{.class_3055} overcome temptation. How do you know? [Your Saviour did it.]{.class_3055} He showed you how: use the word of God to resist the Devil (James 4:7). God will provide ["a way to escape"]{.class_3054} the temptation if you will only seek it in prayer and then [take it]{.class_3055} when it is revealed to you. That's our problem: we don't take the "fire escape" when the Lord shows us where it is. Our problem is we usually don't want or avail ourselves of the ["succour"]{.class_3054} when it is provided to us.

Now there is an interesting "addendum" in verse 13. After telling you that Christ was ["tempted of Satan,"]{.class_3054} Mark adds: ["and was with the wild beasts."]{.class_3054} This certainly could have been literal ["wild beasts"]{.class_3054}[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw91.xhtml_page_26} as you read of in Leviticus 26:22; 1 Samuel 17:46; Psalm 50:11; and Acts 10:12, 11:6. But far more likely, these are [evil spirits]{.class_3055} connected with Satan, as you will find them in Isaiah 13:21--22, 34:14 and Jeremiah 50:39. Colossians 2:15 says that Christ ["spoiled principalities and powers"]{.class_3054} and ["made a shew of them openly, triumphing over them."]{.class_3054} Of course, in the context of Colossians 2, that is a reference to Christ triumphing over Satan and his devils [at the cross,]{.class_3055} but what you read here in Mark 1:13 is certainly a precursor to that triumph, since Christ's victory at His temptation makes His high priestly work of the blood atonement possible (Heb. 2:17--18, 4:15, 7:26--27).

["And the angels ministered unto him."]{.class_3054} The "ministration," here, is probably in the form of food (cf. 1 Kings 19:5--8), but the word [minister]{.class_3055} means "to serve" (if a verb) and "a servant" (if a noun). That is essentially what angels are: spirit beings that attend and serve God (Heb. 1:7). Remember that the next time some Fundamentalist tells you an angel is a "messenger" so he can make the angels of the seven churches in Revelation the pastors of those churches. Delivering messages is only [one]{.class_3055} way angels serve the Lord.

In the life of Christ, angels ministered to the Lord by announcing His birth (Luke 2:9--14) and resurrection (Luke 24:4--6, 23; John 20:12); an angel also appeared to Christ in Gethsemane and strengthened Him (Luke 22:43). And if Christ had wished, 72,000 of them would have come and wiped out every person on earth to prevent Him from being crucified (Matt. 26:53 cf. 2 Kings 19:35).

14 Now after that John was put in prison, Jesus came into Galilee, preaching the gospel of the kingdom of God,

15 And saying, The time is fulfilled, and the kingdom of God is at hand: repent ye, and believe the gospel.

Mark jumps abruptly to the beginning of Christ's ministry, which starts in earnest after Herod throws John in prison. The details of that are found in Mark 6:17--20, and we will cover that when we get to those verses. The import of what you read here is that John's ministry effectively ends. After Christ returns from His temptation, []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw91.xhtml_page_27}John points his own disciples to Jesus (John 1:35--37), and John's ministry decreases while Christ's ministry increases (John 3:30) until John is finally imprisoned by Herod. In the chronology of the life of Christ, the events of the end of John 1 through the end of John 4 take place before verse 14 here in Mark 1.

Now notice what Jesus preaches here in verse 14: ["the gospel of the kingdom of God."]{.class_3054} That is NOT the Gospel you believe now, in this age, to be saved. [That]{.class_3055} Gospel is called ["the gospel of the grace of God"]{.class_3054} (Acts 20:24), and Paul defines it in 1 Corinthians 15:1--4. It is the death of Christ for sin, His burial, and His resurrection, all according to Scripture. That is NOT what Jesus is preaching here in Mark 1.

What is He preaching? ["The time is fulfilled, and the kingdom of God is at hand: repent ye, and believe the gospel."]{.class_3054} No blood atonement for sins, no burial, no resurrection, and nothing from the Scriptures about any of those things.

Of course, you can make spiritual application, especially that last part about repenting and believing the gospel, but you can only do that in light of what Paul says in his epistles. [Nobody in Matthew, Mark, Luke, or John had anything from Romans--Philemon;]{.class_3055} and Christ hadn't died for anyone's sins yet in Mark 1. Moreover, whenever Christ did speak of His death, it was never in connection with dying for anyone's sins, at least not until you get to the Last Supper (Matt. 26:28). Jesus doesn't tell anyone in Mark 1 that He is going to die for his sins and rise for his justification.

Now notice the difference between what Christ preaches here in Mark 1:15 and the same account as given by Matthew in Matthew 4:17---["Repent: for the kingdom of HEAVEN is at hand."]{.class_3054} Because of that, nearly all the expositors and commentators will tell you that the Kingdom of God and the Kingdom of Heaven are the same thing. They do that with no regard at all to the [differences]{.class_3055} between the two kingdoms as given in the Scriptures.

What is the first difference? God is not Heaven and Heaven is not God, not unless you are a pantheist. God is the Creator of Heaven, whether you are talking about the first heaven where the birds fly (Gen. 1:20) or the second heaven where the stars are (Gen. 1:15--17) or the third heaven where He lives (2 Cor. 12:2--4).

Secondly, the Holy Spirit defines both kingdoms for you in the Scriptures. The definitive verse on the Kingdom of God is found in Romans 14:17.

["For the kingdom of God is NOT MEAT AND DRINK]{.class_3054} \[i.e., it's not anything [physical]{.class_3055} you can stick in your mouth\][; but RIGHTEOUSNESS, and PEACE, and JOY in the HOLY GHOST."]{.class_3054}

So the Kingdom of God is a [spiritual]{.class_3055} kingdom. Jesus affirms this when He says, ["the kingdom of God is WITHIN you"]{.class_3054} (Luke 17:21). Then it's not an [outward, visible]{.class_3055} kingdom (Luke 17:20). A man enters this kingdom when he gets a new birth---a [spiritual]{.class_3055} birth (John 3:5--7).

That is [not]{.class_3055} the case of the Kingdom of Heaven. In the definitive verse on that kingdom, Jesus said, ["the kingdom of heaven suffereth violence, and the violent take it BY FORCE"]{.class_3054} (Matt. 11:12). Well, if it is a kingdom that suffers violence and is taken by force, then it is not a spiritual kingdom. It is a kingdom of swords, knives, clubs, staves, spears, shields, bows and arrows, missiles, etc.---those are the instruments of violence and force; they are all [physical]{.class_3055} (cf. 2 Cor. 10:4).

The truth of the matter is that when Jesus started His ministry, He preached [both]{.class_3055} the Kingdom of Heaven [and]{.class_3055} the Kingdom of God. That is because He is the King of both. He promised a spiritual kingdom in the bodies of those who believed Him and in whom the Holy Ghost would come. He promised a physical kingdom for those who would accept His spiritual kingdom in them first (Matt. 6:33). That kingdom is promised to those Jews over and over again in the Old Testament (e.g., read Isa. 2, 65--66; Zech. 14; Psa. 2, 110). Its capital will be Jerusalem (Matt. 5:35), and I don't mean New Jerusalem (Ezek. 48:30--35 cf. Rev. 21:12--17).

When Christ showed up, both kingdoms were ["at hand"]{.class_3054} because the King of both kingdoms was right there. If those Jews had repented as they were told by John the Baptist and Jesus, and had accepted Jesus Christ as their Messiah, the Lord would have put them in a spiritual kingdom in preparation for the physical kingdom that He would bring in by throwing Caesar off his throne and taking over the rulership of this world Himself. But the Jewish leadership []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw91.xhtml_page_29}rejected Him, and they got the nation as a whole to reject Him. So off goes the literal, physical Kingdom of Heaven for 2,000-plus years until the Second Advent, and the only kingdom left is the spiritual Kingdom of God which Paul preaches (Acts 20:25, 28:23, 31) and into which a man enters when he believes and receives the risen Saviour through ["the gospel of the grace of God."]{.class_3054} When it comes to the physical manifestation of that kingdom (note ["SEE the kingdom of God"]{.class_3054} in John 3:3) that shows up when the physical Kingdom of Heaven shows up (and that kingdom comes in with violence and force just as Christ said it would---Rev. 19:11--15), the only way a saint in this age can get an inheritance then is to live a sanctified life of service and suffering for his Lord and Saviour right now (Acts 14:22; 1 Cor. 6:9--10; Gal. 5:19--21; 2 Tim. 2:12).

The whole thing is perfectly evident to anyone who accepts what the Scriptures say, as they say it, in the context in which they say it. The only way you could screw it up is to go to the "original Hebrew" or the "original Greek" and make the Scriptures say what they do not say, but what you want them to say, in order to prove your own pet hobbyhorse. The "hobbyhorse," in this case, turns out to be a [braying jackass.]{.class_3055}

16 Now as he walked by the sea of Galilee, he saw Simon and Andrew his brother casting a net into the sea: for they were fishers.

17 And Jesus said unto them, Come ye after me, and I will make you to become fishers of men.

18 And straightway they forsook their nets, and followed him.

19 And when he had gone a little further thence, he saw James the son of Zebedee, and John his brother, who also were in the ship mending their nets.

20 And straightway he called them: and they left their father Zebedee in the ship with the hired servants, and went after him.

This is the calling of the first disciples, and the first four men Christ calls to follow Him are all commercial fishermen. This is not a []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw91.xhtml_page_30}coincidental choice on the part of Jesus. He calls fishermen and then says He will make them ["fishers of men."]{.class_3054} Then men are like fish (Hab. 1:14).

This brings up a great picture of the universe ignored by Fundamental, Conservative "creationists" who try to explain Genesis 1:2 and Genesis 7:11 from a naturalistic viewpoint, acceptable to lost scientists, with ice canopies and underground oceans. You see, fish live underwater. If men are likened to fish, that means that every man, woman, and child who has ever lived on this earth has lived underneath a body of water that would make the combined waters of the Atlantic and Pacific Oceans look like a drop in a bucket in comparison.

That water first shows up in Genesis 1:2, where the earth stood in it and out of it after being overflowed with it (see 2 Pet. 3:5--6 and our comments on the passage in the [Bible Believer's Commentary on the General Epistles,]{.class_3055} Vol. I). In Genesis 1:6--8, God made a ["firmament,"]{.class_3054} which He called ["Heaven"]{.class_3054} to separate the waters beneath it on earth (the oceans, rivers, and underground aquifers) from the waters above it. The waters above the firmament had nothing to do with clouds or any "ice canopy" because that firmament included the place where God put the sun, moon, and stars (Gen. 1:14--16). How do you know that for sure? Psalm 148:3--4.

["Praise ye him, sun and moon: praise him, all ye stars of light."]{.class_3054} Do you have the context? It's not the first heaven where the clouds are; it's the second heaven: outer space. But just so there is no mistaking what the Lord is saying, look at the next verse: ["Praise him, ye heavens of heavens, and ]{.class_3054}\[get it!\][ ye waters that be above the HEAVENS]{.class_3054} \[plural\][."]{.class_3054}

That's water beyond Alpha Draconis. It's described in Job 38:30 and Revelation 4:6, 15:2.

So every human being on earth is a Baptist: they're immersed underwater. And one of these days, if you are a "fish" that has been "caught," you are going to be hauled out of the water, up to where the real "Big Fisherman" is (and I don't mean Peter). So in baptism, you are put under the water (death) and brought up out of the water (resurrection), just as you will be brought up past that ["sea of glass"]{.class_3054} one day at the Rapture (Rev. 4:1--6). That is what all is []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw91.xhtml_page_31}involved in Christ's baptism and in His choice for His first disciples.

Now, commercial fishermen can be some of the roughest individuals you will ever encounter. Peter had a foul mouth; James and John had bad tempers (Mark 3:17; Luke 9:54).

I knew a fellow named Coy Raffield. He ran away from home when he was fifteen and stayed out on the water, working on those fishing boats, until he was 55. He got called to preach, but he never really did leave the water. He would still go out on that boat and fish all night. Then there was Wayne Mund. He worked in a fishing fleet there in Alabama. He got saved and called to preach. He pastored a church up north for a while, but finally ended up back in Bon Secour, Alabama, with a ministry to commercial fishermen there on the Bon Secour River.

Those fellows all carried knives (supposedly to clean fish), and they would get into knife fights. That's because they are out there, with no feminine influence, doing a dirty job. But God can save a roughneck like that and clean him up and get him to preach. That's what He did with Peter, James, John, and Andrew.

["Come ye after me, and I will make you to become fishers of men"]{.class_3068-0} (vs. 17). If I wanted to "test your water" to find out if you are a real disciple of Christ, do you know what I would do? Well, I wouldn't check your doctrinal statement. There are churches these days that have a doctrinal statement as straight as a gun barrel (and just as empty), and after you have talked with the pastor for five minutes, you can tell he doesn't believe half of what's on the list.

I wouldn't ask you what church you attend or from what "bastion of orthodoxy" you graduated. Many a Christian joins a work to "get lost in the crowd." He (or she) wants to be associated with the work because of [reputation.]{.class_3055} If the work has a reputation for being a "soulwinning institution," that person can sit back on his dead can and do nothing but still have the reputation of being a soulwinner because he is associated with the work. Oh yeah, man! There is many a professor at Bob Jones, Liberty University, Tennessee Temple, BIOLA, Moody, Clearwater, and Pensacola Christian who hasn't led a soul to Christ in twenty years but has the reputation of being a "soulwinner" because of where he teaches.

If I wanted to find out if you are a real disciple of Jesus Christ, I []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw91.xhtml_page_32}would want to know one thing: are you a fisher of men? Are you actively engaged in trying to catch men for Jesus Christ? I am not concerned with [how many]{.class_3055} fish you have on your stringer as long as you are fishing. And if you are not making an effort to fish for men, you are not a real disciple.

When I wrote the thesis for my Ph.D. from Bob Jones University, it was on "A History of Practical Theology in the Light of the Book of Acts." My thesis was if a man is not making an effort to win lost sinners to Jesus Christ, he is [not orthodox,]{.class_3055} no matter what his profession of faith is. Profession to a lost pile of papers no one in the last 1,900 years has ever seen doesn't mean a hill of beans if one is not [doing]{.class_3055} what the "original autographs" say to do.

That thesis was aimed at the men who taught me at Bob Jones University because that was exactly their case: they were hiding behind the evangelistic reputation of the founder of the institution (Bob Jones Sr.). When it came time to be examined for my doctorate, the whole thing came out as to what "Old Man Bob" had on his faculty: baby-sprinkling, Amillennial, five-point Calvinists who had no burden for lost souls and had [never]{.class_3055} led a soul to Christ. They all could subscribe to "the fundamentals" (as any lost Catholic), and they were all [backslidden humanists]{.class_3055} with no authority higher than the man who gave them their paychecks. But that is nothing peculiar to Bob Jones University. Churches in America are filled with such Christians, even where their theology is different.

Are you a "fisher of men"? What are you doing to get the Gospel to the lost? If all you are doing is occupying a pew and listening to one to three sermons a week, you are [not]{.class_3055} a disciple of Jesus Christ, even if you are saved.

Now there is an interesting "side note" to the ["fishers of men"]{.class_3054} of verse 17 back in the Old Testament. Back in Jeremiah, God told those Jews that if they didn't get right, He was going to throw them out of the Promised Land. But in Jeremiah 16:16, the Lord tells them this: ["Behold, I will send many fishers, saith the LORD, and they shall fish them . . . ."]{.class_3054}

According to Mark 1:17, that's a reference to soulwinners trying to win those Jews to the Lord. So in the book of Acts, the Lord sends "fishers" after those Jews: Peter, John, and the rest of the Twelve. []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw91.xhtml_page_33}Then the Jewish leaders stone Stephen, and the Lord saved the Apostle Paul, and not only sends him to the Gentiles, but to the ["the children of Israel"]{.class_3054} as well (Acts 9:15). When Paul goes to Asia Minor, Greece, and Rome, he preaches ["to THE JEW FIRST, and also to the Greek"]{.class_3054} (Rom. 1:16). He goes to where the Jews meet and preaches to the Jews of the Dispersion before he preaches to the Gentiles. The book of Acts ends with the Jews of the Dispersion rejecting Christ, and the Holy Spirit turning to the Gentiles for the next 1,900 years (Acts 28:25--28). With that, do you know what happens next?

Jeremiah 16:16, [". . . and after will I send for many hunters, and they shall hunt them from every mountain, and from every hill, and out of the holes of the rocks."]{.class_3054}

The "fishermen" try to catch them, and when the nation, as a whole, won't be caught, God sends in the hunters. He sends in the Romans in A.D. 70 and again in A.D. 135. He sends in the Mohammedans in the Seventh Century. He sends in the Catholics in the Dark Ages. He sends in the Russians in the Nineteenth Century. He sends in the Nazis in the Twentieth Century. Why? Those Jews wouldn't take the fishers.

We have discussed the nets of verses 18--19 in our remarks under Luke 5:2 in that Commentary, so we refer you there. The net of verse 16 is a cast net, or throw net. The net is circular with weights around the edges so it will sink down and surround the fish. There is a line from the net to the caster's hand, where it is held so the net can be retrieved after a cast. The net is thrown from the shoulder. While the net can be cast from a boat, here in verse 16, Andrew and Peter are either casting from shore or wading in a little way into the water and casting there. The net of verse 19 is probably a seine net or trammel net---both are kinds of dragnets (see Hab. 1:16).

Verse 18: ["And straightway they forsook their nets, and followed him."]{.class_3054} This is the second of three calls to these disciples. The first was in John 1:40--42. I believe the modern term for that call would be a "meet and greet." Andrew and Peter meet Jesus for the first time in John 1. In Mark 1 (and its parallel passage in Matt. 4), Jesus calls them to follow Him, and they forsake their nets. But it is evident from Luke 5:1--5, that the four were "part-time" disciples. []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw91.xhtml_page_34}When they weren't following Christ, they were attending to their fishing business. But with the call of Luke 5:10, ["they forsook ALL, and followed him"]{.class_3054} (Luke 5:11). You may dismiss all the apostates who try to raise a contradiction in the Scriptures that does not exist.

Although this is only a partial forsaking in verses 18--20, it is still a move in the right direction that makes Luke 5 possible. Notice in verse 20 that James and John leave their father to follow Christ. The demand of discipleship is to love Christ more than family (Matt. 10:37; Luke 14:26), and a reward is promised for leaving family for the sake of Christ and the Gospel (Mark 10:29--30).

21 And they went into Capernaum; and straightway on the sabbath day he entered into the synagogue, and taught.

22 And they were astonished at his doctrine: for he taught them as one that had authority, and not as the scribes.

Now Christ comes to the town He made His "headquarters" during His earthly ministry: ["Capernaum"]{.class_3054} (Matt. 9:1). The name means "city of Nahum." Nahum was one of two prophets who prophesied to Nineveh; the other being Jonah. [Both]{.class_3055} were from Galilee, which means those Jewish Rabbis in John 7:52 were a bunch of lying rascals. Jesus Christ did not hesitate to identify Himself with the two prophets from Galilee. Jonah was the only prophet to whom Jesus personally compared Himself (Matt. 12:40), and the hometown of Nahum was where Jesus performed most of His miracles (Matt. 11:20--23).

The text says that at Capernaum, ["on the sabbath day he entered into the synagogue and taught."]{.class_3054} Luke 4:16 says that was ["his custom,"]{.class_3054} and the example given in that passage is the classic example of ["rightly dividing"]{.class_3054}: Luke 4:17--21 (which see in that Commentary).

Note that this is all a [Jewish]{.class_3055} set-up, not a Christian one. This is a [synagogue,]{.class_3055} not a church.[ "The law and the prophets"]{.class_3054}---the [Old Testament]{.class_3055}---were read, not the New Testament (Acts 13:15). Whenever you read in the book of Acts about Paul going to the local synagogue on the Sabbath day, it has [nothing]{.class_3055} to do with New []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw91.xhtml_page_35}Testament worship. Paul was using his Jewish identity as an opportunity to preach the Gospel to his kinsmen (1 Cor. 9:20). The New Testament believers met on the first day of the week (1 Cor. 16:2; Acts 20:7)---NOT THE SABBATH---because their Saviour arose on the first day of the week (Matt. 28:1) and the Holy Spirit came down on the first day of the week (Acts 2:1--2 cf. Lev. 23:16). So the Seventh-day Adventists are full of beans.

Verse 22: ["And they were astonished at his doctrine: for he taught them as one that had authority, and not as the scribes."]{.class_3054} The teaching of Jesus [with authority]{.class_3055} is contrasted with the teaching of the scribes [with no authority.]{.class_3055} The scribes, of course, were the ones who copied the Scriptures; they were so well versed in its contents that they knew exactly how many letters were in a book. We would compare them today with Bible scholars. If anyone should have had authority, it should have been they. It is ironic, but true, that the ones who profess to know the Bible the most, believe it the least.

Nothing has changed in 2,000 years. The professors and "doctors" in the major Christian institutions of higher learning have no authority any higher than their own opinions or the opinions of those they like. Their authority is not "Thus saith the Lord," but the general consensus of scholars---[humanism.]{.class_3055} If the scholars run across something in the Scriptures they don't understand or don't believe, they simply change the Scriptures to match their own ignorance or infidelity.

The problem since Genesis 1:1 has always been one of authority: who's in charge? Who's in charge of the universe (Isa. 14:13--14)? Who's in charge of the nation? Who's in charge of the home? Who's in charge of the church? Final authority is [the]{.class_3055} issue.

The Christian says, "Well, the Bible is the final authority." Of course, the Catholic Church will make tradition and the Pope equal authorities with the Bible, but don't kid yourself, all three [can't]{.class_3055} be equal.
