When the Bible says one thing, and tradition and the Pope say another, who decides which is right? Why, the Pope does, according to the Council of Trent and Pope Pius IX. That makes the [Pope]{.class_3055} the final authority for a Catholic, not the Bible. You will find the same type of thing going on in the Jehovah's Witnesses, the Seventh-day []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw91.xhtml_page_36}Adventists, the Mormons, or any cult. Where the cult contradicts the Bible, the teachings of the cult (the[ Book of Mormon,]{.class_3055} the teachings of Charles Russell or Ellen G. White, Mary Baker Eddy's [Key to the Scriptures,]{.class_3055} etc.) trump the Holy Scriptures, making the cult the final authority, not the Bible.

The Fundamentalist says, "No, we reject any other authority but the Bible." [Which Bible?]{.class_3055} Are [any]{.class_3055} of them the final authority? Are [all]{.class_3055} of them the final authority? If you are a Fundamentalist, a Conservative, or an Evangelical, what really is your final authority? When one Bible says one thing and another Bible says something else (e.g., John 1:18; 1 Tim. 3:16; 1 John 5:7; Col. 1:14; Luke 2:33; Acts 4:27, 30), who decides which Bible is right? Well, whoever does is [God,]{.class_3055} because only God can decide what are and what are not His words.

When some jackleg stands up and says, "The word in the originals is actually . . . ," "This is an unfortunate rendering in the [King James,"]{.class_3055} "A better translation would be . . . ," "This is not in the best manuscripts," do you know what that person is doing? He's playing [God. He's]{.class_3055} deciding what God says; therefore [he's]{.class_3055} the final authority.

The scholar says, "Well, all the translations have mistakes in them." Then tell us, doctor, which Bible is ["the holy scriptures"]{.class_3054} (Rom. 1:2; 2 Tim. 3:15)? "Well, I [like]{.class_3055} how this translation reads, and I [prefer]{.class_3055} this one, and [I recommend]{.class_3055} this one . . . ." Then [you're]{.class_3055} God. [You]{.class_3055} are the one deciding what is and what is not the word of God: [you]{.class_3055} are the final authority.

Listen, people, I don't believe the [King James]{.class_3055} Bible is the word of God because I "prefer" it. I would just as soon "prefer" some of it wasn't there, to be right frank with you. You say, "How could you say that, Ruckman?" Hey, I know ["all the wickedness"]{.class_3054} my ["heart is privy to"]{.class_3054} (1 Kings 2:44). I know the verses in that Book that cross my old nature, and I'll be honest with you and tell you the [source]{.class_3055} of any animosity I have toward the Book. The scholars won't. They'll change what God said and attribute it to "good, godly" motives. Their heart is just as black as a coal cellar at midnight.

My final authority is a Book, and I can [show]{.class_3055} you the Book. It's not some scraps of lost paper that were never gathered into one book. The [Authorized Version]{.class_3055} of the Holy Bible is my [final]{.class_3055} authority. []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw91.xhtml_page_37}I don't decide what the Book says. I read it and believe it as the words of the living God, not just man's opinion as to what God said (1 Thess. 2:13). And I adjust my doctrine and actions to match it, not the other way around.

23 And there was in their synagogue a man with an unclean spirit; and he cried out,

24 Saying, Let us alone; what have we to do with thee, thou Jesus of Nazareth? art thou come to destroy us? I know thee who thou art, the Holy One of God.

25 And Jesus rebuked him, saying, Hold thy peace, and come out of him.

26 And when the unclean spirit had torn him, and cried with a loud voice, he came out of him.

27 And they were all amazed, insomuch that they questioned among themselves, saying, What thing is this? what new doctrine is this? for with authority commandeth he even the unclean spirits, and they do obey him.

28 And immediately his fame spread abroad throughout all the region round about Galilee.

The first time Christ encounters ["an unclean spirit"]{.class_3054} in His ministry, it is in a [religious service.]{.class_3055} The biggest devils into which you will ever run won't be in a bar, a strip club, or a porn theater. They won't be found in the Illuminati, the Club of Rome, the Bilderbergers, the international bankers, the Congress, the Presidency, the Supreme Court, the European Union, or the UN. According to the Book, the largest concentration of evil spirits in the world is found in a [religious whore]{.class_3055} (Rev. 18:2--3).

The translators and revisers of the new "Bibles" think that when they sit down to do their work that the Devil has nothing to do with it one way or the other. Why, that's exactly the place you would expect to find Satan: questioning (Gen. 3:1) and subtracting from (Luke 4:10--11) what God said. If you want proof, to the tune of 690 pages, that the modern versions are ["given by inspiration of"]{.class_3054} [Satan, ]{.class_3055}get Gail Riplinger's work [New Age Bible Versions.]{.class_3055} She will show you how the "clearer and more accurate" translations take the honor [away]{.class_3055}[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw91.xhtml_page_38} from God and Jesus Christ, and give it to the Devil (e.g., see how the [NIV]{.class_3055} and[ NASV]{.class_3055} take the title of Jesus Christ found in Rev. 22:16 and assign it to Satan in Isa. 14:12).

Back to Mark 1: ["Saying, Let US alone"]{.class_3054}---[plural.]{.class_3055} Notice that ["AN unclean spirit"]{.class_3054}---singular---is made up of a [plurality]{.class_3055} of spirits. That's exactly the case of the maniac of Gadara (["MY name"]{.class_3054}---singular---["is Legion: for WE are MANY"]{.class_3054}---plural \[5:9\]) and those ["fowls of the air"]{.class_3054} (plural) in the Parable of the Sower (4:3--4; referred to as ["Satan"]{.class_3054} in the singular down in 4:15). The Devil is a spirit (singular) composed of unclean spirits (plural).

Verse 24:[ "Saying, Let us alone; what have we to do with thee, thou Jesus of Nazareth?"]{.class_3054} Nothing, which is why Paul didn't let the girl with the ["spirit of divination"]{.class_3054} promote his "evangelistic crusade" in Philippi (Acts 16:16--18). Jesus Christ never allowed devils to give witness to His identity (Luke 4:41) because it would give a false impression about the company He kept (see the accusation of the Pharisees in Matt. 12:24). That is why a Christian is not to be ["unequally yoked together with unbelievers"]{.class_3054} (2 Cor. 6:14), because ["what concord hath Christ with Belial?"]{.class_3054} (2 Cor. 6:15). NONE!

["Art thou come to destroy us?"]{.class_3054} Well, yes (1 John 3:8), but [not right then.]{.class_3055} Satan certainly was [judged]{.class_3055} at Calvary (John 16:11), and Christ certainly ["spoiled principalities and powers"]{.class_3054} and triumphed over them at Calvary (Col. 2:15), but the serpent's head is not bruised (Gen. 3:15) until the Second Advent (Rom. 16:20). But remember, everything is set up, at this point in Christ's ministry, for both Advents to be right together, so the spirit's question was a legitimate one.

["I know thee who thou art, the Holy One of God."]{.class_3054} So every demon is a "Fundamentalist." They all believe in the virgin birth and the Deity of Christ---just like any good Catholic. And they are all as lost as any good Catholic, I might add (James 2:19).

Ascribing to the Apostles' Creed does not make you saved. Any devil can honestly say he believes Jesus Christ is God's "only begotten Son" and "was conceived by the Holy Ghost . . . was crucified" and "rose again from the dead." Of course he can. It is not giving mental assent to those facts that saves you. Salvation is []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw91.xhtml_page_39}personally receiving Christ as your Saviour (John 1:12) and depending solely on His sacrifice on the cross to save you (Rom. 10:9). That is what sets a saved sinner apart from a lost Catholic or a damned devil.

["I know thee who thou art, the Holy One of God."]{.class_3054} Then any demon has more sense than Mohammed or his followers. As we observed back under our remarks on verse 11, Mohammed did not even acknowledge God had a Son. Although he may have been in agreement with the Devil on monotheism (James 2:19), he certainly was more ignorant when it came to the identity of Jesus Christ.

Verse 25:[ "And Jesus rebuked him, saying, Hold thy peace, and come out of him."]{.class_3054} Notice how simple the process of casting out a demon (a devil) is for someone operating in the power of God. Christ doesn't argue with the devil; He doesn't carry on a long conversation with it. There is no ritualism involved: no holy water, no rosary, no chants, no inflicting pain on the one demon-possessed, no hitting the demon-possessed person on the head or kicking his legs out from under him like the faith healers do. Jesus Christ commands the unclean spirit to shut up and get out, and it does.

Notice in verse 6, though, that the devil itself inflicts pain on its host before it leaves. We will run into that again over in 9:26. Sadomasochism is one of the marks of demon possession (5:5).

The reaction of the congregation of the synagogue is one of [amazement]{.class_3055} (vs. 27). A standard greeting between two friends is: "What's new?" Well, [that]{.class_3055} was ["new"]{.class_3054} (["what new doctrine is this?"]{.class_3054}). It had never been seen before (Matt. 9:33). You don't read anywhere in the Old Testament of anyone casting out devils. When Christ cast out a blind and dumb devil over in Matthew 12:22, the people took it, and rightly so, as evidence that Jesus was the Messiah (Matt. 12:23).

["What new doctrine is this?"]{.class_3054} Over in Acts 17:18--19, the unsaved Greek philosophers in Athens thought the resurrection of Christ was a ["new doctrine."]{.class_3054}

["For with authority commandeth he even the unclean spirits, and they do obey him."]{.class_3054} Once more the issue of [authority]{.class_3055} raises its head (see our comments on vs. 22). Christ transfers this []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw91.xhtml_page_40}power over devils to the apostles (Luke 9:1), and it is listed as one of ["the signs of an apostle"]{.class_3054} (2 Cor. 12:12) in Mark 16:17. Later on here in Mark, the scribes attribute this authority to Satan (3:22), which becomes the cause of Christ's statement on "the unpardonable sin" about which the Charismatics and Pentecostals worry so much (see our comments on 3:28--30).

["And immediately his fame spread abroad"]{.class_3054} (vs. 28). Casting out devils impressed the people, and it was no different for the disciples when Jesus gave them power to do the same thing. ["The seventy"]{.class_3054} came back from their preaching junket rejoicing that the devils were subject to them through the name of Christ (Luke 10:17). But to Jesus, that wasn't what should have impressed them. The greater cause of rejoicing was that their names were written in the Book of Life (Luke 10:20). Remember that the next time you hear some nutty Charismatic talking about casting out the "demon of poverty." Those fakirs [never]{.class_3055} try to get people saved; they are only trying to "fleece the flock" for all they can get.

["Throughout all the region round about Galilee."]{.class_3054} This is the spiritual application the Holy Ghost puts on Isaiah 9:1--2, which is actually a Second Advent passage. Nevertheless, the Holy Ghost gives a First Advent application in Matthew 4:13--16 to Christ's earthly ministry there (which see, along with our comments in that Commentary).

29 And forthwith, when they were come out of the synagogue, they entered into the house of Simon and Andrew, with James and John.

30 But Simon's wife's mother lay sick of a fever, and anon they tell him of her.

31 And he came and took her by the hand, and lifted her up; and immediately the fever left her, and she ministered unto them.

After the service at the synagogue, Jesus and His first four disciples go to where Simon lives with his family and his brother Andrew. The apostates try to raise another contradiction at this point by drawing your attention to John 1:44 which says that ["Bethsaida,"]{.class_3054} not Capernaum, was []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw91.xhtml_page_41}["the city of Andrew and Peter."]{.class_3054} But this just shows that such a person is as ignorant of the Scriptures as he is of real life. ["The city of David"]{.class_3054} was not only the city where David was born and raised (Bethlehem---Luke 2:4), it was also the city from which David reigned as a grown man (Zion---2 Sam. 5:7, 9). And who doesn't know that a person can move away from his hometown to get a job in another city. Bethsaida was obviously from where Andrew and Peter were originally, and Capernaum was where their fishing business was. If they were like some businessmen, they may have even maintained a house in both cities.

["But Simon's WIFE's mother . . . ."]{.class_3054} His what?! Did I just read that right? Did the text just say Simon Peter had a wife? It sure did (1 Cor. 9:5)!

You mean to tell me the first "Pope" was [married?]{.class_3055} That's right. Well, why could the first "Pope" have a wife and a mother-in-law, and there has never been another one like that since? Whoever these birds are running around for over 1,500 years calling themselves "Holy Father," they sure weren't the successors of Simon Peter! Not only was Simon Peter married, he never usurped God the Father's title (John 17:11), he wouldn't let anyone bow down to him and kiss his ring (Acts 10:25--26), he would never even think of speaking ["ex cathedra"]{.class_3055} to define doctrine (2 Pet. 1:20), he taught [every]{.class_3055} Christian was a priest (1 Pet. 2:5, 9), he said [Jesus]{.class_3055} (not himself) was the Rock on which the Church was built (1 Pet. 2:6--7), and he held the [Scriptures]{.class_3055} as the final authority (1 Pet. 1:23--25; 2 Pet. 1:19), not any "infallible teaching church."

Notice the healing of verse 31 was [immediate.]{.class_3055} There was no "praying through," "duds," or "short rounds" with the Lord. Someone who has the apostolic gift of healing ["shall lay hands on the sick, and they shall recover"]{.class_3054} (16:18). The healing doesn't depend on the faith of the one being healed (see our comments on James 5:14--15 in that Commentary).

["But Simon's wife's mother lay sick of a fever, and anon they tell him of her."]{.class_3054} The word ["anon"]{.class_3054} is archaic English (an adverb) that means "shortly, after a while." In other words, they didn't tell the Lord the minute they walked in the door, but when it became evident that the matron of the household wasn't going to be []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw91.xhtml_page_42}serving them the expected meal, they told Him she was down with the fever.

Now that wasn't that difficult to understand. You didn't need to "retranslate" the Bible to make it "clearer" in order to get that. You didn't need to get rid of 36,000 ([NASV]{.class_3055}) to 64,000 ([NIV]{.class_3055}) words from the text or attack every major fundamental of the faith to get it. All you had to do was look up the word in a dictionary. If the publishers wanted to "help you out," all they had to do was put a note in the margin. You don't need 323 conflicting authorities to clear up an occasional archaic word in the [King James,]{.class_3055} but that is the excuse that has been used once every six months since 1880. That is an [alibi]{.class_3055} to [get rid of the Scriptures.]{.class_3055}

32 And at even, when the sun did set, they brought unto him all that were diseased, and them that were possessed with devils.

33 And all the city was gathered together at the door.

34 And he healed many that were sick of divers diseases, and cast out many devils; and suffered not the devils to speak, because they knew him.

35 And in the morning, rising up a great while before day, he went out, and departed into a solitary place, and there prayed.

36 And Simon and they that were with him followed after him.

37 And when they had found him, they said unto him, All men seek for thee.

38 And he said unto them, Let us go into the next towns, that I may preach there also: for therefore came I forth.

It doesn't take long for the word to spread about the deliverance of the man with the unclean spirit or the healing of Simon Peter's mother-in-law. So once the Sabbath was over (note: ["at even"]{.class_3054} in vs. 32; i.e., after the Sabbath ended at 6 p.m. Saturday), everyone in Capernaum who is sick or possessed is brought to the door of Simon Peter's house to be healed and delivered.

Note the word ["devils"]{.class_3054}[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw91.xhtml_page_43} in verse 32. The Fundamentalists don't like that word. The standard hackneyed cliché used as an excuse to reject the [KJV's]{.class_3055} wording is: "There is only one Devil but many demons." That is one of those old wives' tales (1 Tim. 4:7) like Jesus died on Good Friday or the children of Israel passed through the Reed Sea instead of the Red Sea during the exodus. In John 6:70, you read about ["a devil"]{.class_3054} who is [not]{.class_3055} THE Devil, and the Greek word used is [diabolos]{.class_3055} (devil), not [daimonion]{.class_3055} (demon). The truth is that just as there is THE Son of God (vs. 11) but MANY sons of God (John 1:12), there is THE Devil and [many devils]{.class_3055} (see our remarks on the Devil under vss. 23--24).

Now why did the [King James]{.class_3055} translators [translate]{.class_3055} the Greek word [daimonion]{.class_3055} as [devil]{.class_3055} instead of [transliterating]{.class_3055} it as [demon?]{.class_3055} Well, in Greek philosophy, a demon could be a [good]{.class_3055} thing. The word was synonymous with the word [genius.]{.class_3055} God wanted you to know that no "demon" in that Bible is good, so He had the [KJV]{.class_3055} translators make it [devil]{.class_3055} to show you with whom the demons were connected. Even today, after 400 years of being told demons are bad, people will still use the word [demon]{.class_3055} to mean a non-personal, bad influence on an individual (e.g., "everyone has his own demons with which to contend") instead of real evil spirits connected to the Devil, so the word [devil]{.class_3055} is [still]{.class_3055} the better choice. You will often hear me use the term [demon possessed,]{.class_3055} even though the Scriptural term is ["possessed with devils"]{.class_3054} (vs. 32), for the same reason I put wings on angels when I draw: so Christians who have been brainwashed by radio, television, and the movies will know about what I'm talking.

We have already commented on Christ not allowing ["the devils to speak, because they knew him"]{.class_3054} under our remarks on verse 25 (which see). Note, again, the use of archaic words in verse 34 and recall how we said to treat them back under our remarks on ["anon"]{.class_3054} in verse 30. It is obvious that ["divers"]{.class_3054} isn't talking about scuba divers, and nobody is suffering (["suffered"]{.class_3054}) in verse 34.

Here at PBI, when the class sizes were smaller, I used to give a list of 100 archaic words (I bet the scholars led you to believe there were more than that, didn't they?) that are found in a [King James]{.class_3055} Bible. In those classes were men who had never gotten past the sixth grade in grammar school to men who had gone to graduate school []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw91.xhtml_page_44}after College. Even the least educated of those men could understand the meaning of over half of the words. Of the remaining words, they could guess correctly as to the meaning of about 25. In the end, they only had to look up 25 words in a dictionary to get the correct meaning. You mean to tell me you have to make up to 64,000 changes in the Bible to define 25 words? I don't believe it!

Here in verse 34, the word ["divers"]{.class_3054} isn't so much an archaic word as it is an archaic spelling. The word today is spelled [diverse.]{.class_3055} It means "a variety" or "different." The word ["suffered"]{.class_3054} obviously means "allowed," but you probably could have understood that from the context. If you couldn't, it wouldn't hurt you to do a little [studying,]{.class_3055} as you were commanded by the Holy Spirit (2 Tim. 2:15).

Verse 35 is the first time the prayer life of Jesus shows up in the Gospel of Mark. Christ often prays in ["a solitary place,"]{.class_3054} and real prayer is like that. Most of the real prayers you will ever pray will be alone with just you and God there. The principle is laid down by Christ Himself in Matthew 6:6.

["And Simon and they that were with him followed after him"]{.class_3054} (vs. 36). Well, that was what Jesus told them to do back in verse 17. The command of Jesus for discipleship is ["follow me"]{.class_3054} (Matt. 4:19; Mark 2:14, 8:34, 10:21; Luke 9:59; John 1:43). That's the motto at the Infantry School at Ft. Benning: "Follow me." The idea is follow the CO (Commanding Officer) [where]{.class_3055} he leads and follow his [example.]{.class_3055} So the Apostle Paul says, ["Be ye followers of me, even as I also am of Christ"]{.class_3054} (1 Cor. 11:1), and what he is saying by telling you that is to follow the example he set (Phil. 3:17; 2 Thess. 3:9).

The new "Bibles" change the command of 1 Corinthians 11:1 so you will [imitate]{.class_3055} Christ. But the word [imitate]{.class_3055} can mean "to counterfeit" as well as "to follow as a model." An imitation is [not]{.class_3055} the real thing. The Devil is the greatest imitator of Jesus Christ you will ever find in the Bible, and you shouldn't follow him across the street.

The Devil is a King, like Jesus (Job 18:14, 41:34 cf. 1 Tim. 6:15). He is a Prince, like Jesus Christ (John 12:31, 14:30, 16:11; Eph. 2:2 cf. Acts 3:15, 5:31). He is a god, as is Jesus (2 Cor. 4:4 cf. 1 John 5:20). He appears as an angel, like Christ (2 Cor. 11:14 cf. Acts 27:23; Gal. 4:14). He is a lion, just like Christ (1 Pet. 5:8 cf. Rev. 5:5). He has his []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw91.xhtml_page_45}own apostles and ministers, just as Jesus Christ does (2 Cor. 11:13, 15). And he has a city that is his bride (Rev. 17), just as the Lord Jesus Christ does (Rev. 21). You had better skip the imitating and stick with following the example (1 Pet. 2:21).

Now if you are going to follow Christ, there are some things He did you [can't]{.class_3055} do, there are some things He did you [can]{.class_3055} do, and there are some things He [never did]{.class_3055} so you shouldn't either. You take that matter of things He did you can't do, the Charismatics will quote John 14:12 just like they had good sense.

"Verily, verily, I say unto you, He that believeth on me, the works that I do shall he do also; and GREATER WORKS than these shall he do; because I go unto my Father."

Wanna bet? Try walking across Pensacola Bay on your own power. No? Try raising a dead man after four days and see how you make out.

Some poor farmer from the north end of Escambia County took the Charismatics seriously during the "Brownsville Revival" and brought his dead baby in a cooler to the meetings for the evangelist (Stephen Hill) to raise from the dead, like he had been bragging to the press. That's when the daddy of that dead baby found out the Charismatics couldn't revive a dead horse, much less raise a dead baby. You see, all those dead people being raised to life again were only [spiritual]{.class_3055} resurrections, according to the pastor (John Kilpatrick). Don't you know, baby! You had better learn to read that Bible in context, people, and to rightly divide it, or you are going to be in one flat-footed mess.

Now here is something Christ did that you can do: He came to ["bear witness unto the truth"]{.class_3054} (John 18:37). What is ["the truth"]{.class_3054}? According to Jesus Christ, it's the [word of God]{.class_3055} (John 17:17) and [Himself]{.class_3055} (John 14:6). Can't you witness for Jesus Christ from the Book?

Jesus Christ came ["to seek and to save that which was lost"]{.class_3054} (Luke 19:10). Can't you beat the bushes to bring lost sinners to Jesus Christ (Luke 14:23)? Jesus said, ["as my Father hath sent me, even so send I you"]{.class_3054} (John 20:21). You can't die for sins, but you can tell people about the One who did.

Jesus Christ [preached]{.class_3055} (vs. 38). He preached [on the street.]{.class_3055} So did the [apostles.]{.class_3055}[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw91.xhtml_page_46} They preached on the street because back in those days [there were no church buildings.]{.class_3055} Do you preach on the street? Well, if you don't, you are not following the example of your Saviour or the Apostle Paul (1 Cor. 11:1).

You see, none of this has to do with blabbering like an idiot or getting a crowd pumped up by singing "Only believe, only believe. All things are possible; only believe" so you can strip them of their last dollar in an offering, or trying to act like you think Jesus Christ would act so you can get to Heaven by your own self-righteousness (Charles Sheldon, [In His Steps]{.class_3055}), or praying for "peace on earth." It has to do with searching the Scriptures (John 5:39), rightly dividing them (2 Tim. 2:15), and then following the example the Holy Spirit gave you this side of the ascension of Christ (1 Cor. 11:1).

39 And he preached in their synagogues throughout all Galilee, and cast out devils.

40 And there came a leper to him, beseeching him, and kneeling down to him, and saying unto him, If thou wilt, thou canst make me clean.

41 And Jesus, moved with compassion, put forth his hand, and touched him, and saith unto him, I will; be thou clean.

42 And as soon as he had spoken, immediately the leprosy departed from him, and he was cleansed.

43 And he straitly charged him, and forthwith sent him away;

44 And saith unto him, See thou say nothing to any man: but go thy way, shew thyself to the priest, and offer for thy cleansing those things which Moses commanded, for a testimony unto them.

45 But he went out, and began to publish it much, and to blaze abroad the matter, insomuch that Jesus could no more openly enter into the city, but was without in desert places: and they came to him from every quarter.

Now Jesus may have started out preaching in the synagogues (vs. 39), but that is not where He conducted the "lion's share" of His []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw91.xhtml_page_47}ministry. He ended up out in the open fields (vs. 45). That is how John Wesley's ministry went. He wasn't allowed to preach in his deceased father's pulpit, so he went out in the cemetery and preached from his father's tombstone.

Notice again the prevalence of devils in Israel at this time (vs. 39). Verse 34 says Jesus ["cast out MANY devils."]{.class_3054} The place is chocked full of them. You are living in a day and age when society is stuffed as full of demons as a Thanksgiving turkey full of dressing.

Verse 40:[ "And there came a leper to him."]{.class_3054} Leprosy is a type of sin. Like sin, leprosy defiles its victim (Lev. 13:46), causing him to be cast forth from the camp (cf. Rev. 21:27). Like sin, leprosy requires a cleansing that is accomplished through a blood atonement (Lev. 14:18--21, 31). Like sin, leprosy can only be healed by God (even today, leprosy is accounted an incurable disease after it reaches a certain stage).

Now this leper comes to Jesus and kneels before Him in an act of [worship,]{.class_3055} according to Matthew 8:2. In that same verse, the leper calls Jesus ["Lord,"]{.class_3054} so this leper recognizes the fact that Jesus is [Deity incarnate]{.class_3055} (1 Tim. 3:16). That's the reason those Jews over in John 5:18, 10:31--33 tried to stone Him. The question is: was Jesus who He claimed to be, or was He not? This leper is betting everything that [He is.]{.class_3055}

That's [faith]{.class_3055}; that's [salvation.]{.class_3055} I am betting my soul on a [Book:]{.class_3055} I am betting my eternal destiny on that Book being [right]{.class_3055} about what it says about Jesus Christ. You say, "You could be wrong, Ruckman." Yeah, I could be, but we'll see. I know this: I wouldn't trust the rest of the religious junk for fifteen feet. "Why?" you ask. [Because I tried them out.]{.class_3055} I tried them out, and [they don't work.]{.class_3055} That Book took away my dirty mouth, my dirty drink, my dirty cigars, my dirty women, my dirty music; and it cleaned me up. Now do you really think you are going to talk me out of my faith in the Book? Why, you couldn't talk me out of it with a talking machine.

This leper hears about Jesus casting out all those devils, and he hears about Christ healing a bunch of sick folks. He figures what those lepers figure back there in 2 Kings 7:3--4---"What have I got to lose?" So he co[mes to Jesus in faith and worship:]{.class_3067}[ "If thou wilt .]{.class_3068-1}[ . . ."]{.class_3057-1}[ He didn't doubt Christ's ability; he was absolutely confident in the Lord's ability to heal:]{.class_3056}[ ". ]{.class_3057-1}[. . thou canst make me clean."]{.class_3054}[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw91.xhtml_page_48} What the leper was concerned about was Jesus' [willingness]{.class_3055} to heal him.

Now this is not the "age of miracles." No one is going around with the apostolic signs of Mark 16:17--18, no matter what these religious fakirs on TV or radio claim. That doesn't mean, though, that you can't come to the Lord in faith, asking Him to heal you. What you have to keep in mind, though, is the [will of God.]{.class_3055} It may not be the Lord's will to heal you.

It wasn't with the Apostle Paul. He had ["a thorn in the flesh"]{.class_3054} that he asked God three times to remove, and each time God said "No!" and shoved that thorn in a little deeper (2 Cor. 12:7--8). Why? Because the Lord wanted to perfect His strength through Paul's weakness (2 Cor. 12:9). God may have a purpose for you going through your infirmity. If that's the case, you need to rely on God for strength to bear that infirmity and learn to rejoice in the Lord as you go through it (2 Cor. 12:9--10).

Here in Mark 1, Jesus has compassion on the leper and heals him. When He does, He touches the leper, but it is not Christ's touch that heals the man. It is His [words.]{.class_3055} Verse 42: ["as soon as he had SPOKEN, immediately the leprosy departed from him."]{.class_3054} The disciples may have had to ["lay hands on the sick"]{.class_3054} for them to ["recover"]{.class_3054} (Mark 16:18), but Jesus needed no "point of contact," as is evidenced when He healed the nobleman's son in John 4:47--53 and the Centurion's servant in Matthew 8:5--13. This is the Creator (John 1:1--3) speaking and the thing happens, just as He did it in Genesis 1.

Now Jesus touches the leper, and technically speaking, that was forbidden in the Law (Lev. 13:45--46; Num. 5:2--3). But the high priest could touch the leper when it came time to apply the blood and pronounce the leper cleansed of his leprosy (Lev. 14:14). Jesus is exercising His authority as THE High Priest (Heb. 3:1) and bearing the sicknesses and infirmities of His people for which He would pay the blood atonement on Calvary (Matt. 8:17).

Verse 43:[ "And he straitly charged him, and forthwith sent him away."]{.class_3054} Now there are a couple of words you might need to look up, but I dare say you got the gist of what the verse said. Jesus told the healed leper to do something---[rather strongly]{.class_3055}---and sent the fellow away [right away.]{.class_3055}[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw91.xhtml_page_49} If you need to know the exact definition of ["straitly"]{.class_3054} and ["forthwith,"]{.class_3054} they are:

1\) ["Straitly"]{.class_3054}---"narrowly" (a strait is a narrow passage for a ship, as in the Straits of Magellan at the southernmost tip of South America). In this case, narrowly in the sense of "strictly"; i.e., there is only one way of understanding the charge Christ gave the leper.

2\) ["Forthwith"]{.class_3054}---"immediately, without delay."

Now Jesus charged the leper He had healed with two things. The first was: ["See thou say nothing to any man."]{.class_3054} That is, the leper wasn't to tell who healed him or how he had been cleansed of his leprosy. While Jesus' rejection at Nazareth and the people's reaction to Him publicly proclaiming His Messiahship certainly had something to do with this charge (see Luke 4:16--29), the context shows you the more practical reason Jesus told the leper to keep his mouth shut.

["But he went out, and began to publish it much, and to blaze abroad the matter, insomuch that Jesus could no more openly enter into the city"]{.class_3054} (vs. 45).

In His earthly ministry, Jesus never sought "publicity" (see John 7:4--10). In fact, as you can see here, unwanted publicity hindered His public ministry. He could no longer preach in the synagogues of Capernaum because He was "mobbed." The only way He could accommodate the crowds was to go outside the city and preach in the fields.

Now we can understand the reaction of the leper. If you had terminal cancer and a fellow could send it into remission just by telling it to go away (I mean, no drugs, no radiation, and no operation, man), you would want all your friends to know about someone who could do that. We can forgive this healed leper of his misguided zeal.

But what I will never understand is a sinner who was on his way to Hell. He wasn't interested in God or the Bible; he was only interested in self-preservation, self-propagation, and self-gratification like any dog. The Holy Spirit puts a Gospel witness in the path of that sinner and "dogs" him until he trusts Christ as his Saviour. Now that sinner is not only not going to Hell, he is actually headed for Heaven. His sins are all forgiven, and he stands justified []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw91.xhtml_page_50}before God in the righteousness of Jesus Christ. And that saved sinner never speaks up for his Saviour?! I don't understand that. That is one of the most ungrateful rascals I know. I don't know how a person like that can look himself in the mirror every morning.

I have been saved for 67 years as of this writing, and I haven't gotten over what Jesus Christ did for me yet. Of course, with my hearing and eyesight both almost gone, it is hard for me to witness anymore, but as long as I could, I did. I preached or witnessed on the streets, in bars, in a skating rink, in hospitals, in classrooms, in prisons, in jails, in churches, and on the mission field. While I could, I "published it much" and "blazed abroad the matter."

What's your excuse for not speaking up for Jesus Christ? I know a young woman who was bedridden and couldn't get out and about to witness. Do you know what she did? She would write letters to Mohammedans and send them Bibles; she even was able to lead two or three to the Lord. And your excuse is what?

I knew a guy in Baltimore who was so ugly, you wouldn't think anyone would talk to him. He had warts all over his face and was hair-lipped. He would go out on the streets and pass out tracts and say, "Do you love Jesus?" I saw people cuss him out and try to avoid him, but every now and then, I would see him step off to the side with a person and kneel in prayer with him as he led him to Christ. And you don't witness why?

You say, "I'm very shy, Dr. Ruckman." You are not as shy as Bob Price. Bob Price was so shy, he didn't even like to go out grocery shopping because he didn't want to talk to people. That's shy! But that old boy heard me preach about speaking up for Jesus Christ, and he said, "How can I do that, Dr. Ruckman?" That fellow would become physically ill if he had to look anyone in the eye and speak to him.

I suggested to Bob Price that he mail out gospel tracts. He started mailing out a booklet the Bookstore puts out entitled [Millions Disappear, Fact of Fiction?]{.class_3055}. It's a booklet on the Second Coming of Christ. Bob Price mailed out those booklets with a card to mail back if the person got saved. Bob Price got back over [500]{.class_3055} decision cards from people who had accepted Christ from reading that booklet Bob sent to them. Bob Price, as shy as he was, found a way to publish the []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw91.xhtml_page_51}good news and blaze it abroad. What was your excuse, again?

Christ's second charge to the leper was to present himself to the high priest and make the offering for his cleansing that was prescribed in Leviticus 14:12--24, which see.

Another time, ten lepers asked Jesus to have mercy on them and cleanse their leprosy. Jesus told them to go show themselves to the high priest, just as He told the leper here in Mark 1, but they weren't healed until they went to do what Jesus told them. Having discovered they were healed, only one of them came back to thank Jesus for healing him, and that fellow was a Samaritan. You can read the account in Luke 17:12--19. That passage ends with Jesus commending the faith of that Samaritan.

The lesson for the believer is, while trying to be Scripturally right in what you do in the Christian life, don't forget your love for Jesus Christ. The Lord Jesus commended the church at Ephesus for their doctrinal purity and faithful service (Rev. 2:2--3). The problem He had with them was the members ["left" ]{.class_3054}their ["first love"]{.class_3054} for the Saviour (Rev. 2:4). The Christian should guard against losing the love and gratitude he originally had for Jesus Christ for what He did for him (1 John 4:19).

[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw343.xhtml}

# Chapter 2 {.heading_97}

2:1 And again he entered into Capernaum after some days; and it was noised that he was in the house.

2 And straightway many were gathered together, insomuch that there was no room to receive them, no, not so much as about the door: and he preached the word unto them.

3 And they come unto him, bringing one sick of the palsy, which was borne of four.

4 And when they could not come nigh unto him for the press, they uncovered the roof where he was: and when they had broken it up, they let down the bed wherein the sick of the palsy lay.

5 When Jesus saw their faith, he said unto the sick of the palsy, Son, thy sins be forgiven thee.

6 But there were certain of the scribes sitting there, and reasoning in their hearts,

7 Why doth this man thus speak blasphemies? who can forgive sins but God only?

8 And immediately when Jesus perceived in his spirit that they so reasoned within themselves, he said unto them, Why reason ye these things in your hearts?

9 Whether is it easier to say to the sick of the palsy, Thy sins be forgiven thee; or to say, Arise, and take up thy bed, and walk?

10 But that ye may know that the Son of man hath power on earth to forgive sins, (he saith to the sick of the palsy,)

11 I say unto thee, Arise, and take up thy bed, and go thy way into thine house.

12 And immediately he arose, took up the bed, and went forth before them all; insomuch that they were all amazed, and glorified God, saying, We never saw it on this fashion.

Chapter 1 ended with Christ outside of Capernaum, preaching in the fields because of the mobs of people. ["Desert places"]{.class_3054}[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw343.xhtml_page_53} doesn't mean a dry, barren wasteland with small shrubs, cacti, rattlesnakes, lizards, etc., like the Mojave, Gobi, or Sahara. It is talking about [deserted]{.class_3055} places: places where there weren't a lot of buildings or people.

Chapter 2 opens with Jesus reentering Capernaum to minister ["after some days."]{.class_3054} The problem was the news spread quickly (["noised"]{.class_3054}---to spread a report like gossip or rumor) that Jesus was back in town. ["The house"]{.class_3054} of verse 1 might be the same house where He was back in 1:33 (Simon Peter's house---1:29), but more likely it is just a reference to the particular house where He was.

Once again, the crowds gather until the house is full and overflowing out the door into the street.

["And he PREACHED the word unto them"]{.class_3054} (vs. 2). Notice the constant emphasis in Mark on [preaching.]{.class_3055} John [preached]{.class_3055} about repenting and being baptized (1:4); he [preached]{.class_3055} about Christ showing up (1:7). Jesus began His ministry with [preaching]{.class_3055} (1:14); He [preached]{.class_3055} in the synagogues (1:39). He said He came to [preach]{.class_3055} (1:38).

One of the reasons this country is in such rough shape is a dearth of sound, strong, Biblical [preaching.]{.class_3055} I am not talking about "rap sessions" or "motivational speeches" or feel-good drivel or "talks" or even [teaching,]{.class_3055} whether good, bad, or indifferent. I am talking about [preaching]{.class_3055}---"reading them the riot act," "skinning them alive," "soaking my hogs in boiling water before I scrape the bristles off them" (Sam Jones, the Methodist evangelist). Occasionally you will run across a real preacher on the radio (hardly ever on TV anymore). If you do, it will either be someone whose ministry was back in the 50s or 60s, like Oliver B. Greene, Maze Jackson, or Lester Roloff; or it will be some Holiness preacher with little or no formal training---and with no real [content]{.class_3055} to his preaching either, I might add.

Now, among the crowd that came to see Jesus, there were four guys carrying their buddy on a "stretcher" because he was ["sick of the palsy"]{.class_3054} (vs. 3). Palsy is a nerve disorder that causes paralysis and tremors, and it takes several forms. Whatever form this fellow had, it caused him not to be able to walk.

["And when they could not come nigh unto him for the PRESS"]{.class_3054} (vs. 4). Now we understand the plain meaning of the word. ["The press"]{.class_3054} is the crowd of people all pressed together to try to get in the house to hear Jesus. But don't you find the wording "highly instructive," given the modern meaning of the word [press]{.class_3055}? When you boil everything down to the most basic elements, the job of "the press" (the [news media]{.class_3055}) is to prevent people from coming to Jesus Christ and the Bible for [truth.]{.class_3055}

In the account here, these four guys bring their buddy to Christ to be healed, and they can't even get near the door of the house due to the crowd. But the stairway leading to the roof was clear. Back in Bible times in the Middle East, houses had flat roofs where the people would sit in the evening to catch a cool breeze (which explains the command given in Deut. 22:8). The four men haul their palsied friend up to the roof and begin removing the tiles of the roof over where Jesus was sitting and preaching.

The scene must have been wild, man. Here is Jesus preaching away when suddenly there is a noise on the roof over His head. As the first tile is taken away, sunlight from outside begins to flood into the house from overhead. As the four men continue to widen the hole in the roof, dust and pieces of tiling fall down onto the head of Jesus and His congregation. Jesus doesn't let it faze Him; He just keeps on preaching until finally this fellow on a cot is lowered down in front of Him.

["When Jesus saw THEIR faith"]{.class_3054} (vs. 5)---the faith of the four men, [not]{.class_3055} the faith of the sick fellow. There's the application of James 5:14--16. When a faith healer flops in healing someone, it is [his]{.class_3055} fault, not the fault of the sick person. The faith is on the part of the [healer: he]{.class_3055} has to have the faith; [he]{.class_3055} has to have "effectual prayer." In a few chapters here in Mark, we are going to read about Jesus visiting His hometown of Nazareth. The people there had [no faith]{.class_3055} in their "native son" at all (6:6), yet Jesus was still able to heal ["a few sick folk"]{.class_3054} who didn't have enough faith to put in the eye of a blind mosquito (6:5).

["He said unto the sick of the palsy, Son, thy sins be forgiven thee."]{.class_3054} There's James 5:15.

["And the prayer of faith]{.class_3054} \[the faith of the healer, not of the sick---James 5:14\][]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw343.xhtml_page_55}[ shall save the sick and the Lord shall raise him up; and if he have committed sins, they shall be forgiven him."]{.class_3054}

["Son"]{.class_3054}---the palsied man is a [son of Abraham,]{.class_3055} not a son of God. So while there may be some application to the New Testament church in James 5 (see vs. 14 of that chapter), the main emphasis is still to the twelve tribes of Israel, just as James 1:1 tells you (see our comments on that verse in that Commentary). What you are reading here in Mark 2 and over in James 5 is a [Jewish]{.class_3055} set-up. Forgiveness of sins [in this age]{.class_3055} comes from a sinner placing his faith in the death and resurrection of Jesus Christ for sins (Col. 2:13), and from the saved confessing their sins directly to God to stay in fellowship with Him (1 John 1:9). [Right now]{.class_3055} (rightly divide that Book, people!), I may pray for you if you are sick, and God may hear my prayer and heal you (or He may NOT); but if He does, there is nothing about [my]{.class_3055} prayer that will get [your]{.class_3055} sins forgiven. You have to do business directly with God for that.

Now when Jesus said ["thy sins be forgiven thee,"]{.class_3054} He "done put a chill on de meetin'." ["Certain of the scribes"]{.class_3054} (vs. 6)---there's your College-educated boys.[ "The common people"]{.class_3054} weren't the ones who had a problem with Jesus Christ; they ["heard him gladly"]{.class_3054} (12:37). It was the [scribes]{.class_3055} (the copiers of the Scriptures), the [Pharisees]{.class_3055} (the Bible teachers), and the [Sadducees]{.class_3055} (the priests) who had the problem; they were the [educated]{.class_3055} class (see John 7:15, 49; Acts 4:13).

["And reasoning in their hearts,"]{.class_3054} and then Mark tells you what they were [thinking]{.class_3055} (vs. 7). How can that be? Look at verse 8: ["Jesus perceived in his spirit that they so reasoned within themselves."]{.class_3054} Jesus ["knew what was in man"]{.class_3054} (John 2:25). This is not just some prophet from Galilee, like Jonah or Nahum; this is the God who tries the hearts of men (John 1:1 cf. 1 Chron. 29:17). This is the One who will one day ["judge the secrets of men"]{.class_3054} (Rom. 2:16). It was [His Spirit]{.class_3055} that revealed the scribes' thoughts to Mark so he could record them for you to read (cf. 1 Pet. 1:11).

["Why doth this man thus speak blasphemies? who can forgive sins but God only?"]{.class_3054} (vs. 7). That last statement is true (Job 14:4; Psa. 130:4; Isa. 43:25; Dan. 9:9; Mic. 7:18), but the first statement can only be true if Jesus Christ were [not God. That]{.class_3055}[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw343.xhtml_page_56} was the issue (John 5:18, 10:30--33). Later on here in Mark, a fellow calls Jesus ["Good Master,"]{.class_3054} and Jesus says to him, ["Why callest thou me good? there is none good but one, that is, God"]{.class_3054} (10:17--18). Jesus was telling that rich young ruler, "If I'm not God, I'm not good. I would be a sinner [just like you."]{.class_3055}

Jesus is about to prove His Deity to these scribes in no uncertain terms. The scribes will be amazed, and they will glorify God as they should (vs. 12), but they won't believe that Deity incarnate is the One preaching to them. Because of this fence-straddling, their amazement and glorifying God turns to ["madness"]{.class_3054} (Luke 6:10), persecution (John 5:16), and in the end, [murder]{.class_3055} (John 12:10--11). Today, the Christ-rejecting Rabbis say that the reason Jesus was able to perform His miracles was that when He was twelve years old, He stole a talisman from the Temple with the Tetragrammaton (the four Hebrew letters for the name [Jehovah]{.class_3055}) on it. This begs the question that if the Jewish leaders had such a talisman, why didn't [they]{.class_3055} use it to heal folks and deliver them from devils when they had it? The Jews' alibi for rejecting Christ's ministry doesn't fare any better than their alibi for rejecting His resurrection (Matt. 28:12--15).

["Why REASON ye these things in your hearts?"]{.class_3054} Their problem is they are trying to figure out Jesus Christ with their own "noodle." No prayer, no searching of the Scriptures (John 5:39), no revelation from God; nothing but [man's wisdom.]{.class_3055} Man's wisdom, though, can't figure out the things of God (1 Cor. 2:13--14) because it comes from a nature that has been blinded by ["the god of this world"]{.class_3054} (2 Cor. 4:4).

Verse 9:[ "Whether is it easier to say to the sick of the palsy, Thy sins be forgiven thee; or to say, Arise, and take up thy bed, and walk?"]{.class_3054} For those scribes, [neither one]{.class_3055} was ["easier"]{.class_3054}; they couldn't forgive sins or get the man off his sick bed walking. But as proof of His spiritual power to forgive sins, Jesus heals the man.

The response of everyone in the room, including the scribes, was:[ "We never saw it on this fashion"]{.class_3054} (vs. 12). I guess they hadn't. The scribes couldn't do it; the Pharisees couldn't do it; the Sadducees couldn't do it. So why didn't they believe in Him? Envy []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw343.xhtml_page_57}(15:10)---the chief priests and elders of the people were jealous of Him. Instead of taking the attitude of John the Baptist (John 3:30), they wanted to remain in charge of the ["vineyard"]{.class_3054} (12:6--8). For others, it was the fear of man (cf. John 12:42--43): they believed but wouldn't come out publicly for Jesus Christ because it would cost them their standing in the "scholarly community."

Nothing has changed in that regard. The reason there are professors in Fundamental and Conservative Universities, Colleges, and Seminaries who won't take a stand for the [King James]{.class_3055} Bible, even though they were raised to believe it, is because they fear ridicule worse than they fear God, Hell, or the Judgment Seat of Christ. And since ["a little leaven leaveneth the whole lump,"]{.class_3054} the new generation of "scholars" at those institutions has graduated from attacking the Bible through lower (i.e., [textual]{.class_3055}) criticism (Westcott, Hort, Nestle, Metzger, et al.) to attacking the Bible through higher criticism (Graf, Wellhausen, Astruc, DeWette, et al.). It is not just the "Liberals" attacking the historicity of the Gospels anymore; it is now professors and grad students in Evangelical and Conservative institutions. Why? Jealousy of the authority of that Book; fear of not being "accepted" in the Scholars' Union. Things haven't changed in 2,000 years. Adam is still Adam: rotten to the core.

13 And he went forth again by the sea side; and all the multitude resorted unto him, and he taught them.

14 And as he passed by, he saw Levi the son of Alphaeus sitting at the receipt of custom, and said unto him, Follow me. And he arose and followed him.

15 And it came to pass, that, as Jesus sat at meat in his house, many publicans and sinners sat also together with Jesus and his disciples: for there were many, and they followed him.

16 And when the scribes and Pharisees saw him eat with publicans and sinners, they said unto his disciples, How is it that he eateth and drinketh with publicans and sinners?

17 When Jesus heard it, he saith unto them, They that are whole have no need of the physician, but they that are []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw343.xhtml_page_58}sick: I came not to call the righteous, but sinners to repentance.

Verse 13 is where the events of Luke 5:1--11 take place.

The ["Levi"]{.class_3054} of verse 14 is, of course, Matthew, the writer of the first Gospel. In all likelihood, Levi was his original Hebrew name, and he took the name [Matthew]{.class_3055} (meaning "gift of God") after his conversion. Many saints in both Testaments took on new names after they decided to follow God and Christ: Abram to Abraham, Sarai to Sarah, Jacob to Israel, Simon to Peter, Saul to Paul. The picture is the new man in Christ. "There's a [new name ]{.class_3055}written down in glory, and it's MINE."

Notice that Levi is ["the son of Alphaeus."]{.class_3068-0} Although the connection is never made outright in the Gospels, here is a [possible]{.class_3091} third set of brothers among Christ's disciples (the first two being Andrew and Peter, and James and John). Another of the disciples was ["James, the son of Alphaeus"]{.class_3068-0} (3:18). While the father of Matthew and the father of James could be two different Alphaeuses, it is just as likely that the same Alphaeus was the father of both James and Matthew.

Note ["the receipt of custom"]{.class_3054} there in verse 14. That's where Levi would receive (["receipt"]{.class_3054}) the taxes due on goods being brought into the city for sale (["custom"]{.class_3054}). So Matthew is a tax collector. The term for this in the Gospels was a [publican]{.class_3055} (vs. 15). That's not a Republican (as in the political party or form of government); that's a [public]{.class_3055} servant.

A publican served the Roman government and was hated by his fellow Jews for it. In modern parlance, he would be called a "collaborator." That is why the Jews always associated the publicans with very wicked people:[ "sinners"]{.class_3054} (vs. 15). Those aren't sinners in the Pauline sense (see Rom. 3:23); that's a reference to the very worst people (e.g., Gen. 13:13).

The publicans were considered to be just as bad as thieves, prostitutes, and murderers; and it wasn't just because they collected taxes for the Jews' oppressors. When the publicans collected taxes, they would always collect more than what was owed to Rome, and they would pocket the extra cash. The Romans didn't care as long as they got their cut. So the publicans became rich through []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw343.xhtml_page_59}[extortion.]{.class_3055} That is why the hatred of the Jews for the publicans was so intense: the publicans "added insult to injury," as it were.

That being the case, you can understand why ["the scribes and Pharisees"]{.class_3054} are so shocked to see Jesus and the disciples go to a "dinner party" at Levi's house. Jesus, along with Peter, Andrew, James, and John, are sitting there eating with a crowd rougher, morally, than commercial fishermen. The meal signified fellowship and acceptance to the Pharisees, so the accusation against Christ was: ["This man receiveth sinners, and eateth with them"]{.class_3054} (Luke 15:2).

Now that wasn't true in the sense of [approving of their sin.]{.class_3055} The command of Christ to the sinner was: ["sin no more"]{.class_3054} (John 5:14, 8:11). But Jesus did receive sinners in the sense of allowing them to repent, which is evident by what follows:[ "I came not to call the righteous, but sinners to repentance"]{.class_3054} (vs. 17).

The comparison used by Christ is to that of a sick person: ["They that are whole have no need of the physician, but they that are sick."]{.class_3054} That doesn't sit well with some "hardcore" Charismatics. Here is the One who could heal someone just by speaking (see our remarks under 1:42), recommending a [doctor]{.class_3055} if you are sick. So do you know what Paul, who could heal people with handkerchiefs (Acts 19:12), carried with him on his missionary journeys? A [doctor]{.class_3055} (Col. 4:14; 2 Tim. 4:11)! Tell [that]{.class_3055} to some crazy Charismatic nut who believes it is never God's will for you to be sick.

Back when Nathan Bemis (who pastored Bible Baptist Church in Kalispell, Montana for years) went to school here, some Charismatic faith healer came to town and held a tent meeting complete with a healing line. Nathan went down to see what was going on, and he was shocked (he was pretty naive at the time). He came to me and said, "Bro. Ruckman, that man is lying to those people." He didn't need to tell me that; I had been to enough of them to know what they were like.

Nathan said, "He's stealing their paychecks, their welfare checks, and the money from their insurance policies. What are [we]{.class_3055} going to do about it, Bro. Ruckman?"

I knew there was nothing that could be done (legally) to stop that []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw343.xhtml_page_60}godless, depraved mess, but I didn't want to destroy the zeal of a young babe in Christ, so I said, "I guess the only thing to do is pray about it."

Well, Nathan had already done more than "pray about it." He had confronted that faith healer behind the tent after the service and asked him, "Was the Apostle Paul ever sick?"

"Oh yes," said the healer.

Then Bemis asked him, "Did he ever get healed?" Now that Charismatic fakir no more knew the answer to that question than he knew what was going on on the backside of Jupiter. You see, that would have required [knowing the Bible]{.class_3055} (2 Cor. 12:5--9; 2 Tim. 4:11), and that nut didn't know enough Bible to teach a "Little Lambs" Sunday school class (three year olds).

"Yes, God healed him," bluffed the faith healer.

Bemis held out his New Testament to the healer and said, "Would you mind showing me that?"

"Come back tomorrow night; that's what I'll be preaching on," said the healer.

"That's all right," said Nathan, "I'll take it now if you'll show it to me."

But that religious charlatan wasn't about to display his ignorance then nor the next night. He walked away, and of course, he didn't preach the following evening on Paul being healed.

In the meantime, though, Bro. Bemis took my advice and got to praying and fasting for God to reveal that fellow for the hypocrite he was. [Nathan Bemis got his prayers answered.]{.class_3055} Right in the middle of "healing" some big, fat colored woman, that sister keeled over dead of a heart attack. The "healer" had to get out of town before he was lynched, with the police protecting him as he packed up (evidently God couldn't). In his newsletter, he told his followers Pensacola had "blasphemed the Holyghost" and had "Ichabod" written across its churches for persecuting "the man of Gawd" (where that left the "Brownsville Revival," I have no idea).

Brethren, there are good doctors and bad doctors just as there are good preachers and bad preachers. If you are sick, yes, seek God's help [first]{.class_3055} (see 2 Chron. 16:12), but [go to a doctor]{.class_3055} if you need to. Why? [Because your Saviour told you to]{.class_3055} (vs. 17).

["I came . . . ."]{.class_3054}[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw343.xhtml_page_61} Here is another reason Jesus said He came (see our remarks under 1:38). Notice that it has nothing to do with "showing the love of God" to anyone. It has nothing to do with bringing "peace on earth" or "pray for peace." It has nothing to do with being a moral example so you can work your way to Heaven by following it.

Why did [Jesus Christ]{.class_3055} say He came? Never mind what the Pope says or what Rick Warren says or what Joel Osteen says. What did [Jesus Christ Himself]{.class_3055} have to say about it? He said, ["I came not to call the righteous, but sinners to repentance."]{.class_3054} He came to tell sinners to [get right]{.class_3055}!

Now there is definitely a spiritual application you can make to the passage. After all, Paul preached repentance to the Gentiles (Acts 20:21, 26:20). If you aren't willing to admit you are a sinner who needs to repent of your own self-righteousness and turn to Christ for salvation (Phil. 3:4--9), then the sacrifice of Christ is totally inefficacious to you. But that is not about what Christ is talking here.

Jesus is making an Old Testament distinction between those who are [keeping the Law]{.class_3055} (["the righteous"]{.class_3054}) and those who [don't]{.class_3055} (["sinners"]{.class_3054}). As we pointed out under remarks on verse 15, the word ["sinners"]{.class_3054} is not being used here as Paul uses it in Romans 5:8 or 1 Timothy 1:15. In those verses, [everyone,]{.class_3055} good or bad, is a sinner because everyone has broken God's Law at some point. But in the Old Testament, a distinction is made between those who are trying to keep the Mosaic Law and those who are flagrantly violating it with no regard to it. ["The righteous"]{.class_3054} delight in and meditate in ["the law of the LORD"]{.class_3054} (Psa. 1:2), not the grace of God through Jesus Christ; and ["the ungodly"]{.class_3054} [do not]{.class_3055} (Psa. 1:4).

In the Old Testament, a man could be living right, and if he turned from the righteous life he was living and began to live like the devil, he would die in his sins and go to Hell (Ezek. 18:24--26). That is not your case as a New Testament believer (1 Cor. 5:5).

In the Old Testament, a believer could lose the Holy Spirit and go to Hell, which is why David prayed, ["take not thy holy spirit from me"]{.class_3054} (Psa. 51:11). He had seen someone who had (1 Sam. 16:14). Again, that is not your case in the New Testament (Eph. 4:30).

By the same token, though, a wicked man could turn from his sins and keep the Law, and he would make it to Paradise (Ezek. 18:21, 27). If you tried that, you would split Hell wide open (Eph. 2:8--9; Tit. 3:5). Brethren, no matter what the Fundamentalists tell you, salvation in the Old Testament was NOT the same as salvation in the New Testament.

What is so for you [now]{.class_3055} isn't necessarily so for someone before the Resurrection or after the Rapture. For example, nobody lives ["by faith"]{.class_3054} (Rom. 1:17) in the Millennium because Jesus Christ is right there where everyone can see him; everybody in the Millennium is walking ["by sight"]{.class_3054} instead of faith (cf. 2 Cor. 5:7). Salvation in the Millennium [can't]{.class_3055} be by grace THROUGH FAITH, by the very definition of faith given you by the Holy Spirit (Heb. 11:1).

Fundamentalists have a bad habit of forcing the whole Bible to match something they know is so in the Church Age. You can't force the whole Old Testament or everything in the Gospels or everything in Acts 1--8 or everything in Hebrews--Revelation to match what you read in Romans--Philemon. It won't work. You had better adjust your theology to the Book instead of the Book to your theology.

18 And the disciples of John and of the Pharisees used to fast: and they come and say unto him, Why do the disciples of John and of the Pharisees fast, but thy disciples fast not?

19 And Jesus said unto them, Can the children of the bridechamber fast, while the bridegroom is with them? as long as they have the bridegroom with them, they cannot fast.

20 But the days will come, when the bridegroom shall be taken away from them, and then shall they fast in those days.

Now the question of fasting arises. Fasting is different from a diet or going hungry from lack of food (see 2 Cor. 11:27 and our comments on the verse in that Commentary). Fasting is going without food for a [spiritual]{.class_3055} reason, usually to seek God's will in a matter or to get an answer to prayer.

Verse 18 says, ["the disciples of John and of the Pharisees used to fast"]{.class_3054}[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw343.xhtml_page_63}; both Matthew and Luke add the fact that those fasts were [often]{.class_3055} (Matt. 9:14; Luke 5:33). How often? The example given by Christ in the Parable of the Pharisee and the Publican was twice a week (Luke 18:12). Of course, the Pharisees did it to show off (Matt. 6:16). That was probably not true of John's disciples; nevertheless, both the Pharisees and the followers of John the Baptist have the same question for Jesus: "Why don't your disciples fast as we do?"

["And Jesus said unto them, Can the children of the bridechamber fast, while the bridegroom is with them? as long as they have the bridegroom with them, they cannot fast"]{.class_3054} (vs. 19). Now we get into some pretty touchy "fine lines" here when it comes to ["rightly dividing the word of truth"]{.class_3054} (2 Tim. 2:15). It is obvious that [Jesus ]{.class_3055}is ["the bridegroom"]{.class_3054} of the passage. And the Bride is identified by Paul as the Church (Eph. 5:25--32), but there is no Church in Mark 2. Our "Baptist Brider" brethren who trace their Baptist lineage back to John the Baptist have a problem at this point. John the Baptist was NOT a New Testament Baptist (Matt. 11:13; Luke 16:16), nor was he in the Bride of Christ (John 3:29).

["The children of the bridechamber"]{.class_3054} aren't marrying the Bridegroom. They are the attendants at the wedding (Matt. 25:1): the bridesmaids and the groomsmen. In the context, ["the children of the bridechamber"]{.class_3054} are obviously the Jewish disciples. As long as Jesus is there with them, those Jewish disciples don't fast. When Christ goes back to Heaven, then the disciples fast (Acts 13:2--3, 14:23). Paul fasted (2 Cor. 6:5, 11:27) and recommended fasting (1 Cor. 7:5). If you Christians want to know if an Old Testament practice is for you or not, there are two things you need to find out: 1) Is it practiced or commended to you in the Pauline epistles? 2) Is it against anything in the Pauline epistles? Something like tithing may not be commanded to the New Testament believers by Paul, but there's nothing wrong with setting that as a [minimum]{.class_3055} standard for giving; it doesn't violate anything Paul said about giving as long as you do it with the right attitude (2 Cor. 9:7). In the case of fasting, it remains a New Testament practice of the Church.

The problem, dispensationally, here in Mark 2:19--20, is that when those Jewish disciples get to the book of Acts, they are baptized by the Holy Spirit into the Body of Christ, the Bride (Acts 1:5 cf. 1 Cor. 12:13). So ["the children of the bridechamber"]{.class_3054}[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw343.xhtml_page_64} who don't fast in verse 19 aren't the same ones who do fast in verse 20, from a [doctrinal]{.class_3055} standpoint.

But there are some attendants at the wedding of Christ to His Bride. They are found in Psalm 45:14; they are called ["virgins"]{.class_3054}---[plural]{.class_3055} (see also Song of Sol. 6:8 and our remarks on that verse in that Commentary). That is not the Church, because the Church is ["A chaste virgin"]{.class_3054} (2 Cor. 11:2)---[singular.]{.class_3055} The ["virgins"]{.class_3054} turn out to be [Jewish]{.class_3055} (Rev. 14:4), and they are the subject of the Parable of the Wise and Foolish Virgins in Matthew 25:1--13. That parable is not about the Rapture of the Church, because those virgins go to ["meet"]{.class_3054} the Bridegroom, not to marry Him (Matt. 25:6). So those virgins are caught up to Heaven in a post-Tribulation rapture of Tribulation saints (see our work on [The Two Raptures]{.class_3055}).

Now all of that is not evident to the Pharisees in Mark 2, for nobody even knew about the Body of Christ. Contrary to the teachings of the "Dry Cleaners" (the Hyperdispensationalists), you [can]{.class_3055} find the Bride of Christ in the Old Testament [in type,]{.class_3055} but the only way you would know the type is with the New Testament revelation. As far as what these Jews could get from the Old Testament without any New Testament, the Bride of Jehovah was Israel that had been put away by the Lord but would someday be remarried to Him (read Hos. 1--2). That is so, but we are dealing here with the difference between God the Father and God the Son. Israel is the Bride of the Father; the Church is the Bride of the Son.

21 No man also seweth a piece of new cloth on an old garment: else the new piece that filled it up taketh away from the old, and the rent is made worse.

22 And no man putteth new wine into old bottles: else the new wine doth burst the bottles, and the wine is spilled, and the bottles will be marred: but new wine must be put into new bottles.

For the first time here in the Gospel of Mark, New Testament salvation is alluded to. The point of both illustrations in verses 21--22 is the need for a new nature that comes through the New Birth of []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw343.xhtml_page_65}John 3. In the illustration of the garment, the new nature is not just grafted onto the old. The Holy Spirit cuts away the soul from the sinful body of flesh so He can give you the new man in Christ (Col. 2:11--13). The Lord doesn't join your new nature to your old Adamic nature.

In the Old Testament, the soul was stuck to the body, so whatever the body did affected the soul. That's why a person's soul could be affected by what he touched and ate there in Leviticus (Lev. 5:2, 7:18, 20--21, 25, 27, 22:6, etc.). That is not your case in the New Testament. ["If the Son therefore shall make you free, ye shall be free indeed"]{.class_3054} (John 8:36), and when you get saved, you were freed from your sin nature (Rom. 6:22, 8:2). Of course, you have to yield yourself to God for that thing to work out [practically]{.class_3055} in your life (Rom. 6), but [doctrinally,]{.class_3055} sin and death have no more dominion over you (Rom. 6:9, 14).

While ["the new wine"]{.class_3054} of verse 22 can be a picture of the blood of Christ (Matt. 26:28--29; John 2:3--4), in the context here it is a picture of the Holy Ghost indwelling the believer (Acts 2:13; Eph. 5:18). Before the Holy Spirit will live in your body, He requires a new vessel---a clean one. So not only does He circumcise your soul from your body, He puts you in the Body of Christ by a spiritual baptism so Christ can dwell in you through the person of the Holy Spirit (1 Cor. 12:13; Col. 1:27).

23 And it came to pass, that he went through the corn fields on the sabbath day; and his disciples began, as they went, to pluck the ears of corn.

24 And the Pharisees said unto him, Behold, why do they on the sabbath day that which is not lawful?

25 And he said unto them, Have ye never read what David did, when he had need, and was an hungred, he, and they that were with him?

26 How he went into the house of God in the days of Abiathar the high priest, and did eat the shewbread, which is not lawful to eat but for the priests, and gave also to them which were with him?

27 And he said unto them, The sabbath was made for []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw343.xhtml_page_66}man, and not man for the sabbath:

28 Therefore the Son of man is Lord also of the sabbath.

The modern translations don't like the words ["corn"]{.class_3054} or ["ears of corn."]{.class_3054} They say ["grain"]{.class_3055} and ["heads of grain."]{.class_3055} Once more, the translators and revisers of the modern versions play the "old shell game": if the [AV]{.class_3055} text is clear, change it to make it "more accurate"; if the text of the [King James]{.class_3055} is accurate, change it to make it "clearer." Whatever the case, the God-honored text of the Protestant Reformation [must be changed]{.class_3055} to match the divine fiats of the scholars.

Here is a case where the [AV]{.class_3055} is absolutely accurate. Although [corn]{.class_3055} is now associated with [maize]{.class_3055} (also called "Indian corn" or "New World corn"), the actual definition is not that. Corn is the [kernels,]{.class_3055} the [seeds,]{.class_3055} of [any]{.class_3055} "cereal" plant, maize being [one]{.class_3055} type of those plants. You can have a corn of wheat, a corn of barley, a corn of oat, a corn of rye, or a corn of "corn." An ["ear of corn"]{.class_3054} is the tip of [any]{.class_3055} of those plants where the kernels are in the husks. Those are not "archaic" terms; they are [agricultural]{.class_3055} terms. So the highly accurate text of the [Authorized Version]{.class_3055} tells you about ["a corn of wheat"]{.class_3054} in John 12:24.

What the Fundamentalists don't like about me is that I don't dismiss the idea that Christ and the disciples could actually be eating what we call "corn"; i.e., maize. Stewart Custer calls that one of "Ruckman's peculiar doctrines." I have discussed the [possibility]{.class_3055} (no definite "doctrine") of corn in the Bible being "New World corn" under our remarks on Luke 6:1 in that Commentary, and we refer you to those comments.

In a case like this, just take for granted that "recognized scholarship" doesn't have good sense half the time, and you'll be safe. There is plenty of evidence that the New and Old Worlds had contact with each other all the way back to the time when Egypt was the dominant world power: that's back there around Genesis 12. It might upset Stewart Custer's applecart to find out the Hebrews ate the same kind of corn we do, but I wouldn't take a baby aspirin over it. But that's because I believe the [King James]{.class_3055} is right, and where scholarship disagrees with it, scholarship can stick its head in a []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw343.xhtml_page_67}bucket of water three times and pull it out twice.

Now the problem the Pharisees have here is that the disciples are plucking ["the ears of corn"]{.class_3054} on the Sabbath. They are always worried about that ceremonial law (Mark 3:2--6; Luke 13:10--14, 14:1--6; John 5:9--16). The reason is that back in Numbers 15:32--36, the Lord ordered a man stoned for gathering sticks on the Sabbath. The command was: ["whosoever doeth any work in the sabbath day, he shall surely be put to death"]{.class_3054} (Exod. 31:15); they weren't even to kindle a fire on the Sabbath (Exod. 35:23). Of course, that ["work"]{.class_3054} had to do with ["doing THY pleasure on my holy day"]{.class_3054} (Isa. 58:13); that's what Leviticus 23 would call ["servile work"]{.class_3054} (Lev. 23:7--8, 21, 25, 35--36). It was work to take care of [one's self.]{.class_3055}

How do you know that to be so? Well, in the parallel passage in Matthew, Jesus says the priests worked in the Temple on the Sabbath but were ["blameless"]{.class_3054} (Matt. 12:5). Why? Because they were doing what [God]{.class_3055} told them to do on the Sabbath (Num. 28:9--10). Over in John 7:22--23, Jesus points out to the Jews that a baby could be circumcised on the Sabbath without the Sabbath being broken, because that is what God said in the Law---[His written word.]{.class_3055}

I don't know if you have ever thought about it before, but when God commanded Joshua to march around the city of Jericho seven days (Josh. 6:3--4), one of those days was the [Sabbath.]{.class_3055} The only reason the Israelites weren't guilty of breaking the Sabbath then was because [they were doing what God called them to do.]{.class_3055}

When Jesus healed on the Sabbath, He was doing the work God the Father had sent Him to do (John 10:25, 32, 37--38, 14:10). The Pharisees [knew]{.class_3055} that (John 3:2), and they [still]{.class_3055} rejected Jesus Christ (John 15:24).

Now take the case here in Mark 2:24. Were the disciples really breaking the Law? Of course not! The Law made allowance for what they were doing.

["When thou comest into the standing corn of thy neighbour, then thou mayest pluck the ears with thine hand; but thou shalt not move a sickle unto thy neighbour's standing corn"]{.class_3054} (Deut. 23:25).

God did not count satisfying immediate hunger as "work"; "work" []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw343.xhtml_page_68}would have been harvesting the crop. Do you know what those hypocrites were doing while complaining about the disciples breaking the Sabbath here? They would loose their beasts from their stalls and lead them to get water (Luke 13:15). The problem these hypocrites had with Jesus Christ was not the Sabbath or the Law; it was [envy]{.class_3055} (15:10).

Now notice how Jesus answers the Pharisees. He answers them exactly as He answered Satan during the temptation: [with Scripture]{.class_3055} (read the accounts in Matt. 4 and Luke 4). That is significant, because later on here in Mark, you read that the Pharisees ask questions of Jesus to [tempt]{.class_3055} Him (8:11, 10:2). That may not be the exact case here, but it is certainly a precursor to those temptations.

["Have ye never read . . . ?"]{.class_3054} (vs. 25). Well, of course they had, but they certainly had no understanding of what they read (12:24).

["Have ye never read what DAVID did . . . ?"]{.class_3054} RED ALERT! This is ["the Son of David"]{.class_3054} (12:35) comparing Himself to His forefather, and the comparison is to David in exile running from his enemy, Saul (1 Sam. 20 cf. 1 Sam. 21:1--6). Although David was anointed to be King, he didn't sit down on any throne for ten years. Here in Mark 2, you have the rightful ["King of the Jews"]{.class_3054} (Matt. 2:2), but He doesn't get ["the throne of his father David"]{.class_3054} (Luke 1:32) for 2,000-plus years. So what you are reading here in Mark 2 is an account of the King in His exile.

Verse 26:[ "How he went into the house of God in the days of Abiathar the high priest."]{.class_3054} There are a couple of things of which we should take note here. The first is ["the house of God"]{.class_3054}: that's a reference to the Tabernacle (Josh. 18:31; Judg. 20:18, 26, 31; 1 Chron. 6:48). Well, the Tabernacle was no "house"; it was a tent (cf. 2 Sam. 7:2). But notice what the Tabernacle is called in 1 Samuel 1:9, 3:3---it is ["the TEMPLE OF THE LORD."]{.class_3054} The Temple was a ["house"]{.class_3054} (1 Kings 6:17). ["The house of God"]{.class_3054} was a particular place and structure where God's presence dwelt among His people. The prophetic significance of all this is that when the time comes for the Jews to rebuild the temple in which the Antichrist will present himself as God (2 Thess. 2:4), they don't necessarily need an elaborate stone structure like Solomon or Herod built; all they need is a [tabernacle]{.class_3055} (Amos 9:11).

The doctrinal application for us today is that there is no church building that is ["the house of God"]{.class_3054}; that is one of those "urban myths" or "Grimm's Fairy Tales" for Baptists. The temple of God in this age is the body of the believer (1 Cor. 6:19), so when Paul and Peter talk about ["the house of God"]{.class_3054} (1 Tim. 3:15; 1 Pet. 4:17), they are talking about the corporate body of believers, not any building. Do you honestly think the National Cathedral in Washington, D.C.---with Moslems leading in prayer!---is "the house of God"? How about Robert Schuller's "Crystal Cathedral," or an ecumenical hellhole like Riverside Church in New York City? ["I trow not."]{.class_3054}

The second thing you need to notice is ["the days of Abiathar the high priest."]{.class_3054} This is supposed to be one of those "contradictions in the Scripture" about which you hear so much because Ahimelech was the priest when David ate the showbread (1 Sam. 21:2--6). Now aside from the fact that there could be two high priests serving at the same time (see our comments on Luke 3:2 in that Commentary), the text does not say Abiathar was high priest [right then.]{.class_3055} ["In the days of"]{.class_3054} can be anytime in the lifetime of such a public figure. As is often the case in such "contradictions," the scholars, who may know Greek, Hebrew, Aramaic, Latin, and any number of other languages, have a hard time reading and comprehending [sixth-grade English.]{.class_3055}

["And did eat the shewbread, which is not lawful to eat but for the priests."]{.class_3054} The showbread was the bread laid out for [show]{.class_3055} on the golden table in the Tabernacle. It "showed" God's daily provision for the twelve tribes of Israel.
