If the Devil can't stop you from hearing the word of God or receiving it, he will work to confuse you so you don't [understand]{.class_3055} it. I was witnessing to a fellow at the gym one time. To get out of believing what the Bible said about Jesus Christ and salvation, he said to me, "Well how do I know what you are saying is right? After all, the Catholics say one thing; the Methodists say another; the Jehovah's Witnesses say something else. How do I know which one is []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw621.xhtml_page_101}right?" I asked him, "Did you go to College?" "Yes," he said. "Did you graduate?" I asked. "Yes." I told that fellow, "You mean to tell me you're a College graduate, and you can't tell when someone is lying to you from Scripture?" What was his problem? The Devil made sure that guy got so many conflicting things from Scripture that he didn't know which end was up, and he wasn't willing to search the Scriptures to see ["whether those things were so"]{.class_3054} (Acts 17:11).

If Satan can't stop you from hearing the word or receiving the word or understanding the word, he will cause you to [forget]{.class_3055} the word. He'll get you thinking about what you are going to have for lunch or the ball game or what's on television; that way you will forget what the preacher said that got you under conviction. And if none of that will work, the final play is to get you to [disobey]{.class_3055} the word.

That's the Devil's work; it's [spiritual.]{.class_3055} It's not pornography or abortion or "LGBT" or liquor or "sexuality." All those things are flesh. Since Genesis 3, the Devil's job has been to [attack what God said.]{.class_3055} Satan is concerned with [that Book.]{.class_3055}

The ["stony ground"]{.class_3054} hearers are [temporary]{.class_3055} because they are [shallow]{.class_3055} (["not much earth"]{.class_3054}---vs. 5). They receive the word, but it doesn't take root. To all appearance, they become "Christians," but they are [lost.]{.class_3055} With no [root,]{.class_3055} there is no [fruit]{.class_3055} (Prov. 12:12). The ["root"]{.class_3054} is [Jesus Christ]{.class_3055} (Rom. 15:12; Rev. 5:5, 22:16). Without Him, the facade of conversion is quickly shed ["when affliction or persecution ariseth for the word's sake"]{.class_3054} (vs. 17).

Now, the hearers ["among thorns"]{.class_3054} (vs. 18) are different. They [do]{.class_3055} have root; that means they are [saved.]{.class_3055} The reason they don't bear fruit is not because they have a careless, hard heart or because they have a shallow, temporary heart. The word takes root and begins to grow, but ["it BECOMETH unfruitful"]{.class_3054} (vs. 19) because they are [choked.]{.class_3055} What's the problem? They are [worldly.]{.class_3055}

That is the case of Demas. Demas goes from being a fellow-laborer with the Apostle Paul (Phile. 24) to a dropout in the Lord's service because he ["loved this present world"]{.class_3054} (2 Tim. 4:10). That is why the Christian is commanded: ["Love not the world, neither the things that are in the world"]{.class_3054} (1 John 2:15). Loving ["this present evil world,"]{.class_3054} from which Christ delivered you at salvation (Gal. 1:4), will [kill]{.class_3055}[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw621.xhtml_page_102} any fruit-bearing you can do for your Saviour.

Now look at the three things, in verse 19, that the world brings to bear on the believer. One---["the cares of this world,"]{.class_3054} called ["cares of this life"]{.class_3054} in Luke 21:34. What's that? Oh, that's the fight you had with your wife on the way to church; that's the kids needing braces on their teeth and how in the world are you going to get the money to pay for it; that's the washing machine breaking down and having to go down to the laundromat to get the wash done; that's the lawn needing to be mown and the tires on the car needing to be rotated and the kids needing to be driven to their game and the in-laws coming for a visit. ["The cares of the world"]{.class_3054} are all the little, piddly things of life that can keep you from the word of God, prayer, and witnessing. They aren't necessarily wrong; they are just a [distraction.]{.class_3055}

Two---["the deceitfulness of riches."]{.class_3054} That's ["the love of money"]{.class_3054} about which Paul warned you in 1 Timothy 6:10. The desire for riches is a [lie.]{.class_3055} It promises to solve all your problems, but all it does is give you a whole new set of worries (Eccl. 5:10). The old saying is: "Money talks; it says 'bye-bye'" (Prov. 23:5). A study was done on these lottery winners, and what was discovered was that within as little as two years, they were flat broke, and their families were busted up. Money [lies.]{.class_3055} Money is a trap (1 Tim. 6:9); it will cause you to "err from the faith" (1 Tim. 6:10).

Three---["the lusts of other things."]{.class_3054} That's [covetousness]{.class_3055} (Exod. 20:17). A person may not be "hell bent" to get rich; but he may want fame, sex, food, sports, power, land, comfort---["other things."]{.class_3054} That's called "hedonism" in philosophy, and it was practiced by ["the Epicureans"]{.class_3054} Paul encountered in Acts 17:18. Solomon, the wisest man on earth outside of Christ (1 Kings 3:12), tried it all, and his assessment was: ["this also is VANITY"]{.class_3054} (Eccl. 2:1). Lust brings forth sin, and sin brings forth death (James 1:15). Covetousness will kill your fruit-bearing ability for Jesus Christ deader than a mackerel on the beach last month.

The fourth set of hearers is the [fruitful]{.class_3055} group because they had ["an honest and good heart"]{.class_3054} (Luke 8:15). Now naturally, no man has a ["good heart"]{.class_3054} (Jer. 17:9), but when a person responds [correctly]{.class_3055} to the reproving work of the Holy Spirit (John 16:7--11), that is when the Lord counts that heart as ["honest and good."]{.class_3054}[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw621.xhtml_page_103} That heart receives God's word as [God's]{.class_3055} word and not as man's (1 Thess. 2:13), and it keeps it (Rev. 3:8) by doing what it says (James 1:22). Here, the hearer not only receives it and does what it says in regards to salvation, but in regards to bearing fruit, as well (John 15:1--8; Gal. 5:22--25).

Notice the three levels of fruit bearing in verse 20. This matches what Christ said in John 15: ["thirty fold"---"fruit"]{.class_3054} (John 15:2), ["sixty"---"more fruit"]{.class_3054} (John 15:2), ["an hundred"---"much fruit"]{.class_3054} (John 15:5, 8). A good example of this is the Syrian palm tree. It comes in three sizes: thirty feet, sixty feet, and an hundred feet.

These three levels of Christian experience are found throughout the New Testament in different forms. The way the Christian gains victory over Satan is: 1) ["by the blood of the Lamb,"]{.class_3054} 2) ["by the word of their testimony,"]{.class_3054} and 3) ["they loved not their lives unto the death"]{.class_3054} (Rev. 12:11). The three levels of Christian commitment are: 1) ["called,"]{.class_3054} 2)[ "chosen,"]{.class_3054} and 3)[ "faithful"]{.class_3054} (Rev. 17:14). The three stages of Christian growth in 1 John 2:12--13 are: 1) ["little children,"]{.class_3054} 2)[ "young men,"]{.class_3054} 3)[ "fathers."]{.class_3054} The Jews had three levels of mastery when it came to teaching the Old Testament: Rab, Rabbi, Rabban. In the Twenty-first Century, that translated to: a Bachelor's degree, a Master's degree, and a Doctor's degree. In the labor unions, it's: apprentice, journeyman, master. In education, it's: kindergarten, grade school, and College; grade school is: elementary, junior high (or middle school), and high school. It's all through there.

21 And he said unto them, Is a candle brought to be put under a bushel, or under a bed? and not to be set on a candlestick?

22 For there is nothing hid, which shall not be manifested; neither was any thing kept secret, but that it should come abroad.

23 If any man have ears to hear, let him hear.

24 And he said unto them, Take heed what ye hear: with what measure ye mete, it shall be measured to you: and unto you that hear shall more be given.

25 For he that hath, to him shall be given: and he that hath not, from him shall be taken even that which he hath.

This is the Parable of the Candle, and the application is apparent: a candle is lit to give light and not be hidden. In the Scriptures, Jesus Christ is ["the light of the world"]{.class_3054} (John 8:12). Jesus said, ["As long as I am in the world, I am the light of the world"]{.class_3054} (John 9:5). Well, Jesus Christ is no longer ["in the world,"]{.class_3054} not physically, at least; He's up in Heaven at the Father's right hand.

[But Jesus Christ is ]{.class_3062}[in the believer:]{.class_3063}[ "Christ in you, the hope of glory"]{.class_3061}[ (Col. 1:27). So Jesus said, ]{.class_3062}["Ye]{.class_3061}[ \[believers\]]{.class_3062}[ are the light of the world"]{.class_3061}[ (Matt. 5:14). As a ]{.class_3062}["child of light"]{.class_3061}[ (1 Thess. 5:5), a Christian is commanded to ]{.class_3062}["walk in the light"]{.class_3061}[ (1 John 1:7), and when he doesn't, he is commanded to awake from sleep, and let Christ give him light (Eph. 5:14).]{.class_3062}

The children's Sunday school song goes: "This little light of mine, I'm gonna let it shine." From where do they get that? Matthew 5:16---["Let your light so shine before men, that they may see your good works, and glorify your Father which is in heaven."]{.class_3054}

Southerners have all kinds of ways of getting around it. "Well, preacher, I don't believe in talking about it; I believe in living it," meaning salvation and the Christian life. The idea is if you're "living it," then you're saved, and if you aren't, then you're not. That's why many a "good ole' boy" doesn't get saved: "Well, I ain't gonna get saved until I can live it."

But the verse didn't say, "Let your works shine so men can see your light"; it said, ["Let your light shine . . . that they may see your good works."]{.class_3054} In other words, the ["light"]{.class_3054} is [not]{.class_3055} the ["works,"]{.class_3054} but the ["works"]{.class_3054} are the result of the ["light."]{.class_3054} You'll go to Hell trying to "live it" if you don't have the ["light"]{.class_3054} in you.[ "He that followeth ME shall not walk in darkness, but shall have the light of life"]{.class_3054} (John 8:12).

These "good ole' boys" who don't believe in "talking about it" don't "talk about it" because they don't want people looking too closely at the way they live. If you really want people to ["see your good works"]{.class_3054} so they can ["glorify your Father which is in heaven,"]{.class_3054}[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw621.xhtml_page_105} I'll tell you how to do it: you just speak up for Jesus Christ, and they'll watch you like a hawk. You let the ["light shine,"]{.class_3054} and you will get all the scrutiny you can stand, plus some. Just "living it" doesn't do anything for a lost sinner because he figures all you are doing is trying to get to Heaven by your own self-righteousness just like him. You have to ["let your light shine."]{.class_3054}

Verse 22: ["For there is nothing hid, which shall not be manifested; neither was any thing kept secret, but that it should come abroad."]{.class_3054} That's rough, boy. That's Jesus Christ judging ["the secrets of men"]{.class_3054} at the Judgment (Rom. 2:16). Back there in Ezekiel 11:5, the Holy Spirit said, ["I know the things that come into your mind, every one of them."]{.class_3054} Solomon said in Ecclesiastes 12:14 that God will ["bring every work into judgment, with every secret thing, whether it be good, or whether it be evil."]{.class_3054} That goes for the Christian as much as for the lost person. For the Christian, it is at the Judgment Seat of Christ (2 Cor. 5:10; 1 Cor. 4:5).

Now look at how verses 21--22 follow on the heels of the Parable of the Sower. The idea is that you can't hide how you react to the word of God. Sooner or later, it will be evident to everyone how you received the "seed." Someone once said, "When I get to Heaven, I will have two surprises. It will surprise me to find out who [is]{.class_3055} there, and it will surprise me to find out who [isn't]{.class_3055} there." Those Jews would have sworn those Pharisees and scribes were headed for Paradise, and the publicans and sinners were headed for Hell. But as Jesus revealed in the Parable of the Pharisee and the Publican in Luke 18:10--14, those Pharisees who were touting their own self-righteousness were as lost as a goose in a horse race, and the publicans and harlots who repented and threw themselves on the mercy of God were the ones justified before Him. Some day, you are going to find out that many a bum down at the rescue mission made it to Glory because he trusted Christ, while many a "televangelist," pastor, and church prelate, who were relying on their works, didn't (Matt. 7:22--23).

Again, in verse 23, Jesus gives the same warning He gave in verse 9: ["If any man have ears to hear, let him hear."]{.class_3054} Now why does He repeat the warning? Verse 24: ["Take heed WHAT ye hear."]{.class_3054} You had better watch out to [what]{.class_3055}[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw621.xhtml_page_106} you are listening. It had better be the [word of God]{.class_3055} (vs. 14) and not some man's [tradition]{.class_3055} (7:9, 13) or [private interpretation]{.class_3055} (2 Pet. 1:20). Watch out that what you are hearing isn't some "prosperity gospel" or extrabiblical revelation or someone's "vision" or religious "psychobabble" or "Jesus Calling" or "I'm OK; you're OK," etc.

["With what measure ye mete, it shall be measured to you."]{.class_3054} The application in Luke 6:37--38 is judging with mercy and forgiveness, and giving. The idea is if you show mercy and forgiveness, God is going to requite those things to you when you need them (see Matt. 18:23--35). The principle, as applied to giving, is that if you give generously, the Lord will use men to give it back to you "in spades" (2 Cor. 9:6--8).

The application here is the attention you give to the word of God: ["and unto you that hear shall more be given." ]{.class_3054}That is why in Luke 8:18, the parallel passage to verse 24 here in Mark 4, Jesus says, ["Take heed . . . HOW ye hear."]{.class_3054} Make sure that not only do you listen to the [word of God]{.class_3055} but that you do so in [faith]{.class_3055} and [obedience]{.class_3055} (1 Thess. 2:13; James 1:22). The characteristic of Christ's sheep is that they hear the voice of their Good Shepherd and follow Him (John 10:27).

Now there are two parables given by Christ that illustrate the principle of verse 25. One is the Parable of the Talents in Matthew 25:14--30; that deals with the Jew in the Tribulation. The other is the Parable of the Pounds in Luke 19:12--27; that deals with Gentiles in this age. In both parables, the servants who gained more for their master by trading, received more as a reward. The servant who hid his lord's money in the ground lost what he had. The principle is, don't be idle with what the Lord has given you. You've been entrusted with the Gospel; put it out. If you've been given a certain ability, use it for the Lord. "Use it or you'll lose it"; that's the old saying.

26 And he said, So is the kingdom of God, as if a man should cast seed into the ground;

27 And should sleep, and rise night and day, and the seed should spring and grow up, he knoweth not how.

28 For the earth bringeth forth fruit of herself; first the blade, then the ear, after that the full corn in the ear.

29 But when the fruit is brought forth, immediately he putteth in the sickle, because the harvest is come.

In Matthew 13, the Parable of the Sower is followed by the Parable of the Wheat and the Tares. That is not the case here. This is the Parable of the Seed and its Growth. This is not a contrast between the good seed (the wheat) and the bad (the tares). This is the growth of the good seed.

The applications here have some differences from that of the Parable of the Sower, which should be noted. The sower here can't be Jesus Christ (cf. Matt. 13:37) because this man sleeps and lacks knowledge of how the seed grows. In the Parable of the Wheat and the Tares, it is ["men"]{.class_3054} who sleep (Matt. 13:25)---the servants, whom the Sower entrusted with guarding the field from the enemy---not ["the householder"]{.class_3054} who sowed the seed.

Moreover, in the Parable of the Wheat and Tares, the ["seed"]{.class_3054} is defined as ["the children of the kingdom"]{.class_3054} (Matt. 13:38)---those saved, who are taken to Heaven in a rapture (Matt. 13:30, 43). Why the switch from the word of God? Because in Psalm 22:30, the ["seed"]{.class_3054} is equated with ["a generation,"]{.class_3054} and that ["generation"]{.class_3054} is produced by a new birth that comes from ["the word of God"]{.class_3054} (1 Pet. 1:23).

The application is better suited here in Mark to an earthly soulwinner. He sows the good seed in the world (["the ground,"]{.class_3054} cf. Matt. 13:38), and as time passes (["And should sleep, and rise night and day"]{.class_3054}---vs. 27), the seed sprouts and grows in a way that ["he knoweth not how"]{.class_3054} (vs. 27).

Of course, infidels will grasp onto that last phrase and say, "See, the Bible can't be from God because here in the Twenty-first Century, we do know how a plant grows." But [describing]{.class_3055} something is not [explaining]{.class_3055} it. Anybody in Christ's day could describe in generalities how a plant grew. The fact that you can add a few more details to the description from a genetic and cellular standpoint doesn't mean you've explained anything.

A seed of corn not any bigger than your thumb can produce []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw621.xhtml_page_108}stalks bearing ninety kernels just like the one you planted in the ground. Every one of those ninety kernels can produce more stalks bearing ninety more kernels just like it. Let's see you actually [explain]{.class_3055} that. Why, you can use all the scientific jargon you want, and in the end, you haven't really explained anything. All you have done is describe the process as you saw it.

Suppose I told you about a man who had ten billion children. You say, "That's impossible." No, it isn't. Every man, woman, and child who has ever lived on this earth came from [Adam.]{.class_3055} You sitting here in the Twenty-first Century were in Adam back in 4004 B.C.; it just took you sixty centuries to get here. Explain that. You can talk about genes, chromosomes, and zygotes all you want; and you haven't explained anything. The very idea that that came about by random chance (evolution)! Why, it's [supernatural!]{.class_3055}

["For the earth bringeth forth fruit of herself"]{.class_3054} (vs. 28). That is the case in Genesis 1:11--12. I bet some of you thought I was just "talking" when I said it is supernatural. God told ["the earth"]{.class_3054} to ["bring forth grass, the herb yielding seed, and the fruit tree yielding fruit after his kind, whose seed is in itself"]{.class_3054}; and it did. There was no seed in the earth to produce all of that. That was a supernatural act of God.

The old "chestnut" is: "Which came first, the chicken or the egg?" That is a question posed by evolutionists that is supposed to be unanswerable. It's not if you believe what God said: the chicken, of course. God created the birds and then told them to multiply. How hard was that to figure out, at least if you accepted the creation account of the Bible instead of some "fairy tale for grown-ups"?

Now when it comes to leading a soul to Christ, that's a supernatural thing, man. You may plant the seed, you may water the seed, but it is [God]{.class_3055} who gives the increase, NOT YOU (1 Cor. 3:6). When it comes to causing the word of God to sprout in the heart of a sinner and grow and produce fruit, the whole thing is of God; you are NOTHING (1 Cor. 3:7).

Evangelical Christians, especially Fundamental Baptists, talk about "getting people saved." I understand what they mean by that, and I have used the same terminology. It means you presented the Gospel to someone, and they received Christ. But you didn't get anybody saved---[God]{.class_3055}[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw621.xhtml_page_109} did that. All you did was plow, plant, water, or reap; but God caused His word to produce the fruit.

When I used to go on these prison junkets during the summer and saw hundreds of men saved, I have no idea how many times someone witnessed to those men before I got there. I may have been reaping a harvest begun years earlier by some saved momma or grandma or bus worker or Sunday school teacher or personal worker. There may have been any number of people in the interim who watered that seed---concerned relatives who mailed a Bible or tracts to that prisoner, chaplains, missionaries to the prisons, Christian radio programs; you name it. All God allowed me to do was be there when it was time to put the sickle to the corn (vs. 29).

30 And he said, Whereunto shall we liken the kingdom of God? or with what comparison shall we compare it?

31 It is like a grain of mustard seed, which, when it is sown in the earth, is less than all the seeds that be in the earth:

32 But when it is sown, it groweth up, and becometh greater than all herbs, and shooteth out great branches; so that the fowls of the air may lodge under the shadow of it.

Verse 30 is a good definition for a parable: it is a likening or a comparison. The two greatest words for understanding the Bible are [like]{.class_3055} and [as.]{.class_3055} God says in Hosea 12:10 that He ["used similitudes, by the ministry of the prophets."]{.class_3054} A good example here in Mark, in chapter 9, is when Christ was trying to teach people what Hell was like, He said, "Look over there to those fires burning in the city dump ([gehenna]{.class_3055}); Hell is like that." Here in this passage, Christ uses the similitude of a mustard seed to teach the people what the Kingdom of God is like in this age.

The infidels (among whom are apostate Evangelicals and "Conservatives") once more try to find an "error" in the Bible in verse 21. There are seeds smaller than the mustard seed (e.g., the orchid seed). But again, the infidel and apostate ignore to whom Christ is speaking and refuse to let the Scriptures interpret the Scriptures. ["All"]{.class_3054} here in verse 31 doesn't mean "all without []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw621.xhtml_page_110}exception" any more than it did in 1:5. Christ is talking to Old Testament Jews in Palestine, not orchid growers in the tropics. He is talking about the seeds these Old Testament Jews in Palestine would sow in their field (Matt. 13:31) or garden (Luke 13:19). Among [those]{.class_3055} seeds, the mustard seed was ["less than all the seeds that be in the earth."]{.class_3054} Folks do have a time of it, don't they?

["But when it is sown, it groweth up, and becometh greater than all herbs, and shooteth out great branches; so that the fowls of the air may lodge under the shadow of it"]{.class_3054} (vs. 32). Here all the apologetic literature fails to grasp the import of what the Lord is saying. While it may be true that the mustard seed of Palestine can grow into a twelve-foot tree in which birds can nest, that is hardly what Jesus is trying to get across with this ["comparison."]{.class_3054} The commentators will go into a pious discourse about the Gospel increasing around the world and being a shelter to the nations of the world, which is private interpretation of the most horrendous sort. Setting aside the apologists and commentators, let us take up the Holy Scriptures (Rom. 1:2; 2 Tim. 3:15) and let the Spirit of God lead us into all truth (John 16:13) by ["comparing spiritual things with spiritual"]{.class_3054} (1 Cor. 2:13) in His Book.

The ["mustard seed"]{.class_3054} is not the Gospel; it is [faith]{.class_3055} (Matt. 17:20; Luke 17:6). The problem with faith, in regards to the Kingdom of God in this age, is that it degenerates until the Lord questions whether He will find any true faith upon His return (Luke 18:8).

The faith here becomes a nesting place for [evil spirits]{.class_3055} (Rev. 18:2). Remember those ["fowls of the air"]{.class_3054} back up in verse 4? They weren't any "nations"; they were ["Satan"]{.class_3054} (vs. 15).

Any student of the Old Testament should be familiar with what he is reading here in verse 32. It's Nebuchadnezzar's tree in Daniel 4; it's the Assyrian's tree in Ezekiel 31. It's a picture of the [Antichrist's kingdom.]{.class_3055} This mustard seed here in Mark 4 isn't the Gospel growing and spreading throughout the whole earth until it takes it over (Postmillennialism). It is a [religious monstrosity]{.class_3055} full of [demons,]{.class_3055} and it is associated with [Rome.]{.class_3055}

33 And with many such parables spake he the word unto them, as they were able to hear it.

34 But without a parable spake he not unto them: and when they were alone, he expounded all things to his disciples.

Notice again, the multitudes get the parables; the disciples get the explanations. We discussed the reasons for this back up in verses 11--12. According to Matthew 13:35, this was a fulfillment of Psalm 78:2--3.

Jesus gave the parables ["as they were able to hear it."]{.class_3054} That is, ["hear it"]{.class_3054} in the sense of [receiving]{.class_3055} what He said. There were ["many things"]{.class_3054} Christ wanted to say to His disciples, but they weren't ready for them (John 16:12). Paul couldn't give some things to his converts in Corinth because they hadn't grown up yet, spiritually (1 Cor. 3:1--2). The writer of Hebrews couldn't tell some things to the Jews to whom he wrote for the same reason: they were ["dull of hearing"]{.class_3054} because they had been sucking on the bottle for longer than they should have (Heb. 5:11--14). Milk is good (1 Pet. 2:2), but sooner or later, you need to grow up so you can eat some beef steak along with it.

Note, [again,]{.class_3055} the use of the word ["all"]{.class_3054} in verse 34. It's not "all without exception"; it rarely is. Jesus expounded to His disciples all the parables He had taught to the crowd. He didn't tell them the date of the Rapture or the Advent. At this time, He didn't even tell them about Him dying for their sins.

35 And the same day, when the even was come, he saith unto them, Let us pass over unto the other side.

36 And when they had sent away the multitude, they took him even as he was in the ship. And there were also with him other little ships.

37 And there arose a great storm of wind, and the waves beat into the ship, so that it was now full.

38 And he was in the hinder part of the ship, asleep on a pillow: and they awake him, and say unto him, Master, carest thou not that we perish?

39 And he arose, and rebuked the wind, and said unto the sea, Peace, be still. And the wind ceased, and there was a []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw621.xhtml_page_112}great calm.

40 And he said unto them, Why are ye so fearful? how is it that ye have no faith?

41 And they feared exceedingly, and said one to another, What manner of man is this, that even the wind and the sea obey him?

At the end of the day, Jesus, who had been teaching the crowds from a boat in the water (vs. 1), decides He wants to ["pass over unto the other side"]{.class_3054} (vs. 35); i.e., sail over to the other side of the Sea of Galilee. What happens is a terrible storm arises when the ship gets halfway across the lake, and the disciples panic, thinking they are going to die. But Jesus didn't say, "Let's sail to the middle of the lake and drown"; He said, ["Let us pass over unto the other side."]{.class_3054} That is a plain statement that they were going to make it, no matter what arose in the journey. That's why He chides them for their lack of faith in verse 40; they didn't believe what He told them.

Let that be a lesson to you when you doubt your salvation. If you are saved, your salvation doesn't depend on what you or anybody else does. It depends on the One who saved you, and He said that when He gives you ["eternal life,"]{.class_3054} you will ["never perish"]{.class_3054} (John 10:28). Is that clear? The promise is:

["Being confident of this very thing, that he which hath begun a good work in you will perform it until the day of Jesus Christ"]{.class_3054} (Phil. 1:6).

When you get saved, your destiny has already been determined. That is what [predestination]{.class_3055} means. You have the promise of God that you will end up exactly like your Saviour (Rom. 8:29), and that [nothing]{.class_3055} can stop that (Rom. 8:38--39). So if you constantly doubt your salvation, do you know what you are doing? You are calling God a liar! He said you can [know]{.class_3055} you are saved and on your way to Heaven (1 John 5:13). Now do you know or don't you?

You say, "I don't know, Dr. Ruckman." Then you had better examine yourself to see whether you are ["in the faith"]{.class_3054} or not (2 Cor. 13:5). What are you depending on [right now]{.class_3055} to get you to Heaven? If you are trusting the fact that you are a good person, then you have reason to doubt your salvation. If you are trusting your []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw621.xhtml_page_113}baptism, church attendance, or giving, you have just cause for wondering if you are saved or not. If you are trusting in how much you believed or repented, no wonder you are screwed up. But if you are trusting in the righteousness of Jesus Christ afforded you by faith in His sacrificial death on Calvary and His resurrection from the dead, I have bad news for you: you are going to make it to Heaven, whether you like it or not. You might as well quit your doubting and enjoy the ride. [Believe the promises of God!]{.class_3055}

["Who shall also CONFIRM YOU UNTO THE END, that ye may be BLAMELESS in the day of our Lord Jesus Christ"]{.class_3054} (1 Cor. 1:8).

Back to Mark 4: ["And when they had sent away the multitude, they took him even as he was in the ship"]{.class_3054} (vs. 36). In other words, they didn't change vessels; they set sail in the ship from which He taught. ["And there were also with him other little ships."]{.class_3054} Mark gives you a detail here not found in the parallel accounts in Matthew 8:23 and Luke 8:22. Some of the multitude followed Christ and the disciples in smaller vessels, so there were more than just one boat out on the sea when the storm hit.

Now you can understand why the disciples were upset. It was not just a storm; it was ["a GREAT storm"]{.class_3054} (vs. 37). And despite their bailing, the ship was filling up with water. I imagine that would shake you up a bit if you were in a similar situation. When a hurricane hits, folks either panic, run, or pray; and sometimes, they do all three.

Where is Jesus while all of this is going on? He's ["in the hinder part of the ship, asleep on a pillow"]{.class_3054} (vs. 38). After all, He told them they were going to make it to the other side, so the storm didn't bother Him.

Verse 38 is a great picture of the [humanity]{.class_3055} of Christ. Fundamentalists focus so much on His Godhood that sometimes they overlook His humanity. He was [both:]{.class_3055} 100% God, 100% man. As a man, He got tired and had to sleep; He grew hungry and had to eat; He thirsted and required drink. As God, ["he that keepeth Israel shall neither slumber nor sleep"]{.class_3054} (Psa. 121:4). But here in Mark 4, this is ["the man Christ Jesus" ]{.class_3054}(cf. 1 Tim. 2:5) asleep in the back of the boat.

I have done that, but I didn't sleep on a pillow. I have been fishing all night with Coy Raffield. After bringing in a big haul with hundreds and hundreds of fish dumped all over the deck where some of them were sliding off the back end, everybody was so tired after they got through at about three in the morning they just fell down where they were and went to sleep. I just laid down on a pile of dead fish and went to sleep.

Back to verse 38:[ "and they awake him, and say unto him, Master, carest thou not that we perish?"]{.class_3054} That's from where the song "Master, the Tempest is Raging" comes. It likens the storms of life to what you are reading here. The idea of the hymn writer is if Christ could get the disciples here in Mark 4 through the storm, He's not going to have any problem with your troubles.

Verse 39 is Jesus exercising His authority as Creator. This is Jehovah God who is ["mightier than the noise of many waters, yea, than the mighty waves of the sea"]{.class_3064-0} (Psa. 93:4). ["He maketh the storm a calm, so that the waves thereof are still"]{.class_3064-0} (Psa. 107:29).

Did you ever read about Mohammed doing anything like that? Of course not! All Mohammed was was a camel trader turned military dictator after receiving messages from a devil during epileptic fits. Mohammed couldn't calm a tempest in a teapot.

Verse 40: ["Why are ye so fearful? how is it that ye have no faith?"]{.class_3054} Well, you can understand why they were ["so fearful"]{.class_3054}---see our remarks under verse 37. But the point Christ is making is that they didn't have to be fearful of the storm if they had just believed what He had said.

That Bible says, ["So then faith cometh by hearing, and hearing by the word of God"]{.class_3054} (Rom. 10:17). If those disciples had been in their Old Testament Scripture like they should have been, they would have read this:

["God is our refuge and strength, a very present help in trouble. Therefore WILL NOT WE FEAR, though the earth be removed, and though the mountains be carried into the midst of the sea; THOUGH THE WATERS THEREOF ROAR AND BE TROUBLED, though the mountains shake with the swelling thereof. Selah"]{.class_3054} (Psa. 46:1--3).

The Lord told those Jews, through Isaiah: []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw621.xhtml_page_115}["When thou passest through the waters, I will be with thee]{.class_3054} \[like He was there in Mark 4\][; and through the rivers, they shall not overflow thee ]{.class_3054}\[like they didn't in Mark 4\]["]{.class_3054} (Isa. 43:2).

So Christian, the way to make it through troubles in this life is to get in the Scriptures; that is the way you will build up your faith. Find the promises, write them down, memorize them, and claim them when you go through trouble.

["And they feared exceedingly"]{.class_3054} (vs. 41). Good; they were getting some wisdom in the matter (Psa. 111:10; Prov. 9:10). Modern Christians have been talked out of fearing God, and that is why this country is in such a mess (see Job 28:28; Prov. 3:7). They have been told the fear of God is "reverencing" Him when it isn't (read Heb. 12:28 and note that reverence and fear are [two separate things]{.class_3055}). Some fool has told them they shouldn't fear God because ["There is no fear in love; but perfect love casteth out fear"]{.class_3054} (1 John 4:18). They never ["searched the scriptures"]{.class_3054} (cf. Acts 17:11) to see if they had been lied to. If they had, they would have discovered in the preceding verse (1 John 4:17) that the fear being discussed in 1 John 4:18 was the [fear of going to Hell]{.class_3055} if you were a believer. Fearing God enough to avoid sin and do right wasn't even in John's mind when he wrote 1 John 4:18.

Peter tells you to ["fear God"]{.class_3054} (1 Pet. 2:17). Paul tells you to have ["the fear of God"]{.class_3054} (2 Cor. 7:1; Eph. 5:21), and I don't mean "holy reverence," either. A man is a [fool]{.class_3055} who doesn't fear a God of absolute holiness and righteousness; I don't care if he is saved.

["What manner of man is this, that even the wind and the sea obey him?"]{.class_3054} He's the Creator in human form; that's who (John 1:1--3, 14; Col. 1:16--17; Heb. 1:2). He's the man who does away with Mohammed and Buddha and Mary and the Popes and Joseph Smith and the rest of the "wannnabees" by coming up out of the grave ahead of schedule. None of the rest of them did. ["What manner of man is this . . . ?"]{.class_3054} He's the [God-man.]{.class_3055}

[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw759.xhtml}

# Chapter 5 {.heading_97}

5:1 And they came over unto the other side of the sea, into the country of the Gadarenes.

2 And when he was come out of the ship, immediately there met him out of the tombs a man with an unclean spirit,

3 Who had his dwelling among the tombs; and no man could bind him, no, not with chains:

4 Because that he had been often bound with fetters and chains, and the chains had been plucked asunder by him, and the fetters broken in pieces: neither could any man tame him.

5 And always, night and day, he was in the mountains, and in the tombs, crying, and cutting himself with stones.

The Bible correctors get off to a roaring start right out of the gate in verse 1. You see, Matthew 8:28 reads ["the country of the Gergesenes,"]{.class_3054} while verse 1 here in Mark 5 reads ["the country of the Gadarenes."]{.class_3054} In order to correct this "problem," the modern versions, following the Liberal idea that Mark was the first Gospel written and that Matthew copied from him, change ["Gergesenes"]{.class_3054} to ["Gadarenes"]{.class_3055} ([RV, ASV, RSV, NASV, NIV, ESV,]{.class_3055} etc.). Then for some peculiar reason, they then change ["Gadarenes"]{.class_3054} here in Mark 5:1 to ["Gerasenes"]{.class_3055} (an "updated" version of Gergasenes), thus leaving you right back where you started, but now with the God-honored text in Greek (the [Received Text]{.class_3055}) and English (the [Authorized Version]{.class_3055}) left in shambles.

The problem, you see, was not in the text at all. The majority of manuscripts have the reading of the [TR]{.class_3055} and the [AV]{.class_3055} in both passages. The problem was the limited intelligence of "scholars" who elevated novelty (Acts 17:21) over the time-honored testimony of the Holy Spirit, through the Body of Christ, to the Biblical text. If the scholars had studied their [Bibles,]{.class_3055} instead of the "original text" or "older and better manuscripts," they would have known that towns in the Bible could have [more than one name]{.class_3055} (e.g., see our remarks about Debir in Judges 1:11 in the []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw759.xhtml_page_117}[Bible Believer's Commentary on Judges and Ruth]{.class_3055}). Gergasa and Gadara were two names for the same area on the eastern shore of Galilee, and there never was any "error" that needed correcting in either passage.

But the infidels and apostates don't stop with verse 1, they move on to verse 2, in hopes of shaking your confidence in the authority of the [Authorized]{.class_3055} text. Did Jesus encounter [two]{.class_3055} demon-possessed men (Matt. 8:28) or only one (Mark 5:2)? Well, I'll tell you, if Jesus encountered two, then He "sure as shootin'" encountered one of the two. You talk about grasping for straws to find a contradiction in the Bible, [that's it.]{.class_3055}

Matthew will give you the exact number of people with whom Jesus deals (as the maniacs in Matt. 8:28 and the blind men in Matt. 20:30), while Mark will focus on Christ's dealing with a particular one of them (5:2, 10:46). Now how hard was that to figure out, at least if you were trying to believe and justify what God said instead of casting doubt on it because you were lazy, stupid, or just as crooked as a dog's hind leg?

Now what you are reading is the definitive passage on demon possession. Notice the characteristics in verses 3--5:

1\) [An affinity for dead bodies]{.class_3055} (vs. 3). This fellow ["had his dwelling among the tombs"]{.class_3054}: he lived out in the cemetery with the dead.

You couldn't pay me to do that. When it comes to that sort of thing, I'm just like a black man. There are a couple of jokes about colored boys going through the cemetery at night and getting chased by ghosts. In the first, the ghost gets to chasing this one colored fellow, and he falls down and skins his elbows and knees. He gets up and runs a little further and bangs his shins on a tombstone. He hobbles on until he comes to the cemetery fence and cuts himself all up on the barb wire going over it. The next morning, his neighbor sees him as he comes home all battered, bruised, and bloodied, and says, "What happened to you?!" The black guy says, "A ghost done chased me all night." "Don't you know a ghost can't hurt you," said the neighbor. "I know," said the "darkie," "but they sho' do make you hurt yo'self."

A ghost chases a little colored boy through the cemetery one []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw759.xhtml_page_118}night. After running about two miles, the little boy sits down against a tree to rest. About then the ghost catches up and plops himself down by that kid, just a huffing and a puffing. "Boy, we sho' did a mess a runnin'," said the ghost. "Yeah," replied the boy, "and when I catches my breath, we gonna do a heap mo'."

Now that's funny, but it wouldn't be if you actually knew somebody living out in the graveyard. Many years ago, now, somebody came into the Whitmire Cemetery down the road from the Bible Baptist Church here in Pensacola. He dug up the body of a twelve-year-old girl who had been recently buried, and he gnawed on that corpse. Ed Gein, up in Wisconsin, from 1947 to 1952, dug up dead bodies and made things out of the skin and bones, including masks from women's heads.

That Bible says, ["He]{.class_3054} \[i.e., [God]{.class_3055}\][ is not the God of the dead, but the God of the living"]{.class_3054} (12:27). When Christ arose, the angels asked the women who came to the tomb, ["Why seek ye the living among the dead?"]{.class_3054} (Luke 24:5). If you want a ["living God"]{.class_3054} (1 Tim. 4:10) instead of a dead one, don't go to Mohammed; he's still dead and buried there in Medina. Don't go to Buddha; those Hindus parade his bones around every year and build shrines to them. Don't go to Lenin; his desiccated corpse is entombed in a glass coffin and on display for everyone to see. Ditto many bodies of the Popes. Those are all [dead gods.]{.class_3055} My Saviour didn't stay dead; His tomb is [empty.]{.class_3055}

2\) [Unnatural strength]{.class_3055} (vs. 4). The fellow could break the chains and fetters with which he was bound. Now I understand that under great stress, the body can produce enough adrenaline to give a person extraordinary strength. From time to time, you will read in a newspaper about some mother or daddy lifting up a car to free a trapped child. I'm not talking about that; that is a physical reaction of the body to a panic situation.

Much closer are those teenage kids on drugs like "angel dust" (PCP). Those kids get so hyped-up on those drugs that ten grown men have a hard time holding them down. Like I said, that is much closer to demon possession because there is some indication in Scripture that sorcery is connected with drug use. Still, that's the body's [physiological]{.class_3055} reaction to a chemical.

Inmates in insane asylums and psychiatric wards sometimes display great strength. As I said in our remarks under 3:21--22, some madness is caused by devils, but some is not. We will reserve further comment on that until we get to chapter 9 here in Mark.

What you read here is unnatural strength gained through [spiritual]{.class_3055} means. Here is a good example: "Little Mose." Little Mose was a Jew named Lewis Moses, down at Daytona Beach. He had a standing offer of \$5,000 to anyone who could lift him off the ground. Different people like the bodybuilder Charles Atlas and a heavyweight prizefigher named Tony Galento tried it, and they couldn't lift him up, either from the boardwalk or the sand. What Little Mose was doing was using what is known in martial arts as "ch'i" on them. "Ch'i" is the "energy force" of the body. In the martial arts, a person is taught to concentrate it as if he is "planting" it in the ground like a root. That's what Little Mose was doing, and when those bodybuilders and heavyweights tried to lift him up, they about tore him apart---[but they couldn't lift him up.]{.class_3055} That's something [spiritual]{.class_3055} going on there.

3\) [Nakedness, ]{.class_3055}according to Luke 8:27, is a characteristic of demon possession. When our first parents sinned there in Genesis 3, they discovered they were naked, and the first thing God did for them was put clothes on them (Gen. 3:21). There is something about your nakedness you instinctively [know]{.class_3055} you should be ashamed of (Gen. 3:7--10). That's why a "streaker" [runs]{.class_3055} instead of [walks;]{.class_3055} that's why a "flasher" is in such a hurry. They have a knowledge of good and evil; they know something is wrong with that flesh.

The desire to strut the flesh is from the Devil; it's not from God. Nakedness is connected to [drunkenness]{.class_3055} (Hab. 2:15); it's connected to [idolatry]{.class_3055} (Exod. 32:25); and it's connected to [sex perversion]{.class_3055} (Lev. 18, 20).

4\) [Unnatural responses]{.class_3055} (vss. 3--4). He couldn't be chained and he couldn't be tamed. Love, kindness, and tenderness couldn't get him right; and neither could chains and punishment. He didn't respond right to either one.

Demon-possessed people don't respond right. Bob Jones Sr. said you can tell what a man is like by his [responses.]{.class_3055} You can counterfeit a response, but you can't do it forever. I can always tell when a []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw759.xhtml_page_120}congregation is either "bracing for the blast" or relaxed. If people are relaxed, they'll laugh when you tell a joke; they'll sniffle or cry when you say something sad; they'll cringe when you say something scary. That's because they're the same on the inside as they are on the outside. But if you get someone who is just putting on a show, there's a delay in the response, as if he is trying to figure the thing out before he responds.

A demon-possessed person won't respond at all. You say something funny---[nothing.]{.class_3055} You say something sad---[nothing.]{.class_3055} You say something scary---[nothing.]{.class_3055}

Back when I pastored over at Brent Baptist, there was a lady there who would have an odd look on her face and mess with a strand of hair the entire time I preached. Her expression never changed whether I was sad, scary, or serious. What was the problem? She was in a [passive state.]{.class_3055} That is a sign of demon-possession.

I have talked to a fellow who was demon possessed just as sure as you are reading this page. He had gotten caught up in Hyper-Calvinism. I noticed as he talked to me that his eyes drooped, and he spoke in a monotone that was abnormal for him. Demon possession will nearly always manifest itself in the [eyes]{.class_3055} and the [voice]{.class_3055} first. That's because ["The light of the body is the eye"]{.class_3054} (Matt. 6:22; Luke 11:34), and ["out of the abundance of the heart the mouth speaketh"]{.class_3054} (Matt. 12:34; Luke 6:45). The demon-possessed fellow here in Mark 5 ["cried with a loud voice"]{.class_3054} (vs. 7) when there was no reason for it. See the same kind of thing in Acts 16:17--18.

I have noticed in Christians who get messed up doctrinally that something goes wrong with their speech. The Hyperdispensationalist will talk about "the church of the oneBody" (they'll run the two words together every time). You mean, the church of the one Body? That is not even a Bible expression. There's ["the church, Which is his body"]{.class_3054} (Eph. 1:22--23) and the ["one body"]{.class_3054} in Ephesians 4:4. But there is no "church OF the one Body"; that's nonsense. The church [is]{.class_3055} His Body, and that Body is the only one. There aren't ten thousand "Bodies of Christ," like the Baptist brider Noel Smith taught (every local church being a separate "Body of Christ").

The old-time Holiness preachers used to insert "uh" every few []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw759.xhtml_page_121}words: "Glory to God-uh!-Moses went up to the mountain-uh!-and God gave Him the Ten Commandments-uh!" Charismatics talk about the "baptism of the HolyGhost"; they can't seem to separate those words [Holy]{.class_3055} and [Ghost]{.class_3055}. I would know a Campbellite preacher the moment he opened his mouth. They can't pronounce [baptize;]{.class_3055} they'll always say "buptize," with the accent on the first syllable: "BUPtize." They all speak with the same spirit.

Listen, people, the Spirit of God doesn't lead every preacher to speak the same way, with the same inflections. Each one of the Bible writers had his own style of writing, and the Holy Spirit didn't override it. God uses the distinctive personality of every one of His children. He doesn't turn them out like a string of bologna sausages the way some Christian schools do. That Bible creates a rugged individualism, and if you see a school or denomination where everyone is just a "carbon copy," that is the work of some spirit other than the Holy Spirit.

5\) [Consistency in their devilment]{.class_3055} is a characteristic of demon-possessed people[ ]{.class_3055}(vs. 5). Naturally, the Adamic nature prefers darkness to commit its sins (John 3:19), but this fellow here was at it ["always, night and day."]{.class_3054} When you have a class of sinners who are not only identified by their particular sin but openly parade it and flaunt it, that's not normal; there's something spiritual going on there.

6\) [Hanging around high places]{.class_3055} is a characteristic of demon possession[:]{.class_3055}[ "he was in the mountains"]{.class_3054} (vs. 5). Why is that? Because the spirits inside them are trying to [imitate their master]{.class_3055}---Isaiah 14:13. Back in the Old Testament, when those Jews went into apostasy, they sacrificed to devils on ["the high places"]{.class_3054} (2 Chron. 11:15).

7\) [Excessive crying]{.class_3055} is a characteristic of demon possession (vs. 5). Pagan peoples weep, wail, and carry on when a loved one dies because they ["have no hope"]{.class_3054} (1 Thess. 4:13). Part of the idolatry of the Israelite women was ["weeping for Tammuz"]{.class_3054} (Ezek. 8:14), a pagan sun god who "died" at the Summer solstice and was "resurrected" at the Winter solstice (Christmas!). That was [demonism]{.class_3055} (1 Cor. 10:20).

8\) [Penance by self-mutilation]{.class_3055} characterizes demon possession (vs. 5): ["and cutting himself with stones."]{.class_3054}[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw759.xhtml_page_122} The prophets of Baal there on Mt. Carmel ["cut themselves AFTER THEIR MANNER with knives and lancets, till the blood gushed out upon them"]{.class_3054} (1 Kings 18:28)---self-mutilation as part of worship. If you want to see the same thing today, go over to the Philippines just before Easter and watch the members of one particular church nail themselves to crosses to pay for their sins. Go over to Spain and watch that same church's members crawl on their knees in burlap sacks, whipping themselves. That's not the church Christ founded; it's a church of [demons]{.class_3055} (Rev. 17--18).

You can't pay for your sins by shedding [your blood]{.class_3055} because [your blood]{.class_3055} is [no good.]{.class_3055} Only [pure blood]{.class_3055} can take away sin (Heb. 9--10). Your blood was contaminated back in Genesis 3, and it has never been the same since. You could shed all the blood in your body, and it couldn't save you at all. You need ["the precious blood of Christ, as of a lamb without blemish and without spot"]{.class_3054} (1 Pet. 1:19).

Now do you know from where this [sadism]{.class_3055} on the part of the Devil comes. I mean, the devils in this man are the ones causing him to cut himself. Those demons are taking [pleasure]{.class_3055} in the guy's pain: that's sadism. Do you know why they do that?

Well, back in Ezekiel 32, Pharaoh is given as a type of Satan, and he ends up in the Bottomless Pit. He ends up there with all the people he has [damned.]{.class_3055}

Verse 31 of Ezekiel 32 says this: ["Pharaoh shall see them, and shall be COMFORTED over all his multitude."]{.class_3054}

That's the Devil there after he has been chained in the Bottomless Pit of Hell, looking out there over this vast expanse of billions of people he got to go there, saying, "I may be down here in the fire, burning, but I got [you]{.class_3055} and [you]{.class_3055} and [you]{.class_3055} and [you.]{.class_3055}" That's Satan taking [pleasure]{.class_3055} in the lives he ruined. Sinner friend, if you go to Hell, you'll only be a blessing to one person: [the Devil.]{.class_3055}

People say, "Well, Heaven for climate; Hell for company." [Not that company,]{.class_3055} boy! Imagine spending an eternity with someone so sick that he gets a thrill over seeing you suffer. You can have that company if you want; ["I pray thee have me excused"]{.class_3054} (cf. Luke 14:18--19).

A couple of other characteristics of demon possession not associated with this text, but which are nevertheless true, are:

9\) [An affinity for warm, wet places.]{.class_3055} The demon-possessed boy in Matthew 17:15 ["ofttimes . . . falleth into the FIRE, and oft into the WATER."]{.class_3054} That is what Stewart Custer of Bob Jones University considered one of Ruckman's "odd doctrines." What was Custer's Scriptural refutation of the "strangest" of my interpretations? [None!]{.class_3055} After giving you some pious drivel about "no shortage of temptation in any geographic location or climate" and "Satan's minions . . . always on the attack, regardless of the believer's location" (Stewart Custer, [The Truth About the King James Version Controversy,]{.class_3055} Bob Jones University Press, 1981, pg. 32)---neither of which have anything to do with what I said---do you know what his "proof text" was? [Not one verse in any Bible.]{.class_3055} How is that for "serious Bible study"?

Don't try to kid me, "Stew baby." I have been to Manila in the Philippines---a warm, wet place---and Honolulu in Hawaii---a warm, wet place---and New Orleans---a warm, wet place---and Key West---a warm, wet place. I've talked to sailors in the merchant marine; they'll tell you the dirtiest city, morally, on this earth is Cairo, Egypt---a warm, wet place. Even in colder climates, a port city will always be filthier, morally, than an inland city. The issue is not whether demons are active in Prattville, Alabama or Holt, Florida or Missoula, Montana or Columbia, Tennessee; the issue is the [prevalence]{.class_3055} of demons and their activity.

Here in Mark 5, when the demons leave the fellow and go into the swine, do you know where they go? Verse 13---["into the sea,"]{.class_3054} a [wet]{.class_3055} place. Do you know [why]{.class_3055} demons like to inhabit a human body? Because it is [warm]{.class_3055} (98.6 degrees) and [wet]{.class_3055} (75% water). Otherwise they would be out wandering ["through dry places"]{.class_3054} (Matt. 12:43; Luke 11:24). Do you know where demons eventually end up? In the [Lake]{.class_3055} of [Fire.]{.class_3055}

10\) [Forced celibacy and abstaining from meat]{.class_3055} is associated with demons, according to Paul (1 Tim. 4:1--3). Do you know any church that forces its clergy to remain unmarried and that says it is a mortal sin to eat meat during Lent (or on Fridays or whenever)? Well, that church is a church of ["seducing spirits"]{.class_3054} teaching ["doctrines of devils"]{.class_3054}[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw759.xhtml_page_124}; it is NOT "the church Christ founded" that possesses "supreme magisterium" to lay down authentic teaching for a Christian. It is so demonic that it will have you practice vampirism and cannibalism, and pray to a female demon, to get you to Heaven.

Now the question arises, can a Christian be demon possessed? The average Charismatic and Fundamentalist will tell you [no,]{.class_3055} and their proof text will be 1 John 4:4.

"Ye are of God, little children, and have overcome them: because GREATER IS HE THAT IS IN YOU, THAN HE THAT IS IN THE WORLD."

Of course, that speaks [nothing]{.class_3055} as to whether a Christian can be possessed of the Devil. The reasoning is that the Holy Spirit and an unclean spirit can't dwell in the same vessel. They sure can if a Christian doesn't ["depart from iniquity"]{.class_3054} to be ["a vessel unto honour"]{.class_3054} (2 Tim. 2:19--20). A Christian can ["give place to the devil"]{.class_3054} (Eph. 4:27); he can ["fall into the condemnation of the devil"]{.class_3054} (1 Tim. 3:6); he can ["fall into the reproach and snare of the devil"]{.class_3054} (1 Tim. 3:7); he can be delivered ["unto Satan for the destruction of the flesh"]{.class_3054} (1 Cor. 5:5). Having Someone living in you greater than Satan means nothing if you don't yield the members of your body to Him to control (Rom. 6:13), mortify the deeds of your sinful flesh (Rom. 8:13; Col. 3:5), and ["walk in the Spirit"]{.class_3054} (Gal. 5:16--25). A Christian can be filled with Satan (Acts 5:3) as much as he can be ["filled with the Spirit"]{.class_3054} (Eph. 5:18).

Brethren, all being saved guarantees is that the Devil won't get your [soul.]{.class_3055} Between salvation and Heaven, there is [plenty]{.class_3055} Satan can "possess" of you. He can get your eyes, your hands, your ears, your legs, your brain, your liver, your kidneys, your heart, etc. (1 Cor. 5:5). He can get your [ministry]{.class_3055} (Demas---2 Tim. 4:10), your [testimony]{.class_3055} (Lot---\
2 Pet. 2:7--8), your [joy]{.class_3055} (David---Psa. 51:12), your [witness]{.class_3055} (Peter---Luke 22:31--32), your [money and property]{.class_3055} (Job---Job 1), your [heavenly rewards]{.class_3055} (2 John 8), your [Millennial inheritance]{.class_3055} (1 Cor. 6:9--10; Gal. 5:19--20), your [family]{.class_3055} (Samuel---1 Sam. 8:1--3), your [health]{.class_3055} (1 Cor. 11:30), and your [life]{.class_3055} (Rom. 8:13; 1 John 5:16). You reckon that's "possession" enough? If the Devil can get all of that, you aren't going to parse words like [demon obsession]{.class_3055} and [demon oppression]{.class_3055}[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw759.xhtml_page_125}.

6 But when he saw Jesus afar off, he ran and worshipped him,

7 And cried with a loud voice, and said, What have I to do with thee, Jesus, thou Son of the most high God? I adjure thee by God, that thou torment me not.

8 For he said unto him, Come out of the man, thou unclean spirit.

9 And he asked him, What is thy name? And he answered, saying, My name is Legion: for we are many.

10 And he besought him much that he would not send them away out of the country.

11 Now there was there nigh unto the mountains a great herd of swine feeding.

12 And all the devils besought him, saying, Send us into the swine, that we may enter into them.

13 And forthwith Jesus gave them leave. And the unclean spirits went out, and entered into the swine: and the herd ran violently down a steep place into the sea, (they were about two thousand;) and were choked in the sea.

Note once again that demon-possessed people are [religious]{.class_3055} (see our comments on 1:24). This fellow not only acknowledged the Deity of Christ, he [worshiped Him]{.class_3055} (vs. 6); that is more than you can say about some professing Christians.

["I adjure thee by God, that thou torment me not"]{.class_3054} (vs. 7). That is a typical response of unsaved people, especially teenagers. They think God and the Bible are there to spoil their "fun."

Jesus Christ didn't come to torment any unsaved person. He came ["to seek and to save that which was lost"]{.class_3054} (Luke 19:10). He came ["that they might have life, and that they might have it more abundantly"]{.class_3054} (John 10:10). When you get saved, you don't get your life ruined or wrecked; you get the [abundant life.]{.class_3055}

I don't recommend the life of sin I lived before I was saved. If you got saved at a young age and managed to miss a lot of the []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw759.xhtml_page_126}devilment into which I got growing up, then count yourself blessed. Nevertheless, some of us old dogs who got saved later in life and lived like the devil for years have an advantage over those of you who didn't. You have a lot of advantages we didn't have, but we have one that works in our favor. [We know the difference between the life of sinful pleasure and abundant living.]{.class_3055} The years I have had in Christ aren't to be compared with my first 27 years as a lost, Hell-bound sinner, alone in the world without hope ["and without God"]{.class_3054} (Eph. 2:12).

A preacher once had a widow woman in his church who was pretty well "strapped for cash"; she didn't even have the money to pay her rent. Well, someone in the church heard of her situation and donated a substantial amount to help her out, and the preacher went to her house to give it to her. He knocked on the door and got no answer. He rang the doorbell, but no answer. Finally, there was nothing further to do than to leave. The next time he saw her in church, he went up to her and said, "I came by to see you on such-and-such a day, but you weren't home." "Oh, yes," she said, "I was home." "Well, why didn't you answer the door when I knocked and rang the doorbell?" asked the preacher. "Was that you?" said the widow, "I thought it was the landlord come to collect the rent. I didn't have it, so I didn't open the door."

That is how some unsaved people are. Jesus Christ says, ["Behold, I stand at the door, and knock: if any man hear my voice, and open the door, I will come in to him, and will sup with him, and he with me"]{.class_3054} (Rev. 3:20). Jesus Christ stands at the heart's door, knocking, waiting for the sinner to invite Him in; and the sinner thinks the Lord is there to do him harm. "Well, if I let Him in, He'll say, 'Stop that and stop that and get rid of that, etc.'"

Jesus Christ is not there to do something bad to you. He's there to sup with you, to give you something worthwhile.

Notice, again, that the ["unclean spirit,"]{.class_3054} [singular,]{.class_3055} is ["many,"]{.class_3054} [plural]{.class_3055} (vs. 9), just as we saw back in 1:24 and 4:4, 15. The ["many"]{.class_3054} here are ["Legion."]{.class_3054} The word [legion]{.class_3055} was used of a division in the Roman army. The standard size was about 6,000 men, although it could have been as few as 3,000. The word has come to be used of [a vast number of things or people.]{.class_3055} In this instance, between the two men, there were [2,000 devils]{.class_3055}[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw759.xhtml_page_127} (see the number of pigs they entered when cast out of the two men). That is why devils (demons) are described as birds or small flying insects instead of angels that appear as men (see our comments on Beelzebub in 3:22).

In verse 10, the demons ask Christ to ["not send them away out of the country."]{.class_3054} The first reason is the chance that they might be able to move back into their former abode (Matt. 12:43--45). Reformation is not salvation. A sinner can stop smoking, stop drinking, stop cussing, join the church, get baptized, take the eucharist, tithe, AND DIE LOST AND GO TO HELL! Should either of these men have not come in faith to the One who delivered him, the demons had a chance to get back in.

I am also sure that Gadara made a good "hunting ground" for these evil spirits. They had already found two men they could possess. Even if both men trusted Christ, there were still plenty of prospects. When faced with the choice between Jesus Christ and their pigs, the Gadarenes chose [their pigs]{.class_3055} (vs. 17).

These hogs were obviously a major source of income for the city. Although as Jews they could not eat pork under the Mosaic Law (Lev. 11:1--7), in all probability they were raising them for the Gentiles in that area. The region had both Jewish and Graeco-Roman populations. The ["great herd of swine"]{.class_3054} (vs. 11) turns out to be 2,000 hogs (vs. 13). At sixty cents a pound, with an average butcher weight of 220 pounds, these Jews were about to lose over a quarter of a million dollars. ["The love of money is the root of all evil"]{.class_3054} (1 Tim. 6:10).

["And all the devils besought him, saying, Send us into the swine, that we may enter into them"]{.class_3054} (vs. 12). If a devil can't live in a human body, his next best choice is a [pig.]{.class_3055} Don't you find that flattering? In the Bible, that pig is a picture of an unsaved woman (Prov. 11:22; 2 Pet. 2:22). Second Peter 2:22 says ["the sow that was washed"]{.class_3054} returns ["to her wallowing in the mire."]{.class_3054} So do you know what you find advertised out in the world? [Women mud wrestling.]{.class_3055} You won't get away from that Book no matter how hard you try.

These pigs remind me of a story that comes from back in the days of slavery in this country. A white master who was a godless fellow []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw759.xhtml_page_128}saw his slaves going down to the swamp one night. He followed them and saw those black slaves hooping, hollering, and carrying on praising God out there among the cypress stumps. (As late as 1950, in the "black belt" of Alabama, when a black man got saved, he would walk the roads all night long with a friend or a family member, shouting, "Glory to God! Praise God!" He would do that till daybreak.) That master laughed himself silly.

The next day, that white man asked one of those black fellows, "What were you doing down there at the swamp last night?"

"We was praisin' de Lawd for savin' us, suh," said the slave.

"Well that was the craziest thing I ever saw," said the master. He let it go until the next week when he saw those black folk going out to the swamp, and shouting and "running the bases." This time that white fellow wasn't laughing.

The next day, he said to his servant, "What do you have to do to get what you've got?"

"You mean get saved?" said the darkie.

"Yeah, whatever it is that makes you all so happy," said the master.

They were by the pig pen talking, so that black Christian said, "Well, boss, the first thing you gotta do is climb ovah dis heah fence and get in the pen wid them deah pigs."

That white man got all red in the face and said, "Watch it, now."

"Well you said you wanted to know what to do to get saved, boss; I's just tellin' ya," said the slave.

"I'm not about to get in no pen with no pig," said that master, and off he stomped. But the next week he followed those black Christians out to the swamp again and watched them praise God. This time he was scared.

The next day he came to that slave and said, "I'm ready to do whatever it takes to get saved."

The slave said, "OK, boss. You just climb ovah dat fence and get in de pen wid dem deah hogs."

"All right! OK!" said the master, and he did.

"Now get out deah in the middle wid dem pigs, boss," said the slave.

"I most certainly will not!" yelled that white fellow, and he got []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw759.xhtml_page_129}out of that pen and stomped off again.

Of course, the next week that master followed those black Christians out to the swamp. He got under such terrible conviction, he couldn't stand it. So the next day he said to his slave, "I'll do whatever you tell me to do. Just tell me how to get saved."

So off they went to the pig pen. Over the fence went that master and got out in the middle of the pen with the pigs.

"Now, massa, kneel down in dat mud wid dem pigs."

This time that white man didn't put up any fuss. He started to get down on his knees with the pigs when his slave yelled out, "Get up, boss! Da Lawd don't want you down deah wid dem pigs."

"But that's what you told me to do!" said the master in consternation.

"I know," said the saved slave. "You don't have to do dat to get saved, but until yo' is willin' to do dat, you can't get saved."

AMEN! Until you are willing to take your place as a hog or a dog, you can't get saved (see 7:25--30).

Once those devils asked to enter into those hogs, ["Jesus gave them LEAVE"]{.class_3054} (vs. 13); i.e., He [allowed]{.class_3055} them to go. He did it ["FORTHWITH"]{.class_3054}---[right away.]{.class_3055} Those demons entered those pigs, and as some wit observed, that was the first case of "deviled ham" in the Bible. All 2,000 pigs ran down that mountain (cf. vs. 5) into the Sea of Galilee and committed "hogicide."

Now that is significant, because over in the parallel account in Luke 8:31, those devils ["besought him that he would not command them to go out into THE DEEP."]{.class_3054} Then ["the deep"]{.class_3054} can't be the sea, because that's exactly where they went. ["The deep"]{.class_3054} is that body of water out there over your head (see our remarks under 1:16--17).

14 And they that fed the swine fled, and told it in the city, and in the country. And they went out to see what it was that was done.

15 And they come to Jesus, and see him that was possessed with the devil, and had the legion, sitting, and clothed, and in his right mind: and they were afraid.

16 And they that saw it told them how it befell to him []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw759.xhtml_page_130}that was possessed with the devil, and also concerning the swine.

17 And they began to pray him to depart out of their coasts.

18 And when he was come into the ship, he that had been possessed with the devil prayed him that he might be with him.

19 Howbeit Jesus suffered him not, but saith unto him, Go home to thy friends, and tell them how great things the Lord hath done for thee, and hath had compassion on thee.

20 And he departed, and began to publish in Decapolis how great things Jesus had done for him: and all men did marvel.

The men paid to "slop the hogs," after seeing their "job security" run down the hill and drown in the sea, ran back to the city to tell the "co-op" that owned the swine what had happened to their "investment." But of course, the news didn't stop at the owners of the pigs; it spread through the whole city and into the countryside. Soon a "delegation" came out to see Jesus and the maniacs.

Now as we saw over in Matthew 8, there were two maniacs delivered. Mark only deals with one of those maniacs. We may assume that what was true of the one was also true of the other. The maniac with whom Mark deals was ["sitting"]{.class_3054} (vs. 19); he was no longer running around "24-7," yelling and screaming.

He was ["clothed"]{.class_3054}; when he got saved, he didn't keep going around naked. Do you know what the desire of a saint is when he makes it to Glory? He desires ["to be clothed"]{.class_3054} so he won't be ["found naked"]{.class_3054} (2 Cor. 5:2--4). That clothing turns out to be ["white raiment"]{.class_3054} (Rev. 3:5, 18), which is ["the righteousness of saints"]{.class_3054} (Rev. 19:8). That's not a reference to the imputed righteousness of Christ given a believer at salvation. That is the believer's own personal righteousness [after]{.class_3055} salvation for which he receives rewards at the Judgment Seat of Christ. A Christian who doesn't live right can lose that reward (2 John 8) and have to walk around naked in Christ's kingdom (Rev. 16:15).

Finally, those Gadarenes find that maniac ["in his right mind."]{.class_3054} []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw759.xhtml_page_131}When you get saved, the lost world thinks you've lost your mind. That's when you finally start to think right, man (see our comments on Luke 15:17 in that Commentary). When you get saved, you finally have the ability to [think straight,]{.class_3055} because God gives you ["a sound mind"]{.class_3054} (2 Tim. 1:7)---["the mind of Christ"]{.class_3054} (1 Cor. 2:16).

When I got saved, my parents thought I had lost my mind for sure. I had a brother who got "shell-shocked" in World War II and got a "Section 8." He was in and out of mental hospitals till the day he died. Not long after I got saved, I drove up to Rehoboth Beach, Delaware to witness to my momma and daddy. They thought, "Surely he's lost his mind, just like Johnny." When I witnessed to my dad, he said, "That's all right, son. Don't take it to heart. We'll see a doctor in the morning." That was his "solution."

[I was just getting my mind back,]{.class_3055} man. Those dirty so-and-so's in the schools and Colleges I had attended had [stolen it from me;]{.class_3055} Father Sullivan of St. Michael's Catholic Church here in Pensacola and Dean Day of Grace Cathedral Episcopal Church back in Topeka, Kansas [had stolen it from me.]{.class_3055} I lost 27 years of my life because of those people. I just began to get [sane]{.class_3055} when I got saved.

Now look at how verses 16--17 are worded: ["And they that saw it told them how it befell to him that was possessed with the devil, and ALSO CONCERNING THE SWINE. And they began to pray him to DEPART OUT OF THEIR COASTS."]{.class_3054} They want Christ to leave because they just lost \$264,000 on the hoof (see our remarks under vs. 10). They aren't concerned for those demon-possessed fellows at all.

Do you realize how calloused, inordinate, and wicked that attitude was? There was a reason those Gadarenes had tried locking those fellows up. No one could pass by that area, day or night, without being attacked (cf. Acts 19:16). It wasn't a safe place to live. Here Jesus comes through and removes the threat completely, and they want Him to leave?!

You would have thought the reaction would have been like that of those Jews in Capernaum back in chapter 1: "Won't you stay and help my daddy get off liquor?" "My son is on drugs; can you please help him?" "My grandmother is dying; can't you do something for her?" But no, it was: "Get outta here; you are costing us too much []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw759.xhtml_page_132}money!"

Now I have had since 1949 to watch America slowly go to pieces. As a preacher, I am interested in [revival.]{.class_3055} I have read about revival and heard sermons on God bringing revival to this nation. Do you know what I have observed in my 66 years as a Christian? I have seen the Holy Spirit brooding over this country. About the time He gets something going, some Christian pops up and jams it. The Lord has said to this nation, "If you want revival, then this has got to go and that has got to go." American Christians have looked God Almighty in the face and said, "I just can't live without it." So the Holy Spirit said, "OK," and off He went. ["And they began to pray him to depart out of their coasts."]{.class_3054}

That's why there's no revival. Don't look for one because [it ain't coming.]{.class_3055}

But for the fellow who got delivered from those devils, it was different. When the other Gadarenes sent Jesus away, that guy was ready to leave and go with Christ. He was indebted to the Lord, and he knew it. He was willing to follow the Lord wherever He went, in gratitude for what had been done for him.

That wasn't what Jesus needed from that fellow, though. The Lord needed Him a witness there in Decapolis, so He told that fellow, ["Go home to thy friends, and tell them how great things the Lord hath done for thee"]{.class_3054} (vs. 19). That city in Gadara may not have cared anything for what Jesus did for that man, but there were other cities all throughout Decapolis that marveled over what Jesus had done.

I have won men to Christ like that. When they went and told their family and friends what had happened to them, I am sure those folks were stunned. There was a fellow that got saved in the first jail service I held in Spartanburg, South Carolina. His name was Whitey Woods. Whitey was lying in his bunk when I came in to preach, and when he saw me, he pulled the newspaper over his face in order to ignore me. I didn't let it bother me. I went on and preached, and there were a couple of the other inmates that got saved.

About three weeks later, I was in downtown Greenville, South Carolina, with a couple of other students from Bob Jones, passing out tracts after I had gotten through studying one night. As we got []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw759.xhtml_page_133}done and were headed back to the car, one of my buddies said, "We met a guy sleeping in the park who says he knows you, and he wants to talk to you." So they brought the guy to me, and it was that fellow from the jail who had put the newspaper over his face while I preached. I could tell he was a little bit drunk. He said, "How you doin', preacher?"

"OK," I said, "How are you doing? Not so good, huh?"

"No," he replied, "but I need to apologize to you about how I treated you in the jail. I was the fellow who covered his face with the newspaper so I wouldn't have to listen to you, but I heard every word you said. I was raised right. I should have listened to you, you being a minister. My mother is a Christian. I'm no Christian myself, but she is."

"Well, what do you want?" I asked.

"Can you get me a place to stay tonight?" he said.

"Not that I know of," I said. "I don't have any contacts."

"Can you get me into the mission?" he asked.

"You're a little bit drunk, aren't you?" I replied.

"Not much," he said.

"Well, I know rescue missions," I said. "I've worked in a few myself, and they won't take you in if you are drunk. We'll try, though, if you want to."

So we drove him down to the rescue mission. Well, the rescue mission was about a thirty-minute drive away, so on the way, I sat in the back seat with him and dealt with him about his soul. He got really quiet and began to listen. Finally, he bowed his head and asked Jesus Christ to save him.

When we got to the rescue mission, they wouldn't take him because he wasn't sober enough. He came back to the car and wanted to be taken back to the park where he had been sleeping. I said, "I've got a place out behind my trailer. It's a warm summer night. I'll give you a blanket to lie on, and you can sleep out there."

He said, "OK; thank you, preacher."

As we were driving down the road to my place, that old boy started laughing. I said, "What's the deal?"

He looked at me and said, "You know, I think it took." The guy had gotten assurance of salvation, and you couldn't shut his mouth. []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw759.xhtml_page_134}He hadn't said ten words going to the rescue mission, but now he talked for fifteen minutes straight until we pulled into the trailer park.

I took him out back and gave him a rolled-up blanket, a flashlight, and a Bible. Before I went in for the night, he said, "I'm going back home and tell my momma what I did."

"Good," I said, "When are you leaving?"

"In the morning," he replied.

I said, "You can take the Bible with you, but I would appreciate it if you would leave the flashlight and the blanket."

I started to go back to the trailer, and he stopped me and said, "Preacher, how do you pray?"

I said, "You just talk to God."

"Show me," he said.

I told him, "All you have to do is talk to God just like you are talking to me right now."

"Show me," he insisted; so I did. For the next hour I could hear that guy there in the backyard, praying. When I came out the next morning, there was the blanket folded up with the flashlight on top of it; he hadn't taken them to sell for liquor. [The Bible was gone:]{.class_3055} he had taken it with him.

Of course, I never knew what happened to him after that, but when he got home and told everyone he had gotten saved, I just bet you that ["all men did marvel."]{.class_3054} I can hear them now: "What happened to you?!" That old boy [got saved!]{.class_3055}
