21 And when Jesus was passed over again by ship unto the other side, much people gathered unto him: and he was nigh unto the sea.

22 And, behold, there cometh one of the rulers of the synagogue, Jairus by name; and when he saw him, he fell at his feet,

23 And besought him greatly, saying, My little daughter lieth at the point of death: I pray thee, come and lay thy hands on her, that she may be healed; and she shall live.

Having left the country of the Gadarenes, Jesus returned to []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw759.xhtml_page_135}Capernaum. Notice the difference in reception, though. The Gadarenes wanted Christ to leave because He was interfering with their livelihood; the people of Capernaum were eager to see Him, though. The problem was, there was not much of a difference in the heart attitude of the people of Capernaum than there was in the people of Gadara. After the incident with the two maniacs in Matthew 8, Jesus said:

["And thou, Capernaum, which art exalted unto heaven, shalt be brought down to hell: for if the mighty works, which have been done in thee, had been done in Sodom, it would have remained until this day. But I say unto you, That it shall be more tolerable for the land of Sodom in the day of judgment, than for thee"]{.class_3054} (Matt. 11:23--24).

There was a lot of "hype," but not much real repentance and faith. Most of the people in the crowd were eager to see Jesus perform a miracle, but when it came to believing on Him and becoming one of His disciples, that was taking things just a bit too far.

Now, ["one of the rulers of the synagogue"]{.class_3054} comes to Jesus, and his name is ["Jairus"]{.class_3054} (vs. 22). You see that falling at Christ's feet there in verse 22? That's an act of [worship]{.class_3055} (Matt. 9:18, 2:11; Rev. 22:8). Now if Jesus never claimed to be God, like the Liberals and Mohammedans believe, what do you make of that? Jesus didn't tell the guy to get up because He wasn't God. Why, [Peter did]{.class_3055} over in Acts 10:25--26. Here is Jesus accepting worship as God. If He weren't who He said He was---[God Almighty in the flesh]{.class_3055}---then He was guilty of exactly what the Jews accused Him: [blasphemy]{.class_3055} (John 10:30--33).

Verse 23 is self-explanatory. Jairus' little girl is about to die, and he wants Jesus to come and heal her. Although it was not necessary for the Lord to lay His hands on the sick in order to heal them (see our remarks under 1:42), He often did (see Luke 4:40). The laying on of hands in the Old Testament could symbolize the transference of a blessing (Gen. 48:14) or the transference of guilt upon a sacrifice (Exod. 29:10, 15, 19; Lev. 8:14, 18, 22). When Jesus laid His hands on the sick to heal them, He was transferring the blessing of healing to the sick, while at the same time transferring the sickness to Himself, spiritually (Matt. 8:17).

In Isaiah 53:4, which is the verse Matthew 8:17 is quoting, Christ's miracles of healing are connected to His blood atonement (Isa. 53:5). Because of that, the Charismatics and Pentecostals have what is known as "Whipping Post Theology"; i.e., there is physical healing in the atonement. Of course, the miraculous healings were signs given to [Israel]{.class_3055} (Mark 16:17--18 cf. 1 Cor. 1:22), not the Body of Christ. The apostle who could heal folks with handkerchiefs (Acts 19:12), couldn't heal one of his buddies at the end of his ministry (2 Tim. 4:20). So any physical healing in the atonement is not applicable to [today.]{.class_3055} It will be at the Rapture, though, when every believer receives a body just like his risen Saviour (Phil. 3:21; 1 John 3:2). [Right now,]{.class_3055} the "healing" in the atonement is a [spiritual]{.class_3055} healing from [sin]{.class_3055} (1 Pet. 2:24).

24 And Jesus went with him; and much people followed him, and thronged him.

25 And a certain woman, which had an issue of blood twelve years,

26 And had suffered many things of many physicians, and had spent all that she had, and was nothing bettered, but rather grew worse,

27 When she had heard of Jesus, came in the press behind, and touched his garment.

28 For she said, If I may touch but his clothes, I shall be whole.

29 And straightway the fountain of her blood was dried up; and she felt in her body that she was healed of that plague.

30 And Jesus, immediately knowing in himself that virtue had gone out of him, turned him about in the press, and said, Who touched my clothes?

31 And his disciples said unto him, Thou seest the multitude thronging thee, and sayest thou, Who touched me?

32 And he looked round about to see her that had done this thing.

33 But the woman fearing and trembling, knowing what []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw759.xhtml_page_137}was done in her, came and fell down before him, and told him all the truth.

34 And he said unto her, Daughter, thy faith hath made thee whole; go in peace, and be whole of thy plague.

Back in verse 21, we read of the crowd of people that came out to see Jesus. That throng of people follow Him as He makes His way to Jairus' house. In fact, the crowd is so thick that they are pressing up against the Lord (Luke 8:45).

But in that crowd there was one particular woman hoping to receive something from the Lord while lost in the crowd. This woman ["had an issue of blood"]{.class_3054} (vs. 25) that was not the "norm" for a woman. Under the Levitical Law, that made her unclean (Lev. 15:19--20, 25--27). Moreover, her condition was a chronic one for which there was no cure (vs. 26). So what you are reading here is a picture of sin (cf. our remarks under 1:40).

Now this woman ["had an issue of blood TWELVE YEARS"]{.class_3054}; that was the same as the age of Jairus' daughter (vs. 42). The number twelve points to Israel as a nation. Undoubtedly what you have is a picture of Israel in her unbelief. Right now, Israel is unclean to God (Isa. 64:6; Hag. 2:14) and spiritually dead (Ezek. 37:11; Rom. 11:15; Luke 9:60; Eph. 2:1).

["Suffered"]{.class_3054} in verse 26 means the woman was [forced to endure]{.class_3055} a lot of different treatments from a lot of different doctors over the years, all with no success. Instead of getting better, her condition only worsened. That is why the expression is doctors [practice]{.class_3055} medicine; they don't always get it right.

We have already discussed the word ["press"]{.class_3054} (vs. 27) back under 2:4 (which see). And we have already discussed why the woman thought she could be healed by touching Christ's garment back under 3:10. It worked (vs. 29), but it was not the "relic" that healed her. It was the Lord rewarding her faith in Him (vs. 34).

While virtue is mainly the high moral character or value of a person or thing (Phil. 4:8; 2 Pet. 1:3, 5; Ruth 3:11; Prov. 12:4, 31:10), here in verse 30, it is the efficacious power and strength of Christ to heal. Notice that the "touch" of verse 30 is more than the mere touching of the crowd (vs. 31). It is a [purposeful]{.class_3055} touch, and the Lord []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw759.xhtml_page_138}knows it. But He does not exercise His full omniscience to identify who touched Him. He is giving this woman the chance to come forward so He can deal with her one on one. Undoubtedly part of His purpose in doing so was to dispel the misguided notion that Roman Catholic relic worship could do anything for anyone. It was [faith in Jesus Christ]{.class_3055} that effected her cure, not some magic "robe."

35 While he yet spake, there came from the ruler of the synagogue's house certain which said, Thy daughter is dead: why troublest thou the Master any further?

36 As soon as Jesus heard the word that was spoken, he saith unto the ruler of the synagogue, Be not afraid, only believe.

37 And he suffered no man to follow him, save Peter, and James, and John the brother of James.

38 And he cometh to the house of the ruler of the synagogue, and seeth the tumult, and them that wept and wailed greatly.

39 And when he was come in, he saith unto them, Why make ye this ado, and weep? the damsel is not dead, but sleepeth.

40 And they laughed him to scorn. But when he had put them all out, he taketh the father and the mother of the damsel, and them that were with him, and entereth in where the damsel was lying.

41 And he took the damsel by the hand, and said unto her, Talitha cumi; which is, being interpreted, Damsel, I say unto thee, arise.

42 And straightway the damsel arose, and walked; for she was of the age of twelve years. And they were astonished with a great astonishment.

43 And he charged them straitly that no man should know it; and commanded that something should be given her to eat.

One should note the difference between the accounts in verses 35--36 here and in Luke 8:41--42, 49--50 and the account in Matthew 9:18. In Mark and Luke, the girl is ["dying"]{.class_3054} (Luke 8:42) and []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw759.xhtml_page_139}["at the point of death"]{.class_3054} (Mark 5:23), but the word doesn't come to Jairus that she is dead until after the incident of the woman touching Christ's garment. But in Matthew, before the encounter with the woman with ["the issue of blood,"]{.class_3054} Jairus says to Christ, ["My daughter is even now dead."]{.class_3054}

This apparent discrepancy shows you two things. First, it shows you the human quality of the Scriptures. The Holy Spirit did not override the individual styles of the human authors. Matthew, Mark, and Luke reported the same event from different perspectives, just as one would expect witnesses in a court of law to do. If all the witnesses said the exact same thing in the exact same words, that would show they were in collusion with each other, and their testimonies would be thrown out. Matthew is giving a synopsis of the event, while Mark and Luke are going into more detail.

Second, it shows you that events in the Scriptures aren't always given in exact chronological order. We will witness this further in Mark 14 in regards to the woman anointing Jesus' feet. Sometimes a parenthetical account is placed in the middle of another account. It is evident from verse 36 here in Mark 5 that when Jairus was informed his daughter had died that Jesus encouraged him to believe she could be raised from the dead. That is exactly what is recorded in Matthew 9:18. So Matthew 9:18 is Jairus' response to Christ's words in Mark 5:36; they are just given out of chronological order.

Verse 37 is Christ's famous "inner circle." They see the Lord raise Jairus' daughter; they see Christ transfigured (9:2); along with Andrew, they hear Christ's "Olivet Discourse" (13:3); and they are with Jesus, apart from the other disciples, in the Garden of Gethsemane (14:33).

Notice the pagan ["ado"]{.class_3054} (vs. 39) in verse 38. Whether they were "professional" mourners or not, they were carrying on like a bunch of lost heathen (cf. 1 Thess. 4:13). Do you know what ["ado"]{.class_3054} means? It is a [fuss over nothing.]{.class_3055} Job, back in 1800 B.C., had enough sense to know he would be resurrected some day (Job 19:26--27). When Lazarus died, Mary and Martha may have been in mourning and blaming Jesus for not getting there on time to heal their brother, but []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw759.xhtml_page_140}they weren't carrying on like this bunch in verse 38. Why? Because even in their grief, they still had the hope of the resurrection (John 11:24 cf. Acts 23:6). The Christian has an even better hope than that (Tit. 2:13); he is coming up [before]{.class_3055} the Last Day (John 11:25--26).

Now if we could see death as the Lord does, it wouldn't bother us as much as it does. Notice verse 39: ["the damsel is not dead, but sleepeth."]{.class_3054} That's the same thing He said about Lazarus in John 11:11--14. When Stephen was stoned to death, the Scripture says, ["he fell asleep"]{.class_3054} (Acts 7:60). ["The dead in Christ"]{.class_3054} (1 Thess. 4:16) are said to ["sleep in Jesus"]{.class_3054} (1 Thess. 4:14).

That is not "soul sleep"; it is a "sleep" of the [body.]{.class_3055} And when Jesus comes back, He intends to wake up those bodies, no matter if they have rotted away to dust (1 Thess. 4:16; 1 Cor. 15:51--54 cf. Dan. 12:1).

Brethren, nobody likes death, but to the Christian, death is no threat (1 Cor. 15:55--57). He is just moving from an old worn-out house to temporary lodgings (2 Cor. 5:1) until his "new digs" are done (Phil. 3:21). For the believer in Christ, ["mortality"]{.class_3054} is ["swallowed up of life"]{.class_3054} (2 Cor. 5:4), not death.

Back to Mark 5: ["And they laughed him to scorn"]{.class_3054} (vs. 40). Then not only do they not believe in the resurrection, they don't believe the Old Testament. Elijah raised the son of the widow of Zarephath back to life (1 Kings 17:21--22), and Elisha raised the son of the great woman of Shunem up from the dead (2 Kings 4:32--34). Well, here is someone who has proven Himself to be greater than Elijah and Elisha, and they make fun of Him. Because of their unbelief, they end up missing one of the greatest miracles in the Gospels (vs. 40).

Notice the Aramaic words in verse 41: ["Talitha cumi."]{.class_3054} Aramaic is a Syriac form of the Hebrew language. Parts of Ezra and Daniel are in Aramaic (see Ezra 4:7; Dan. 2:4). One of the earliest "Aramaisms" in your Bible is found in Genesis 31:47, where the name ["Jegarsahadutha"]{.class_3054} is given by ["Laban the Syrian"]{.class_3054} (Gen. 31:24) right alongside a Hebrew word (["Galeed"]{.class_3054}).

The appearances of the "Aramaisms" in the Bible have led the higher critics (who don't believe the Holy Ghost gave any ["prophecy of the scripture"]{.class_3054}---2 Pet. 1:20--21) to theorize that the []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw759.xhtml_page_141}writers of the so-called "synoptic Gospels" (Matthew, Mark, and Luke) used some kind of Aramaic "Q document" as a source. The theory has no more grounding in reality than Darwin's "missing link." If Mark is translating from Aramaic into Hebrew, why even record the Aramaic words and then give the translation in the translation? Unbelief causes the scholars to lose their minds. Mark is recording the events in Greek and inserts the actual Aramaic words Jesus used because he is writing an [historical document,]{.class_3055} not a "religious" book.

As we pointed out back in verse 25, the age of the girl in verse 42 points to Israel in type, so her resurrection is a type of Israel's resurrection at Christ's Advent (Ezek. 37).

["Thy dead men shall live, together with my dead body shall they arise. Awake and sing, ye that dwell in dust . . . and the earth shall cast out the dead"]{.class_3054} (Isa. 26:19).

Verse 42: ["And they were astonished with a great astonishment."]{.class_3054} I imagine they were. All He had to do was say two words to that dead body, and the girl was able to get up and walk around. Not only did this Man have authority over devils (1:27) and the elements (4:41), He had authority over [the dead]{.class_3055} as well.

["And he charged them straitly that no man should know it"]{.class_3054} (vs. 43); in other words, He gave strict orders for them to keep quiet. We discussed the practical reason for this back in 1:45. But since then, Jesus has come under the scrutiny of the religious leaders (see our comments on 3:6). Do you know what those religious leaders wanted to do to Lazarus after Jesus raised him from the dead? They wanted to [kill him]{.class_3055}[ "Because that by reason of him many of the Jews . . . believed on Jesus"]{.class_3054} (John 12:10--11). Jesus doesn't want the news spread around for the girl's sake as much as for His own.

Of course, Christ's charge was not heeded any more than it was in 1:44--45 (see Matt. 9:31). Now don't you know that with the people of Tyre and Sidon coming to see Jesus, that pretty soon the news of a man who could raise the dead had spread all over the Mediterranean. Tyre and Sidon were the home of the Phoenician sailors who sailed all over the Mediterranean clear to Spain. It wouldn't surprise me if the "scuttlebutt" about Jesus Christ made it []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw759.xhtml_page_142}clean up to the British Isles before Christ's death, burial, and resurrection.

That being the case, what is the excuse of God's people today for not spreading abroad the fame of the Saviour? Why, with all the advances in communication (TV, radio, Internet) and transportation (jets, automobiles, etc.), there should be no reason that every person on the globe shouldn't know about Jesus Christ. What's the problem? Someone's not [going]{.class_3055} as the Lord commanded (16:15).

[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw956.xhtml}

# Chapter 6 {.heading_97}

6:1 And he went out from thence, and came into his own country; and his disciples follow him.

2 And when the sabbath day was come, he began to teach in the synagogue: and many hearing him were astonished, saying, From whence hath this man these things? and what wisdom is this which is given unto him, that even such mighty works are wrought by his hands?

3 Is not this the carpenter, the son of Mary, the brother of James, and Joses, and of Juda, and Simon? and are not his sisters here with us? And they were offended at him.

4 But Jesus said unto them, A prophet is not without honour, but in his own country, and among his own kin, and in his own house.

5 And he could there do no mighty work, save that he laid his hands upon a few sick folk, and healed them.

6 And he marvelled because of their unbelief. And he went round about the villages, teaching.

While verse 1 is pretty straight forward in the God-honored text of the Reformation, it has not escaped the efforts of the monkey men who monkey with the text. You see, there are three verbs in the sentence: ["went out"]{.class_3054} ([exelthen]{.class_3055}), ["came"]{.class_3054} ([elthen]{.class_3055}), ["follow"]{.class_3054} ([akolouthousin]{.class_3055}). The first two are in the "aorist" tense; i.e., the [past]{.class_3055} tense.

The corrupt Alexandrian text of Hort and Nestle changes the second verb to a [present]{.class_3055} tense ([erchetai]{.class_3055}), but after complaining about the [AV]{.class_3055} not translating the tense of the Greek verb correctly, do you know what the [NASV]{.class_3055} does? It rejects its own text on the second verb and sticks with the past tense. Then it refuses to translate the third verb in the present tense, [as its own Greek text reads,]{.class_3055} and instead translates it as an aorist (past tense). Ditto the [ESV,]{.class_3055} the current "darling" of Conservatives.

These apostate hypocrites don't care what [any]{.class_3055} Greek text says. They will [invent]{.class_3055} readings, if necessary, to carry out their own agendas: \$\$\$ (1 Tim. 6:10) and []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw956.xhtml_page_144}[exalting themselves as the final authority over the Body of Christ.]{.class_3055} That's the "good," "godly," "dedicated" scholarship at Christian schools.

Now the people of Jesus' hometown were ["astonished"]{.class_3054} at His teaching just like people at Capernaum were (1:21--22), but instead of coming to Him in faith for healing and deliverance, ["they were offended at him"]{.class_3054} (vs. 3). The problem was ["their unbelief"]{.class_3054} (vs. 6). As during His previous visit to Nazareth, they were indignant over His claim to be the Messiah of Old Testament prophecy (Luke 4:17--21). They figured they knew His family.

Over in John 6:42, those Jews say, ["Is not this Jesus, the son of Joseph, whose father and mother we know? how is it then that he saith, I came down from heaven?"]{.class_3064-0} But the fact of the matter was, they didn't really know Him or His real Father (John 8:19). Those Jews may ["have known Christ after the flesh"]{.class_3064-0} (2 Cor. 5:16), but they didn't know Him as the only way to God the Father (John 14:6--9). That Bible says:

["He was in the world, and the world was made by him, and the world KNEW HIM NOT. He came unto his own, and his own received him not. But as many as received him, to them gave he power to become the sons of God, even to them that believe on his name"]{.class_3054} (John 1:10--12).

"Knowing" Christ isn't being able to recite a laundry list of things about Him, as in the Apostles' Creed or the Nicene Creed. It's not performing some religious ritual, like baptism or communion. It's [personally trusting and receiving Him as your Saviour.]{.class_3055} Until you do that, you don't know Him or God, and [you don't have eternal life]{.class_3055} (John 17:3; 1 John 5:20).

Verse 3:[ "Is not this the carpenter . . . ."]{.class_3054} Well, up until He was thirty years old (Luke 3:23), yes: He took up His foster father's profession (Matt. 13:55). But He didn't come to this earth to make furniture or houses; He came to do the will of His real Father (John 6:38, 4:34, 5:30; Heb. 10:7).

[". . . the son of Mary."]{.class_3054} That is Mohammed's favorite title for Jesus Christ ([Koran,]{.class_3055} Sura 5:17, 4:157, 2:253, 9:31). One should note that it only appears [one time]{.class_3055} in the entire Bible, and it is used by Christ's [enemies,]{.class_3055} who [didn't believe in Him.]{.class_3055} So when you hear a []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw956.xhtml_page_145}Moslem or Roman Catholic use the term, you know with what you are dealing. He referred to Himself as ["the Son of God"]{.class_3054} (John 9:35--37, 10:36), ["the Son of man"]{.class_3054} (Mark 2:10--11, 2:28), and ["the Son of David"]{.class_3054} (Mark 12:35--37); but [never]{.class_3055} the Son of Mary.

["The brother of James . . . Joses . . . Juda . . . Simon . . . his sisters."]{.class_3054} For being "ever virgin," Mary sure did have a lot of kids, didn't she? There are at least [six]{.class_3055} right there. The standard Catholic answer to this is that these are [cousins]{.class_3055} in Mark 6:3. The problem with that is when the Bible wants to say ["cousin"]{.class_3054} ([sungenes]{.class_3055}), it says ["cousin"]{.class_3054} (Luke 1:36), and when it wants to say ["brother"]{.class_3054} ([adelphos]{.class_3055}), it says ["brother."]{.class_3054} The two are not the same in Greek; the two are not the same in English.

But if that were not enough, Psalm 69, a Messianic psalm dealing with Christ's First Advent (Psa. 69:9 cf. John 2:17), says, ["I am become a stranger unto my brethren . . . ."]{.class_3054} Were they His "cousins" or merely His Jewish kinsmen? Not on your life! Keep on reading:[ ". . . and an alien unto MY MOTHER'S CHILDREN."]{.class_3054}

[Jesus Christ's mother had other children.]{.class_3055} His brothers are given [by name]{.class_3055} in Mark 6:3. He had at least two sisters, perhaps more. If Mary had been a "perpetual virgin" all her life, then she certainly was not "immaculately conceived" (i.e., conceived without original sin) because [she defrauded her husband]{.class_3055} (1 Cor. 7:5).

Now, I have given you what the [Scriptures]{.class_3055} say about Mary having other children after she bore Jesus Christ. Do you know what the Catholic lawyer I debated years ago out in Long Beach, California---Karl Keating of "Catholic Answers"---gives as proof that she didn't? Well, here we go. This is from his on-line tract "Mary: Ever Virgin":

1\. "The Infancy Gospel of James," written 20--25 years after the close of the canon. It is an [Ebionite]{.class_3055} document. The Ebionites were Jews who, though they accepted the Messiahship of Jesus, rejected His Deity. They believed in [salvation by works.]{.class_3055} The ["Protoevangelium]{.class_3055} of James" is NOT Scripture. It is not Jewish Scripture, Christian Scripture, or even Catholic "Scripture" ("deutero-canonical").

2\. Origen (!)---NOT Scripture.

3\. Hilary of Poitiers---NOT Scripture.

4\. Athanasius---NOT Scripture.

5\. Epiphanius of Salamis---NOT Scripture.

6\. Jerome---NOT Scripture.

7\. Didymus the Blind (what a name!---2 Cor. 4:4)---NOT Scripture.

8\. Ambrose of Milan---NOT Scripture.

9\. Pope Siricius I---NOT Scripture.

10\. Augustine of Hippo---NOT Scripture.

11\. Leporius---NOT Scripture.

12\. Cyril of Alexandria---NOT Scripture.

13\. Pope Leo I---NOT Scripture.

Karl Keating doesn't give you one verse from any Bible---including a Catholic Bible---that Mary was a perpetual virgin. He just gives you the beliefs of "early Christians," meaning [not one Christian in the Scriptures.]{.class_3055} That's the "infallible teaching church" for you. That tract was declared ["NIHIL OBSTAT"]{.class_3055} (i.e., nothing objectionable to Catholic doctrine) by the Catholic censor, Bernadeane Carr, and bears the "IMPRIMATUR" of the Bishop of San Diego, Robert Brom. None of them know enough Scripture to fill the left eye of a blind mosquito.

The ["James"]{.class_3054} of Mark 6:3 is ["James the Lord's brother,"]{.class_3054} said to be an apostle in Galatians 1:19. He was not one of the Twelve, before or after the Resurrection. Before Christ's death, he, along with all of his brothers, did not believe on Christ (John 7:5). After the Resurrection, James received a special appearance of the risen Christ and was converted then (1 Cor. 15:7). Although there is no record of the conversion of Christ's other brothers and sisters, they are mentioned (for the last time in the Scriptures) in Acts 1:14, where they are said to be in ["one accord in prayer and supplication"]{.class_3054} with the apostles and other disciples. James went on to be the presiding bishop of the church at Jerusalem (Acts 15:13, 21:18). (James the brother of John had been killed by Herod back in Acts 12:2.) You will notice there in Acts 15 that "the first Pope" (if you believe the nonsense put out by the Catholic Church) was not in charge of the so-called "council of Jerusalem"; Peter was just one witness among many (Acts 15:6--7).

Verse 4 is one of those few things in the life of Christ that is recorded in all four Gospels (Matt. 13:57; here; Luke 4:24; John 4:44). The rule of thumb in the Scriptures is if the world thinks []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw956.xhtml_page_147}something is wonderful, God thinks it stinks, and whatever God speaks well of, the world will attack, ridicule, and degrade (Luke 16:15; 2 Cor. 2:15--16). If you are a Christian, you had better reevaluate your life if everyone speaks well of you. Christ said that is one of the signs of a [false prophet]{.class_3055} (Luke 6:26). If the news media "puffs" someone (say like JFK, MLK, "Slick Willie" Clinton, "Hillary," Obama, et al.), just count on it, that person is in the [wrong,]{.class_3055} as far as God and the Bible are concerned.

If you are a Pentecostal or Charismatic, verse 5 presents a problem: healing is [not]{.class_3055} ["a mighty work."]{.class_3054} Moreover, Christ healed these folks [despite]{.class_3055} ["their unbelief]{.class_3054} (vs. 6). "Only believe, only believe. All things are possible; only believe." Not there, boy. Their faith didn't make them whole, because they had no faith at all. So when the faith healer slaps you on the head (or blows on you or slaps his coat at you or whatever) and says, "Be healed in the name of Jesus," and [nothing happens,]{.class_3055} it's not your fault that you didn't get healed; it's [his]{.class_3055} (see our comments on 2:5). Either he didn't have the faith, or [he didn't have the gift.]{.class_3055}

7 And he called unto him the twelve, and began to send them forth by two and two; and gave them power over unclean spirits;

8 And commanded them that they should take nothing for their journey, save a staff only; no scrip, no bread, no money in their purse:

9 But be shod with sandals; and not put on two coats.

10 And he said unto them, In what place soever ye enter into an house, there abide till ye depart from that place.

11 And whosoever shall not receive you, nor hear you, when ye depart thence, shake off the dust under your feet for a testimony against them. Verily I say unto you, It shall be more tolerable for Sodom and Gomorrha in the day of judgment, than for that city.

12 And they went out, and preached that men should repent.

13 And they cast out many devils, and anointed with oil many that were sick, and healed them.

This is the commissioning of the apostles. Note that they are sent forth in groups of two. There are several reasons for that. The first is a [legal]{.class_3055} reason.

["It is also written in your law, that the testimony of two men is true"]{.class_3054} (John 8:17).

The second reason is [practical.]{.class_3055} The two men could encourage one another. (God help whoever had Judas Iscariot as a partner.)

["Two are better than one; because they have a good reward for their labour. For if they fall, the one will lift up his fellow: but woe to him that is alone when he falleth; for he hath not another to help him up"]{.class_3054} (Eccl. 4:9--10).

And thirdly, it pointed [prophetically]{.class_3055} to the two witnesses who will preach in the Tribulation.

["And I will give power unto my two witnesses, and they shall prophesy a thousand two hundred and threescore days]{.class_3054} \[the length of Christ's earthly ministry\][, clothed in sackcloth"]{.class_3054} (Rev. 11:3).

Now it must be evident from verses 8--9 that the verses have nothing to do with the Church Age; in fact, at the Last Supper, Christ allows the apostles to do what He did not allow them here in this passage (see Luke 22:35--36). The student of the Bible should keep this in mind when he encounters one of the "dearly beloved brethren" trying to land on a place like Matthew 10:8--10 (the parallel passage to vss. 8--9) to define or justify his ministry. No Charismatic healer or revivalist observes the restrictions of either passage.

The Bible-believing brethren won't try to exercise the apostolic signs, but they will try to use ["freely ye have received, freely give"]{.class_3054} to pirate and ride other men's ministries without having to put in the work or the money it took to put out the original work. Every now and then, some well-meaning Christian decides it is his ministry to put out my work on the Internet, with no regard to my wishes or the work the Bookstore put in to turn out that work or the expenses in production, salaries, utilities, etc., or even the legality of what he is doing (Rom. 13:1).

That Bible says, ["But let every man prove HIS OWN WORK, and then shall he have rejoicing in himself alone, and not in another"]{.class_3054} (Gal. 6:4). Don't make [your ministry]{.class_3055} stealing []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw956.xhtml_page_149}[another man's work.]{.class_3055}

The apostles were to depend on the generosity of those who believed their preaching (vss. 10--11). You see a little bit of that going on in the book of Acts in Paul's ministry (see Acts 16:15, 17:7, 13:50--51, 18:6). But the allusion to ["Sodom and Gomorrha"]{.class_3054} should tell you there is a prophetic significance to Christ's words that goes beyond the Gospels and Acts. The judgment of Sodom and Gomorrha is a picture of the Second Advent (Luke 17:28--30). So what you are reading has something to do with the preaching of Moses and Elijah, and the 144,000, during the Tribulation (Rev. 11). For further comment on this line of thought, we refer you to our comments on Matthew 10:23 in that Commentary.

["It shall be more tolerable for Sodom and Gomorrha in the day of judgment, than for that city"]{.class_3054} (vs. 11). You may not realize what all is involved in that statement, but that is quite a condemnation. Those sex perverts God blasted back there in Genesis 19 are [still burning.]{.class_3055}

["Even as Sodom and Gomorrha, and the cities about them in like manner, giving themselves over to FORNICATION, and going after STRANGE FLESH, are set forth for an example, suffering the vengeance of ETERNAL FIRE"]{.class_3054} (Jude 7).

The Lord Jesus said those sex perverts from Sodom and Gomorrha down in Hell are going to have it better than the Jews of Christ's day who rejected Him. So there are [degrees of punishment ]{.class_3055}in Hell and the Lake of Fire, and one of the determining factors is the [light you rejected.]{.class_3055}

Jesus said if Sodom had been able to see ["the mighty works"]{.class_3054} He had performed in Capernaum, those sex perverts would have gotten right. Those Jews in Capernaum had more light than the Sodomites, which made their sin of rejecting Christ the greater sin. [Light rejected becomes lightning.]{.class_3055}

You would do better to go to Hell from the deepest, darkest part of Africa, India, or the Amazon than go to Hell from a place like Pensacola, Florida; Chattanooga, Tennessee; Greenville, South Carolina; Grand Rapids, Michigan; Hammond, Indiana; Jacksonville, []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw956.xhtml_page_150}Florida; Atlanta, Georgia; or Dallas, Texas. Why? Because you have more churches, more Christian radio, more Bibles, more Gospel tracts, more personal workers, and a greater chance to receive Christ here in the United States than folks do in those other places. That makes your damnation all the greater.

For the message the apostles preached in verse 12, we refer you to our comments on 1:15. The message is: "Get right because the King is here." That is not the message after the Resurrection.

Repentance is, first and foremost, a [change of heart and mind.]{.class_3055} When the Old Testament says God ["repented"]{.class_3054} (Gen. 6:6; Exod. 32:14; Judg. 2:18; 1 Sam. 15:35; 2 Sam. 24:16; Psa. 106:45; Jon. 3:10; etc.), it wasn't saying God stopped committing some particular sin. God changed His mind concerning a thing, a person, or an action He intended to take.

When Jesus said ["repentance and remission of sins should be preached in his name among all nations"]{.class_3054} (Luke 24:47), He wasn't saying you have to stop sinning before you can get your sins forgiven. If that were the case, we would all be "up the creek without a paddle" (1 John 1:8). He's talking about a change of heart and mind about our own righteousness (Rom. 10:3); it's a change of heart and mind about God (1 Thess. 1:9; Acts 14:15, 26:18). And true ["repentance  toward God" ]{.class_3054}is always accompanied by ["faith toward our Lord Jesus Christ"]{.class_3054} (Acts 20:21). The ["works meet for repentance"]{.class_3054} (Acts 26:20) [follow]{.class_3055} salvation (1 Pet. 2:9--12); you do not do them to [get]{.class_3055} salvation (Eph. 2:8--10; Tit. 3:5).

Back to Mark 6. For the first part of verse 13, we refer you to our remarks under 1:28. Notice the use of oil in verse 13 in the exercise of the apostolic sign of healing. This once more demonstrates the [Jewish]{.class_3055} application of James 5:14--15 (see our comments on 2:5).

14 And king Herod heard of him; (for his name was spread abroad:) and he said, That John the Baptist was risen from the dead, and therefore mighty works do shew forth themselves in him.

15 Others said, That it is Elias. And others said, That it is a prophet, or as one of the prophets.

16 But when Herod heard thereof, he said, It is John, []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw956.xhtml_page_151}whom I beheaded: he is risen from the dead.

The fame of Jesus finally reaches Herod, and Herod assumes it is ["John the Baptist . . . risen from the dead"]{.class_3054} (vs. 14). Why? [Bad conscience:]{.class_3055} Herod had had John beheaded (vs. 16).

In his play [Hamlet,]{.class_3055} Shakespeare wrote, "Conscience doth make cowards of us all." In other words, the reason we don't do right, sometimes, is because we are trying to cover up for a bad conscience; we don't want folks discovering the "skeletons in our closet." The Bible says, ["The fear of man bringeth a snare"]{.class_3054} (Prov. 29:25). So Herod is a good study on a defiled conscience due to ["the fear of man,"]{.class_3054} and we'll be covering that as we go through this passage.

Now between verses 14--15, you have the three standard views of Christ by the masses (cf. 8:28). They thought He was either John the Baptist come back from the dead as a miracle worker, or He was Elijah (["Elias"]{.class_3054} being the Greek form of the name) who was to show up ["before the coming of the great and dreadful day of the LORD"]{.class_3054} (Mal. 4:5), or He was like one of the Old Testament prophets, and the one given by name in Matthew 16:14 is [Jeremiah.]{.class_3055} None of those perceptions match the Liberal "Jesus" who swishes around in long, soft robes, with honey oozing from His lips, saying, ["Blessed are the peacemakers, for they shall see Gawd."]{.class_3055}

Those prophets were some of the roughest characters in that Book. John the Baptist stood in front of those Pharisees who came out to see his baptism and said, "Ya bunch of snakes in the grass, you had better repent before God cuts you off and burns ya" (see the "original" in Luke 3:7--9).

Elijah mocked those Baalite priests as they twiddled their beads and said their "Our Fathers" and "Hail Marys" (1 Kings 18:26--27). Imagine a preacher making fun of someone else's religion?! That was Elijah.

Jeremiah preached to those Jews and said, "Nebuchadnezzar is going to come in here, and when he does, he's not going to spare the women, the babies, or the 'senior citizens.' He's going to kill your kids right in front of you, and those he doesn't kill, he's going to haul off to Babylon in chains. He's going to leave this city in rubble and []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw956.xhtml_page_152}burn down the Temple." That was the "weeping prophet."

I read one time there in the Prophets where a priest came up to Amos and said, "You hadn't better preach like you do here at the house of God (Bethel) because it's ['the king's chapel']{.class_3054}" (Amos 7:13). Amos looked that bird in the eye and said, "You see your wife over there. The Lord's going to take her and make her a 'street walker.' He's going to bring the Assyrians in, and they're going to kill your kids. And to top it all off, God is going to dump garbage and filth all over this land, and you're going to die in it" (Amos 7:17). That was Amos---["one of the prophets."]{.class_3054}

Have you ever read what the last prophet in the Old Testament wrote? He wrote, ["Behold, I will corrupt your seed, and spread DUNG upon your faces"]{.class_3054} (Mal. 2:3). Do you think the modern versions update [that word?]{.class_3055}[ "I trow not."]{.class_3054} God said through Malachi, "I am going to rub your faces in [manure." That's]{.class_3055} to whom Jesus was compared---["one of the prophets."]{.class_3054} Does that sound like the "meek, mild, gentle" Jesus?

Bob Jones Sr. said, "The Old Testament prophets were not 'sugar sticks'; they were [salty."]{.class_3055} Salt [stings]{.class_3055} when it is put into a wound. Those are the ones to whom Jesus was likened. Do you know what the head of the Bible department at Bob Jones University wrote years later, after the founder of the institution was dead and gone? Stewart Custer, [The Truth About the King James Version Controversy, ]{.class_3055}Bob Jones University Press, 1981, page 30: "There is never a particle of bitterness or unkindness in the words of the Lord Jesus Christ." There's not?!

["Ye serpents, ye generation of vipers, how can ye escape the damnation of hell?"]{.class_3054} (Matt. 23:33).

["Let them alone: they be blind leaders of the blind. And if the blind lead the blind, both shall fall into the ditch"]{.class_3054} (Matt. 15:14).

["Ye fools and blind"]{.class_3054} (Matt. 23:17, 19).

["Thou blind Pharisee"]{.class_3054} (Matt. 23:26).

["Go ye, and tell that fox"]{.class_3054} (Luke 13:22).

["Ye are of your father the devil, and the lusts of your father ye will do"]{.class_3054} (John 8:44).

That's a teacher at a "godly institution" (whatever [that]{.class_3055} is) talking []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw956.xhtml_page_153}like an unsaved Liberal. Stewart Custer was trying to convert a salty prophet into a "sugar stick" like Joel Osteen.

Verse 16 is another proof that John the Baptist is a type of Elijah. John the Baptist had his head cut off, [as Elijah will before the Second Advent]{.class_3071} (Rev. 11:7 cf. Rev. 20:4). Herod thought John the Baptist had ["risen from the dead"]{.class_3054}; [that is exactly what happens to Elijah before the Second Advent]{.class_3071} (Rev. 11:11). Those Tribulation saints are killed at the altar of the temple (Rev. 6:9) as sheep that are sacrificed (Psa. 44:22), and back there in Leviticus, those sheep, goats, and oxen were beheaded (Lev. 1:8, 12). For further study, we refer you to Psalm 16:4 and Isaiah 6:13.

17 For Herod himself had sent forth and laid hold upon John, and bound him in prison for Herodias' sake, his brother Philip's wife: for he had married her.

18 For John had said unto Herod, It is not lawful for thee to have thy brother's wife.

19 Therefore Herodias had a quarrel against him, and would have killed him; but she could not:

Verses 17--18 show you why John was ["put in prison"]{.class_3054} back in 1:14. Herod had committed adultery and broken the Mosaic Law by marrying the wife of his brother Philip (Lev. 18:16, 20:21), and John had read Herod the "riot act" for that.

Notice in verses 17 and 19 that it is [Herodias,]{.class_3055} the ["wife"]{.class_3054} in question, who has "the beef" with John the Baptist. She had "hitched her pony" to Herod's "wagon" because he was the first in line of succession to Herod the Great's throne, and no Baptist preacher was going to frustrate her ambitions.

There has been many a woman who drop-kicked a man right into Hell or derailed him from the will of God for his life. That's what happened to Ahab back in the Old Testament: Jezebel stirred that old boy up to become the most wicked King the northern ten tribes ever had (1 Kings 21:25). I have been in many a Christian youth camp where a young man was getting under conviction to receive Christ, and about that time, his girlfriend sitting beside him reached over and put her hand on his knee, so he would "get the message," you []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw956.xhtml_page_154}know.

There has been many a man come to the services of the Bible Baptist Church and heard real preaching and Bible doctrine for the first time in his life. He wanted to keep coming back for more, but his wife couldn't stand that male, "macho," "chauvinistic" approach. She let her husband know in no uncertain terms that he had to choose between her and the preacher. So off they went to the "coping and sharing" church down the road where the "preacher" told them how to let the wife run the family.

20 For Herod feared John, knowing that he was a just man and an holy, and observed him; and when he heard him, he did many things, and heard him gladly.

21 And when a convenient day was come, that Herod on his birthday made a supper to his lords, high captains, and chief estates of Galilee;

22 And when the daughter of the said Herodias came in, and danced, and pleased Herod and them that sat with him, the king said unto the damsel, Ask of me whatsoever thou wilt, and I will give it thee.

23 And he sware unto her, Whatsoever thou shalt ask of me, I will give it thee, unto the half of my kingdom.

24 And she went forth, and said unto her mother, What shall I ask? And she said, The head of John the Baptist.

25 And she came in straightway with haste unto the king, and asked, saying, I will that thou give me by and by in a charger the head of John the Baptist.

26 And the king was exceeding sorry; yet for his oath's sake, and for their sakes which sat with him, he would not reject her.

27 And immediately the king sent an executioner, and commanded his head to be brought: and he went and beheaded him in the prison,

28 And brought his head in a charger, and gave it to the damsel: and the damsel gave it to her mother.

29 And when his disciples heard of it, they came and took up his corpse, and laid it in a tomb.

As I have said, Herod is a good study in conscience. In verse 20, Herod's conscience is [awakened:]{.class_3055} he heard John the Baptist ["gladly"]{.class_3054} and ["did many things."]{.class_3054} But in verse 22, Herod's conscience became [seared]{.class_3055} at a dance. And when Pilate finally sends Jesus Christ to Herod, the Lord doesn't say a word to him (Luke 23:8--9). Jesus had nothing to say to a man who had killed his conscience the way Herod had.

Like Pilate, Herod was a [moral coward:]{.class_3055} he didn't have the courage of his convictions. There are men who have all kinds of physical courage who have no moral courage at all. Even a man like David was susceptible to moral cowardice: he was afraid of a Philistine King one time to the point where he pretended to be crazy (1 Sam. 21:12--14). Elijah got so scared of Jezebel that he ran for his life. Why, that bird had killed 450 prophets of Baal. Over in John 9, the parents of the blind man Jesus healed on the Sabbath were afraid to testify on their son's behalf because they feared being ["put out of the synagogue"]{.class_3054} (John 9:22).

I suppose that's why the expression ["Fear not"]{.class_3054} appears so many times in the Bible. Men get afraid and they don't do right. In Revelation 21:8, in the list of the inhabitants of the Lake of Fire, ["the fearful"]{.class_3054} top the list, even before queers (["the abominable"]{.class_3054}), murderers, adulterers, and idolaters. If a Catholic won't receive Christ because he is fearful of not being buried in a Catholic graveyard, he's headed for Hell. If a Jew rejects Christ because he's fearful of losing an inheritance, he's bound for the Lake of Fire. If a Mohammedan won't believe in Jesus as the Son of God because he's afraid of what his Moslem family and friends will do to him if he does, he'll burn right along with his "prophet."

What was Herod's problem? He was [fearful.]{.class_3055} You can tell the measure of a man by two things: what he [loves]{.class_3055} and what he [fears.]{.class_3055} Those are matters of the [heart,]{.class_3055} not the head. The intellect doesn't even factor into it. That Bible says of man: ["For as he thinketh IN HIS HEART, so is he"]{.class_3054} (Prov. 23:7). That's why the wisest man who ever lived said, ["Keep THY HEART with all diligence; for OUT OF IT are the issues of life"]{.class_3054} (Prov. 4:23). Saving faith in Christ comes from the [heart]{.class_3055} (Rom. 10:9--10).

Herod feared [his own conscience]{.class_3055} (see our remarks under vs. 14). []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw956.xhtml_page_156}The old saying is: "a good conscience can sleep in a hurricane." Paul talked about a ["good conscience"]{.class_3054} (Acts 23:1; 1 Tim. 1:5, 19),[ "a conscience void of offence toward God, and toward men"]{.class_3054} (Acts 24:16), a ["weak conscience"]{.class_3054} (1 Cor. 8:12), ["a pure conscience"]{.class_3054} (1 Tim. 3:9; 2 Tim. 1:3), a ["seared"]{.class_3054} conscience (1 Tim. 4:2), a ["defiled"]{.class_3054} conscience (Tit. 1:15), and ["an evil conscience"]{.class_3054} (Heb. 10:22).

If you want your conscience to be right as a Christian, then you need to have what Martin Luther said: a conscience "bound by the Scripture." A man with no final authority has a conscience bound by [nothing;]{.class_3055} he is an outlaw, an anarchist. If the Book of God can't control your conscience, then you are outside of God's law (an outlaw) and have no rule higher than your own whims (an anarchist).

Secondly, ["Herod feared John"]{.class_3054} (vs. 20). Herod had "Bibliophobia": he was afraid of [the Book,]{.class_3055} afraid of [the truth]{.class_3055} (John 17:17). So he [reformed,]{.class_3055} but he [didn't get saved.]{.class_3055} Reformation without salvation is [damnation.]{.class_3055} Herod ["observed"]{.class_3054} John, he ["heard him gladly,"]{.class_3054} he even ["did many things"]{.class_3054} John told him to do, but he [didn't get right.]{.class_3055}

Herod feared [his wife.]{.class_3055} That's why he had John arrested.

A little boy asked his daddy once, "Daddy, are you afraid of a dinosaur?" "No," said his daddy. "How about an elephant?" "Nope." "What about a lion?" "No." "How about a booger-bear?" "Of course not." "Well, Daddy," said the boy, "aren't you afraid of anything besides mommy?"

A fellow who had just had a big fight with his wife told his buddy, "She finally came crawling to me on her knees." "What'd she say?" asked his friend. "She said, 'Get out from under that bed, you coward.'"

A man was trying to compliment his friend for having a beautiful wife, and he said, "If I had a wife like yours, I'd stay home every night of the week." "You bet your life you would," said his buddy, "otherwise she'd wale on you from here to Sunday."

"I know he's the King of his castle because I saw his wife crown him."

"I know he wears the pants in his family because I saw them []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw956.xhtml_page_157}under his apron."

A bunch of men wind up at the "pearly gates" with their wives. St. Peter comes out and says, "All right, all you men who ran your home step over here, and all you men who didn't stay where you are." One man stepped over to the other side. In wonderment, St. Peter said, "You mean you actually ran your home instead of your wife running it?!" "No," said the man, "but my wife told me to come over here or else."

Herod also feared [public opinion.]{.class_3055} That's why Herod didn't have John killed to begin with. The people believed John was a prophet, and if Herod had had him killed outright, public opinion would have turned against him. That's the problem the priests and elders had in 11:32.

Public opinion isn't worth anything. People are fickle; they change opinions constantly. Over in Acts 14, after Paul healed that cripple, the people of Lystra proclaimed him and Barnabas gods (Acts 14:12). By the time Paul and Barnabas got through preaching, those same people ended up [stoning]{.class_3055} Paul and leaving him for dead (Acts 14:19). Over in Acts 28, Paul gets bitten by a poisonous snake and the people of Malta say, "Uh huh, he must have been a real 'bad cat' for fate to get him like that after he escaped shipwreck" (Acts 28:5). But when he didn't swell up and die, they're saying, "He must be a god" (Acts 28:6).

I will give you some sound advice. Don't "play" to the crowd; they aren't worth it. Don't believe every bad thing people say about you, and don't believe every good thing people say about you.

It must be terrible to be a politician. They always have to worry what the news media says about them because the news media forms public opinion. Your President, Congressmen, Senators, and Supreme Court judges don't make decisions based on what is constitutional or what is right. Every move they make is weighed in the balances of what the majority thinks, and what the majority thinks is fed to them by the news media.

Here in verse 26, it is the public opinion of his [companions]{.class_3055} about which he is worried. Herod's birthday rolled around, and he held a party for all the "high muckety-mucks." In the middle of that thing, his step-daughter (identified as Salome by Josephus) came in and did []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw956.xhtml_page_158}a seductive dance in front of him and inflamed his lust. That's how he seared his conscience and killed it.

That's when he said one of the most stupid things he said in his life: ["Ask of me whatsoever thou wilt, and I will give it thee . . . unto the half of my kingdom"]{.class_3054} (vss. 22--23). That's the same kind of an offer Ahasuerus made to Esther (Esth. 5:3, 6, 7:2), but that was an offer of love from a husband to his wife. Herod is what is known as "a dirty old man"; he's treating his step-daughter like a high-priced "call girl."

Salome goes to her momma and says, "What should I ask for?"

And momma says, "You ask for the head of that Baptist preacher on a platter."

"But Momma, that's gross! I don't want that!"

"You just do this for me, honey, and I'll buy you whatever you want, sweetheart," says Herodias.

So in Salome goes to Herod and says, ["I will that thou give me by and by in a charger the head of John the Baptist"]{.class_3054} (vs. 25).

Herod didn't want to do it (["the king was exceeding sorry,"]{.class_3054} vs. 26), but by now his conscience had been so violated, defiled, and seared that it was beyond repair. All he could think of was: "What will my buddies think? I'll look like a fool in their eyes if I become an 'Indian giver.'"

That Bible says, ["a companion of fools shall be destroyed"]{.class_3054} (Prov. 13:20). It says, ["evil communications]{.class_3054} \[i.e., those with whom you commune, your friends\][ corrupt good manners]{.class_3054} \[i.e., your good manner of living\]["]{.class_3054} (1 Cor. 15:33).

One time, a Christian young lady was going to a dance. She knew it was wrong, but she was going anyway, against the wishes of her Christian daddy. Before she left though, her father wanted to give her an object lesson she wouldn't forget.

He asked her to go pick up a coal from the fireplace. She looked over at the fireplace and saw that it was cold; no fire had been lit in it for several days. Without fear she picked up a cold coal and said to her daddy, "See, I didn't get burned." "But look at your hand," said her father. She looked down at her hand and saw it was black from the soot of the coal. Her daddy said, "See there. Bad company will []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw956.xhtml_page_159}smut you every time."

That is one of the problems (among many) with Christianity these days. The leaders have been keeping bad company. That goes for the Fundamentalists and Conservatives as much as for the Modernists and Liberals. The professors at the Bible Colleges and Christian Universities crave the recognition of their scholastic peers. I call it the "Scholars' Union." They all agree that [scholarship]{.class_3055} is to be given precedence over the Book. So unless a professor or teacher criticizes the Book ([AV]{.class_3055} 1611), he doesn't get "recognized."

Otherwise good leaders become [cowards]{.class_3055} when it comes to the authority of that Book. Lee Roberson, Curtis Hutson, and even Jack Hyles, for a while, were more afraid of what the "brethren" would say about them than they were of the Judgment Seat of Christ.

Even a firebrand like Ian Paisley was like that. He wasn't afraid to stand up in the European Parliament and call the Pope an antichrist, but when it came to Bob Jones Jr., he was as timid as a bunny rabbit. Beauchamp Vick's grandsons, the Bartlett brothers, were over in Ulster one time with Ian Paisley, and they asked him, "Have you ever read Dr. Ruckman's works?" "Oh yes," said Bro. Paisley, "I've read Dr. Ruckman." "Well what do you think of his stand on the [King James]{.class_3055} Bible?" the Bartletts asked him. "He's absolutely right," said Paisley in that thick Scottish brogue of his. "But don't tell Dr. Bob I said that."

What was the problem? [The fear of man:]{.class_3055} Paisley feared his buddy, Bob Jones Jr. If Paisley took a stand on that Book, Bob Jones Jr. wouldn't let him preach at Bob Jones University any more. Paisley had a daughter who attended BJU, and he didn't want to "rock the boat."

Herod was afraid of "losing face" before ["his lords, high captains, and chief estates"]{.class_3054} (vs. 21). That is one of the worst things that can happen to an Oriental: to lose the respect of his peers, to lose his prestige and standing in the community. Herod was more worried about his [reputation]{.class_3055} than doing what was right.

One time, this story was being told in Sunday school, and a little girl asked her teacher, "Why did Herod have to kill John the Baptist if he didn't want to?" "Well," said the teacher, "he had promised the girl to give her up to half his kingdom, and he was afraid of breaking []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw956.xhtml_page_160}his promise in front of all those important people." "Why didn't he just tell her she couldn't have the half with John the Baptist in it?" asked the little girl in all innocence.

Why not indeed? If Herod had acted like Solomon back there in 1 Kings 2:20--24, he would have come out as a strong leader in his own right instead of a weak coward who could be manipulated with ease through his own lust by a woman. But Herod didn't have the courage of his convictions; no guts.

30 And the apostles gathered themselves together unto Jesus, and told him all things, both what they had done, and what they had taught.

31 And he said unto them, Come ye yourselves apart into a desert place, and rest a while: for there were many coming and going, and they had no leisure so much as to eat.

32 And they departed into a desert place by ship privately.

33 And the people saw them departing, and many knew him, and ran afoot thither out of all cities, and outwent them, and came together unto him.

34 And Jesus, when he came out, saw much people, and was moved with compassion toward them, because they were as sheep not having a shepherd: and he began to teach them many things.

This is the prelude to the miracle of the feeding of the 5,000. You will recall that, before the parenthetical account of the death of John the Baptist, the Lord had sent the apostles out to preach (vs. 12). Now their preaching junket was over, and they had returned to Jesus to report on their meetings.

The problem, though, was the same as back in 3:20---["there were many coming and going, and they had no leisure so much as to eat"]{.class_3054} (vs. 31). So Jesus invited them to ["Come . . . apart" ]{.class_3054}before they came apart. The great practical lesson for the minister is that he needs to take some leisure time to rest.

That doesn't mean the ministry is characterized by leisure. Anybody involved in true ["ministry"]{.class_3054} knows that it is ["work"]{.class_3054}[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw956.xhtml_page_161} (Eph. 4:12). You have demands on your time to study and prepare lessons and sermons. You have demands on your time to make hospital visits. You have demands on your time to do visitation. If you are just starting out in the ministry or have a small work, you may be the one who has to clean the church building, mow the grass, stock the bathrooms, etc. You need some time off occasionally to rest and "recharge."

One thing that helped me throughout my ministry was [daily naps.]{.class_3055} After lunch every day, I would take the phone off the hook, put on some classical music, lay down on the floor, and "zonk out" for an hour. Some preachers like to relax by golfing; others by fishing. Do anything within reason and propriety to give yourself a break, and then get up and go back to work for the Lord.

We have already commented on the definition of ["a desert place"]{.class_3054} in our remarks under 1:45, which see. If there is any doubt that Christ is not talking about a sandy desert, look at verse 39: there was ["green grass"]{.class_3054} in the ["desert place"]{.class_3054} of verse 31. John adds that it ["was MUCH grass"]{.class_3054} (John 6:10).

This ["desert place"]{.class_3054} was a short way across the northern part of the Sea of Galilee. Luke 9:10 tells you the name of the city was Bethsaida, and you can see our comments on the two Bethsaidas in that Commentary. It was obvious to the people where Jesus and the disciples were headed, for the crowd outran them by foot and arrived there ahead of them (vs. 33).

Verse 34 is Jesus' typical response to ["the multitudes"]{.class_3054} (see Matt. 9:36). He had [sympathy]{.class_3055} for them; He had [pity]{.class_3055} on them; He was [concerned]{.class_3055} for them. That is what ["compassion"]{.class_3054} means. What evoked this compassion from Christ was that those Jews had no one to lead them right: ["they were as sheep not having a shepherd."]{.class_3054} The problem was the [religious leaders]{.class_3055}---the Pharisees, Sadducees, and scribes. They substituted [traditions]{.class_3055} for the word of God (7:7--13).

Jeremiah wrote: ["My people hath been lost sheep: THEIR SHEPHERDS HAVE CAUSED THEM TO GO ASTRAY, they have turned them away on the mountains: they have gone from mountain to hill, they have forgotten their resting place"]{.class_3054}[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw956.xhtml_page_162} (Jer. 50:6).

So given the neglect by the ["shepherds"]{.class_3054} of the people---their [pastors]{.class_3055} (Jer. 2:8, 10:21, 12:10, 23:1--2)---how does the compassion of Christ manifest itself [first and foremost?]{.class_3055}[ "He began to TEACH them many things."]{.class_3054} It is also true that [healing]{.class_3055} accompanied the teaching as a tangible manifestation of His compassion (Matt. 14:14), but [the proclamation of truth]{.class_3055} was the primary way the Lord showed compassion on the multitudes. Jesus Christ never commissioned the apostles to give out a "social gospel" that treated people's [physical problems]{.class_3055} with [no spiritual solution.]{.class_3055} I can take a poor, starving African and feed him, clothe him, give him money (or better yet, a job), and educate him---AND HE WILL STILL WIND UP IN HELL.

I am not saying Christians should ignore the poor, the hungry, the homeless, the addicts, or the uneducated; but fixing [those problems]{.class_3055} does nothing for the [sin problem.]{.class_3055} The preaching of the Gospel and the declaration of the whole ["counsel of God"]{.class_3054} (Acts 20:27) should be the ultimate goal, and all the other stuff should be aids to accomplishing those two goals, not take their place. If you have to make a choice between the teaching and preaching of the word of God and all the social stuff, you had better take the proclamation of the Scriptures every time.

Any chance you have to give someone the Scriptures, take advantage of it. Use common sense about it. Don't be obnoxious when you don't have to; don't steal your employer's time when you should be working. But you can always give someone something about which to think that the Holy Spirit can use when the person is alone. ["It is appointed unto men once to die, but after this the judgment"]{.class_3054} (Heb. 9:27) is a "goodie."

35 And when the day was now far spent, his disciples came unto him, and said, This is a desert place, and now the time is far passed:

36 Send them away, that they may go into the country round about, and into the villages, and buy themselves bread: for they have nothing to eat.

37 He answered and said unto them, Give ye them to eat. And they say unto him, Shall we go and buy two []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw956.xhtml_page_163}hundred pennyworth of bread, and give them to eat?

38 He saith unto them, How many loaves have ye? go and see. And when they knew, they say, Five, and two fishes.

39 And he commanded them to make all sit down by companies upon the green grass.

40 And they sat down in ranks, by hundreds, and by fifties.

41 And when he had taken the five loaves and the two fishes, he looked up to heaven, and blessed, and brake the loaves, and gave them to his disciples to set before them; and the two fishes divided he among them all.

42 And they did all eat, and were filled.

43 And they took up twelve baskets full of the fragments, and of the fishes.

44 And they that did eat of the loaves were about five thousand men.

["The day was now far spent"]{.class_3054} (vs. 35). As a matter of fact, ["it was evening"]{.class_3054} (Matt. 14:15). ["The time is far passed"]{.class_3054}: suppertime had come and gone. So it was probably around 7:00 in the evening. It is probably Summertime, which is why there was still enough daylight for the people to find their way to the surrounding villages to get food (vs. 36).

The Lord tells the disciples, ["Give ye them to eat"]{.class_3054} (vs. 37). The problem is the number of people they had to feed. Verse 44 tells you there ["were about five thousand men,"]{.class_3054} but that number didn't include the ["women and children"]{.class_3054} (Matt. 14:21). Without a doubt, the disciples were looking at feeding 10,000--15,000 people. Some of the disciples ask, ["Shall we go and buy two hundred pennyworth of bread, and give them to eat?"]{.class_3054} A penny was a day's wage in Bible times (Matt. 20:2), so 200 pennies was a large sum of money. But even Philip realized it was not enough to feed the crowd (John 6:7).

So Jesus says, ["How many loaves have ye? go and see"]{.class_3054} (vs. 38). Mark doesn't tell you how the disciples found out how many loaves there were, but John does. Andrew found ["a lad . . . which hath five barley loaves, and two small fishes"]{.class_3054}[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw956.xhtml_page_164} (John 6:9).

That is significant because the Liberals explain the miracle away by saying that when the crowd saw the generosity of the boy giving his lunch to Jesus, everyone in the crowd took out the food he had been saving and shared it. HOGWASH!

Jesus knew what the people had and what they didn't have. If the people actually had food, they would have taken it out and eaten it when they got hungry. If the people had the food, there would have been no problem to begin with. If the crowd had the food, Jesus would have told the apostles, "Well, let's pray and let the people eat the food they brought."

The search was made, and the only one with food in a crowd of several thousand people was ["a lad."]{.class_3054} He had ["five barley loaves"]{.class_3054}---the cheapest bread around. He had two fish, and they were ["small."]{.class_3054} If ["two hundred pennyworth of bread"]{.class_3054} would not be enough to feed the multitude, how could five loaves of cheap bread and a couple of "sardines" be enough?

Now Jesus organized that huge mass of people into ["ranks"]{.class_3054} to feed them; they were seated in rows of ["hundreds"]{.class_3054} and ["fifties"]{.class_3054} (vs. 40). So there are so many rows of a hundred people and so many rows of fifty people until all of nearly 10,000 to 15,000 people are seated. There couldn't have been any less than 150 rows of people, figuring a hundred to a row, and with some of the rows with only fifty people in them, there were certainly more. Consequently, I would say there were at least 200 rows of people sitting out there on the grass. [And Jesus fed all of them with only five loaves and two fish.]{.class_3055}

It astounds me that the people there saw that thing, and a lot of them still didn't believe on Christ. You talk about a demonstration of His power and divinity, [that was it!]{.class_3055} Yet all many of those people saw was the [food,]{.class_3055} not the miracles (John 6:26). The day following what you read here (John 6:22), Jesus had one of the largest defections of people from following Him that He had in His entire ministry (John 6:66). Why? Because they got offended at [His words]{.class_3055} (John 6:60--61).

That's why the "social gospel" is so dangerous: you only get people joining the church for the "loaves and fishes." When you start putting [the word]{.class_3055} on them, they can't take it. They go back to their animism or their Buddhism or their Hinduism or their []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw956.xhtml_page_165}Mohamedanism. Why? Because, although they may have gotten their bellies filled (or clothes on their back or a roof over their heads or their children educated, etc.), [nothing changed in their hearts.]{.class_3055}

This boy giving his lunch to Jesus is a wonderful picture of what happens when you give to the Lord. The boy gave what he had to Jesus, and he got back more than he gave (vs. 43). That's part of the law of the harvest: you always reap more than you sow. The promise when you give to the Lord cheerfully is this:

["And God is able to make all grace abound toward you; that ye, always having all sufficiency in all things, may abound to every good work"]{.class_3054} (2 Cor. 9:8).

That boy didn't have to give his lunch to Jesus Christ. He could have thrown it away, like a lot of young men throw their lives away. He could have kept it for himself, like a lot of folks do: take care of "number one." He could have lied about what he had when the disciples asked. You have a militant movement in America today where women are pretending to be men and men are pretending to be women. The Bible calls people like that [hypocrites.]{.class_3055} They're [liars]{.class_3055} (see Rev. 21:8).

I know of two boys who grew up in the South. Both of them were given great talents by God, but what each one did with what God gave him was drastically different. One boy grew up in a Holiness home in Mississippi. That boy wanted to play football in high school, but he couldn't because he wouldn't cut his pretty hair. Some talent scout was looking to "strike it rich" by finding a white man who could sing like a black man, and he found one in that boy. That kid spent the rest of his life calling attention to [himself]{.class_3055} with his music. He even let people call him "The King." He ended up dying before he was fifty years old. He overdosed himself on prescription drugs by taking three doses of five prescription drugs at the same time. His doctor who prescribed the drugs to him said he was a "walking pharmacy" (that's a coy way of saying [drug addict]{.class_3055}). That was Elvis Presley. He took what God gave him and wasted it on himself.

The other boy grew up in Alabama. He grew up on a peanut farm. His momma was a Baptist, and his daddy was a Methodist. Although he was ordained as a Methodist, he was baptized as a Baptist. When he was ten years old, the profound thought struck him that if he were going to live forever (he had gotten saved), []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw956.xhtml_page_166}[he had better learn how to live.]{.class_3055} That was the great thought that motivated him all his life. He became an old-time Methodist evangelist and was probably responsible for leading half a million people to Christ. You know him by the school he set up: Bob Jones University. Bob Jones Sr. took his "basket" and gave it to the Lord to let Him use it, and boy, did God use it.

Now Christian, the lost world is looking to you to give them the Bread of Life: ["Give ye them to eat."]{.class_3054} But there is no way you can do it by yourself, in your own power, with your own resources. If that little boy had tried to feed that multitude himself, he couldn't have fed more than a few people, and then not much. He had to give what he had to Jesus and let Him bless it (vs. 41) so the multitude could be fed.

Verses 42--43: ["And they did all eat, and were filled. And they took up twelve baskets full of the fragments, and of the fishes."]{.class_3054} This is one of the greatest miracles in Christ's ministry. It is so great that it is one of the few events in the life of Christ that shows up in [all four Gospels:]{.class_3055} Matthew 14:13--21; here in Mark 6:32--44; Luke 9:10--17; John 6:5--13. Moreover, it is repeated with the feeding of the 4,000 (see 8:1--9 here in Mark). That being the case, there is something of great importance about this miracle for God to emphasize it with so much repetition (see Gen. 41:32).

Well, over in John 6:30--31, the Old Testament miracle that comes to mind to the people questioning Jesus' credentials as the Messiah was God feeding the children of Israel with manna when they wandered in the wilderness for forty years. While that was an actual, historical occurrence, the Lord tells us in Psalm 78:2 that it was recorded as ["a parable"]{.class_3054} to teach us something beyond the historical event (note Psa. 78:24--25).

The lesson, in this case, turns out to be a [prophetic]{.class_3055} lesson about the [Second Advent.]{.class_3055} Micah 7 is a prophecy about Israel in the Tribulation: note the invasion of the Antichrist in Micah 7:12 (cf. Isa. 10:5) and the Advent in Micah 7:17 (cf. Rev. 6:15--17). When the Antichrist comes in, those Jews who believe and heed Christ's words in Matthew 24:15--16 ["flee into the mountains."]{.class_3054} The locations of those ["mountains"]{.class_3054} are given in Micah 7:14, where it is said that God will feed His people ["as in the days of old."]{.class_3054} Which []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw956.xhtml_page_167}["days of old"]{.class_3054}? Micah 7:15---["According to the days of thy coming out of the land of Egypt."]{.class_3054} God is going to feed those Jews who flee from the Antichrist (Rev. 12:6) with manna just as He fed those Jews who fled from Pharaoh there in Exodus (see Psa. 105:38--40).

That is why the Devil tempted Christ there in the wilderness to turn those stones to bread. He was trying to get the Lord to do what He will do at the Second Advent, but at the [wrong time.]{.class_3055} The Devil is subtle, boy (Gen. 3:1). If He can't get you to do wrong, one of the things he will try to get you to do is run ahead of God and do what [you should do,]{.class_3055} but [not when you should be doing it.]{.class_3055}

45 And straightway he constrained his disciples to get into the ship, and to go to the other side before unto Bethsaida, while he sent away the people.

46 And when he had sent them away, he departed into a mountain to pray.

47 And when even was come, the ship was in the midst of the sea, and he alone on the land.

48 And he saw them toiling in rowing; for the wind was contrary unto them: and about the fourth watch of the night he cometh unto them, walking upon the sea, and would have passed by them.

49 But when they saw him walking upon the sea, they supposed it had been a spirit, and cried out:

50 For they all saw him, and were troubled. And immediately he talked with them, and saith unto them, Be of good cheer: it is I; be not afraid.

51 And he went up unto them into the ship; and the wind ceased: and they were sore amazed in themselves beyond measure, and wondered.

52 For they considered not the miracle of the loaves: for their heart was hardened.

By the time the multitude has eaten their fill and the fragments have been taken up, it is dusk. Jesus sends the disciples ahead by ship to sail to the other Bethsaida on the western shore of Galilee []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw956.xhtml_page_168}(see our comments on Luke 9:10 in that Commentary). He remains behind and goes up on ["a mountain to pray"]{.class_3054} (vs. 46).

Hidden in this account is the time of Christ's Advent. What you have, prophetically, is Israel being fed miraculously in the wilderness, followed by the Lord coming back in verse 48. The ["mountain,"]{.class_3054} in [type,]{.class_3055} is a literal mountain: [Mt. Sion upstairs]{.class_3055} (Heb. 12:22--24). Right now, the Lord is up there praying for His people: the Body of Christ right now (Rom. 8:34), the Jews in the Tribulation (Heb. 7:25).

When He comes back, He has to walk through that water [upstairs]{.class_3055} about which I told you in 1:16--17 (which see). Hence the disciples were ["in the MIDST of the sea"]{.class_3054} (vs 47). The picture is of the saint down here, between the waters below the firmament and the waters above the firmament. That's the picture involved when Israel passes through the Red Sea there in Exodus (see 1 Cor. 10:1--2).

Do you know why nearly all Bible maps have the exodus go through the "Reed Sea" instead of the [Red Sea?]{.class_3055} Someone is trying to deny the [blood atonement,]{.class_3055} whether he realizes it or not: he is being "led."

Having made the blood atonement for our sins, Christ entered into the ["true" "holy places"]{.class_3054} of ["heaven"]{.class_3054} (Heb. 9:24) ["BY]{.class_3054} \[[not with]{.class_3055}\] [his own blood"]{.class_3054} (Heb. 9:12). When He did, He passed through the waters beyond Alpha Draconis that had been dyed with the eternal blood of God that had flowed through His system before the Crucifixion (Acts 20:28), staining that water into a [Red Sea.]{.class_3055} It is pictured by Moses turning the waters of Egypt into blood and the waters on earth during the Tribulation becoming blood (Rev. 8:8, 16:3--4). That is why Christ's first public miracle was turning water into a type of His blood (John 2:1--11). So when the blood of the Passover lamb was applied, it was not only put on ["the two side posts"]{.class_3054} of the door, but on the ["UPPER"]{.class_3054} one as well. So every believer on this earth is literally "under the blood." And when you appropriate the blood of Christ [spiritually,]{.class_3055} you are made a part of a Body (1 Cor. 12:13; Eph. 5:30) that has been [literally]{.class_3055} washed in the blood of the eternal God (Rev. 1:5).

For further study, we refer you to our comments on Hebrews 9:11--14 in that Commentary (pp. 202--211 in 1998 edition).

Verse 48: []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw956.xhtml_page_169}["And he saw them toiling in rowing; for the wind was contrary to them."]{.class_3054} So while you are down here ["in the midst of the sea,"]{.class_3054} you are going to be ["toiling"]{.class_3054} if you are doing what you are supposed to be doing as a Christian. [Toil]{.class_3055} is "hard, physical work." The Pauline word is ["labour"]{.class_3054} (1 Cor. 15:58; 2 Cor. 5:9).

Moreover, it is labor in a [battle:]{.class_3055} ["the wind was CONTRARY to them."]{.class_3054} So Paul's ["companion in labour"]{.class_3054} was his ["fellowsoldier"]{.class_3054} (Phil. 2:25). The old divines used to talk about "the Church Militant"---that's the Body of Christ down here on earth engaged in warfare against the world, the flesh, and the Devil---and "the Church Triumphant"---that's the Body of Christ up in Heaven who are at rest from their labors.

["And about the fourth watch of the night he cometh unto them, walking upon the sea"]{.class_3054}---there's the date of the Second Advent. Christ likened His Second Coming to ["a man taking a far journey"]{.class_3054} and leaving work for his servants to do (13:34). Christ's command to the disciples He left behind to do His work is:

["Watch ye therefore: for ye know not when the master of the house cometh, at even ]{.class_3054}\[that's a [watch]{.class_3055}\][, or at midnight]{.class_3054} \[that's a [watch]{.class_3055}\][, or at the cockcrowing]{.class_3054} \[that's a [watch]{.class_3055}\][, or in the morning ]{.class_3054}\[that's a [watch]{.class_3055}\]["]{.class_3054} (Mark 13:35).

The parallel passage in Luke 12:38 is absolutely clear in this matter:

"And if he shall come in the second WATCH, or come in the third WATCH, and find them so, blessed are those servants."

There is no doubt about the context---it's the Second Advent.

["Be ye therefore ready also: for the Son of man cometh at an hour when ye think not"]{.class_3054} (Luke 12:40).

Well, verse 48 here in Mark 6 tells you exactly which ["watch"]{.class_3054} He comes: ["the fourth watch of the night."]{.class_3054} That's the ["morning"]{.class_3054} watch of Mark 13:35. The Second Coming of Christ is likened to the sun coming up in the morning. Over in Malachi 4:2, in regards to the day of the Lord (Mal. 4:1), the verse says, ["But unto you that fear my name shall the Sun of righteousness arise with healing in his wings."]{.class_3054}[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw956.xhtml_page_170} When Peter commented on the Transfiguration of Christ, which was a type of the Second Advent (Mark 9:1), he said, ["until the DAY DAWN, and the DAY STAR ARISE in your hearts"]{.class_3054} (2 Pet. 1:19). You will find the same thing in 2 Samuel 23:4; Isaiah 60:1--2, 19--20; Hosea 6:3; Psalm 19:4--6, 89:36; and Matthew 13:43.

That means the Church Age is like nighttime. If you want to figure out when the Second Advent is, you take the seven system of the Bible in connection with the watches laid down by Jesus Christ, and you can get a good idea of about when it will take place.
