As I pointed out to you in our comments on 2:27--28, the Sabbath is a picture of the Millennial rest of the earth. So using the millennial days set down in 2 Peter 3:8, you would figure 6,000 years from Genesis 1 (4004 B.C.). That would put the beginning of the Millennial sabbath roughly at A.D. 2000. Well, Jesus Christ didn't come back in the year 2000, so what you have is God not counting some years (Job 3:6); and if you want a good example of that, look at Matthew 1. In Matthew 1:8, the Holy Spirit drops three generations of Kings because of the sin of Ahab that had been introduced into the line of the Kings of Judah (see our comments on the verse in that Commentary).

That being the case, the time between Acts 2 and the Rapture of the Church is likened to nighttime (John 9:4--5 cf. 1 Thess. 5:10). That nighttime is divided into four watches of three "hours" each (Mark 13:35 cf. John 11:9). The morning watch would be from three in the morning to six in the morning. That means, brethren, that you are at the last few ticks of the second hand before the clock strikes six. You had better buckle your seat belt and put your trays in the upright and locked position, because you are about to [take off!]{.class_3055}

Back to verse 48: ["and would have passed by them."]{.class_3054} Look at how "nonchalant" that statement is. Just as back in 4:35--41, Jesus is unconcerned over the storm. He had sent the disciples ["to the other side"]{.class_3054} of the lake, and He was headed that way to meet them when they got there.

There was another time Jesus acted this way, and it was after His resurrection. In Luke 24, the resurrected Christ met two disciples on the road to Emmaus, but He hid His identity from them. After a Bible []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw956.xhtml_page_171}study through the Old Testament on His sufferings and glorification, ["he made as though he would have gone further"]{.class_3054} (Luke 24:48). He wanted to see if those disciples were hungry enough for the word of God to want to continue to have fellowship with Him.

Here in Mark 6:48, Jesus certainly is not trying to hide the fact that He is [walking on the water past their ship.]{.class_3055} It makes you wonder if He weren't trying to see if they would invite Him into the boat.

Now you can understand the disciples' reaction upon seeing Jesus walk past them in the darkest part of the night, on a stormy sea: they think they've just seen a ghost (vs. 49). They cry out (vs. 49) in fear (vs. 50).

Notice, again, how closely all of this resembles the reaction of the disciples to seeing the resurrected Christ (see our comments above). When Jesus appeared to them in the upper room in Luke 24, they were ["terrified," "affrighted,"]{.class_3054} and ["troubled"]{.class_3054} because they thought ["they had seen a spirit"]{.class_3054} (Luke 24:37--38). He told those women to whom He appeared, ["Be not afraid"]{.class_3054} (Matt. 28:10).

This too points to the Lord's Second Coming. The same resurrected Christ who appeared to those disciples will be the One who returns (Acts 1:11), both to take the members of His Body home to Heaven (1 Thess. 4:16--17) and to return in power to take the kingdoms of this earth away from the Devil (Rev. 19:11--21; Zech. 14:1--4).

For comment on verse 51, we refer you to our remarks on 4:39--41, which see. What amazed them more here than even in Mark 4 was that, this time, Jesus had not even been with them in the boat. He could not only calm the storm, He could walk through it, on the water, with ease. You have to admit, "that am sumptin'." None of these Charismatic fakirs would try it, no matter how much they quote and claim John 14:12.

Verse 52 is absolutely astounding. After everything they had seen, there was no reason the disciples should have been ["sore amazed . . . beyond measure"]{.class_3054} (vs. 51). Not only had they seen Jesus heal lepers and lame men, deliver maniacs full of devils, exercise authority over nature, and raise a dead girl, they had just seen Him create food out of basically "thin air." Put yourself in the shoes of those disciples. After Jesus divided those five loaves and []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw956.xhtml_page_172}two small fish between twelve men to pass out, there was no way they should have been able to make it past the first person of the row on which each of them waited, much less the first row of fifty. But no matter how much they passed out, they never ran out. How could that be? The One who fed two million Jews out in the wilderness, every day for forty years, was creating bread and cooked fish to feed 10,000--15,000 people on an early Summer evening. Nothing Jesus did should have surprised those disciples anymore.

What was the problem? ["Their heart was hardened"]{.class_3054} (vs. 52). That's not talking about some infidel like Pharaoh (Exod. 8:15, 19); these are Christ's closest followers. What's more, they do it again after the feeding of the 4,000 (8:17--21). That is why they didn't believe in the Resurrection when told about it (16:14). Those Jews back there in Numbers 13--14 hardened their hearts through ["an evil heart of unbelief"]{.class_3054} and ["the deceitfulness of sin"]{.class_3054} (Heb. 3:12--13). The Lord Jesus took it for granted that even His closest followers had an evil human nature (Matt. 7:11; Luke 11:13). The greatest Christian who ever lived said, ["For I know that in me (that is, in my flesh,) dwelleth no good thing"]{.class_3054} (Rom. 7:18). Christian, if you don't put a guard on your heart and mind, you can commit any sin an unsaved person can. Jesus said those apostles who had gone out preaching repentance, casting out devils, and healing the sick had hard hearts just like those lost Jews who didn't believe on Him (John 12:37--40).

53 And when they had passed over, they came into the land of Gennesaret, and drew to the shore.

54 And when they were come out of the ship, straightway they knew him,

55 And ran through that whole region round about, and began to carry about in beds those that were sick, where they heard he was.

56 And whithersoever he entered, into villages, or cities, or country, they laid the sick in the streets, and besought him that they might touch if it were but the border of his garment: and as many as touched him were made whole.

["Gennesaret" ]{.class_3054}[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw956.xhtml_page_173}(vs. 53) is the Greek form of the Hebrew name ["Chinnereth"]{.class_3054}: it was a town in the tribe of Naphtali on the west shore of the Sea of Galilee (Josh 19:32, 35). The Sea of Galilee took its name from that city, so you will sometimes see it called in Scripture ["the sea of Chinnereth"]{.class_3054} (Josh. 13:27) and ["the lake of Gennesaret"]{.class_3054} (Luke 5:1).

["And when they were come out of the ship, straightway they knew him"]{.class_3054} (vs. 54). Another type of the Second Advent. When Christ shows up at the Advent, all the Jews left on this earth, who have survived the persecution and attacks of the Antichrist, will see Jesus and recognize Him as their Messiah; and the whole nation will be converted in a day.

["And I will pour upon the house of David, and upon the inhabitants of Jerusalem, the spirit of grace and of supplications: and they shall look upon me whom they have pierced, and they shall mourn for him, as one mourneth for his only son, and shall be in bitterness for him, as one that is in bitterness for his firstborn"]{.class_3054} (Zech. 12:10).

["And so all Israel shall be saved: as it is written, There shall come out of Sion the Deliverer, and shall turn away ungodliness from Jacob"]{.class_3054} (Rom. 11:26).

Verses 55--56 need no comment. They are the typical scenes we have read over and over again in the Gospel of Mark (1:32--34, 45, 2:1--3, 3:7--10, 5:24--29). Notice in verse 56 that Jesus ministered in the ["cities"]{.class_3054}---those were the big towns, the ["villages"]{.class_3054}---what we would call the  "suburbs" today, and the ["country"]{.class_3054}---those "desert places," like in 1:45 and 6:31--32. Paul told Festus and Agrippa, ["this thing was not done in a corner"]{.class_3054} (Acts 26:26). By the time Jesus finished His ministry, I dare say there wasn't a Jew in all of Galilee or Judea who didn't know about Him.

[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1185.xhtml}

# Chapter 7 {.heading_97}

7:1 Then came together unto him the Pharisees, and certain of the scribes, which came from Jerusalem.

2 And when they saw some of his disciples eat bread with defiled, that is to say, with unwashen, hands, they found fault.

3 For the Pharisees, and all the Jews, except they wash their hands oft, eat not, holding the tradition of the elders.

4 And when they come from the market, except they wash, they eat not. And many other things there be, which they have received to hold, as the washing of cups, and pots, brasen vessels, and of tables.

5 Then the Pharisees and scribes asked him, Why walk not thy disciples according to the tradition of the elders, but eat bread with unwashen hands?

6 He answered and said unto them, Well hath Esaias prophesied of you hypocrites, as it is written, This people honoureth me with their lips, but their heart is far from me.

7 Howbeit in vain do they worship me, teaching for doctrines the commandments of men.

8 For laying aside the commandment of God, ye hold the tradition of men, as the washing of pots and cups: and many other such like things ye do.

9 And he said unto them, Full well ye reject the commandment of God, that ye may keep your own tradition.

10 For Moses said, Honour thy father and thy mother; and, Whoso curseth father or mother, let him die the death:

11 But ye say, If a man shall say to his father or mother, It is Corban, that is to say, a gift, by whatsoever thou mightest be profited by me; he shall be free.

12 And ye suffer him no more to do ought for his father or his mother;

13 Making the word of God of none effect through your tradition, which ye have delivered: and many such like things do ye.

Once more, Jesus runs afoul of the Scholars' Union of His day: the scribes and Pharisees (see 2:16, 24, 3:6). This time, they are worried about Christ's disciples not washing their hands before they eat. Those Pharisees and scribes were always concerned with how clean someone was on the outside while they themselves harbored a heart full of filth and wickedness (Matt. 23:25). Over in John 18:28, they wouldn't enter into Pilate's judgment hall because they were worried about being defiled and not being able to observe Passover; the whole time they were accomplices to the murder of Jesus Christ (Acts 7:52).

It is amazing how many people have dirty hands and a clean heart, and how many have clean hands and a dirty heart. Pilate tried washing away the guilt of turning Jesus Christ over to a mob to be killed (Matt. 27:24), and it didn't work (Acts 4:27--28). Water can't absolve you of the moral filth of sin (see our comments on 1 Pet. 3:21 in that Commentary).

Notice in verse 3 that the practice of often washing the hands wasn't taken from Scripture; it came from [tradition.]{.class_3055} This wasn't part of the washings found throughout the Levitical Law (e.g., read Lev. 15); this was something [added to]{.class_3055} what God said. This is one of the "big three" of which the Christian is to beware. This is ["the tradition of men"]{.class_3054} (Col. 2:8). The other two are ["philosophy"]{.class_3054} (Col. 2:8) and ["science"]{.class_3054} (1 Tim. 6:20---changed in all the new "Bibles" so you will take the word of science over the words of God). All three are designed to destroy your faith in the Bible; they are all three satanic.

As always, Mohammed, unable to come up with anything original on his own, copies Talmudic Judaism here. Before prayer, a Moslem is required to wash his hands up to the elbows and his feet to his ankles. If he is sick, traveling, coming from the bathroom, or has touched a woman (Mohammed claimed he had sex with all his wives before prayers; according to his favorite wife whom he married when she was six years old and consummated the marriage when she was nine---Aisha---he was [bewitched]{.class_3055} \[[Hadith,]{.class_3055}[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1185.xhtml_page_176} Bukhari, Vol. VII, nos. 658--661\]), he must wash his whole body so Allah can "purify" him. If he can't find any water with which to wash, then he has to smear "pure earth" (whatever that is) all over his face and hands in order to become "pure" enough for Allah to complete his favor on the Moslem. You can read that in the [Koran]{.class_3055}, in Sura 5:6.

To that nonsense, you can add snorting water up your nose three times before the morning prayer in order to drive out Satan, who sleeps there overnight ([Hadith,]{.class_3055} Bukhari, Vol. I, no. 462). The footnote to Bukhari's [Hadith,]{.class_3055} Volume IV, number 516 makes it [obligatory]{.class_3055} for a Moslem to believe that. You are to take Mohammed's word for that, even though Allah forgot to mention it in the [Koran.]{.class_3055} You are to take that over Acts 15:9; Titus 2:14; Hebrews 9:14; 1 Peter 1:22; and 1 John 1:7.

That is the tradition of a man---a [sex-obsessed, illiterate, epileptic, cruel, vengeful, demon-possessed man.]{.class_3055}

Now, how did the Lord Jesus feel about such ["traditions"]{.class_3054}? Verse 6: ["Well hath Esaias prophesied of you HYPOCRITES . . . ."]{.class_3054} "Why don't your disciples wash up before dinner, Jesus? It's tradition." "I'll tell you what you are; you're a bunch of hypocrites." [That's]{.class_3055} the Jesus Christ of that Bible. He's nothing like the Jesus Christ of Stewart Custer or Joel Osteen.

What are these scribes and Pharisees? They're [pretenders;]{.class_3055} they're [actors.]{.class_3055} That's what a hypocrite is. They were pretending to be, and acting like, something they were not. They were pretending to honor God when they were doing nothing of the sort.

["This people honoureth me with their lips, but their heart is far from me."]{.class_3054} Their mouth doesn't match their heart. The rule of thumb is: ["out of the abundance of the heart the mouth speaketh"]{.class_3054} (Matt. 12:34). Normally, what's in the heart of the man will come out of his mouth, but these people are acting and pretending. They act like they know God spoke through Moses (John 9:29), and yet they are ignorant of the One of whom Moses wrote (John 5:45--46). They pretend to be Abraham's children while doing exactly the opposite of what Abraham would do when confronted with the truth (John 8:39--40).

Things haven't changed. Paul wrote[ "that in the last days,"]{.class_3054} there would be those []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1185.xhtml_page_177}["Having a form of godliness, but denying the power thereof"]{.class_3054} (2 Tim. 3:1, 5). He wrote of another bunch: ["They profess that they know God; but in works they deny him, being abominable, and disobedient, and unto every good work reprobate"]{.class_3054} (Tit. 1:16).

Back to Mark 7: ["Howbeit in vain do they worship me, teaching for doctrines the commandments of men"]{.class_3054} (vs. 7). Now before we get into the application of Christ's words, look at how the corrupt modern versions handle ["doctrines"]{.class_3054} here. After changing the word ["doctrine"]{.class_3054} to ["teaching"]{.class_3055} three times so far in the Gospel of Mark (i.e., [every time it has shown up so far]{.class_3055}---1:22, 27, 4:2), suddenly, when confronted with how ridiculous it would sound to say "teaching as teachings," they retain the word [doctrine.]{.class_3055} That's the [ASV,]{.class_3055} the [NASV,]{.class_3055} the [RSV,]{.class_3055} the [ESV ]{.class_3055}(the "Conservative" translation of the [RSV]{.class_3055}), the [NRSV,]{.class_3055} and the [NKJV.]{.class_3055} The [NIV]{.class_3055} remains consistent in using the word ["teachings,"]{.class_3055} but only because it [doesn't bother translating the passage.]{.class_3055} It [paraphrases]{.class_3055} Christ's words (called "dynamic equivalence" so you won't know what's going on) as: ["their teachings are merely human rules."]{.class_3055}

In other words, when identifying the ["hypocrites"]{.class_3054} of verse 6, don't forget to include Bible-correcting, Bible-denying Fundamentalists and Conservatives. They will complain about the translators of the [AV]{.class_3055} not being "uniform" in their translating, while [they do the same thing]{.class_3055} when it suits their own purposes. [They're hypocrites!]{.class_3055}

Now, verse 7 covers all the extra-biblical teachings of not only Talmudic Judaism, but also Catholics, Protestants, the teachers and professors in Fundamental and Evangelical Colleges and Seminaries, the Charismatics, the Mormons, the Jehovah's Witnesses, the Seventh-day Adventists, the Christian Science practitioners, the "purpose-driven," "seeker-sensitive," and "emergent" crowd, and the Moslems. It covers anybody who replaces the [authority of Scripture]{.class_3055} with the [authority of man]{.class_3055} (Joseph Smith, the Church Fathers, Ellen G. White, Mary Baker Eddy, church councils, the "consensus of scholars," the [Hadith,]{.class_3055} Charles Taze Russell, Brigham Young, "early Christians," Popes, presidents and chancellors of Universities, "good, godly, dedicated" men, et al.).

["IN VAIN do they worship me."]{.class_3054}[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1185.xhtml_page_178} It doesn't do any good to go to church and act like you're serving God. All the religious service and ritual is for [nothing]{.class_3055} if you are going by ["the commandments of men."]{.class_3054} Why? Because ["the tradition of men"]{.class_3054} is directly associated with basic, fundamental principles of the lost world under the control of Satan (["the rudiments of the world"]{.class_3054}---Col. 2:8, 20--22). It involves the bondage and course of the unregenerate nature (Gal. 4:3; Eph. 2:2). It is a system of self-righteousness and self-gratification apart from the revelation of God in His word.

Those Pharisees and scribes were trying to "one up" God by [adding]{.class_3055} to what He said in His Book. They were ["going about to establish their own righteousness"]{.class_3054} (cf. Rom. 10:3) apart from what God said. It is a satanic operation from start to finish. Back in Genesis 3, the Devil got Eve to sin by getting her to [subtract from]{.class_3055} what God said (she got rid of ["freely"]{.class_3054} from God's original instructions---Gen. 2:16 cf. Gen. 3:2) and to [add to]{.class_3055} what God said (she added ["neither shall ye touch it"]{.class_3054} to God's original instructions---Gen. 2:17 cf. Gen. 3:3). If those hadn't worked, the next step would have been to take what God said [out of its context.]{.class_3055}

Notice again in verses 8--9 that ["the commandment of God"]{.class_3054}---another term for the Scriptures (Psa. 19:7--8, 119:6, 10, 19, 21, 32, 35, 47--48, etc.)---is laid aside and rejected for [tradition.]{.class_3055} This is significant, because to justify tradition supplanting the Scriptures as the final authority for the Christian, the Catholic Church will take you to 2 Thessalonians 2:15 and 3:6 to justify tradition as an authority equal to (meaning [superior to]{.class_3055} when the two conflict) the written word of God. Of course, the traditions referred to by the Apostle Paul have nothing to do with baby sprinkling, the rosary, indulgences, celibacy for the ministry, Purgatory, transubstantiation, papal supremacy, prayers to dead saints, veneration of relics and images, confessing sins to a priest, etc. They have to do with [working for a living]{.class_3055} and not [being lazy]{.class_3055} (2 Thess. 3:10). They include the decrees of the apostles and elders in Jerusalem for the Gentile converts to abstain from fornication, eating blood (as in the [Catholic Mass!]{.class_3055}), and eating things strangled (Acts 15:20). They would also be the ordinances (1 Cor. 11:2) and doctrines (Rom. 16:17) Paul taught the local churches he started. They would certainly NOT be anything contrary to []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1185.xhtml_page_179}[revealed Scripture]{.class_3055} (2 Tim. 3:15--17; 2 Pet. 1:19--21). According to your Saviour, ["the commandment of God"]{.class_3054} trumps ["the tradition of men,"]{.class_3054} at least if you want to worship God ["in spirit and in truth"]{.class_3054} (John 4:24 cf. 1 Cor. 2:13; John 17:17).

Now, here is the testimony of a church that, according to the Lord Jesus Christ, is absolutely ["vain"]{.class_3054} in its worship. These are taken from official publications and leaders of the Roman Catholic Church.

This is from the Archbishop of Baltimore back in 1876, James Cardinal Gibbons: "We must, therefore, conclude that the Scriptures alone cannot be a sufficient guide and rule of faith because they cannot, at any time, be within the reach of every inquirer, because they are not of themselves clear and intelligible even in matters of the highest importance, and because they do not contain all the truths necessary for salvation" ([The Faith of Our Fathers,]{.class_3055} John Murphy Company, 1917, pg. 73).

This is from a Father DuBois, in the work [O Biblismi]{.class_3055} (Para, 1921, pg. 96): "Today, we (Roman Catholics) live one-tenth on the Bible, and [nine-tenths on Tradition.]{.class_3055} The Bible perhaps does not contain all essential truths. [Tradition is greater than the word of the Bible."]{.class_3055}

According to the last Roman Catholic Archbishop of Canterbury, Cardinal Reginald Pole, who held the office during the reign of "Bloody Mary": "Our worship and our beliefs [in their entirety,]{.class_3055} depend upon [tradition"]{.class_3055} (cited by Russell and Colin Standish in their work [The Rapture and the Antichrist,]{.class_3055} Hartland Publications, 2004, pg. 161).

A Jesuit named Father Hunter, in his work [Outlines of Dogmatic Theology]{.class_3055} (Vol. I, pp. 153--155), wrote, "The Church could [dispense with Holy Scriptures,]{.class_3055} but cannot dispense with tradition---tradition is of a wider scope and [more necessary."]{.class_3055}

In his book [Our Priceless Heritage]{.class_3055} (1900, pg. 24), Henry Woods quotes Cardinal Robert Bellarmine (1542--1621), a Jesuit involved in Rome's Counter-Reformation, as saying: "We ought to serve God according to the tradition of the ancients. We ought to receive with the obedience of faith many things which are [not in the Scripture."]{.class_3055}

A Fundamentalist tradition just as hellish as any Roman tradition is that [only]{.class_3055} the original manuscripts were []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1185.xhtml_page_180}["given by inspiration."]{.class_3054} That tradition is held to get rid of the [Authorized Version]{.class_3055} of the Holy Bible. It is held despite what the Scriptures say about the inspiration of the Scriptures. There isn't one verse in either Testament, in any language, in any translation, that says [only]{.class_3055} "the originals" were ["given by inspiration."]{.class_3054} That's a tradition held in order to get rid of the words of God that He gave you in your own language.

Now, in verses 10--12, Jesus gives a tradition that was being practiced in His day that violated the Mosaic Law as given in Exodus 20:12 and Deuteronomy 5:16, as compared with Exodus 21:17; Leviticus 20:9; and Deuteronomy 27:16 (see also Prov. 20:20, 30:17). And you might have known that the example the Lord gives hits those Pharisees right where it hurts the most: in the [pocket book,]{.class_3055} the [wallet]{.class_3055} (1 Tim. 6:10).

The case is this: a fellow sees how much it will cost him to take care of his parents in their old age, and he is covetous and wants the money. So he would go to the Rabbis and dedicate the money to Jehovah as a gift. When it came time to care for his parents, he could say he didn't have the money to take care of them because it was ["Corban"]{.class_3054}; i.e., a gift dedicated to the Lord. So the parents went uncared for until their deaths, and the son was guiltless because he had given the money to God.

Well, in the Old Testament, a gift could be redeemed, and you can read the law on that in Leviticus 27. The standard fee for redeeming any gift was the fifth part added to the value: 20%. So the priests and Rabbis got the use of the money while it was in their possession and the fee when it was redeemed, the son got a portion of his money back to spend on himself, and his momma and daddy [got the shaft.]{.class_3055}

And then Christ winds up this discourse on tradition with a great verse. This is how to take a Book that ["is quick, and powerful"]{.class_3054} (Heb. 4:12) and make it absolutely [useless]{.class_3055} to the recipient. ["Making the word of God of NONE EFFECT through your TRADITION"]{.class_3054} (vs. 13).

The word of God can be made ineffective through[ tradition.]{.class_3055} That goes for [Catholic]{.class_3055} tradition, [Protestant]{.class_3055} tradition, [Fundamentalist]{.class_3055} tradition, [Evangelical]{.class_3055} tradition, and [Baptist]{.class_3055} tradition. Any man-made tradition contrary to the words of Holy Scripture, that is []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1185.xhtml_page_181}exalted over the words of Holy Scripture, makes those words where they will have no effect on the one who reads them or hears them. The ["commandments of men"]{.class_3054} will ["turn"]{.class_3054} you ["from the truth"]{.class_3054} if you ["heed"]{.class_3054} them (Tit. 1:14).

14 And when he had called all the people unto him, he said unto them, Hearken unto me every one of you, and understand:

15 There is nothing from without a man, that entering into him can defile him: but the things which come out of him, those are they that defile the man.

16 If any man have ears to hear, let him hear.

["Hearken . . . and understand"]{.class_3054} (vs. 14). Notice, it is not just hearing the words Christ said; it is hearing with understanding. Hence, ["If any man have ears to hear, let him hear"]{.class_3054} (vs. 16). It is an ear that not only hears what God says, but receives it as God's very words and not man's (1 Thess. 2:13; 1 Cor. 2:13).

The point the Lord wants you to get here is that the source of sin is [inside]{.class_3055} you, not outside (vs. 15). If a man takes something inside of him that defiles him (like liquor, cigarettes, or drugs), he was already defiled before he took it inside of him. The sin originated in the man's heart before it manifested itself on the outside. If a fellow down in his heart didn't want to take drugs, he wouldn't put them in his body.

The source of sin, brethren, is "us'ns." It's not the external temptation that gets us; it's entertaining the possibilities of the sin in our hearts. Bob Jones Sr. said, "Behind every tragedy in character is a long process of wicked thinking." In other words, before a person ruins his life on the outside, he [debates]{.class_3055} the sin, [entertains]{.class_3055} the sin, [meditates]{.class_3055} on the sin, [contemplates]{.class_3055} the sin, and [mulls the sin over in his mind]{.class_3055} until he ends up doing it.

That Bible says, ["The heart is deceitful above all things, and desperately wicked"]{.class_3054} (Jer. 17:9). God's evaluation of ["EVERY imagination of the thoughts of ]{.class_3054}\[man's\][ heart"]{.class_3054} is that it is ["only evil continually"]{.class_3054} (Gen. 6:5), and it is that way ["from his YOUTH"]{.class_3054} (Gen. 8:21). In other words, there is no such thing as the "innate goodness of man" until it is ruined by his "environment." []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1185.xhtml_page_182}A man is wicked from the time he is a little baby (Psa. 51:5).

That can't be taught to any child in the federal school system; it is considered "child abuse." You'll warp little Johnny's and Suzie's "psyche" if you tell them they are basically evil and need to learn to control their baser instincts. No! My God, no! They must "express themselves."

17 And when he was entered into the house from the people, his disciples asked him concerning the parable.

18 And he saith unto them, Are ye so without understanding also? Do ye not perceive, that whatsoever thing from without entereth into the man, it cannot defile him;

19 Because it entereth not into his heart, but into the belly, and goeth out into the draught, purging all meats?

20 And he said, That which cometh out of the man, that defileth the man.

21 For from within, out of the heart of men, proceed evil thoughts, adulteries, fornications, murders,

22 Thefts, covetousness, wickedness, deceit, lasciviousness, an evil eye, blasphemy, pride, foolishness:

23 All these evil things come from within, and defile the man.

Just as they did back in 4:10, the disciples awaited the exposition of Christ's words when they were alone with Him, away from the crowds. They ["asked him concerning the parable"]{.class_3054} (vs. 17). [But Jesus didn't speak in any parable in verse 15.]{.class_3055} Go back and read verses 1--16 again. Do you see the word [parable]{.class_3055} or [parables]{.class_3055}? Neither one is there.

The disciples had the same problem Catholics have when it comes to interpreting the Bible. They took things [literal when they were figurative]{.class_3055} (see John 6:51--63), and they took things [figurative]{.class_3055} [when they were literal]{.class_3055} (Matt. 16:18).

Verse 18: ["Are ye so without understanding also?"]{.class_3054} Jesus is indignant with them. Back in verse 14, ["he had called ALL the people unto him"]{.class_3054} and told them, ["Hearken unto me EVERY ONE OF YOU, and understand."]{.class_3054}[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1185.xhtml_page_183} That commandment was given to everyone in the crowd, and the ones who should have taken heed to it the most---[His disciples]{.class_3055}---didn't.

Now, the defilement under discussion is moral defilement before God. It is not the defilement of the [body;]{.class_3055} it is the defilement of the [man himself]{.class_3055} (vs. 20). No meat a man eats defiles him morally ["Because it entereth not into his heart"]{.class_3054} (vs. 19). It goes through the digestive system and passes from the body in the excretory system.

The word ["draught"]{.class_3054} in verse 19 is a shortened form of ["draught house"]{.class_3054} (2 Kings 10:27). It is an [outhouse,]{.class_3055} a [privy.]{.class_3055} The idea is that food goes through your system and ends up in the toilet. The word is used differently than in Luke 5:4, where it means "to draw or pull out."

While certain meats were counted as unclean and abominable for that Jew by the commandment of God in the Old Testament (see Lev. 11), that had nothing to do with the morality of eating those creatures. Those dietary laws were given to set those Jews apart from the heathen nations (Deut. 14:2). In fact, those unclean meats were pictures of lost Gentiles, and when God cleansed the Gentiles by the sacrificial death of Christ so they could come to God for salvation through His Son, the Lord cleansed those meats as well (read Acts 10). That is why Paul says, ["I know, and am persuaded by the Lord Jesus, that there is nothing unclean of itself"]{.class_3054} (Rom. 14:14), and ["every creature of God is good, and nothing to be refused, if it be received with thanksgiving"]{.class_3054} (1 Tim. 4:4).

What does defile a man? It's ["the heart"]{.class_3054} (vs. 21). The problem is what a man [loves.]{.class_3055} That is why the Christian is commanded not to love the evil world system (1 John 2:15 cf. Gal. 1:4). If you love the lusts of your flesh, the lusts of your eyes, and the pride of your life, you aren't loving God (1 John 2:15--16).

Jesus lists what comes ["out of the heart of men"]{.class_3054} (vs. 21), and [none]{.class_3055} of it is good. It is [thirteen]{.class_3055} sins (watch out for the number thirteen---see Gen. 13:13 in that Commentary). Here is what is in man's heart.

1\. ["Evil thoughts."]{.class_3054} Mankind hasn't changed one iota since 3000 B.C. ["Every imagination of the thoughts of his heart"]{.class_3054} is still ["ONLY EVIL CONTINUALLY"]{.class_3054} (Gen. 6:5). God says, []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1185.xhtml_page_184}["the thoughts of man . . . are VANITY"]{.class_3054} (Psa. 94:11). One of the ["six things"]{.class_3054} God [hates]{.class_3055} is: ["An heart that deviseth wicked imaginations"]{.class_3054} (Prov. 6:18). ["The unrighteous man"]{.class_3054} is commanded to ["forsake"]{.class_3054} ["his thoughts"]{.class_3054} and ["return unto the LORD"]{.class_3054} (Isa. 55:7). In the New Testament, the Christian is commanded to bring ["into captivity every thought to the obedience of Christ."]{.class_3054} The picture is that of a conquering army hauling away a people in chains like the Assyrians did to the ten northern tribes and Nebuchadnezzar did to Judah and Benjamin.

2\. ["Adulteries."]{.class_3054} This is not only a reference to breaking the marriage bond physically (Rom. 7:2--3), it is a reference to [lust]{.class_3055} (Matt. 5:28) and [spiritual adultery.]{.class_3055} In the Old Testament, that was [idolatry]{.class_3055} (Ezek. 6:9); God called it ["a whoring"]{.class_3054} after other gods (e.g., Exod. 34:15--16; Deut. 31:16; Judg. 2:17; 1 Chron. 5:25). In the New Testament, it is associated with a Christian being ["a friend"]{.class_3054} of the lost world system (James 4:4).

3\. ["Fornications."]{.class_3054} That is an all-inclusive term for any sexual sin; like incest, homosexuality, and bestiality (see Lev. 18). It is part and parcel of the sinful flesh (Gal. 5:19; Col. 3:5), and the Christian is commanded to ["abstain"]{.class_3054} from it (1 Thess. 4:3) and ["flee"]{.class_3054} from it (1 Cor. 6:18).

4\. ["Murders."]{.class_3054} That's one of Rome's big two "mortal" sins; the other being adultery. Whenever a sinner wants to justify himself by his own self-righteousness, he will say, "Well, I never killed anyone." That is the response of someone trying to absolve himself of the fact that [his sins put Jesus Christ on the cross]{.class_3055} (1 Pet. 2:24). According to Jesus Christ, being angry with your brother ["WITHOUT A CAUSE"]{.class_3054} (get it! The new "Bibles" delete that phrase to make a sinner out of God's Son---see our comments on Mark 3:5) is the equivalent of murder, and so is hating your brother (1 John 3:15). According to that Book, murder is the product of [unfulfilled lust]{.class_3055} (James 4:2).

5\. ["Thefts."]{.class_3054} That's pretty plain. The commandment is ["Thou shalt not steal"]{.class_3054} (Exod. 20:15). Most people, if you asked them, would deny being a thief. That's because they overlook taking what they consider to be small things. To prove my point, I will ask a congregation to stand. Then I will say, "Sit down if you have ever []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1185.xhtml_page_185}taken an apple or a cherry from a neighbor's tree without his permission." Down South, it's: "Sit down if you have ever stolen a watermelon from someone's patch." I'll get nine-tenths of the congregation every time. I tell them, "I'm in ['a den of thieves' ]{.class_3054}(see Matt. 21:13)."

Now maybe you could pass that little test, but have you ever thought of this: Have you ever stolen [time]{.class_3055} from your employer? He paid you for eight hours of work, but you goofed off for a quarter hour off and on throughout the day. Have you ever stolen years off your momma's and daddy's lives by the way you lived? Have you ever stolen the bloom of purity off a young girl's cheek? I said that in a prison, and one of those inmates who denied (out loud!) being a thief said, "I never thought of that." Well, you had better think of it.

Are you sure you're not another John Dillinger or Jesse James? You say, "Of course not, Ruckman!" Well, how about this:

["Verily, verily, I say unto you, He that entereth not by the door into the sheepfold, but climbeth up some other way, the same is a THIEF and a ROBBER"]{.class_3054} (John 10:1).

If you are trusting any other way besides Jesus Christ to get you to Heaven (John 10:9), you are no better than Butch Cassidy, Bonnie and Clyde, Pretty Boy Floyd, or Baby Face Nelson.

6\. ["Covetousness."]{.class_3054} According to Paul, covetousness is a form of [idolatry]{.class_3055} (Col. 3:5). Jesus told you to [beware]{.class_3055} of it (Luke 12:15). You are to remove it from your life by being content with what God has given you (Heb. 13:5). You are not even to let it be ["named among you, as becometh saints"]{.class_3054} (Eph. 5:3).

7\. ["Wickedness."]{.class_3054} That's just generally living like the devil. It's a general disregard for righteousness, and a Christian is as susceptible to that as any lost person (cf. 1 Cor. 5:13; 2 Pet. 3:17)

8\. ["Deceit."]{.class_3054} The Bible says, ["The heart is deceitful above all things, and desperately wicked."]{.class_3054} Salvation doesn't change things when it comes to the old, Adamic nature. Old man Adam is still ["corrupt according to the deceitful lusts"]{.class_3054} (Eph. 4:22).

9.[ "Lasciviousness."]{.class_3054} Lasciviousness is unbridled or excessive lust and wickedness. It is best described in 1 Peter 4:3--4. It is the mark of an unsaved heathen who has thrown off all moral restraint. You wouldn't think a Christian could live like that, but he can (2 Cor. []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1185.xhtml_page_186}12:21). Christian, all you have to do is put down that Book, stop praying, stop exposing yourself to the preaching of the word of God, and you can end up committing any sin a lost person can commit.

10.[ "An evil eye."]{.class_3054} That's not some superstitious thing where someone glares at you and causes you bad luck. That's looking at something or someone with evil intent or lust (Deut. 15:9, 28:54, 56; Prov. 23:6, 28:22). It's called ["the lust of the eyes"]{.class_3054} in 1 John 2:16. A Christian should not only be careful at [what]{.class_3055} he looks (Psa. 101:3), but [how]{.class_3055} he looks at a thing.

11.[ "Blasphemy."]{.class_3054} That's one of the things a Christian is to ["put off,"]{.class_3054} along with ["filthy communication out of your mouth"]{.class_3054} (Col. 3:8). I had a problem with that when I first got saved. I had only been saved a month when a friend of mine, a Christian who had been saved longer than I had, told me right in the middle of a conversation, "Pete, that's got to go."

"What's got to go?" I asked, not knowing about what he was talking.

"That language, Pete; that language has GOT TO GO!"

I still didn't know what he meant, so I asked him, "What language?"

"Those [damns, hells,]{.class_3055} and [bastards,]{.class_3055} Pete; they have GOT TO GO!"

Honest to goodness, I had not even thought about what I was saying. After cussing out troops as a D.I. in the infantry, checking drinks at a bar, and playing drums in a dance band (occupations from my unsaved days), foul language had become second nature to me. I was not even conscious of the fact I was using it.

It took me a good while to weed those words out of my vocabulary, but they went. I must confess, however, that now and then, some of those old, familiar words do come to mind and, in one or two instances, have found expression. Like the Apostle Paul, sometimes, ["what I hate, that do I"]{.class_3054} (Rom. 7:15).

12\. ["Pride."]{.class_3054} That's the original sin (Ezek. 28:12--15; Isa. 14:12--13). It is thinking ["more highly"]{.class_3054} of yourself than you ["ought to think"]{.class_3054} (Rom. 12:3). It is the sin that is characteristic of the children of the Devil (Job 41:34). The child of God is commanded to humble himself before God so he can receive grace and exaltation from the Lord's hand (1 Pet. 5:5--6), and not his own.

13\. ["Foolishness."]{.class_3054}[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1185.xhtml_page_187} Uh oh! Why did He have to say that? It's not just filthy talk we are to avoid; it's ["foolish talking"]{.class_3054} and ["jesting, which are not convenient"]{.class_3054} (Eph. 5:4). It's humor with no point, made just to get a laugh. It degrades, over time, until the comedian is forced to say something dirty to get a laugh. It ends in ["mischievous madness"]{.class_3054} (Eccl. 10:13).

The Bible says, ["The thought of foolishness is sin"]{.class_3054} (Prov. 24:9). A fool is someone without good sense. Foolish thinking is dwelling on pointless, wicked, senseless, or carnal things. God called that rich man over in Luke 12:20 a ["fool"]{.class_3054} because he took no thought for eternity. Jesus called those disciples ["fools"]{.class_3054} over in Luke 24:25 because they didn't ["believe ALL that the prophets have spoken"]{.class_3054} (Luke 24:25). Those things are [foolishness;]{.class_3055} they corrupt a man, whether he is saved or lost.

Now look at those thirteen things. That is what God thinks of you good, fine folks. That is Adam, and he is the same in a saved person as he is in a lost person. The only difference between the two is that the saved have a ["new man"]{.class_3054} (Eph. 4:24; Col. 3:10) to offset the old. As a believer with the indwelling Holy Spirit of God, you have the ability to overcome those thirteen things that can defile you.

The example I give in my sermon "The Gospel Punchboard" is one given by a saved Indian whom a missionary asked to explain the two natures in the believer, to see if he understood the doctrine. That Indian said, "In me dwell white dog and black dog, and they always fight." "Well, which one wins?" asked the missionary. "Him whom I say, 'sic 'em' to," said the Indian. In Romans 6, that is yielding the members of your body to God ["as instruments of righteousness"]{.class_3054} (Rom. 6:13).

I'll tell you another way to figure out which "dog" will win---the one whom you [feed]{.class_3055} the most. If you feed that "black dog" with movies, TV, bad companions, rock music, magazines, newspapers, the "Internet," pornography, and the like, he will win. If you feed that "white dog" with Bible reading, prayer, Christian fellowship, good preaching, and hymns, he will win. Feed the new nature and starve the old.

24 And from thence he arose, and went into the borders []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1185.xhtml_page_188}of Tyre and Sidon, and entered into an house, and would have no man know it: but he could not be hid.

25 For a certain woman, whose young daughter had an unclean spirit, heard of him, and came and fell at his feet:

26 The woman was a Greek, a Syrophenician by nation; and she besought him that he would cast forth the devil out of her daughter.

27 But Jesus said unto her, Let the children first be filled: for it is not meet to take the children's bread, and to cast it unto the dogs.

28 And she answered and said unto him, Yes, Lord: yet the dogs under the table eat of the children's crumbs.

29 And he said unto her, For this saying go thy way; the devil is gone out of thy daughter.

30 And when she was come to her house, she found the devil gone out, and her daughter laid upon the bed.

Now, Jesus is as far north in the land of Israel as He can get without crossing over into Gentile territory. He told His disciples when He sent them out to preach: ["Go not into the way of the Gentiles . . . But go rather to the lost sheep of the house of Israel"]{.class_3054} (Matt. 10:5--6). So He doesn't go into Tyre and Sidon proper, because that is ["the way of the Gentiles." ]{.class_3054}He does go up to the border, though, and when He does, He encounters a Gentile woman.

["But he could not be hid"]{.class_3054} (vs. 24). That is a great statement that transcends the historical context in which it appears. You can't hide someone like Jesus Christ; He's too powerful. How are you going to hide Someone who can pull dead people out of graveyards? How are you going to "keep the lid" on Someone who can feed 5,000 people by creating food out of practically nothing? You won't.

Every time Jesus told someone, "Don't say anything about what I did for you," the news spread all over the countryside like wildfire. Jesus Christ always had crowds around Him for which He never sought. Once He started His ministry, He was always looking for some leisure time to be alone with His disciples and couldn't get it. If Christ is "hid" to anyone today, it is because someone is not speaking up for Him like he ought. When Paul went into an area, he didn't stop []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1185.xhtml_page_189}telling people about the Lord Jesus until everyone in that place had heard of Him (Acts 19:10, 20:31).

["For a certain woman, whose young daughter had an unclean spirit, heard of him, and came and fell at his feet"]{.class_3054} (vs. 25). This was nothing unusual in and of itself. People were always coming to Jesus for something (see Matt. 4:24). But this ["woman was a Greek"]{.class_3054} (vs. 26); that is, she was a Gentile (cf. Gal. 3:28; Col. 3:11). ["A Syrophenician by nation"]{.class_3054}; that is, she was from the Phoenician part of the province of Syria. When Alexander the Great conquered that part of the Middle East, he put it all into the province of Syria; that included Lebanon (Phoenicia) and Israel. But ethnically, this woman was a Canaanite (Matt. 15:22), because the Sidonians came from Ham's son Canaan (Gen. 10:15).

Even though this woman was a Gentile and an idolater, she was "converting" to get help for her daughter. You see that ["fell at his feet"]{.class_3054} in verse 25? That was an act of [worship]{.class_3055} (Matt. 15:25). Look at verse 28: she calls Jesus ["Lord."]{.class_3054} That Gentile woman believed what the Pharisees and Sadducees didn't: that Jesus Christ was God Almighty in the flesh.

Now Mark gives you an abbreviated account of Christ's encounter with this woman. The fuller account is found in Matthew 15:21--28. There we learn that the reason Jesus couldn't be hid in the house was because when this woman found out He was there, she didn't just come in and ask once politely. She ["cried unto him"]{.class_3054} (Matt. 15:22) just like blind Bartimaeus did in Mark 10:46--48. When Jesus ignored the woman, she went after the disciples (Matt. 15:23). In other words, she "made a scene" until she got everyone's attention.

Not only does Jesus treat this woman rudely by ignoring her, when He finally does answer her, look at how rudely He talks to her: ["Let the children first be filled: for it is not meet to take the children's bread, and to cast it unto the dogs"]{.class_3054} (vs. 27).

The children are, of course, the [Jews.]{.class_3055} They are ["the children of the kingdom"]{.class_3054} to whom the covenants and promises of the Old Testament were made (Matt. 8:12 cf. Rom. 9:4). The problem is, a lot of those ["children of the kingdom"]{.class_3054} rejected their King. So a bunch of Gentiles who received that King as their Saviour wind up in Heaven as ["sons of God"]{.class_3054} (John 1:12), while []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1185.xhtml_page_190}["the children of the kingdom,"]{.class_3054} who rejected the King, end up in [Hell]{.class_3055} (Matt. 8:11--12).

["The dogs,"]{.class_3054} here, is obviously a reference to [lost Gentiles,]{.class_3055} as it is in Psalm 22:16; Philippians 3:2; and Revelation 22:15. So the first insult to this woman is: "You're not a Jew. Go to the back of the bus and wait your turn." Rosa Parks, MLK, and the NAACP would have a fit! There's Jesus Christ insulting a whole race of people, just as Paul does to the same bunch in Titus 1:12 (see our comments on the verse in the [Bible Believer's Commentary on the Pastoral Epistles.]{.class_3055}).

But Jesus Christ doesn't just insult her race; He insults her gender. He's "sexist." This is a woman, and He calls her a [dog.]{.class_3055} Do you know what the word is for a female dog? How rude can you get?! He didn't treat that Gentile Centurion like that in Matthew 8:5--10.

Now this woman is about to do something no Pharisee or Sadducee could ever do: catch Christ in His words (see Mark 12). She does it by [agreeing with Him.]{.class_3055}

There is all this talk about repentance being necessary for salvation. Well, I'll tell you what repentance is, mainly: it's a [change of heart and mind]{.class_3055} about a thing. God never had to stop sinning a day in His life, but He certainly did [repent]{.class_3055} of some things (Gen. 6:6; Exod. 32:14; Judg. 2:18; 1 Sam. 15:35; etc.); that is, He changed His mind about something He had done or was intending to do. You can quit all kinds of sins and still wind up in Hell. You need to change your mind about your lost condition and agree with God in order to be saved.

"She stoops to conquer," as the English playwright Oliver Goldsmith wrote. This woman doesn't "raise her hackles" over what Christ said. She wants her daughter delivered, and she is willing to take any insult as long as her daughter is delivered from that devil.

She says, "All right, I'm a dog. I'm just a dirty, unclean, Gentile dog." This woman takes her place, and that place is under the Lord's table waiting for the crumbs that ["the children of the kingdom"]{.class_3054} let fall to the floor.

I'll tell you something else she did: she obligated the Lord to take care of her. Look at Matthew 15:27.

"And she said, Truth, Lord: yet the dogs eat of the crumbs which fall from THEIR MASTER'S table."

That heathen woman said, "You're right, Lord; I am a dog. But I am YOUR dog, and if I am YOUR dog, you have to take care of me."

That's how you catch the Lord in His words. He says, "You're nothing but a filthy sinner who deserves Hell." Your reply should be: "I sure am, Lord, but you said if I trusted your Son, you would forgive me of all that sin and clean me up, so I am going to believe what you said no matter what." You get what God said, and you believe it and hold Him to His word.

Jesus can't argue with what she said, so He grants the woman her petition. That woman's little girl got delivered, even though that woman wasn't a Jew. That woman was importunate in prayer (see Luke 11:8); she was humble; and she had faith. That will do the job.

31 And again, departing from the coasts of Tyre and Sidon, he came unto the sea of Galilee, through the midst of the coasts of Decapolis.

32 And they bring unto him one that was deaf, and had an impediment in his speech; and they beseech him to put his hand upon him.

33 And he took him aside from the multitude, and put his fingers into his ears, and he spit, and touched his tongue;

34 And looking up to heaven, he sighed, and saith unto him, Ephphatha, that is, Be opened.

35 And straightway his ears were opened, and the string of his tongue was loosed, and he spake plain.

36 And he charged them that they should tell no man: but the more he charged them, so much the more a great deal they published it;

37 And were beyond measure astonished, saying, He hath done all things well: he maketh both the deaf to hear, and the dumb to speak.

Jesus leaves ["the coasts of Tyre and Sidon"]{.class_3054} (vs. 31) and travels to the east side of the Sea of Galilee to the border of Decapolis (near where He delivered the maniac of Gadara---5:20). There, the people bring a fellow to him who is deaf and can't talk right because []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1185.xhtml_page_192}he can't hear anything.

Now Jesus does something very strange here. The people ask Jesus to lay His hand on the deaf fellow and heal him. But Jesus does much more than just lay hands on the man. He sticks His fingers in the guy's ears; He spits and touches the fellow's tongue. One might infer that He touched the man's tongue with His spit. When we get over to 8:23, Jesus is going to spit in a blind man's eyes to heal him. He then looks up to Heaven and with a sigh says, ["Ephphatha"]{.class_3054} (vs. 34). That is another Aramaism (see our comments on 5:41); it means ["Be opened."]{.class_3054}

Again, it was Christ's [words]{.class_3055} that effected the healing (see our comments on 1:41). He didn't have to touch a person to heal him. But Jesus did such things so the Jews could [see]{.class_3055} something (see what Jesus said in John 4:48).

Like the palsied man in 2:12, this fellow is healed immediately (["straightway"]{.class_3054}---right away; immediately). He can hear and speak plainly. This is a fulfillment of Isaiah 35:5--6.

"Then the eyes of the blind shall be opened, and the ears of the deaf shall be unstopped. Then shall the lame man leap as an hart, and the tongue of the dumb sing . . . ."

Verse 36 is that old familiar scene from 1:44--45 and 5:43 (see our comments on those verses).

["And were beyond measure astonished"]{.class_3054} (vs. 37). As they were in 1:27, 2:12, and 5:42.

["He hath done ALL THINGS WELL."]{.class_3054} That can't be said of any other man, woman, or child on earth. You think Mohammed did ["all things well"]{.class_3054}? Why, he took the virginity of a nine-year-old girl as a middle-aged man; he stole his son's wife and claimed "Allah" told him it was all right; he ordered the executions (in some of the most horrific ways possible) of anyone who made fun of him. You call that sort of stuff doing ["all things well"]{.class_3054}?!

Buddha didn't do ["all things well."]{.class_3054} That fellow left his wife for thirty years to sit under a Bo tree. The lazy bum never did a lick of work a day in his life. He went from a privileged Prince to a lazy beggar without ever working.

["He maketh both the deaf to hear, and the dumb to speak"]{.class_3054}---and [the dead to live,]{.class_3055} I might add. No medicine, no []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1185.xhtml_page_193}radiation, no surgery, no therapy. He spoke and it was so.

And when it comes to salvation, He does ["all things"]{.class_3054} just as ["well."]{.class_3054} He can take a murderous blasphemer and turn him into the greatest missionary who ever lived (Paul). He can take a demon-possessed prostitute and make her a shining trophy of His grace (Mary Magdalene). And he can take a cussing, fornicating, proud, arrogant, drunken infantryman and dance band drummer, and clean him up, give him the Book, and make him a preacher of the Gospel (ME!).[ "He hath done all things well."]{.class_3054}

[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1326.xhtml}

# Chapter 8 {.heading_97}

8:1 In those days the multitude being very great, and having nothing to eat, Jesus called his disciples unto him, and saith unto them,

2 I have compassion on the multitude, because they have now been with me three days, and have nothing to eat:

3 And if I send them away fasting to their own houses, they will faint by the way: for divers of them came from far.

4 And his disciples answered him, From whence can a man satisfy these men with bread here in the wilderness?

5 And he asked them, How many loaves have ye? And they said, Seven.

6 And he commanded the people to sit down on the ground: and he took the seven loaves, and gave thanks, and brake, and gave to his disciples to set before them; and they did set them before the people.

7 And they had a few small fishes: and he blessed, and commanded to set them also before them.

8 So they did eat, and were filled: and they took up of the broken meat that was left seven baskets.

9 And they that had eaten were about four thousand: and he sent them away.

This is a different feeding than the one about which we read in chapter 6. This is a second feeding that took place, and this time, the Lord feeds 4,000 men (vs. 9).

Now, not only does the feeding of the 5,000 appear in all four Gospels, but the miracle is repeated with the feeding of the 4,000 and recorded in Matthew (Matt. 15:32--39) and Mark (here). As I told you back under my comments on 6:42--43, this is a type of the Lord feeding the Jewish remnant three-and-a-half years in the wilderness during the Tribulation (Rev. 12:14). That Book is [always]{.class_3055} more concerned with the events connected with Christ's Second Coming than it is with the events of His First Coming. Of course, []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1326.xhtml_page_195}thank God for the First Coming; that is where I get in. But God the Father is more concerned with the day His Son gets what He deserves than He is with the day wicked men rejected His Son and murdered Him. In the Tribulation, the Lord is going to save a remnant of His chosen people and feed them miraculously, just as He did in both Testaments.

As I said above, this is a second, different feeding than the feeding of the 5,000, and we should note the differences. The first is that this multitude had been following Christ for ["three days"]{.class_3054} (vs. 2), not just one, as in chapter 6. While they may have started out with their "picnic lunches," by the third day, the food was gone. Since many of them came from a far distance, Christ was afraid many of them would faint from hunger before they got home to get something to eat.

In verse 5, Christ asks them, ["How many loaves have ye?"]{.class_3054} as He did back in 6:38. But this time, they don't have to ["go and see"]{.class_3054} to answer (see 6:38). The implication is that the food came from the disciples themselves and not from someone in the crowd, like the boy over in John 6:9.

Another difference is the amount of food with which Jesus fed the multitude. Before, it was ["five barley loaves, and two small fishes"]{.class_3054} (John 6:9). Here in verse 5, all you are told is that there are seven loaves; verse 7 adds: ["And they had a few small fishes."]{.class_3054} So although the food with which Christ had to work was more than before, it still could not feed the multitude there, from a human standpoint.

Unlike the account of the feeding of the 5,000, no ranks of fifty and a hundred are ordered by the Lord. Verse 6 simply states: ["And he commanded the multitude to sit down on the ground"]{.class_3054} (the same is stated in Matt. 15:35).

Whereas at the feeding of the 5,000 twelve baskets of "leftovers" were taken up, here only seven baskets remained. However, from the two different Greek words used, we may infer that more was left over here than before. In 6:43, the ["baskets"]{.class_3054} are [kophinous]{.class_3055}---small baskets. Here in verse 8, the ["baskets"]{.class_3054} are [spuridas]{.class_3055}---large hamper-size baskets.

But we didn't really need Greek to let us know beyond any []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1326.xhtml_page_196}shadow of a doubt that once more Jesus Christ has exercised His creative power to multiply food. There shouldn't have been enough to feed a dozen people more than a little bit, much less feed over 4,000 and have seven large baskets of leftovers.

Having already seen this miracle once, the question the disciples pose in verse 4 is baffling: ["From whence can a man satisfy these men with bread here in the wilderness?"]{.class_3054} Why, from the same place that same Man did two chapters earlier. The only explanation for the disciples' lack of faith, even after seeing all they have, is the hardness of their hearts (vs. 17).

In closing, verse 9 says the multitude was ["about four thousand,"]{.class_3054} but as with the feeding of the 5,000, this number did not include ["women and children"]{.class_3054} (Matt. 15:38). So undoubtedly, the number is somewhere between 8,000 and 12,000, all told.

The devotional, spiritual lesson we can take from the passage is that before the multitude could be fed, the bread had to be [broken]{.class_3055} (vs. 6). Before that palsied man back in chapter 2 could be healed, that roof had to be [broken up]{.class_3055} (2:4). For the spikenard to be poured on Christ's head, the box that held it had to be [broken open]{.class_3055} (14:3).

Do you get the message? If the Lord is ever going to use you, if He is ever going to get the fruit He wants from you, you will have to be [broken]{.class_3055} first. Do you know one of the reasons the Lord was able to use the Apostle Paul the way He did? He [broke]{.class_3055} Paul. He knocked him down on the road to Damascus and blinded him; then He gave him ["a thorn in the flesh"]{.class_3054} (2 Cor. 12:7).

Brethren, I don't like it any more than you do, but if ["patience"]{.class_3054} is going to ["have her perfect work"]{.class_3054} in you (James 1:4), then you are going to have to go through some ["tribulations"]{.class_3054} (Rom. 5:3). If you are going to bear fruit for Christ, you are going to have to be purged (John 15:2).

10 And straightway he entered into a ship with his disciples, and came into the parts of Dalmanutha.

11 And the Pharisees came forth, and began to question with him, seeking of him a sign from heaven, tempting him.

12 And he sighed deeply in his spirit, and saith, Why doth this generation seek after a sign? verily I say unto you, There shall no sign be given unto this generation.

13 And he left them, and entering into the ship again departed to the other side.

Whereas the feeding of the 5,000 took place on the north side of the Sea of Galilee, the feeding of the 4,000 took place in Decapolis on the eastern shore of the Sea of Galilee (see 7:31). One could speculate that the multitudes who followed Jesus for three days in Decapolis were the result of the witness of the maniac of Gadara who had been delivered by Jesus (see 5:20).

Verse 11: ["And the Pharisees came forth, and began to question with him, seeking of him a sign from heaven, TEMPTING HIM."]{.class_3054} Now ["the Jews require a sign"]{.class_3054} (1 Cor. 1:22) because that is how their nation began (Exod. 4:8). But notice that these Pharisees are not seeking a sign from Jesus to believe on Him; they are seeking a sign in order to [tempt]{.class_3055} Him. They are trying ["to catch him in his words"]{.class_3054} (12:13 cf. 12:15).

Interestingly enough, this is the same thing they did after the feeding of the 5,000 (see John 6:30). That time, Jesus gave them all the words they needed to offend them so they would leave (John 6:60--66). After all, they had just witnessed one of the greatest miracles in the ministry of Christ, and that wasn't a "sign" enough for them.

Here in Mark, Jesus had cast out devils, had healed hundreds (if not thousands), had calmed a storm, had raised the dead, had created food in the wilderness out of basically thin air---TWICE(!); and they want ["a sign from heaven"]{.class_3054}? What do you call all of that?! No wonder He says they weren't going to get one (vs. 12); they had [rejected all the signs]{.class_3055} they had been given.

Now, verse 12 is a perfect example (along with 10:11) of how [not]{.class_3055} to interpret the Bible. You see, [Mark 8:12 is not everything Jesus said.]{.class_3055} One of the rules of Biblical hermeneutics (how to properly interpret the Bible) is never interpret a complete statement by an incomplete statement. Always interpret an incomplete statement by a complete statement.

The complete statement is given in Matthew 16:4---[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1326.xhtml_page_198}["there shall no sign be given unto it, BUT THE SIGN OF THE PROPHET JONAS."]{.class_3054} It's a reference to His death, burial, and resurrection (Matt. 12:39--40).

The one sign that was above every other miracle performed by Christ was Him coming out of the grave on His own after three days and nights. The problem with these lost religionists was [no sign]{.class_3055} was going to convince any man who rejected Christ's words (John 12:37--38). When a sinless man arose from the grave, they spoke against it (Luke 2:34) and hired men to lie about it (Matt. 28:11--15). So when it comes time for the Jews to accept the Antichrist (John 5:43), do you know what God does? He allows Satan to perform ["signs and lying wonders"]{.class_3054} to deceive them (2 Thess. 2:9) and even sends them ["strong delusion, that they should believe a lie"]{.class_3054} (2 Thess. 2:11). Why? Because they rejected the one sign that was given to convince them and [save them]{.class_3055} (Rom. 6:3--11).

With that thought to mull over, Christ ["left them, and entering into the ship again departed to the other side"]{.class_3054} (vs. 13). ["The other side,"]{.class_3054} in this case, turns out to be the ["Bethsaida"]{.class_3054} on the northeast side of the Sea of Galilee (vs. 22), near where He had fed the 5,000 (see our comments on Luke 9:10 in that Commentary).

["And he left them."]{.class_3054} We understand, of course, that it is talking about the physical act of leaving to go somewhere else. But before the events of this chapter, the Lord had already told His disciples concerning the Pharisees: ["Let them alone: they be blind leaders of the blind"]{.class_3054} (Matt. 15:14). Back in the Old Testament, God said of the ten northern tribes: ["Ephraim is joined to idols: let him alone"]{.class_3054} (Hos. 4:17).

In other words, one of the worst things God will do to a sinner who rejects what He says is to leave that sinner to his own devices (Psa. 81:11--12; Rom. 1:24--27). "Why don't you just leave me alone, God?" "OK; see you at the judgment." The Holy Spirit stops dealing with the sinner; He stops putting Christians across his path to witness to him. No tracts, no preaching, no Bible; God just lets the sinner do whatever he wants to do until he dies and ends up in Hell.

These Pharisees didn't want the truth. They weren't going to []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1326.xhtml_page_199}accept the truth if they got it. If they got it, they were seeking for a way to get rid of it. So Jesus ["left them,"]{.class_3054} and He told His disciples to ["Let them alone"]{.class_3054} and not deal with them.

That's what's going on in America right now. This country has kicked God out of its public schools. It has told its chaplains to keep their mouths shut when it comes to Jesus Christ and the Bible. It has thumbed its nose at God's laws. So the Lord has been slowly taking away the Bible-believing witness and substituting a bunch of teachers and preachers who do nothing but tickle the ears and tell people how good they are. He has allowed the apostates to flood the Bible market with over 323 translations that are no more the word of God than Mother Goose's Nursery Rhymes or Grimm's Fairy Tales. Why? He is leaving this country alone so it will [damn itself.]{.class_3055} The truth is still out there somewhere, but a sinner will have to be awfully serious and diligent to find it.

14 Now the disciples had forgotten to take bread, neither had they in the ship with them more than one loaf.

15 And he charged them, saying, Take heed, beware of the leaven of the Pharisees, and of the leaven of Herod.

16 And they reasoned among themselves, saying, It is because we have no bread.

17 And when Jesus knew it, he saith unto them, Why reason ye, because ye have no bread? perceive ye not yet, neither understand? have ye your heart yet hardened?

18 Having eyes, see ye not? and having ears, hear ye not? and do ye not remember?

19 When I brake the five loaves among five thousand, how many baskets full of fragments took ye up? They say unto him, Twelve.

20 And when the seven among four thousand, how many baskets full of fragments took ye up? And they said, Seven.

21 And he said unto them, How is it that ye do not understand?

The disciples are in the boat headed toward the north shore of []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1326.xhtml_page_200}the Sea of Galilee when they discover they haven't brought enough food for all of them for the journey. They only have ["one loaf"]{.class_3054} to split between thirteen people (vs. 14). Christ uses this to warn the disciples.

["Beware of the leaven of the Pharisees, and of the leaven of Herod"]{.class_3054} (vs. 15). ["Leaven,"]{.class_3054} in the Bible, is [never]{.class_3055} a picture of anything good. So in the Parable of the Leaven, in Matthew 13:33 and Luke 13:21, the leaven isn't the Gospel spreading through the "lump" until the whole world is evangelized.

Those Jews were to get all the leaven [out]{.class_3055} of their houses during Passover and the Feast of Unleavened Bread (Exod. 12:15, 19, 13:7), and [no]{.class_3055} leaven was to be connected with any sacrifice made at the altar (Exod. 34:25; Lev. 2:11, 6:17). Why not? Because it represented [sin]{.class_3055} (1 Cor. 5:6--8). Over in Galatians 5:4--9, it represents [adding your own self-righteousness to the righteousness of Christ,]{.class_3055} obtained by faith in His vicarious atonement for sin, [to salvation.]{.class_3055}

Basically, ["leaven,"]{.class_3054} from a spiritual perspective, is mixing two things together that don't belong. Another term for it is [adulterate.]{.class_3055} When something is adulterated, the original substance is made poorer and inferior by what is added to it.

The Bible term is [adultery.]{.class_3055} When a man breaks the marriage bond by joining his body to another woman, or a wife joins her body to another man, the new relationship is inferior to the one God established between the husband and wife. The same thing goes for [spiritual adultery]{.class_3055} (James 4:4). A Christian can't mix the things of this world with the things of God without damaging his relationship with Jesus Christ.

The history of this world, since Genesis 11, has been to take all the different nations and different religions and different races and different cultures, and get them all together in one gray, mongrelized mass. That's [leaven;]{.class_3055} that's [sin.]{.class_3055} You can't get a bunch of sinners together without getting rid of the Book first.

The Lord will show you what He thinks of that by coming down at the Battle of Armageddon and busting up the whole depraved mess. That's God's way of saying, "You can't get together [without me."]{.class_3055} To that I say, ["Even so, come, Lord Jesus"]{.class_3054} (Rev. 22:20). It won't be too soon for me; as a matter of fact, I wonder why He hasn't come a []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1326.xhtml_page_201}lot sooner.

Having heard Christ's warning against the Pharisees and Herod, the disciples try to figure out about what the Lord is talking. In contrast to what they did in 7:17, where they took something figurative that Christ meant literally, here they take something literal that Jesus meant figuratively (see our remarks under 7:17). ["And they reasoned among themselves, saying, It is because we have no bread"]{.class_3054} (vs. 16).

Their problem was the same as in 6:52 (which see)---a hard heart (vs. 17). After Jesus fed over 5,000 people with only five loaves of bread and two fish, and over 4,000 people with only seven loaves of bread and a few fish, did they honestly think He would have any problem feeding thirteen people with only one loaf of bread (vss. 19--20)? They are acting just like the masses from whom Christ hid His doctrine by parables (vs. 18 cf. 4:12).

Now, Mark ends Christ's words to the disciples with verse 21, but just as in verse 12, the revelation is incomplete. You say, "Why does the Holy Spirit do that?" To make you [search]{.class_3055} the Scriptures to see what's so and what's not (Acts 17:11); to make you [study]{.class_3055} the Scriptures to prove yourself a good workman to the Lord when it comes to rightly dividing that Book (2 Tim. 2:15).

Mark doesn't tell you what Christ means by ["the leaven of the Pharisees, and . . . the leaven of Herod,"]{.class_3054} but Matthew and Luke do. The account in Matthew says, ["beware of the leaven of the Pharisees and of the Sadducees"]{.class_3054} (Matt. 16:6). Those are [religious leaders,]{.class_3055} so the disciples finally understand that the Lord was warning about ["the DOCTRINE of the Pharisees and of the Sadducees"]{.class_3054} (Matt. 16:12).

What was the problem with the doctrine of the Sadducees? They were theological [Liberals:]{.class_3055}[ "the Sadducees say that there is no resurrection, neither angel, nor spirit"]{.class_3054} (Acts 23:8). They outright [denied]{.class_3055} the word of God.

Now that wasn't the Pharisees' problem; they believed all those things (Acts 23:8). But they were [hypocrites]{.class_3055} about what they believed (see Luke 12:1 and comments in that Commentary). They would ["say, and do not"]{.class_3054} (Matt. 23:3). They would add things to the Scriptures to make them ineffective (see our comments on 7:13). They were like []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1326.xhtml_page_202}[Catholics.]{.class_3055}

Here in Mark, it is not only ["the leaven of the Pharisees,"]{.class_3054} but ["the leaven of Herod."]{.class_3054} What was Herod's problem? Well, Christ called him a ["fox"]{.class_3054} (Luke 13:32); foxes are known for being crafty and sly. Like the Jews of Jeremiah's day, he was ["wise to do evil, but to do good ]{.class_3054}\[he had\][ no knowledge"]{.class_3054} (Jer. 4:22). As we saw back in chapter 6, Herod [feared man]{.class_3055} and practiced [reformation without repentance or salvation]{.class_3055} (see our comments on 6:20).

So the follower of Jesus Christ is to beware theological Liberalism, hypocrisy, living for the flesh, the fear of man, and mere reformation. To those, you can add Paul's warnings in Philippians 3:2 and Colossians 2:8 (which see).

22 And he cometh to Bethsaida; and they bring a blind man unto him, and besought him to touch him.

23 And he took the blind man by the hand, and led him out of the town; and when he had spit on his eyes, and put his hands upon him, he asked him if he saw ought.

24 And he looked up, and said, I see men as trees, walking.

25 After that he put his hands again upon his eyes, and made him look up: and he was restored, and saw every man clearly.

26 And he sent him away to his house, saying, Neither go into the town, nor tell it to any in the town.

This is one of two blind men in the book of Mark who are healed; the other is blind Bartimaeus in 10:46--52. Now, there are four accounts given in detail in the Gospels of Jesus healing blind men, and every one of them differs in the way Jesus heals the blind men. In Matthew 9:27--30, two blind men are healed by Jesus touching their eyes, which is undoubtedly why the blind man here in Mark 8 ["besought him to touch him" ]{.class_3054}(vs. 22). There are two other blind men healed in Matthew 20:29--34, one of whom was Bartimaeus (Luke 18:35--43 cf. Mark 10:46--52). While Matthew does mention the fact that Jesus did touch their eyes to heal them (Matt. 20:34), Mark and Luke emphasize the fact that the healing was accomplished by Jesus [speaking]{.class_3055}[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1326.xhtml_page_203} to them (Mark 10:52; Luke 18:42--43). In John 9:6--7, Jesus made clay by spitting on the ground and put the clay on the blind man's eyes; then he had the blind man ["wash in the pool of Siloam."]{.class_3054} Here in Mark 8:23, Jesus spits on the man's eyes and puts His hands on the man. (There is a fifth account in Matt. 12:22 of a man who is both ["blind, and dumb"]{.class_3054} because he is ["possessed with a devil"]{.class_3054} whom Jesus heals, but no details are given other than Jesus ["healed him."]{.class_3054})

I have often thought that if those men whom Jesus healed of their blindness ever met, they would leave in four denominations: the Touchites, the Speakites, the Spittites, and the Clayites. The important thing about all of the healing is not [how]{.class_3055} it was done but [Who]{.class_3055} did it. If you are saved, the important thing is not your [experience,]{.class_3055} but your [Saviour.]{.class_3055} Every Christian may have a different experience when it comes to the circumstances surrounding his conversion, but I guarantee you that every one of them has one thing in common: [they all trusted the same Saviour.]{.class_3055} The main thing about salvation is not whether you experienced what I experienced when I got saved. The important thing is did you put your complete faith and trust in the Lord Jesus Christ and His death, burial, and resurrection according to the Scriptures for salvation? If you didn't, no amount of tears, shouting, running the bases, speaking in tongues, or euphoria means you are saved. The Devil will give you any feeling you want if it will keep you from trusting Christ.

Now, Jesus leads this blind man by the hand out of town. Then He spits ["on his eyes"]{.class_3054} (vs. 23). You say, "Why did He do that, Ruckman?" I have no idea in the world, man. When you find out, you let me know.

Brethren, I have read that Book through over 150 times (that's when I stopped counting), and I have listened to it on tape and CD over and over again. There are things in that Book about which I know no more than the first time I read them. This is one.

Spitting is a sign of contempt (Num. 12:14; Deut. 25:9; Job 30:10; Isa. 50:6). I suppose, if I had to make a guess, that Jesus did that for the same reason He insulted that Syrophenician woman back in Mark 7:27. He wanted to see if this fellow meant business about getting healed. When we get to chapter 10, we are going to read about a fellow who asks Jesus what to do to ["inherit eternal life"]{.class_3054}[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1326.xhtml_page_204} (10:17). When Jesus finally tells him to sell everything he's got, give the money to the poor, and follow Him, that guy walks away sad because he loved his money more than he wanted to ["inherit eternal life." ]{.class_3054}He wasn't serious about doing what it took to get what he wanted. This blind man could have taken offence at what Jesus did, but he didn't. He stood there and took it.

After Jesus spit in the guy's eyes and laid hands on him, ["he asked him if he saw ought."]{.class_3054} Why? Didn't this blind man receive his sight ["immediately"]{.class_3054} (Matt. 20:34; Mark 10:52; Luke 18:43)? No, as a matter of fact, he [didn't.]{.class_3055} He saw something, but not clearly:[ "I see men as trees, walking"]{.class_3054} (vs. 24).

Incomplete healing. That's the excuse of all fake Charismatic healers for their "misfires." Of course, they will blame it on the lack of faith of the one seeking healing: "You didn't hang onto your healing." Of course, Jesus didn't leave this man in this state. He finished the job, and the fellow didn't come by at the next tent meeting to get "rehealed" because he had lost it.

You say, "Why wasn't his healing immediate, Ruckman? Why was it 'progressive'?" Beats the fire out of me; I don't know. When you don't know the "whys" and "wherefores" behind a verse of Scripture, don't go into a panic. Just believe and accept what you read, and keep on reading. Pray about it. Maybe the Lord will give you the answer, and maybe He won't. If He doesn't, that doesn't mean the passage is any less true; it just means you don't understand everything in that Book. If you did, it would mean someone with no more sense than you wrote it. (That's what Sam Jones, the old-time Methodist evangelist, said when someone asked him how he felt about not understanding everything in the Bible.)

["I see men AS trees."]{.class_3054} This is the Holy Spirit using ["similitudes"]{.class_3054} to teach you something (Hos. 12:10). God likens men to dogs (Psa. 22:16; Isa. 56:10--11; Matt. 7:6, 15:26--27; Mark 7:27--28; Phil. 3:2; Rev. 22:15). So if you want a good sermon on men, look up some things about dogs, because that is one of the similitudes used by God to compare them.

Here, men are likened to trees. So you have a "family tree"---your lineage and relatives. Men die like trees (Isa. 1:30, 64:6), and they []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1326.xhtml_page_205}will experience a resurrection like trees. Every Winter a tree "dies" and loses all its leaves. You would swear that tree was dead, but come Spring, it buds out and fills with leaves again.
