That's a picture of death and resurrection, and Song of Solomon 2:8--13 places the time of Christ coming for His Bride in the Spring (see our comments on that passage in that Commentary). When that happens, a resurrection takes place (1 Thess. 4:16).

Like trees, men bring forth fruit, either good or bad (Matt. 7:15--18). If a tree doesn't bear good fruit, it is cut down and burned (Matt. 3:10, 7:19), just like a man (see Matt. 13:38--42). Jesus said a Christian is purged to bring forth more fruit just as a tree is pruned (John 15:2).

After Jesus puts His hands on the man a second time, the healing is complete, with the man seeing ["clearly"]{.class_3054} (vs. 25). Jesus sends the man back to his house with the stipulation not to go into the town and tell anybody what had happened. Mark doesn't tell you, here, whether that fellow followed Jesus' instructions or not. If things went like they did elsewhere in the Gospel of Mark (see 1:45, 2:1--2, our comments on 5:43, 6:54--56, 7:36), then he probably [didn't.]{.class_3055}

27 And Jesus went out, and his disciples, into the towns of Caesarea Philippi: and by the way he asked his disciples, saying unto them, Whom do men say that I am?

28 And they answered, John the Baptist: but some say, Elias; and others, One of the prophets.

29 And he saith unto them, But whom say ye that I am? And Peter answereth and saith unto him, Thou art the Christ.

30 And he charged them that they should tell no man of him.

31 And he began to teach them, that the Son of man must suffer many things, and be rejected of the elders, and of the chief priests, and scribes, and be killed, and after three days rise again.

32 And he spake that saying openly. And Peter took him, and began to rebuke him.

33 But when he had turned about and looked on his []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1326.xhtml_page_206}disciples, he rebuked Peter, saying, Get thee behind me, Satan: for thou savourest not the things that be of God, but the things that be of men.

Caesarea Philippi was a city all the way up in northern Israel at the base of Mt. Hermon. It was named after Tiberius Caesar and Philip the tetrarch of Trachonitus (see Luke 3:1). ["The towns"]{.class_3054} of verse 27 would be the villages outside the city walls, like you read all throughout Joshua 15 and 19. Matthew 16:13 calls them ["the coasts of Caesarea Philippi."]{.class_3054} We would call them "suburbs" today.

Verse 27 says, ["by the way, he asked his disciples."]{.class_3054} In other words, they were "pulled off" somewhere along the highway. Mark doesn't tell you why they were off on the side of the road, but Luke does: ["he was alone praying"]{.class_3054} (vs. 18).

At some point, Jesus interrupts His prayer meeting to ask His disciples this question:[ "Whom do men say that I am?"]{.class_3054} The question isn't asked so much for information as it is to contrast how the masses view Jesus as opposed to how His disciples view Him (see vs. 29).

The answers the disciples give to that question stand in stark contrast to the Liberal and the modern Fundamentalist view of Jesus Christ, and we refer you to our comments on 6:14--15 for a discussion on those answers. What I want to point out here is that surely the [eyewitnesses]{.class_3055} to the ministry of Jesus knew more about what He was like than these hot-house, incubator babies in Christian Colleges and Seminaries. The last thing of which Jesus Christ reminded those who saw Him and heard Him was some soft, effeminate, mild-mannered milquetoast. He wasn't some Pope or Episcopalian rector. He wasn't like some professor at the "World's Most Unusual University" who would never say or do anything to embarrass such a "godly institution." He wasn't some politician campaigning to get votes.

Jesus Christ reminded those who personally heard Him teach and preach of some of the roughest characters in that Book. If you are like Stewart Custer, Pope John Paul II, Joel Osteen, Rick Warren, Norman Vincent Peale, and Robert Schuller, you are [not]{.class_3055} "Christ-[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1326.xhtml_page_207}like." The apostle whom God set as a pattern for you to follow as a believer (1 Tim. 1:16; 1 Cor. 11:1) had speech that his listeners thought was ["rude"]{.class_3054} (2 Cor. 11:6) and ["contemptible"]{.class_3054} (2 Cor. 10:10). That's the "sweet spirit of Christ" you are to follow.

Now back in 6:14--15, all you had was the popular view of Christ's identity. Here, Jesus corners the disciples for their opinion. After following Him closely for nearly three years, who did they think He was? Peter answers for all of them: ["Thou art the Christ"]{.class_3054} (vs. 29). ["Christ"]{.class_3054} is the Greek word for Messiah (John 1:41), the One whom God anointed to be the Saviour and King of Israel. That is how Peter was first introduced to Jesus back in John 1:41; three years of being with Jesus hasn't diminished that view one bit.

Luke is more specific; he records Peter as saying, ["The Christ of God"]{.class_3054} (Luke 9:20). Why is that important? Because, according to Jesus Christ, there are ["Christs"]{.class_3054} who are NOT God's Christ (Matt. 24:5, 24). Satan's false Christ is the Antichrist. According to the Apostle John, his "forerunners" are here ["now,"]{.class_3054} and they are ["many"]{.class_3054} (1 John 2:18). There have been 233 of them in one church alone since A.D. 300 (the Popes).

Matthew 16:16 adds ["the Son of the living God"]{.class_3054} to Peter's confession. This is the same profession Peter made after many of Christ's followers left over what Jesus said in His Bread of Life discourse (John 6:66--69). Peter and the other apostles didn't just ["believe"]{.class_3054} Jesus was the ["Christ, the Son of the living God,"]{.class_3054} they were ["sure"]{.class_3054} of it. While it is true that the hardness of their hearts often tarnished their faith, they knew full well that no one but Jesus had ["the words of eternal life."]{.class_3054}

According to the Lord Jesus Christ, this was a divine revelation from God the Father (Matt. 16:17). This is the same revelation Paul received directly from God (Gal. 1:15--16 cf. Acts 9:19--20). That is not a revelation that is peculiar to just Peter and Paul, though; it is available to any sinner who will trust Jesus Christ for eternal life (1 John 4:2, 13--15, 5:1, 9--13).

["And he charged them that they should tell no man of him"]{.class_3054} (vs. 30); that is, that He was the Christ, the Messiah (Matt. 16:20; Luke 9:21). Why not? Because it would have been considered insurrection against Rome. The title of [Christ]{.class_3055} was a political one. When the wise men showed up at Jerusalem, they asked, []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1326.xhtml_page_208}["Where is he that is born KING of the Jews?"]{.class_3054} (Matt. 2:2). That was why Herod tried to have Jesus killed. The accusation the Jews made against Jesus when they brought Him to Pilate was that He claimed to be ["Christ a KING"]{.class_3054} (Luke 23:2), and so the question Pontius Pilate put to Jesus was: ["Art thou the KING of the Jews?"]{.class_3054} (John 18:33). When Pilate tried to release Jesus, the Jewish leadership blackmailed Pilate with:[ "If thou let this man go, thou art not Caesar's friend: whosoever maketh himself a KING speaketh against Caesar"]{.class_3054} (John 19:12).

So Jesus didn't want the disciples going around proclaiming His title of Messiah and Christ lest it appear to the army of occupation that they were trying to stir up political dissent against Rome (see Acts 5:36--37). The testimony of the apostles whom Jesus Himself appointed to preach His message would have been considered a more authoritative testimony than just the opinions of the masses.

["And he began to teach them . . ."]{.class_3054} (vs. 31). For the first time in Christ's ministry, He reveals details of what is called the ["passion"]{.class_3054} of Christ (Acts 1:3); that is, the events surrounding His death, burial, and resurrection. What is given is absolutely astounding, from a human point of view. Here is a man who can give six details of His own death, and they all come to pass right on the money. Luke 18:32 gives five additional details not mentioned here. Could [you]{.class_3055} do that? If you committed suicide, you [might]{.class_3055}---and I emphasize that word [might]{.class_3055}---get one detail right, and then again, you [might not.]{.class_3055} There has been many a man or woman who attempted suicide only to have it [fail.]{.class_3055} One fellow tried shooting himself in the head, and the bullet traveled around the inside of his cranium and out the other side. He survived. If you did succeed, it would be what is called a "self-fulfilling prophecy"---you made it happen yourself.

Jesus gives eleven details of the end of His earthly life over which He had no control, from a human standpoint. He's telling His own future accurately to the "T." That is the mark of a true prophet (cf. Deut. 18:22).

Mohammed couldn't prophesy his way out of a wet paper bag. He was poisoned by a Jewish woman whose husband and family he had murdered. He never "saw it coming" until it was too late. He prayed []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1326.xhtml_page_209}for healing, got "Gabriel" to pray to Allah for his healing, laid his "healing hand" on himself, took the best medicine he knew, AND HE DIED ANYWAY.

Mohammed was no prophet. He couldn't prophesy a woman would poison him, that the poison would break down his health over a period of two years, that no medicine would cure him, or that Allah wouldn't listen to any prayers for his healing. He was completely in the dark when it came to his personal future. Moses knew when his time was up (Deut. 31:14); so did Joshua (Josh. 23:14). Elijah knew when he was going home and how (2 Kings 2). Paul knew when he was going to die (2 Tim. 4:6), and Peter knew when and how (2 Pet. 1:14). Evidently, Moses, Joshua, Elijah, Paul, Peter, and JESUS CHRIST were all more prophets of God than Mohammed could ever hope to be.

People, I don't believe that Book because of anyone's religion or "faith." I believe it due to scientific, mathematical [fact.]{.class_3055} I have covered this many times before, but it deserves repeating. The Bible is proven true by the [mathematical laws of statistical probability.]{.class_3055} A mathematics professor at Wheaton College, named Peter Stoner, got one of his classes to calculate the statistical probability of 48 prophecies, written 400--1,500 years ahead of time, all coming to pass in the life of one man. Peter Stoner and his class took 48 prophecies found in the Old Testament on the First Advent of Jesus Christ and found out the chances of all 48 coming through right on the money. It was one chance in 10 to the 157th power. That's ten with 157 zeroes after it.

That is a [natural impossibility.]{.class_3055} If the chances of something taking place are over one in ten with fifty zeroes after it, that event will [never]{.class_3055} take place naturally. In fact, there are not 10[157]{.class_3099} electrons in three universes the size of ours. There is no natural explanation for the fulfilled prophecy of the Bible; it has not been, and will not be, reproduced by any other religious book, including the [Koran.]{.class_3055} We are talking about prophecy fulfilled in [history,]{.class_3055} not at the Last Judgment when time is no more.

Back to verse 31: ["And after three days rise again."]{.class_3054} That separates your "religion," if you are a Christian, from every other religion on earth. Mohammed can stay dead, and that doesn't affect []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1326.xhtml_page_210}any Moslem: a Moslem can be a good Moslem without Mohammed coming up from the dead. Buddha can stay dead, and a Buddhist can still be a good Buddhist. No Catholic's religion suffers anything at all by a dead Mary. (I know Pius XII decreed her "assumption" into Heaven, but Catholics for 1,400 years previous to that made out just fine not believing that.) But if Jesus Christ stayed dead, there is no way you can be a good Christian.

["Therefore we are buried with him by baptism into death: that like as Christ was raised up from the dead by the glory of the Father, even so we also should walk in newness of life"]{.class_3054} (Rom. 6:4).

No Resurrection, no ["newness of life."]{.class_3054} No Resurrection means you are still headed for Hell.

["And if Christ be not raised, your faith is vain; ye are yet in your sins"]{.class_3054} (1 Cor. 15:17).

The Christian's situation is unique: [his religion is dependent on its Founder being alive.]{.class_3055}

After Jesus told the disciples what was going to happen to Him, ["Peter took him, and began to rebuke him"]{.class_3054} (vs. 32). Mark doesn't tell you what Peter said, but Matthew does: ["Be it far from thee, Lord: this shall not be unto thee"]{.class_3054} (Matt. 16:22).

Now Peter means well; he has nothing but the best of intentions. He is not purposefully trying to stop Christ's atonement; he is just concerned for the well-being of his Lord. He loves Jesus and doesn't want to see Him suffer. But he is as [Satanically inspired]{.class_3055} when he says that as any "God-is-love" Charismatic or modern Bible version translator.

Peter has the bad habit of [denying the Scriptures]{.class_3055} when they cross his feelings. At the Last Supper, Jesus told the disciples, ["All ye shall be offended because of me this night: for it is written . . ."]{.class_3054}; and then He gives them what Zechariah 13:7 said (14:27). Peter's response to the prophecy of Scripture was: "I know the Scriptures say that, Lord, but what it really means in the originals is everyone [except me"]{.class_3055} (["Although all shall be offended, yet will not I"]{.class_3054}---14:29). Here, it's: "I know you said you were going to suffer, die, and rise again, Lord; but it's not going to happen." What is that? That's the work of the Devil contradicting that Book---["Yea, hath God said . . . ?"]{.class_3054}[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1326.xhtml_page_211} (Gen. 3:1).

The implication of verse 33 for the Roman Catholic Church is devastating, for if the Roman Catholic Church is built on the rock of Simon Peter (the Catholic interpretation of Matt. 16:18), then the Catholic Church is built on [Satan.]{.class_3055} It's right in the context of Matthew 16:18 (Matt. 16:23). If Simon Peter were the first Pope, then the doctrine of the infallibility of the Pope is a horselaugh. Simon Peter could be [wrong]{.class_3055} on doctrine, and he was on more than one occasion (see Gal. 2:11--14).

Now, the fact of the matter is, Satan can take advantage of a Christian if allowed (2 Cor. 2:11). He can fill a Christian (Acts 5:3) the same as the Holy Spirit can (Eph. 5:18). A Christian can ["give place to the devil"]{.class_3054} (Eph. 4:27). He can ["fall into the condemnation of the devil"]{.class_3054} (1 Tim. 3:6) and ["fall into reproach and the snare of the devil"]{.class_3054} (1 Tim. 3:7). Just because you have the Holy Spirit living in you, Christian (1 John 4:4), doesn't mean anything if you don't ["Walk in the Spirit"]{.class_3054} (Gal. 5:16), yield yourself to Him (Rom. 6:13), and ["Resist the devil"]{.class_3054} (James 4:7).

["Thou savourest not the things that be of God, but the things that be of men"]{.class_3054} (vs. 33). Satan is ["the god of this world"]{.class_3054} (2 Cor. 4:4), and as such, he is involved with ["all that is in the world"]{.class_3054} (1 John 2:16). What is that? It's ["The lust of the flesh, and the lust of the eyes, and the pride of life"]{.class_3054} (1 John 2:16). Those things are ["not of the Father, but . . . of the world"]{.class_3054} (1 John 2:16). All three are ["the things that be of men,"]{.class_3054} not God.

34 And when he had called the people unto him with his disciples also, he said unto them, Whosoever will come after me, let him deny himself, and take up his cross, and follow me.

35 For whosoever will save his life shall lose it; but whosoever shall lose his life for my sake and the gospel's, the same shall save it.

36 For what shall it profit a man, if he shall gain the whole world, and lose his own soul?

37 Or what shall a man give in exchange for his soul?

38 Whosoever therefore shall be ashamed of me and of []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1326.xhtml_page_212}my words in this adulterous and sinful generation; of him also shall the Son of man be ashamed, when he cometh in the glory of his Father with the holy angels.

[Now look at what follows verses 31--33. After telling His disciples He is going to be killed, and after Peter tries to contradict that out of concern for Him, Jesus tells them: ]{.class_3065}["Whosoever will come after me . . ."]{.class_3064-1} (vs. 34). That's aimed right at the disciples and, in turn, anybody who is going to follow Christ this side of Calvary.

Notice the wording: ["COME after me,"]{.class_3054} not "[follow]{.class_3055} after me." It is aimed at any disciple or follower of Christ after His death and resurrection. Note the ["CROSS,"]{.class_3054} indicating how He will die.

Notice in verse 34 the three things a disciple of Christ is to do:

1.[ "Deny himself."]{.class_3054} As Jesus said, ["not my will, but thine, be done"]{.class_3054} (Luke 22:42). That has never been the philosophy of this world. Here are four typical examples of humanistic slop:

A. From the fruity, Frisco faggot of ancient Athens who liked to molest young boys, Socrates: "Know thyself."

B. From a demon-possessed, epileptic military dictator who liked to go to bed with a nine-year-old girl, Mohammed: "To know one's self is to know God."

C. From William Shakespeare's [Hamlet:]{.class_3055} "To thine own self be true."

D. From a modern Liberal in the National Council of Churches, Ralph Sockman: "Every Christian must discover his true self."

After that you can write, "HOGWASH!" If you "know yourself" and "discover your true self" and are "true" to the "self" you "know" and "discover," do you "know" what you "discover"? You find out this:

["For I know that in me (that is, in my flesh,) dwelleth NO GOOD THING: for to will is present with me; but how to perform that which is good I find not"]{.class_3054} (Rom. 7:18).

If you know yourself, you don't know God at all; all you know is old man Adam. The carnal mind is death (Rom. 8:6), the carnal mind is the enemy of God (Rom. 8:7), and the carnal mind can't please God (Rom. 8:8). You need a new man in you (2 Cor. 5:17), created by a new birth (John 3:6--7; Rom. 8:9--11).

A Christian is to ["deny himself."]{.class_3054} He is to deny []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1326.xhtml_page_213}["ungodliness and worldly lusts"]{.class_3054} (Tit. 2:12). He is not to please himself (Rom. 15:1). The modern calls for [self-love]{.class_3055} and [self-esteem]{.class_3055} are diametrically opposed to the life of [self-denial]{.class_3055} to which Jesus calls us (2 Tim. 3:2; Phil. 2:3).

2\. ["Take up his cross."]{.class_3054} That is not some "burden" the Lord puts on you individually; that's dying to self and the desires of the flesh. The ["cross"]{.class_3054} is an instrument of [execution.]{.class_3055} You are to ["mortify the deeds of the body"]{.class_3054} (Rom. 8:13 cf. Col. 3:5) and ["reckon"]{.class_3054} yourself ["to be dead indeed unto sin"]{.class_3054} (Rom. 6:11). You can do that because, positionally, the old sinful nature ["is crucified with"]{.class_3054} Christ (Rom. 6:6 cf. Gal. 2:20); that's why Jesus mentions the cross here in Mark 8:34. You have to reckon that act to be so in your own life to get the victory over sin (Gal. 5:24), and you have to do it on a [daily]{.class_3055} basis. It is not a one-time act, which is why Luke adds the word ["daily"]{.class_3054} to Christ's words here in the parallel passage in Luke 9:23. Paul said, ["I die DAILY"]{.class_3054} (1 Cor. 15:31). (I might add, from personal experience, that sometimes you have to die [hourly]{.class_3055} and sometimes [minute by minute.]{.class_3055}) That is putting into [practice]{.class_3055} what you have [positionally;]{.class_3055} it is making your [state]{.class_3055} (where you are at any given moment in your Christian life) match your [standing]{.class_3055} (your position[ doctrinally]{.class_3055} in Christ).

3\. ["Follow me."]{.class_3054} This is a deeper commitment than they have made heretofore. When we get to 10:28, we will read of Peter saying, ["we have left all, and have followed thee."]{.class_3054} He's referring to leaving their means of income (1:17), and houses, family, and lands (10:29). Those are all [physical]{.class_3055} things. This is forsaking your own will and desires to follow Christ. This is loving Christ above even your own life (Luke 14:26--27).

You see, up to this point, the disciples had been following a Christ whom they believed would be a conquering King. They had every right to think that, for it's all through the Old Testament (see our remarks on the physical Kingdom of Heaven under 1:15). But up until now, they probably hadn't even considered the Suffering Servant of Isaiah 53, which is why Peter rebuked the Lord for His words about suffering and being murdered by ["the chief priests, and scribes"]{.class_3054} (vss. 31--32). But now, Jesus tells the disciples that He []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1326.xhtml_page_214}will be put to death, and they need to be ready for the same fate. He tells them in John 15:20, ["The servant is not greater than his lord. If they have persecuted me, they will also persecute you."]{.class_3054} [That]{.class_3055} is how the disciple is to follow the Lord.

["Let us go forth therefore unto him without the camp, bearing his reproach"]{.class_3054} (Heb. 13:13).

Back to Mark 8:[ "For whosoever will save his life shall lose it"]{.class_3054} (vs. 35). That's the result of [not]{.class_3055} denying yourself and [not]{.class_3055} taking up your cross and [not]{.class_3055} following Christ into reproach and persecution. That's you looking out for number one and the whole thing going completely to pieces as a result. If you want your life to end in ruin, just live for self and flesh. Make sure you get along with everyone and never offend anyone over Jesus Christ and the Book---that is the surest path to "going bust" you can take, as a Christian.

["But whosoever shall lose his life for my sake and the gospel's, the same shall save it."]{.class_3054} That is a great paradox: a contradiction that is nevertheless true. The way to build muscles is to [wear them out]{.class_3055} by exercise. The way to make money is to [spend it]{.class_3055} in wise investments. If you want to ["lay hold on eternal life"]{.class_3054} as Paul says (1 Tim. 6:12, 19)---that is, get the rewards God wants to give you in the life to come (Phil. 3:14)---you have to give up control of your life down here and hand the reins over to the Lord. You lose your life in order to gain it.

I came face to face with that decision about nine months after I was saved. I was attending Bob Jones University at the time. I was a first-year student, living in a trailer with a wife and a little girl, with a baby boy on the way.

I was headed back to the trailer court one night after an evening service that was held on campus, and I felt led to get on my knees and pray. I felt the Lord asking me, "If I called you to [preach,]{.class_3055} would you preach?"

I said, "Oh yeah. I've got to do something. I lived like the devil for 27 years. I don't want to die and go home to Heaven and not take anyone with me. I want to take someone with me."

Then the Lord dealt with me about this: "Would you go [overseas]{.class_3055} and preach for me?"

And that gave me pause. "What do you mean?" I asked.

"If I sent you back to Japan, would you go?"

I said, "Lord, I don't want to go back there. I've been across that Pacific four times, and I don't want to go again."

"But would you do it for me?"

I said, "All right, I'll do it for you."

The next thing was: "What if I ask you to go over there [alone;]{.class_3055} would you do it?"

I said, "But Lord, I've got a family."

"But if I asked you to go alone, would you go alone?"

Again I said, "All right." I started to get up, but the Lord wasn't done with me yet.

"What if I asked you to go over there and not have anyone saved?"

I hadn't yet read about missionaries like William Carey or Adoniram Judson who labored for seven years without getting any converts. I told the Lord, "I can't do that. I've wasted too much time. I've got to see somebody saved."

You see, I was worried about dying without leading someone to Christ. I had damned enough young men as a drill instructor and a dance band drummer. I didn't want to die without making up for that.

"But would you do it for me?"

"OK, Lord, OK"; and I started to get up again. I thought I was consecrated, but the Lord still had more business with me.

"If I asked you to [die]{.class_3055} over there, would you still go?"

And you know how we saved sinners are. I said, "Yeah, but make it quick." I knew a lot of terrible ways to die, and I didn't want to experience any of them firsthand.

"What if it wasn't quick?"

I ended that conversation with the Lord by making a little covenant with Him. Of course, I may live to regret it, from a human standpoint, but I won't go back on it. I decided that [anything]{.class_3055} He wanted, I would do, no matter how horrible. I got up from that prayer meeting completely [broke.]{.class_3055} When I urge you to pray for the Lord to come back soon, I must admit that it is not without an element of selfishness on my part: [I am trying to save my own neck.]{.class_3055} And if He doesn't and I have to go through the worst I can think of []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1326.xhtml_page_216}for Him, then so be it. I don't intend to go back on my word.

Now the important thing about losing your life to gain it, is the [cause]{.class_3055} for which you lose it. If you give your life for your country, that can be a noble thing, but it doesn't gain you anything in the life to come. If you give your life for "liberty," that can mean [anything]{.class_3055} these days; all you might be doing is dying for someone to fornicate without consequences. If you die saving someone else, that is noble and good, but meaningless if not done for the right reason (1 Cor. 13:3). You had better make sure that what you are doing is for the sake of your Saviour and His Gospel. Of course, we understand that ["the gospel"]{.class_3054} here is not necessarily the Gospel we preach today (see our comments on 1:15), but you can certainly make spiritual application based on the Pauline Epistles.

Now verses 36--37 switch from a disciple (vs. 34) to [any man.]{.class_3055} Note they are addressed to ["a man,"]{.class_3054} not a disciple. It can certainly be applied to a lost sinner because the ["man"]{.class_3054} is in danger of [losing his soul]{.class_3055} (vs. 36).

The two questions Christ asks are:

1\) ["For what shall it profit a man, if he shall gain the whole world, and lose his own soul?"]{.class_3054} (vs. 36).

2\) ["Or what shall a man give in exchange for his own soul?"]{.class_3054} (vs. 37).

Those are rhetorical questions to which the obvious answer should be: ["Nothing."]{.class_3055} A popular theme in horror stories is a person who sells his or her soul to the Devil for something he or she wants more than anything else. The plot, naturally, is taken from the Bible: both here and in Luke 4:5--7. I've got news for you: if you have never trusted Jesus Christ as your Saviour, the Devil doesn't have to buy your soul; [he already has it]{.class_3055} (Eph. 2:1--3). But the Devil will hold out ["the pleasures of sin for a season"]{.class_3054} to keep you from coming to Christ (Heb. 11:25).

The important thing to remember is when it comes to getting the world, the Devil never delivers. No one who has ever tried to ["gain the whole world"]{.class_3054} ever got it. Alexander the Great got a pretty big hunk of it, but he didn't get it all. He died a drunk, crying over the fact that there was no more of the known world to conquer. Genghis Khan got a pretty big piece of it, but he didn't get it all. Napoleon got []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1326.xhtml_page_217}a good bit of it, but after spending some time alone in the Great Pyramid in Egypt, he invaded Russia and lost his shirt. Hitler's cry was: "Today Germany; tomorrow the world." When he invaded France, he spent time alone sitting in Napoleon's chair. After that, he invaded Russia, like Napoleon did, and lost his shirt, just like Napoleon did.

People, the Devil never keeps his promises. He'll promise you whatever it takes to keep you from accepting Christ, but he never will deliver fully. He'll give you just enough to damn you. He'll promise you all the drugs you want, all the women you want, all the food you want, all the money you want, all the popularity you want; and then he'll kill you before you are fifty years old, like he did to Elvis Presley, John Belushi, John Lennon, Kurt Cobain, John Candy, et al.

If you sell your soul for the world, you have sold yourself too cheaply. You have invested all your money in a house scheduled for demolition (2 Pet. 3:10).

["And the world passeth away, and the lust thereof: but he that doeth the will of God abideth for ever"]{.class_3054} (1 John 2:17).

Now when it comes to the soul, I have never read a Bible scholar here in the United States or abroad that could explain it. The soul of man is a separate thing from his body or spirit (1 Thess. 5:23). If your soul is worth more than all the world, you would expect someone who professes to study the word of God to know what it is, but none seem to.

What is a "soul"? The Greeks say it is like a small ball. Some say it is in the left ventricle of the heart, while others say it is at the base of the skull. Back in the Dark Ages, physicians weighed the body of a person who was dying to see if there were any change when the soul left the body. They were trying to see if the soul has a measurable substance that departed at death (cf. Gen. 35:18).

In [the Bible]{.class_3055}---and who among the scholars actually believes the Bible?---the soul is an invisible, spiritual body that lives inside your body of flesh and bone. In Luke 16, that rich man in Hell had [eyes]{.class_3055} to see (Luke 16:23) and a tongue that could feel (Luke 16:24). Over in Paradise, Abraham had a bosom (a chest) on which Lazarus could lay (Luke 16:23), and Lazarus had a finger he could dip in water []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1326.xhtml_page_218}(Luke 16:24). Well, those weren't body parts of their physical bodies; their physical bodies were in a grave somewhere, rotting.

Look at Revelation 6:11. Those ["souls" "under the altar"]{.class_3054} there in Revelation 6:9 ["were given" "white robes" ]{.class_3054}(Rev. 6:11). Well, if a soul is just some ethereal spirit, it can't wear a robe. But if the soul is the bodily shape of a man, do you know what you have? You have "Casper the Friendly Ghost"; you have the classic ghost-in-a-sheet figure. You won't get away from that Book, no matter how hard you try.

Do you know what Paul said about the time he was ["caught up to the third heaven"]{.class_3054} (2 Cor. 12:2)? He said, ["whether in the body, or out of the body, I cannot tell"]{.class_3054} (2 Cor. 12:3). What's going on? Well, his physical body was down on earth, outside of Lystra, covered in stones (Acts 14:19); but Paul was standing up in Heaven in a body he couldn't see. He said over in 2 Corinthians 5:1,

["For we know that if our earthly house of this tabernacle were dissolved ]{.class_3054}\[our physical bodies---2 Pet. 1:13--14\][, we have a building of God, an house not made with hands, eternal in the heavens ]{.class_3054}\[a spiritual body awaiting a glorified body at the resurrection---Phil. 3:21\][."]{.class_3054}

If you want to get what a soul is, look at God. God has a [body]{.class_3055} that could be seen when it was here on earth: the body of Jesus Christ. Jesus said, ["he that hath seen me hath seen the Father"]{.class_3054} (John 14:9). At the same time, God has a [soul]{.class_3055} that cannot be seen. The Bible says, ["No man hath seen God at any time"]{.class_3054} (John 1:18). That means no man has seen God's essence, His soul: the person of God the Father.

Look here, I can say, "If you've seen me, you've seen Pete Ruckman, but no one has ever seen the real Peter Ruckman." What do I mean? I am saying if you have seen my body, you have seen all of me that you can see, but my body is not the "real me." The actual Pete Ruckman is inside that body peeking out at you through the eyes (Matt. 6:22; Luke 11:34). That's the same relation God the Son has to God the Father. Those who saw Jesus Christ here on earth (and [we Christians who will see Him at the Rapture]{.class_3055}) saw all of God that can be seen. ["The only begotten Son, which is in the bosom of the Father, he hath declared him"]{.class_3054} (John 1:18).

Now if that soul is a bodily shape within your physical body, what does it mean to lose your soul? Well, first of all, look at what Jesus says prophetically through David in Psalm 22:6---["But I am a WORM, and no man."]{.class_3054} That's a reference to Jesus Christ on the cross. Do you know to what Jesus Christ likened Himself on the cross?

["And as Moses lifted up the SERPENT in the wilderness, even so must the Son of man be lifted up"]{.class_3054} (John 3:14).

That ["serpent"]{.class_3054} is ["the Devil"]{.class_3054} (Rev. 12:9). That's the Lord Jesus Christ becoming like Satan on the cross. That was Jesus becoming [sin for you]{.class_3055} (2 Cor. 5:21). That's the soul of ["the man Christ Jesus"]{.class_3054} (1 Tim. 2:5) losing its bodily shape of a man as He takes the place of a sinner in Hell (Isa. 53:10 cf. Matt. 26:38). Look at Mark 9, verses 44, 46, and 48.

"Where THEIR WORM dieth not, and the fire is not quenched."

That is such an important statement that Jesus repeats it [three times]{.class_3055} in six verses. The soul of a sinner in the Lake of Fire ceases to be a man and becomes like its father, the Devil (John 8:44)---a [red maggot]{.class_3055} like a [serpent.]{.class_3055} Hell is [Darwin in reverse.]{.class_3055} It is sinners devolving from men to ["natural brute beasts"]{.class_3054} (2 Pet. 2:12 cf. Rev. 9:7--8) and finally ending up in their original state. Have you ever seen a man's seed under a microscope: it looks like a [worm.]{.class_3055} So when a sinner winds up in the Lake of Fire, he loses his bodily shape as a man, and ends up as a creature with no arms, no legs, no eyes, no ears; just a writhing, squirming piece of flesh that can only feel pain.

Is that how you want to be for all eternity? If not, then come to Jesus Christ NOW in faith (2 Cor. 6:2). That way you'll become a son of God (John 1:12) and end up [just like your Saviour]{.class_3055} (Rom 8:29; Phil. 3:21; 1 John 3:1--2).

Verse 38: ["Whosoever therefore shall be ashamed of me and of my words in this adulterous and sinful generation; of him also shall the Son of man be ashamed, when he cometh in the glory of his Father with the holy angels."]{.class_3054} Now we are back to [discipleship.]{.class_3055} The cross-reference in Matthew 10:32--33 deals with being too ashamed of Jesus Christ to confess Him before men. There is no straddling the fence: if you don't []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1326.xhtml_page_220}confess Him, you've denied Him. Now a Christian can be intimidated by a lost world to the point of denying his Saviour; sure he can. Just look at Peter over in chapter 14, verses 66--72. But if he does, that doesn't mean he is lost. Look at 2 Timothy 2:12--13.

["If we suffer, we shall also reign with him: if we deny him, he also will deny us."]{.class_3054} Deny us how? As sons? Of course not! Keep reading. ["If we believe not, yet he abideth faithful: HE CANNOT DENY HIMSELF."]{.class_3054} Jesus Christ will [never]{.class_3055} deny you as a son because He is in you and you are in Him. But He will deny you that ["reign"]{.class_3054} in 2 Timothy 2:12.

That is why Jesus Christ will be ashamed of you at His Second Advent, not the White Throne Judgment. He does not send the believer who is ashamed of Him to Hell; He denies him a reign with Him in His kingdom. As Christians, if we don't want to ["be ashamed before him at his coming,"]{.class_3054} we need to [abide in him"]{.class_3054} (1 John 2:28) in the sense of [fellowship]{.class_3055} (1 John 1:6--7). Part of that is speaking up for Jesus Christ and not being ashamed of Him before a hostile world.

Before closing this chapter, notice that it is not only the Lord Jesus Christ of whom you are not to be ashamed; it is His ["WORDS"]{.class_3054} also. That's especially applicable to this time in America. If you stand up for what that Book says against the Moslems, the Catholics, the Mormons, the queers, drugs, television, rock music, liquor, pornography, abortion, "cross-genders," etc., you are going to come more and more under fire from a lost world. Already since the Supreme Court legalized two queers or two dykes being able to pretend they are married (they [aren't]{.class_3055} married no matter who says they are---10:6--9), Christians have been jailed and fined hundreds of thousands of dollars for refusing to approve or participate in such a farce. They have been fired from their jobs for speaking out privately against such sin when they weren't even at their jobs.

The new move in America by Obama, Hillary, and the Democrats is to change freedom of [religion]{.class_3055} (you can believe and practice your religion anywhere you want) to freedom of "worship" (you can believe and practice your religion as long as it is confined to your [church building]{.class_3055}). People, the Devil doesn't care what you believe as along as you keep your mouth shut about it. Don't witness, don't []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1326.xhtml_page_221}pass out tracts, don't preach on the streets, don't say anything from that Book that will offend anyone, and you'll get along just fine with the Devil and his crowd.

Don't be ashamed of the words of that Book. It's the only Book that will save your soul and keep you alive as long as it exists (1 Pet. 1:23--25).

[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1521.xhtml}

# Chapter []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1521.xhtml_page_222}9 {.heading_97}

9:1 And he said unto them, Verily I say unto you, That there be some of them that stand here, which shall not taste of death, till they have seen the kingdom of God come with power.

The verse is a prelude to what follows in verses 2--4. So according to Jesus, His Transfiguration is a picture of His Second Advent. This is confirmed by Simon Peter in 2 Peter 1:16--17. Note, this is the [spiritual]{.class_3055} Kingdom of God showing up in [physical]{.class_3055} form (see our remarks under 1:14). This is the King of both kingdoms giving "a foretaste of glory divine"---[literally]{.class_3055} (cf. 8:38).

In the context of Mark 9, the ["some of them that stand here, which shall not taste of death, till they have seen the kingdom of God come with power"]{.class_3054} are Peter, James, and John. But John has a special application due to the fact that when he was given the book of Revelation, he was taken ahead in time by the Holy Spirit to the Day of the Lord (see Rev. 1:10 and our comments in that Commentary).

In John 21:22, Jesus said of John, ["If I will that he tarry till I come, what is that to thee?"]{.class_3054} Because of that statement, a rumor went around among the believers of the First Century that John wouldn't die, but that is not what Jesus said (John 21:23). John was allowed to live long enough (he was the last of the Twelve to die) to have the Holy Spirit show him the Second Advent; then he was returned to his own time, where he died just like everyone else. John got to see the real thing before he died, not just the type.

2 And after six days Jesus taketh with him Peter, and James, and John, and leadeth them up into an high mountain apart by themselves: and he was transfigured before them.

3 And his raiment became shining, exceeding white as snow; so as no fuller on earth can white them.

4 And there appeared unto them Elias with Moses: and []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1521.xhtml_page_223}they were talking with Jesus.

Verse 2 gives us the time element of the Second Advent. Mark says, ["after six days"]{.class_3054} (vs. 2); Luke says, ["about an eight days"]{.class_3054} (Luke 9:28). Well, what number is after six and about eight? Seven, of course.

This is what I call the "Seven Sevens" system of the Bible. God goes [six days]{.class_3055} and has something special on the [seventh:]{.class_3055} the Sabbath (Lev. 23:3). He runs [seven weeks]{.class_3055} after the Feast of the Firstfruits, and then the day after the seventh Sabbath of those weeks, He has the Feast of Pentecost (Lev. 23:15--16). The [seventh month]{.class_3055} is a special month in the Jewish calendar because there are three feasts in that month: the Feast of Trumpets (Lev. 23:24), the Day of Atonement (Lev. 23:27), and the Feast of Tabernacles (Lev. 23:34).

Those Jews could work their land six years, and the [seventh year]{.class_3055} was to be treated as a sabbath for the land (Lev. 25:3--4). After [seven of those sabbatical years]{.class_3055} had passed (49 years altogether), the fiftieth year was the year of Jubilee, where all debts were forgiven and indentured servants freed.

Now, do you know what you have in 2 Peter 3:8? It is something of which Peter doesn't want you to be ignorant: ["one day is with the Lord as a thousand years, and a thousand years as one day."]{.class_3054} What's the context? The re-creation of the earth and heavens after the universal flood of Genesis 1:2 (2 Pet. 3:5--7) and after the Second Advent (2 Pet. 3:10). That means the six days of creation in Genesis 1 are a picture of world history from Creation to the Millennial sabbath of Day Seven. Do you know what shows up on Day Four of Creation? The sun (Gen. 1:14--19); that's a picture of Jesus Christ (Mal. 4:2; Psa. 19:4--5). Jesus came the first time 4,000 years after Creation.

What that means is that, based on the Seven Sevens system laid down in Scripture, the Second Advent should have taken place in A.D. 2000. Obviously it didn't happen. So what's the problem? Well, our calendar could very well be [wrong,]{.class_3055} and whenever I preached my sermon "The Seven Sevens" during the 50s, 60s, 70s, 80s, and 90s, I always said of the Lord coming back in the year 2000: "IF our calendar is right." And of course, there is that fact of the Lord not []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1521.xhtml_page_224}counting certain years (see our remarks under 6:48). But whenever the Lord starts His clock again, you can be sure that it will be right on schedule with what He has revealed in His word.

The location of the Transfiguration has been speculated as Mt. Hermon up near the border of Lebanon or Mt. Tabor in Galilee. But this is [tradition,]{.class_3055} not Scripture. Remember, this is a picture of the Second Advent, so we need to look for a mountain connected to that event. Well, the Lord is about to come down on the mountain in a cloud (vs. 7). The original "prototype" of that is Exodus 19:16, where God comes down on [Mt. Sinai.]{.class_3055} In Deborah's prophetic song, Mt. Sinai is part of the path Jesus Christ takes on His way to Jerusalem at the Second Advent (see our comments on Judg. 5:5 in the [Bible Believer's Commentary on Judges and Ruth]{.class_3055}). Mt. Sinai is one of two mountains in the Old Testament considered ["holy"]{.class_3054} (Psa. 68:17; Exod. 3:5), the other being Mt. Zion in Jerusalem (Isa. 27:13). Peter says the Transfiguration took place ["in the holy mount"]{.class_3054} (2 Pet. 1:18). Well, Jesus wasn't transfigured in Jerusalem.

Elijah and Moses appear on the Mount of Transfiguration with Christ (vs. 4). Sinai was the one mountain both men had in common (Exod. 3:1--4; 1 Kings 19:9--12). In the last chapter of the Old Testament, there are four names associated with Christ's Second Coming: Moses, Elijah, the Lord, and Mt. Sinai (["Horeb,"]{.class_3054} Mal. 4:4--5). The Mount of Transfiguration is undoubtedly [Sinai,]{.class_3055} not Hermon or Tabor.

The word ["transfigured"]{.class_3054} means "to change the appearance of something or someone." Luke 9:29 says, ["the fashion of his countenance was altered."]{.class_3054} Christ's ["raiment became shining"]{.class_3054} (vs. 3). Mark says it was ["white as snow"]{.class_3054}; Matthew says it was ["white as the light"]{.class_3054} (Matt. 17:2); Luke says it was ["white and glistering"]{.class_3054} (i.e., [sparkling]{.class_3055}---Luke 9:29). This is Jesus Christ appearing as He will in His glory (cf. Dan. 7:9).

The ["fuller"]{.class_3054} of verse 3 was someone who whitened woolen cloth. They did it through fuller's [earth,]{.class_3055} a clay used to scour the material, and fuller's [soap]{.class_3055} (see Mal. 3:2), an alkali made from plant ashes. We would call it "detergent" today. Fuller's earth is mined in Arkansas today and is used to make one of the strongest soaps there is. Note that the ["fullers' soap"]{.class_3054} of Malachi 3:2 is associated with Christ at His Second Coming (["the day of his coming"]{.class_3054}[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1521.xhtml_page_225}).

Now you should note what Christ's appearance here matches in the Scriptures. When an angel of the Lord shows up, his clothing matches that of the glorified Christ (Matt. 28:3; Acts 10:30). Over in Matthew 17:2, it says Christ's ["face did shine as the sun."]{.class_3054} That is the same thing that is said of the ["mighty angel"]{.class_3054} who comes down in Revelation 10:1. That is why Satan transforms himself[ "into an angel of light"]{.class_3054} (2 Cor. 11:14), even though he is no such thing (Ezek. 28:14; Rev. 12:9). Just because some angel appears to you shining like a 10,000-watt bulb doesn't mean it's an angel of God, or even an angel at all. You have to ["try the spirits whether they are of God"]{.class_3054} (1 John 4:1).

Now, if you can just get into the presence of the glorified Christ, you will take on His countenance. When you get ["washed in the blood of the Lamb"]{.class_3054} (Rev. 1:5), you get cleansed (1 John 1:7) until, spiritually, you are ["white as snow"]{.class_3054} (Isa. 1:18). Back there in Exodus 34, when Moses was in the presence of the Lord talking to Him, ["his face shone"]{.class_3054} (Exod. 34:29--35); that will be true of every saint of God at Christ's return (Matt. 13:43; Dan. 12:3). At the Rapture, Christian, you will receive a glorified body just like your resurrected Saviour's (Phil. 3:21; 1 John 3:2). What you are reading here in Mark 9:3 is a precursor of what you will look like someday.

Verse 4: ["And there appeared unto them Elias with Moses."]{.class_3054} That's the key to understanding the Old Testament and what's coming up in the future. That Old Testament was divided into two basic sections: ["the law and the prophets"]{.class_3054} (Matt. 7:12, 22:40; Luke 16:16; Acts 13:15; Rom. 3:21). Moses represented the Law, and Elijah represented the Prophets. As I pointed out in my remarks under verse 2, the Old Testament ends with the names of three people: Moses, Elijah, and the Lord (Mal. 4:4--5). Zechariah's vision of the two olive trees (Zech. 4) shows up in Revelation 11, and the description is that of Moses and Elijah (Rev. 11:4--6). Like Christ (Rev. 5:6), Moses and Elijah stand in the presence of the Lord (Exod. 33:21; 1 Kings 17:1). That is why James and John couldn't get the right and left hand positions in Christ's kingdom (see Mark 10:37, 40), because they are for Moses and Elijah.

Moses and Elijah both heard from God on Mt. Sinai (Exod. 3:1--4; []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1521.xhtml_page_226}1 Kings 19:9--12). Like Christ (Luke 4:2), both of them fasted forty days and nights (Exod. 24:18; 1 Kings 19:8). When Jesus wanted to discuss ["his decease which he should accomplish at Jerusalem,"]{.class_3054} He chose to do so with Moses and Elijah (Luke 9:30--31). All three accomplished miraculous feedings (Exod. 16; 1 Kings 17; Mark 6, 8), and all three crossed water miraculously (Exod. 14; 2 Kings 2; Mark 6). All three are caught up to Heaven (2 Kings 2; Acts 1; Jude 9; Rev. 11); they all have supernatural power over the elements (Exod. 5--10; James 5:17--18; Mark 4:41); they all personally choose their successors (Deut. 34:9; 1 Kings 19:16; Acts 9:15--16 cf. Gal. 1:15--16); they all destroy their enemies by fire (Num. 16:31--35; 2 Kings 1:9--12; Psa. 97:3, 18:8, 21:9 cf. 2 Thess 1:8).

That's why Moses and Elijah show up here on the Mount of Transfiguration. They are connected with Christ's Second Advent.

5 And Peter answered and said to Jesus, Master, it is good for us to be here: and let us make three tabernacles; one for thee, and one for Moses, and one for Elias.

6 For he wist not what to say; for they were sore afraid.

What you just read gives you the exact month and week of the Second Advent. Those ["tabernacles"]{.class_3054} in verse 5 are the ["booths"]{.class_3054} the children of Israel were supposed to make and live in for seven days during the Feast of Tabernacles (Lev. 23:39--42).

The Feast of Tabernacles takes place in the seventh month, which is September/October on our calendar. That month is so important that it has three feasts in it. Why is that? Because that is the month of the [Second Advent.]{.class_3055}

Get Zechariah 14 and note the context: ["the day of the LORD"]{.class_3054} (Zech. 14:1)---the Second Advent. Now look at what is required of every nation every year after that event.

Zechariah 14:16---["And it shall come to pass, that every one that is left of all the nations which came against Jerusalem shall even go up from year to year to worship the King, the LORD of hosts, and to keep the feast of tabernacles."]{.class_3054}

In the Millennium, the Feast of Tabernacles takes the place of the Feast of Passover as Israel's (and the [world's]{.class_3055}) most important holiday, because it commemorates Christ's return in power to conquer the Gentile nations.

But not only does it look back to Christ's Second Advent, in the Millennium, it looks forward to Eternity where God dwells with men.

Revelation 21:3---["And I heard a great voice out of heaven saying, Behold, THE TABERNACLE OF GOD IS WITH MEN, and he will dwell with them, and they shall be his people, and God himself shall be with them, and be their God."]{.class_3054}

The Feast of Tabernacles points to a time of perfect peace, perfect happiness, and perfect fellowship with God. That is what every human being who has ever lived on earth has ever wanted, whether he will admit it or not (Rev. 21:4).

Now, Peter, James, and John had been sleeping (Luke 9:32). They awake to see Jesus white and shining brightly as sunlight (Matt. 17:2). But not only that, before them stand two of the greatest men of the Old Testament: Moses and Elijah. All of that explains why Peter said what he did: ["For he wist not what to say"]{.class_3054} (vs. 6). When Peter doesn't know what to do, he shoots off his mouth (see 8:32, 14:29). The "old chestnut" is: "Even a fish could stay out of trouble if it would just learn to [keep its mouth shut"]{.class_3055} (cf. Prov. 21:23, 10:19, 13:3).

["For they were sore afraid."]{.class_3054} I imagine they were. Whenever someone in Scripture saw the Lord in His glory, he didn't treat Him like his "Buddy" or lavish affection on Him. The typical response was [fear]{.class_3055} (and I don't mean "reverential trust"). Isaiah said, ["Woe is me! for I am undone; because I am a man of unclean lips"]{.class_3054} (Isa. 6:5). The apostle who was closest to Jesus when He was here on earth ["fell at his feet as dead"]{.class_3054} when he saw Jesus in glory as our High Priest (Rev. 1:12--17).

7 And there was a cloud that overshadowed them: and a voice came out of the cloud, saying, This is my beloved Son: hear him.

8 And suddenly, when they had looked round about, they saw no man any more, save Jesus only with []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1521.xhtml_page_228}themselves.

The ["cloud"]{.class_3054} of verse 7 is the cloud of God's glory (see Exod. 40:34; 1 Kings 8:11). It is associated with Christ's Second Advent (Rev. 1:7). He went up in ["a cloud"]{.class_3054} (Acts 1:9), and that's how He'll return (Mark 13:26). That goes for the Rapture as well as the Advent (1 Thess. 4:17).

["And a voice came out of the cloud, saying, This is my beloved Son: hear him."]{.class_3054} This is the same voice that spoke at Christ's baptism (see 1:11). This time, the Father gives an admonition: ["hear him."]{.class_3054} Peter had lumped Jesus Christ in with two sinners: Moses and Elijah. Moses and Elijah weren't there as equals with Christ; they were there to bear witness to the work He came to accomplish (Luke 9:30--31). After His resurrection, Jesus said to the two disciples on the road to Emmaus:

["Ought not Christ to have suffered these things, and to enter into his glory? And beginning at Moses and all the prophets, he expounded unto them in all the scriptures the things CONCERNING HIMSELF"]{.class_3054} (Luke 24:26--27).

The command of Jesus, changed in all the new "Bibles" so it is not a command, is:

["Search the scriptures; for in them ye think ye have eternal life: and they are they which TESTIFY OF ME"]{.class_3054} (John 5:39).

In the same context, Jesus said:

["For had ye believed Moses, ye would have believed me: FOR HE WROTE OF ME"]{.class_3054} (John 5:46).

["The holy scriptures,"]{.class_3054} symbolized here in Moses and Elijah, ["are able to make thee wise unto salvation through faith WHICH IS IN CHRIST JESUS"]{.class_3054} (2 Tim. 3:15). The culmination of God's revelation to man is [in His Son]{.class_3055} (Heb. 1:1--2).

Jesus Christ is more than just another founder of a religion. He towers so far above Peter, Mary, the Popes, Mohammed, Buddha, Marx, Krishna, the Dalai Lama, Zoroaster, and Confucius that there isn't even a comparison. If you ever want to come to God the Father, you will come through His Son, or [you won't come]{.class_3055} (John 14:6; Acts 4:12; 1 Tim. 2:5).

As quickly as the Transfiguration manifested before them, just as ["suddenly"]{.class_3054} (vs. 8), it was over. The cloud was gone. The Voice was silent. Moses and Elijah were gone. ["They saw no man . . . save Jesus only."]{.class_3054}

Believe it or not, that last statement has been taken as the proof text for the "Jesus Onlyists." It is the teaching of the United Pentecostals ("Apostolic Holiness"). It is the belief that there is no Trinity. The Father is Jesus; the Son is Jesus; the Holy Spirit is Jesus.

Not only is that not the meaning in the context, it ignores everything the Scriptures reveal about the ["Godhead"]{.class_3054} (Acts 17:29; Rom. 1:20; Col. 2:9). The Trinity is not one God acting in three different capacities, like one actor playing three parts on a stage. It is one God manifested in [three Persons;]{.class_3055} each Person being separate from the other two, but at the same time being united in their existence. One God; three Persons. God has a body---Jesus Christ, the Son; God has a soul---the Father; God has a spirit---the Holy Spirit. Each member of the Trinity not only has a different job, each one has his own individual will, separate from the other two. Jesus said, ["not MY will]{.class_3054} \[there's one\][, but THINE]{.class_3054} \[and there's [another]{.class_3055} that's not the same\][, be done"]{.class_3054} (Luke 22:42).

Now, if you are going to make a doctrinal application of ["Jesus only,"]{.class_3054} it would be this: ["the man Christ Jesus"]{.class_3054} (1 Tim. 2:5) is the only bodily representation of God the Father ever seen (John 14:9). The only way the disciples could see the Father was through Jesus (John 1:18). He is ["the fulness of the Godhead BODILY"]{.class_3054} (Col. 2:9).

9 And as they came down from the mountain, he charged them that they should tell no man what things they had seen, till the Son of man were risen from the dead.

10 And they kept that saying with themselves, questioning one with another what the rising from the dead should mean.

Christ charges Peter, James, and John not to tell anyone what they had seen about His transfiguration, and we have already discussed the reasons for this under our comments on 8:30, which see. But notice [when]{.class_3055} He says they can talk about it: []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1521.xhtml_page_230}["till the Son of man were risen from the dead"]{.class_3054} (vs. 9). This is the second time Jesus has brought up His resurrection. Back in 8:31--32, Peter thought it was just defeatist crazy talk. Here, the disciples are ["questioning one with another what the rising from the dead should mean"]{.class_3054} (vs. 10). They don't understand it.

Look at verses 31--32 in this same chapter. Jesus brings it up a third time, and they still don't understand it. In fact, they don't even understand it after it took place. Look at John 20:9 and note the context. It is the morning of Christ's resurrection.

"For as yet they knew not the scripture, that he must rise again from the dead."

That sheds quite a light on John 8:56, wouldn't you say? Fundamentalists use that verse to say Abraham saw the Crucifixion and Resurrection, and was saved by believing in Christ just like you are. That's supposed to mean that salvation is the same in the Old Testament as it is in the New Testament.

What a bunch of tommyrot! Abraham had no idea when he offered up Isaac that it was a type of God the Father offering up His Son. All Abraham believed was that God would be able to raise Isaac ["from the dead"]{.class_3054} to keep the promise He had made (Heb. 11:17--19). The only reason you see it, sitting up in the Twenty-first Century, is because you are reading a complete Bible. Not one Old Testament writer could even guess at such a thing (1 Pet. 1:11). If the disciples who heard Jesus speak plainly about it didn't even get it, what makes you think Abraham, with even less revelation than they had, would get it?

Now go back and read the end of verses 9 and 10 again: ["till the Son of man were risen FROM the dead"]{.class_3054} (vs. 9); ["questioning one with another what the rising FROM the dead should mean"]{.class_3054} (vs. 10). Every orthodox Jew believed in the ["resurrection OF the dead"]{.class_3054} (Acts 23:6, 8). They believed in a general ["resurrection at the last day"]{.class_3054} (John 11:24), with a general judgment to follow, just like any good Catholic or Moslem. What they didn't understand was a resurrection ["FROM the dead"]{.class_3054} where someone came up BEFORE the general resurrection, never to die again.

When Jesus told Martha[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1521.xhtml_page_231}[ "I am the resurrection and the life: he that believeth in me, though he were dead, yet shall he live"]{.class_3054} (John 11:25), He was saying that anyone who believes on Him for salvation is coming up BEFORE the "Last Day." Why? [Because He came up after His death.]{.class_3055}

["Yet a little while, and the world seeth me no more; but ye see me: because I live, ye shall live also"]{.class_3054} (John 14:19).

In fact, if it were not for the resurrection of Christ 2,000 years ago, [no one]{.class_3055} would come up in [any]{.class_3055} resurrection.

["For since by man came death, by man came also the resurrection OF the dead. For as in Adam all die, even so in Christ shall ALL be made alive. But every man in his own order: Christ the firstfruits; afterward they that are Christ's at his coming. Then cometh the end, when he shall have put down all rule and all authority and power"]{.class_3054} (1 Cor. 15:21--24).

Notice the order in the verses and the time gaps at the punctuation marks. The resurrection of Christ is [first;]{.class_3055} then the resurrection of the dead in Christ at the Rapture (cf. 1 Thess. 4:16). There is a 2,000-plus-year gap represented by the semicolon in 1 Corinthians 15:23. Those are the first two parts of what Revelation 20:5 calls ["the first resurrection."]{.class_3054} Following the analogy given in 1 Corinthians 15:23, Christ would be ["the firstfruits"]{.class_3054} of that resurrection, and the Body of Christ at the Rapture would be the main harvest. At the end of the Tribulation, preceding the Advent, there is a rapture of Tribulation saints (see our work on [The Two Raptures]{.class_3055}); that would be "the gleanings."

At the end of the Millennium is the "general resurrection." It involves the unsaved dead of all ages, as well as the ["saints"]{.class_3054} from the Tribulation and Millennium (and possibly saved Gentiles from the Old Testament). That is ["the resurrection OF the dead."]{.class_3054} You don't want to come up at that resurrection. For you, in this age, that is a resurrection where the outcome has already been decided (John 3:18). You will be sentenced for your crimes against a [holy God.]{.class_3055} The dead from this age, that come up at the general resurrection, come up from ["death and hell"]{.class_3054} (Rev. 20:13). That means they died in their sins without Christ, with ["the wrath of God"]{.class_3054} on them (John []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1521.xhtml_page_232}3:36). They are being brought up to be given an appropriate punishment, according to their works, in the Lake of Fire (Rev. 20:12--15).

The saved dead from this age came up over a thousand years before in ["the first resurrection"]{.class_3054} (Rev. 20:5). Those who came up in that resurrection are described as ["Blessed and holy"]{.class_3054} because[ "on such the second death hath no power"]{.class_3054} (Rev. 20:6). So if you have any sense, you don't want ["the resurrection OF the dead"]{.class_3054} that every lost Jew, Catholic, Moslem, Hindu, Buddhist, atheist, agnostic will experience; as well as every Baptist, Methodist, Presbyterian, Episcopalian, Lutheran, and Charismatic who trusted their own works, church attendance, giving, denomination, "initial evidence," signs, wonders, sacraments, baptism, communion, etc., instead of [the finished work of Jesus Christ on Calvary.]{.class_3055} You want the resurrection ["FROM the dead"]{.class_3054} that comes by faith in Jesus Christ and His work for you, where you are ["given the earnest of the Spirit"]{.class_3054} (2 Cor. 1:22, 5:5) and ["buried with him by baptism into death"]{.class_3054} \[and I don't mean [water]{.class_3055}---1 Cor. 12:13\] and ["raised up from the dead"]{.class_3054} to ["walk in newness of life"]{.class_3054} (Rom. 6:4).

11 And they asked him, saying, Why say the scribes that Elias must first come?

12 And he answered and told them, Elias verily cometh first, and restoreth all things; and how it is written of the Son of man, that he must suffer many things, and be set at nought.

13 But I say unto you, That Elias is indeed come, and they have done unto him whatsoever they listed, as it is written of him.

Now we have a problem. Christ has just told the disciples He is coming up ["from the dead"]{.class_3054} (vs. 9). Not understanding what the Lord means (vs. 10), the disciples just assume He is talking about the resurrection associated with ["the great and dreadful day of the LORD" ]{.class_3054}(Mal. 4:5 cf. Isa. 26:19; Dan. 12:1--3). Why? Because that's all the revelation they had.

Brethren, here you are, sitting up here in the year 2016 and []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1521.xhtml_page_233}beyond. You have the completed revelation and the work of men like Bullinger, Larkin, Scofield, Pember, Pentecost, et al. You have the Church Age, the Rapture, the Tribulation, the Second Advent, the Millennium, the White Throne Judgment, and the New Heaven and New Earth all laid out for you. Moreover, you have the indwelling Holy Spirit to teach you the truth (John 16:13).

The disciples didn't have that. All they had was the Old Testament; the Holy Spirit hadn't been given yet (John 7:39). When they read the Old Testament, they often read of the First and Second Advents [right together,]{.class_3055} with no intervening Church Age (see our comments on Luke 4:18--19 in that Commentary). Sometimes, the Advents are even given [backwards.]{.class_3055} Moreover, the Day of the Lord can refer to several different events (see our comments on Joel 1:15 in the [Bible Believer's Commentary on the Minor Prophets,]{.class_3055} Vol. I). Without the New Testament revelation and the guidance of the Holy Spirit, the whole thing can be a convoluted mess.

With that in mind, you can understand the disciples' question in verse 11: ["Why say the scribes that Elias must first come?"]{.class_3054} In other words, "If the general resurrection is about to take place, why hasn't Elijah come as the scribes teach from Malachi 4:5--6?" Even Peter, James, and John could see that the appearance of Elijah on the Mount of Transfiguration was not a fulfillment of the public ministry of Elijah prophesied in Malachi 4:6.

The answer Jesus gives reveals the nature of His First Advent. As we have said before, in the Gospels, everything is set up at the beginning of Christ's ministry for both Advents (see our remarks under 1:3). Jesus' answer is: ["Elias verily cometh first, and restoreth all things . . . BUT I say unto you, That Elias is indeed come"]{.class_3054} (vss. 12--13). In other words, Elijah [is going to come]{.class_3055} (future), but [has already come]{.class_3055} (past). Matthew 17:13 adds after this: ["Then the disciples understood that he spake unto them of JOHN THE BAPTIST."]{.class_3054}

According to Jesus Christ, John the Baptist was the fulfillment of the prophecy about Elijah showing up before the Second Advent. [Not according to John the Baptist he wasn't]{.class_3055}---["And they asked him, What then? ART THOU ELIAS? And he saith, I AM NOT"]{.class_3054} (John 1:21). "Hey, John, are you Elijah?" "Nope."

Now there is a sure enough "contradiction" if you ever saw it. Jesus said John was Elijah, and John said he wasn't. Who's right? [They both are.]{.class_3055} Luke 1:17 says John came ["in the spirit and power of Elias"]{.class_3054} and then gives the prophecy of Malachi 4:6 concerning Elijah's ministry before Christ's Advent.

But John fulfilling that prophecy was [conditional.]{.class_3055} You say, "On what?" [On whether Israel would accept what John the Baptist said about Jesus Christ]{.class_3055} (Matt. 11:14). If Israel, as a nation, had accepted John's testimony about Jesus, then both Advents would have taken place in the Gospels and Acts. The Romans would have put Christ to death, but the Jews would have had no complicity in it. A rapture could have taken place in Acts 7 (see our comments on Acts 7:56 in that Commentary), and Daniel's Seventieth Week could have taken place then and there. But the Jews didn't accept Christ as their Messiah, so John the Baptist wasn't Elijah, and Malachi 4:5--6 still awaits future fulfillment.

That fouls up Calvin's system. God will fulfill His purpose one way or another, but He has things set up so that no matter which way you go, He can work things out the way He said without contradicting Himself. God lets you have your free will and still gets His way.

Back to verse 12: ["and how it is written of the Son of man, that he must suffer many things, and be set at nought."]{.class_3054} A reference to the prophecies of the Suffering Servant of Psalm 22 and Isaiah 53. The greatest type of Christ in the Old Testament, Joseph (a type of Christ in 153 particulars), suffered as a slave and prisoner in Egypt before being exalted to the position of second ruler below Pharaoh. David lived in exile before becoming King of Israel. ["The sufferings of Christ"]{.class_3054} had to precede ["the glory that should follow"]{.class_3054} (1 Pet. 1:11). No cross, no crown.

["Nought"]{.class_3054} is the archaic spelling of our word [naught,]{.class_3055} meaning "nothing." You will find both words used in your [King James]{.class_3055} Bible, but ["naught"]{.class_3054} in the [AV]{.class_3055} is a shortened version of [naughty,]{.class_3055} meaning "bad" (2 Kings 2:19; Prov. 20:14 cf. Prov. 6:12, 17:4; Jer. 24:2). ["Set at nought"]{.class_3054} means the Jews disregarded Christ's claims to being the Messiah and God's Son, and despised Him (Psa. 22:16; Isa. 53:3).

["Whatsoever they listed"]{.class_3054} in verse 13 is an archaic way of saying "Whatever they pleased," "Whatever suited them." What []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1521.xhtml_page_235}suited leaders like the Pharisees and Sadducees was to attribute John's preaching to ["a devil"]{.class_3054} (Matt. 11:18) and ignore it (Mark 11:31). Undoubtedly, ["as it is written of him"]{.class_3054} is a reference to Elijah's general complaint in 2 Kings 19:10, 14. Although nobody killed Elijah as Herod did John the Baptist (the Antichrist will in the future---Rev. 11:7), it was not for want of trying (1 Kings 18:10, 19:2).

14 And when he came to his disciples, he saw a great multitude about them, and the scribes questioning with them.

15 And straightway all the people, when they beheld him, were greatly amazed, and running to him saluted him.

16 And he asked the scribes, What question ye with them?

17 And one of the multitude answered and said, Master, I have brought unto thee my son, which hath a dumb spirit;

18 And wheresoever he taketh him, he teareth him: and he foameth, and gnasheth with his teeth, and pineth away: and I spake to thy disciples that they should cast him out; and they could not.

19 He answereth him, and saith, O faithless generation, how long shall I be with you? how long shall I suffer you? bring him unto me.

20 And they brought him unto him: and when he saw him, straightway the spirit tare him; and he fell on the ground, and wallowed foaming.

21 And he asked his father, How long is it ago since this came unto him? And he said, Of a child.

22 And ofttimes it hath cast him into the fire, and into the waters, to destroy him: but if thou canst do any thing, have compassion on us, and help us.

23 Jesus said unto him, If thou canst believe, all things are possible to him that believeth.

24 And straightway the father of the child cried out, and said with tears, Lord, I believe; help thou mine unbelief.

25 When Jesus saw that the people came running together, he rebuked the foul spirit, saying unto him, Thou dumb and deaf spirit, I charge thee, come out of him, and enter no more into him.

26 And the spirit cried, and rent him sore, and came out of him: and he was as one dead; insomuch that many said, He is dead.

27 But Jesus took him by the hand, and lifted him up; and he arose.

Although Mark and Matthew do not mention the time element between the events of verses 1--13 and verse 14, Luke does: it was ["on the next day"]{.class_3054} (Luke 9:37). Mark mentions the fact that ["the scribes"]{.class_3054} were ["questioning"]{.class_3054} the disciples whom Jesus had left behind (vs. 14), which is not found in the accounts of Matthew and Luke.

Then there is the peculiar observation in verse 15 that the ["great multitude"]{.class_3054} of verse 14 ["were greatly amazed" "when they beheld"]{.class_3054} Jesus. Why? It makes you wonder if there were not some "residual effect" of the Transfiguration that shown on the face of Jesus as Moses' face shone after talking with God on Mt. Sinai (Exod. 34:30).

Now the ["questioning"]{.class_3054} of verse 14 turns out to be grilling the disciples on why the lot of them combined could not cast a devil out of a boy (vs. 18). This devil was ["a dumb spirit"]{.class_3054} (vs. 17); i.e., it was an evil spirit that prevented the boy from talking. The evil spirit would lead this poor child around, causing him to tear himself, foam at the mouth, gnash his teeth at people, and waste away to the point of death (["pineth away"]{.class_3054}). The father of the boy adds in verse 22: ["ofttimes it]{.class_3054} \[i.e., the evil spirit\][ hath cast him into the fire, and into the waters, to destroy him."]{.class_3054} We have already commented on this, and the sadism of devils, in our remarks on the characteristics of demon possession under 5:1--5 (which see). The father had brought his son to the nine apostles Jesus had left behind, but they could not deliver the boy. Their apostolic signs (3:15) were "on the blink."

Now the Bible believer needs to note how the scholars handle the []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1521.xhtml_page_237}case of this demon-possessed boy. In the parallel account in Matthew 17:15, the father says the boy ["is lunatick,"]{.class_3054} a word meaning "moonstruck"; we would say he is [mad]{.class_3055} or [crazy.]{.class_3055} But when the Liberals in the Communist National Council of Churches hit that verse in the [RSV,]{.class_3055} they changed ["lunatick"]{.class_3054} to ["an epileptic."]{.class_3055}

You say, "Well, that's because they were Liberals and didn't believe the Bible." Do you know how the "Conservatives" who revised the [RSV]{.class_3055} to give you the [English Standard Version]{.class_3055} handled the word? [The exact same way.]{.class_3055} Ditto the Evangelicals who put out the [NIV: "He has seizures."]{.class_3055}

Do you know what conclusion you would draw if you [believed]{.class_3055} the text of those "Bibles." You would have to come to the conclusion that epileptics are demon possessed, and that epilepsy is a [spiritual]{.class_3055} condition instead of a [physical]{.class_3055} disorder. Moreover, if you went over to Matthew 4:24 (the only other occurrence of ["lunatick"]{.class_3054} in the Bible), you would be left with no example of Jesus being able to do anything for madness.

Now when it comes to this matter of lunacy, in comparing Matthew 4:24 and Matthew 17:15, we learn that some madness is a [spiritual]{.class_3055} condition that requires [spiritual]{.class_3055} deliverance, while other cases of insanity are medical or psychological disorders requiring medicines or counseling. It takes someone with spiritual discernment to tell which. In the Middle Ages, the Catholic Church treated all insane people as demon possessed; today, it says none of them are. Neither extreme is right. Some insanity is caused by a chemical imbalance or a traumatic incident, but a lot of it is caused by a person giving place to the Devil due to [sin.]{.class_3055}

Back to Mark 9: ["O faithless generation"]{.class_3054} (vs. 19). To whom is Jesus referring? [His disciples!]{.class_3055} In Matthew 17, when the disciples ask Him why they couldn't cast out that devil, Jesus says, ["Because of your UNBELIEF"]{.class_3054} (Matt. 17:19--20). Both Matthew and Luke add the description of ["perverse"]{.class_3054} to Christ's words here in verse 19 (cf. Matt. 17:17; Luke 9:41). So not only did they not believe, they were [wicked.]{.class_3055} Jesus said to the disciples once, ["If ye then, BEING EVIL . . ."]{.class_3054} (Matt. 7:11; Luke 11:13). If that was Christ's assessment of men whom He personally chose to follow Him and be the leaders of the Church, where does that leave [us?]{.class_3055} The Apostle Paul took it for []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1521.xhtml_page_238}granted that he was no good (Rom. 7:18).

Notice the difference in how ["suffer"]{.class_3054} is used in verse 19 as to how it was used in verse 12. Back in verse 12, it was used as we normally use the word today: "to experience something bad or painful." In verse 19, the use might be "dated," as one dictionary put it, but it is not "archaic." There it means "to tolerate" or "to put up with." You could have figured that out from the context; there was no need to corrupt the Scriptures in 64,000 places for something like that. A marginal note could have handled it without destroying the reader's faith in the Book God gave him.

What Jesus is saying in verse 19 is: "How long do I have to stay here and put up with a bunch of people who won't believe what I say because they are obstinate and wicked?" The Lord may be longsuffering, but He won't put up with your foolishness forever (see Num. 14:11--35).

Verse 22:[ "but if thou canst do any thing, have compassion on us, and help us."]{.class_3054} The despair of the father is evident in his request. He is suffering right along with his son: ["have compassion on US . . . help US."]{.class_3054} He desires from Christ what Jesus has shown so many others here in the book of Mark: ["compassion"]{.class_3054} (1:41, 5:19, 6:34, 8:2). If Allah is "the Compassionate" as [some]{.class_3055} (certainly [not all or even many]{.class_3055}) translations of the [Koran]{.class_3055} read in the opening of the first Sura, he certainly never showed his compassion in any substantive way. Jesus [healed]{.class_3055} out of compassion; He [delivered]{.class_3055} out of compassion; He [taught]{.class_3055} out of compassion; He [fed]{.class_3055} out of compassion. If giving the [Koran]{.class_3055} to a sex-obsessed, demon-possessed, vengeful, murderous camel jockey turned military dictator is any sign of Allah's "compassion," he can keep it.

Note Christ's answer to the father in verse 23: ["If thou canst believe, all things are possible to him that believeth."]{.class_3054} Jesus is requiring from this man what His own disciples didn't have---faith enough to believe the boy could be delivered.

Of course, we understand that the ["all things"]{.class_3054} of this promise are subject to the [will and purpose of God]{.class_3055} for the believer. Paul had the faith to be healed of his ["thorn in the flesh, the messenger of Satan to buffet me,"]{.class_3054} and the Lord turned him down three times []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1521.xhtml_page_239}(2 Cor. 12:7--8). Why? Well, it certainly was not over lack of faith. It was so Paul would not be puffed up with pride over what he had seen in Glory (2 Cor. 12:1--7), and so God's strength would be perfected in Paul's weakness (2 Cor. 12:9). Promises like Mark 9:23 and John 14:13--14 are not a [carte blanche]{.class_3055} for getting whatever you want from the Lord so you can ["consume it upon your lusts"]{.class_3054} (James 4:3).

Now we have already seen a certain amount of unbelief in the father when he said, ["IF thou canst do any thing."]{.class_3054} After the failure of the disciples, there is some doubt in the father's mind whether their Master could do any more than they could. Given that, you wouldn't think this fellow would get his request granted. After all, James wrote:

["But let him ask in faith, nothing wavering. For he that wavereth is like a wave of the sea driven with the wind and tossed. For let not that man think that he shall receive any thing of the Lord"]{.class_3054} (James 1:6--7).

The father, though, recognizes his weakness of faith, and in so doing, he makes a great statement: ["Lord, I believe; help thou mine unbelief"]{.class_3054} (vs. 24). When you pray for something that is hard to believe that you'll get granted, don't pretend you believe when you don't. Believe as much as you can and ask God's help with what you can't believe.

Now not only is verse 24 a great statement on how to handle unbelief in our prayer lives, it is also a clear testimony to the Deity of the Lord Jesus Christ.
