Why, it is as simple and easy as [look and live]{.class_3055} (John 3:14 cf. Num. 21:8--9).

The manuscript evidence for dropping ["for them that trust in riches"]{.class_3054} is [four]{.class_3055} Greek manuscripts: [Sinaiticus, Vaticanus, Delta,]{.class_3055}[ ]{.class_3079}and [Psi]{.class_3055}. The footnote to this verse in the [NIV]{.class_3055} and [ESV]{.class_3055} (the [RSV]{.class_3055} in "Conservative" sheep's clothing) reads: "SOME manuscripts add [for those who trust in riches."]{.class_3055} Well, if [only four]{.class_3055} manuscripts omit the phrase, surely "some" can't be that many that support the reading of the [King James,]{.class_3055} can they? Surely these "great, godly, dedicated" translators and revisers wouldn't [lie]{.class_3055} about manuscript evidence, would they? Surely their motives are pure, right? Don't they want you to have what God actually said?

Don't bet your last dollar on it. Here are the "some manuscripts" that have the [KJV]{.class_3055}[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1732.xhtml_page_273} reading: ALL THE REST of the Greek manuscript evidence on the passage, ALL four families of Greek manuscripts, ALL the versions, including both the Old Latin and the Vulgate, and the only church father to quote the passage: Clement of Alexandria from the Second Century, a good 100 years before [Vaticanus]{.class_3055} and [Sinaiticus]{.class_3055} were written. The "some manuscripts" were [everything else]{.class_3055}---the [vast majority]{.class_3055} of the manuscript evidence. Those birds would make a politician look honest.

The warning of Jesus Christ is not to ["trust in riches."]{.class_3054} That warning is all throughout the Bible. Job didn't trust in his riches (Job 31:24--25). David told you trusting in riches couldn't redeem you before God (Psa. 49:6--7). God said He would destroy the man that trusted in his riches and wealth (Psa. 52:5--7). The richest man who ever lived (Solomon) said trusting in riches is a conceit (Prov. 18:11) that will lead to a downfall (Prov. 11:28). The Apostle Paul gave a charge to rich folk not to ["trust in uncertain riches"]{.class_3054} (1 Tim. 6:17). And when the Laodicean church boasted of its riches and goods, the Lord Jesus told them: ["thou art wretched, and miserable, and poor, and blind, and naked"]{.class_3054} (Rev. 3:17). The only thing that makes salvation[ "hard"]{.class_3055} or ["difficult"]{.class_3055} is [trusting something else besides God]{.class_3055} to get it.

Back to Mark 10: ["It is easier for a camel to go through the eye of a needle, than for a rich man to enter into the kingdom of God"]{.class_3054} (vs. 25). Having just eliminated the warning about trusting in riches in verse 24, the scholars can't understand the hyperbole and sarcasm behind Christ's words in verse 25. To make sure you know that although it is ["hard"]{.class_3055} and ["difficult"]{.class_3055} to enter the Kingdom of God it is still doable, they are going to totally screw up Christ's words in verse 25 so that you can accomplish your own salvation by WORKS! They make ["the eye of a needle"]{.class_3054} some "needle's eye" gate to a city where camels could enter at night after the main gates were shut. It was "difficult" and "hard," but a camel could make it through by crawling through on its knees. Of course, no such gate has ever been found anywhere in the land of Israel, but why let facts stop you when you are trying to prove how good enough you are to get to Heaven on your own?

["And they were astonished out of measure, saying among themselves, Who then can be saved?"]{.class_3054}[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1732.xhtml_page_274} (vs. 26). Oh, come on, Peter, James, John, Andrew, Matthew, Thomas, et al., why act so surprised? Anyone can be saved who puts forth the effort of a [trained camel.]{.class_3055} If the "needle's eye" gate did exist, surely it was a common enough occurrence for camels to get through it. So any self-righteous Jew, Catholic, Greek Orthodox, Episcopalian, Lutheran, Methodist, Baptist, Presbyterian, Assembly of God, Church of Christ, etc., can make it on his own.

If that were the case, Jesus wouldn't have said, ["With men it is IMPOSSIBLE"]{.class_3054} (vs. 27). [The camel of verse 25 didn't make it through the eye of that needle.]{.class_3055} It needed a [miracle---]{.class_3055}["with God all things are possible."]{.class_3054} Jesus is making a point with hyperbole (exaggeration) and sarcasm. The only way to get that camel through the eye of a literal needle was for God to shrink it down to size; ergo, the only way for a rich man to be saved is for God to accomplish the miracle of the New Birth in him when he stops trusting his riches and starts trusting Christ.

28 Then Peter began to say unto him, Lo, we have left all, and have followed thee.

29 And Jesus answered and said, Verily I say unto you, There is no man that hath left house, or brethren, or sisters, or father, or mother, or wife, or children, or lands, for my sake, and the gospel's,

30 But he shall receive an hundredfold now in this time, houses, and brethren, and sisters, and mothers, and children, and lands, with persecutions; and in the world to come eternal life.

31 But many that are first shall be last; and the last first.

Like always, Peter speaks up. Whenever there is a situation with the Lord and the disciples, Peter is usually the one to open his mouth and put his foot in it. But even when Peter makes a big blunder like he did back in 8:32--33, his heart is still right.

Peter voices what all the disciples were probably thinking: ["we have left all, and have followed thee"]{.class_3054} (vs. 28). He realizes he and the rest of the Twelve have done what Jesus asked the rich, []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1732.xhtml_page_275}young ruler to do. In the Matthew account, this statement is followed up with a question: ["what shall we have therefore?"]{.class_3054} (Matt. 19:27). In other words, "Since we have left everything and followed you like you required of that rich, young ruler, what are we getting out of this deal?"

Christ's answer in verse 29 shows the level of commitment Jesus required of those disciples in the Gospels. The principle is found in Luke 14:26.

"If any man come to me, and hate not his father, and mother, and wife, and children, and brethren, and sisters, yea, and his own life also, he cannot be my disciple."

Jesus defines that "hatred" in Matthew 10:37. You are not to love your parents, your spouse, your children, your siblings, or [yourself ]{.class_3055}more than you love Him. So when a controversy arises between what that Book says and what momma and daddy say, or your husband or wife says, or what your children or grandchildren say, or what your brothers and sisters say, or what [you]{.class_3055} say, you are to side with the Book. ["The first and great commandment"]{.class_3054} (Matt. 22:38) is to come FIRST. You are to love God with all your heart, soul, strength, and mind BEFORE you love your neighbor as yourself.

["For my sake, and the gospel's"]{.class_3054} (vs. 29). We have already commented on the [motivation]{.class_3055} for the believer under our remarks on 8:35, so we refer you there for those.

Verse 30: ["But he shall receive an hundredfold now in this time, houses, and brethren, and sisters, and mothers, and children, and lands."]{.class_3054} The "prosperity gospel" people like that part of the verse. They figure if you serve God it will automatically mean you'll be healthy and wealthy. But that's not all the verse: ["WITH PERSECUTIONS."]{.class_3054}

The promise is: ["But my God shall supply all your need according to his riches in glory by Christ Jesus"]{.class_3054} (Phil. 4:19). It's: ["And God is able to make all grace abound toward you; that ye, always having all sufficiency in all things, may abound to every good work"]{.class_3054} (2 Cor. 9:8). The Lord will give you anything you need to accomplish whatever He has called you to do, but in the process, you are going to "get it in the neck." ["Yea, and []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1732.xhtml_page_276}all that will live godly in Christ Jesus shall suffer persecution"]{.class_3054} (2 Tim. 3:12).

["And in the world to come eternal life."]{.class_3054} Again, while we can make spiritual application here to New Testament salvation, it is evident [here,]{.class_3055} before the death, burial, and resurrection of Christ, that [works]{.class_3055} are involved in getting ["eternal life."]{.class_3054} But one should also note that, unlike the Gospel of John which was written for the express purpose of bringing a sinner to faith in Christ to get eternal life (John 20:31), that often in the Gospels of Matthew, Mark, and Luke, ["eternal life" ]{.class_3054}or ["everlasting life"]{.class_3054} (Matt. 19:29) is equated with an inheritance in Christ's Millennial kingdom. Look at the parallel passage to this one in Matthew 19:27--30 and note verse 28.

"And Jesus said unto them, Verily I say unto you, That ye which have followed me, in the regeneration when the Son of man shall sit in the throne of his glory, ye also shall sit upon twelve thrones, judging the twelve tribes of Israel."

So you have to be careful as you go through there. A distinction is made for us, doctrinally, this side of Calvary, between inheriting eternal life through faith in Christ's blood atonement and resurrection, and an inheritance when the Kingdom of God becomes visible during Christ's Millennial reign. That distinction is not made for Old Testament Jews under the Law before Christ's death, burial, and resurrection any more than a resurrection ["FROM the dead"]{.class_3054} was (see our comments on 9:9--10). As far as those Jews knew, if they kept the Law the way God intended, with the right heart attitude (1 Sam. 15:22; Isa. 1:11--17; Mic. 6:8; Matt. 9:13, 12:7), they would come up at the Last Day and be given eternal life when the Lord set up His Kingdom here on earth. They knew nothing about the Body of Christ composed of Jews and Gentiles, or a separate Rapture of that Body before ["the time of Jacob's trouble"]{.class_3054} (Jer. 30:7), or salvation by grace alone through faith alone in the finished work of Jesus Christ.

Verse 31:[ "But many that are first shall be last; and the last first."]{.class_3054} Now that's not only given in the parallel passage in Matthew 19:30, it's also given in the Parable of the Laborers in Matthew 20:16. The idea is that you, up here just before the return of []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1732.xhtml_page_277}Christ, can have the same rewards in Christ's kingdom as those apostles and early Christians who went through torture and martyrdom for their faith (James 1:12 cf. Rev. 2:10). Of course, that is a spiritual application to the Body of Christ. If you were to try to nail the thing down doctrinally, it would probably apply to those Tribulation saints who will be ["beheaded for the witness of Jesus"]{.class_3054} (Rev. 20:4).

32 And they were in the way going up to Jerusalem; and Jesus went before them: and they were amazed; and as they followed, they were afraid. And he took again the twelve, and began to tell them what things should happen unto him,

33 Saying, Behold, we go up to Jerusalem; and the Son of man shall be delivered unto the chief priests, and unto the scribes; and they shall condemn him to death, and shall deliver him to the Gentiles:

34 And they shall mock him, and shall scourge him, and shall spit upon him, and shall kill him: and the third day he shall rise again.

Verses 32--34 are a repeat of what Jesus did in 8:31. He is giving details of His death as prophecy, and you can see our comments on 8:31 for that. Here, Jesus gives ten details of His death. It would be like me saying on June 23, 2018, I'll be in downtown Pensacola, walking down the sidewalk, when a car jumps the curb and hits me. I will be rushed to the hospital in an ambulance driven by a man named Ralph. I'll die two minutes before reaching the hospital. My funeral will cost \$20,000, and I'll be buried the following Tuesday at Pensacola Memorial Gardens in a closed casket ceremony at 11:00 in the morning. Why, there is no way I could predict all those details with any certainty and get them all right on the money. Mohammed, Buddha, Confucius, Zoroaster, Joseph Smith, Ellen G. White, and Mary Baker Eddy would never even attempt such a thing. [Jesus Christ did,]{.class_3055} and everything He said came to pass just as He said it.

Folks, that Book does what no other book in this world does: it gives [history ahead of time.]{.class_3055} Not one book among the 23 million in []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1732.xhtml_page_278}the Library of Congress, outside of the Bible, can do that. I am not talking about what happens to you after death or in Eternity when time is no more. I am talking about events in the lives of individuals and nations that can be [verified.]{.class_3055} And I am not talking about lucky guesses as to the outcome of a battle like Mohammed made on [one]{.class_3055} occasion. I am talking about details that couldn't be "set up" or guaranteed to take place. That is why the "higher critics" try so hard to give late dates to the authorship of Bible books: they are trying to put the writings [after]{.class_3055} the prophecies in the books take place. But the evidence is against them. The Bible is a book of [history]{.class_3055} (its events really took place; they weren't "legends" or "fables" made up to teach a moral lesson), and it is a book of [prophecy]{.class_3055} (it tells you history before it takes place). Prophecy is the proof of [Deity]{.class_3055} (Isa. 41:22--23, 42:9, 44:7, 46:9--10), and it is the proof of [inspiration]{.class_3055} (2 Pet. 1:19--21). The Lord has given us enough fulfilled prophecy to prove to us the rest of the prophecies in the Book will also be fulfilled. There are about 400 prophecies on the Second Advent of Jesus Christ that will come to pass exactly as recorded in the Book.

35 And James and John, the sons of Zebedee, come unto him, saying, Master, we would that thou shouldest do for us whatsoever we shall desire.

36 And he said unto them, What would ye that I should do for you?

37 They said unto him, Grant unto us that we may sit, one on thy right hand, and the other on thy left hand, in thy glory.

38 But Jesus said unto them, Ye know not what ye ask: can ye drink of the cup that I drink of? and be baptized with the baptism that I am baptized with?

39 And they said unto him, We can. And Jesus said unto them, Ye shall indeed drink of the cup that I drink of; and with the baptism that I am baptized withal shall ye be baptized:

40 But to sit on my right hand and on my left hand is not mine to give; but it shall be given to them for whom it is prepared.

Verse 35 is supposed to be one of those "contradictions in the Bible" about which you hear so much. The verse says ["James and John"]{.class_3054} made the request, while the parallel account in Matthew 20:20 says their mother did. Of course, Matthew 20:20 says it was ["the mother of Zebedee's children WITH HER SONS."]{.class_3054} Why it never occurred to the nitpickers of Holy Scripture that [both]{.class_3055} the mother and her sons could have made the request and that Jesus answered [both,]{.class_3055} or that the boys asked [through their mother]{.class_3055} (see Matt. 8:5 cf. Luke 7:3--6) is anybody's guess.

Now, James and John ask Jesus for a "blank check" with a certain purpose in mind: ["Master, we would that thou shouldest do for us whatsoever we should desire"]{.class_3054} (vs. 35). Those ["sons of thunder"]{.class_3054} (3:17) had what the Jews call "chutzpah"---[shameless audacity.]{.class_3055}

But in the next chapter, Jesus commands all the disciples to do, basically, what James and John are doing here: ["What things soever ye desire, when ye pray, believe that ye receive them, and ye shall have them"]{.class_3054} (11:24). Of course, as I have pointed out before, that promise is not without [conditions]{.class_3055} (cf. James 4:3). The request of the two brothers, here, is not only selfish, but out of the will and purpose of God. They won't get what they ask because God intended to give it to others.

The request is: ["Grant unto us that we may sit, one on thy right hand, and the other on thy left hand, in thy glory"]{.class_3054} (vs. 37). That ["glory"]{.class_3054} is the Millennial kingdom He sets up at His Second Advent (Matt. 25:31). So they want the right and left hand positions in the kingdom.

Of course, James and John don't comprehend what they are asking (vs. 38). Jesus just told them of His death, burial, and resurrection; and they don't believe it. They still think He was going to set up the kingdom right then, but He wasn't. But did you notice that when He was crucified, He had a sign over His head that read ["THE KING OF THE JEWS"]{.class_3054} (15:26)? That was absolutely true, but He didn't get the crown then. However, He did have somebody on His right side and His left side when He hung on the cross: ["two thieves; the one on his right hand, and the other on his left"]{.class_3054} (15:27). If James and John had known about that, they []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1732.xhtml_page_280}wouldn't have been so hasty to ask what they did.

You say, "That had nothing to do with the kingdom." Sure it did. One of those thieves said, ["Lord, remember me when thou comest INTO THY KINGDOM"]{.class_3054} (Luke 23:42). How could that possibly be, when Jesus was hanging there dying a terrible death with no crown and no throne? But that one thief looked at that inscription over Christ's head and [believed it was so.]{.class_3055} So even as ["Christ died for our sins,"]{.class_3054} the [kingdom]{.class_3055} was still the main emphasis.

Now ["the cup"]{.class_3054} and ["the baptism"]{.class_3054} of verses 38--39 are generalizations by Jesus to apply to the disciples. Technically speaking, no one but Jesus Christ could drink that ["cup"]{.class_3054} or receive that ["baptism,"]{.class_3054} for both had to do with the wrath of God He would take on Himself on the cross. When Jesus said, ["I have a baptism to be baptized with; and how am I straitened till it be accomplished"]{.class_3054} (Luke 12:50), it had nothing to do with [water]{.class_3055} baptism, because He had [already]{.class_3055} been baptized in water (Luke 3:21). That means, contrary to the Campbellite system of baptismal regeneration, water baptism isn't the "real thing." In the case here, water baptism is a [picture,]{.class_3055} just as it is in the case of salvation (Rom. 6:3--4). Baptism is an immersion, and the baptism Christ received on the cross was an immersion in the [wrath of God]{.class_3055} (Psa. 69:1--2, 14, 88:7, 17; Jon. 2:2--3). All those references picture Hell as a flood of water drowning a man.

Likewise ["the cup,"]{.class_3054} in the case of Christ, is not just death, as John R. Rice will tell you (see our comments on 14:36); it is the [cup of God's wrath on sin]{.class_3055} (Jer. 25:15; Psa. 75:8; Isa. 51:22). That being the case, there was no way James or John could partake of that cup and baptism in the exact same manner, so the application is to [suffering]{.class_3055} in general; specifically, suffering for Christ's sake. Jesus said that the world would hate the disciples like it hated Him and would persecute them like it persecuted Him (John 15:18--21).

["Ye shall indeed drink of the cup that I drink of; and with the baptism that I am baptized withal shall ye be baptized"]{.class_3054} (vs. 39). Although according to historical tradition, all of the Twelve (Matthias taking the place of Judas Iscariot---Acts 1) suffered martyrdom, James and John held special places. James was []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1732.xhtml_page_281}the first apostle to be martyred (Acts 12:2), and John was the last of the Twelve to suffer persecution (Rev. 1:9).

Now James and John couldn't get the right and left hands in Christ's kingdom because those positions were [already taken:]{.class_3055}[ "it shall be given to them for whom it is prepared"]{.class_3054} (vs. 40). It's [Moses and Elijah,]{.class_3055} and we have already discussed this under our remarks on 9:4. Over in Zechariah 4, you have two olive trees that are on the right and left sides of the golden candlestick in Heaven. The "menorah" is a picture of the Holy Spirit (Rev. 4:5) and of Jesus Christ as ["the light of the world"]{.class_3054} (John 8:12). They are defined as Moses and Elijah over in Revelation 11:4--6.

Someone will say, "They are Elijah and Enoch because those are the two men in the Old Testament who didn't die." No way in the world one of them is Enoch. Look here: there are people who make it through the Tribulation and enter the Millennial kingdom, and die at a very old age (Isa. 65:20). Elijah pictures them. There are saints who die in the Tribulation and come up in a post-Tribulation rapture (Isa. 26:19). Unlike the Church Age saints in Christ who receive a glorified body like their Saviour's (Phil. 3:21), those Tribulation saints have a normal body, like Lazarus, Jairus' daughter, and the widow of Nain's son, that goes into the Millennial kingdom, lives a long life, and dies. Who pictures that? Why, Moses. He died back there in Deuteronomy 34, was resurrected (Jude 9), and comes back in the Tribulation to die again (Rev. 11). If you make Enoch the second witness there in Revelation 11 instead of Moses, you have no picture of the only group that never dies and never will die---Church Age saints alive at the Rapture (1 Cor. 15:51--53; 1 Thess. 4:17).

41 And when the ten heard it, they began to be much displeased with James and John.

42 But Jesus called them to him, and saith unto them, Ye know that they which are accounted to rule over the Gentiles exercise lordship over them; and their great ones exercise authority upon them.

43 But so shall it not be among you: but whosoever will be great among you, shall be your minister:

44 And whosoever of you will be the chiefest, shall be []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1732.xhtml_page_282}servant of all.

45 For even the Son of man came not to be ministered unto, but to minister, and to give his life a ransom for many.

This is the second time in the Gospel of Mark where the disciples have had a dispute over which of them would be the greatest in Christ's kingdom (see our remarks under 9:33--34). ["The ten"]{.class_3054} are ["much displeased with James and John"]{.class_3054} (vs. 41) for trying to make an "end run" around them to get the chief seats in the kingdom. James and John may have been arrogant and proud in their request, but all the Twelve felt the same way: "We [deserve]{.class_3055} the highest position because we [earned]{.class_3055} it." They were just upset with James and John for "beating them to the punch" and asking first.

Verse 42 is the typical Gentile system from Nebuchadnezzar to Obama (Dan. 2). The "big shots" throw their weight around and order the peons around. But in Christ's kingdom (physical and spiritual), the way up is [down.]{.class_3055} If you want to be in charge, you have to be a [servant.]{.class_3055} The ["minister"]{.class_3054} of verse 43 is the "waiters on tables" of Acts 6:1--3; it is [diakonos]{.class_3055}---"deacon." The ["servant"]{.class_3054} of verse 44 is what Paul called himself in Romans 1:1---[doulos.]{.class_3055} It is a bondslave, knocked down on the block and purchased by God for His service (1 Cor. 6:20).

Jesus Himself is the example of what He spoke in verses 43--44: ["For even the Son of man came not to be ministered unto, but to minister"]{.class_3054} (vs. 45). At the Last Supper, He knelt before the disciples and washed their dirty feet (John 13:5) and set the example for them (John 13:15).

A Christian "minister" is to follow the example of his Saviour. He is to "wait on tables" by feeding ["the flock of God"]{.class_3054} (1 Pet. 5:2). Although an "overseer" of the flock he feeds (Acts 20:28), he is not to lord himself ["over God's heritage"]{.class_3054} (1 Pet. 5:3). The modern philosophy among Fundamental Baptists of "pastoral authority," where the pastor tells everyone in the congregation what to do and dictates to the congregation because he is the one in charge, is of the Devil. I may (or may not!) know the answer to your problem (or at least be able to tell you where to go to get the answer), but I can't []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1732.xhtml_page_283}and WON'T tell you what to do. I can tell you what the Bible says about a situation; but if you want to know whom to marry, where to work, where to live, what car to buy, etc., ["I pray thee have me excused"]{.class_3054} (cf. Luke 14:18--19). The pastor's authority is [spiritual,]{.class_3055} not physical or social. And any authority a pastor has is [from that Book.]{.class_3055} No pastor is the final authority for a believer; [the Bible]{.class_3055} is the final authority.

["And to give his life a ransom for many."]{.class_3054} The word ["ransom"]{.class_3054} implies that someone is being held hostage at a price. This is the doctrine of [redemption:]{.class_3055} "to buy back." The question is: "From whom is the sinner bought back?" "To whom is the ransom paid?" Many Christians are confused on this point. The ["ransom"]{.class_3054} wasn't paid to the Devil. While it is true that ["the devil"]{.class_3054} has ["the power of death"]{.class_3054} (Heb. 2:14), that is a power [designated to him by God.]{.class_3055} Those sacrifices in the Old Testament were offered to [God.]{.class_3055} The Lord said there in Exodus 12:13, ["When I see the blood, I will pass over you."]{.class_3054}[ God]{.class_3055} is the One satisfied with the sacrifice of His Son (Isa. 53:11); that's called ["propitiation"]{.class_3054} in the New Testament (Rom. 3:25; 1 John 2:2, 4:10).

The ["many"]{.class_3054} of verse 45 turns out to be ["ALL"]{.class_3054} in 1 Timothy 2:6. That means Jesus Christ died for more than just the "elect." He is not only ["the propitiation for our sins]{.class_3054} \[i.e., believers\][ . . . but also for THE SINS OF THE WHOLE WORLD"]{.class_3054} (1 John 2:2). And just in case you think that just means anyone in the whole world who trusts Christ for salvation, look over at 2 Peter 2:1 and note that Jesus Christ even died for lost false teachers headed for Hell. Just because sinners don't take advantage of the payment Christ made for their sins doesn't mean the payment wasn't made for them. Calvin's "Limited Atonement" is so much tommyrot.

46 And they came to Jericho: and as he went out of Jericho with his disciples and a great number of people, blind Bartimaeus, the son of Timaeus, sat by the highway side begging.

47 And when he heard that it was Jesus of Nazareth, he began to cry out, and say, Jesus, thou Son of David, have mercy on me.

48 And many charged him that he should hold his peace: but he cried the more a great deal, Thou Son of David, have mercy on me.

49 And Jesus stood still, and commanded him to be called. And they call the blind man, saying unto him, Be of good comfort, rise; he calleth thee.

50 And he, casting away his garment, rose, and came to Jesus.

51 And Jesus answered and said unto him, What wilt thou that I should do unto thee? The blind man said unto him, Lord, that I might receive my sight.

52 And Jesus said unto him, Go thy way; thy faith hath made thee whole. And immediately he received his sight, and followed Jesus in the way.

We come now to the account of blind Bartimaeus. We have already commented on the fact that, like the maniacs in Gadara, Matthew lists two blind men (Matt. 20:30), while Mark goes into detail about one (see our remarks on 5:2). Another so-called "contradiction" found here by the apostate critics of the Holy Bible is that Matthew and Mark say Jesus was [leaving]{.class_3055} Jericho (Matt. 20:29; Mark 10:46), while Luke says He was [coming to]{.class_3055} Jericho. This is easily explained by the fact that there were [two Jerichos:]{.class_3055} one the Jewish city destroyed by Joshua and rebuilt during the reign of Ahab (1 Kings 16:34) and the other a Roman city built by the Herods about a mile down the road. Jesus had left the Jewish city (Matthew and Mark) and was entering the Roman city (Luke) when He encountered Bartimaeus.

Now, this account is a wonderful picture of salvation, both of the Jews at the Second Advent and of a sinner during the Church Age. Notice the Messianic title of ["Son of David"]{.class_3054} used in verse 47. So blind Bartimaeus recognizes Jesus as the Messiah of Israel.

That is a picture of Israel turning to Christ at His return. Right now, Israel, as a nation, is blind (Rom. 11:25). Of course, individual Jews do come to Christ during the Church Age, which is why Romans 11:25 describes Israel's ["blindness"]{.class_3054} as ["in part."]{.class_3054} But at the Second Advent, Israel recognizes her Messiah (Zech. 12:10), and the whole []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1732.xhtml_page_285}nation turns to Christ (Rom. 11:26).

But in a spiritual sense, Bartimaeus is a type of a lost sinner in this age, blinded by ["the god of this world"]{.class_3054} (2 Cor. 4:4) and clothed in the filthy rags of his own self-righteousness (vs. 50 cf. Isa. 64:6). Bartimaeus ["heard that it was Jesus of Nazareth"]{.class_3054} (vs. 47). Someone has said that witnessing is simply one beggar telling another where to get a good meal.

["Have mercy on me."]{.class_3054} That's what the publican said in Luke 18:13---["God be merciful to me a sinner."]{.class_3054} That's from where we get the so-called "sinner's prayer." There's a movement going around among Bible-believing Baptists who aren't seeing anybody saved that you never got saved if you said the "sinner's prayer" because that's a "work" and you aren't saved by works (Eph. 2:8--9).

While it is certainly true that prayer in and of itself won't save any man, for [what]{.class_3055} you are praying can mean the difference between Heaven and Hell. If you are praying the "Lord's Prayer" or "Hail Mary," you are headed for Hell. If you are depending on "saying grace" or "bedtime prayers" to save you, ["ye are yet in your sins"]{.class_3054} (cf. 1 Cor. 15:17). But if you prayed to [accept Christ,]{.class_3055} you are headed for Heaven.

That "calling on the name of the Lord" in Romans 10:13 is an outward expression of the sinner's inward faith in [Christ's righteousness]{.class_3055} for salvation (Rom. 10:9--10). It is a natural thing when you want something to [ask]{.class_3055} for it. That leper in Mark 1:40 [asked]{.class_3055} for cleansing. That Syrophenician woman [asked]{.class_3055} Jesus to deliver her daughter (7:26). Blind Bartimaeus [asked]{.class_3055} Jesus to show mercy on him. Prayer may not be essential to salvation, but it is [natural.]{.class_3055}

Do you really think God is going to turn a sinner away who comes to Him in prayer, saying, "Lord, I know I am a sinner who deserves to go to Hell. I believe your Son died for my sins on the cross and rose again to take me to Heaven. I am trusting nothing but Him and His righteousness to save me. Would you please forgive my sins for Jesus' sake"? Do you think the Lord is going to turn a sinner like that away when He said, ["him that cometh to me I will in no wise cast out"]{.class_3054} (John 6:37)?

I know some of the brethren abuse that just to get numbers, but that is no reason to "retread" your congregation just because you []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1732.xhtml_page_286}aren't seeing anybody saved on a regular basis. Christians are confused enough these days without some pastor talking them out of their salvation.

["And many charged him that he should hold his peace: but he cried the more a great deal."]{.class_3054} As soon as a man wants to get saved, he gets opposition from the world. But Bartimaeus was like that Syrophenician woman: he was going to keep on asking until he got heard. That's known as ["importunity"]{.class_3054} in prayer (Luke 11:8). Bartimaeus had stopped begging for the scraps of this world and was going to the only One who could really help him.

["And Jesus stood still, and commanded him to be called . . . Be of good comfort, rise; he calleth thee"]{.class_3054} (vs. 49). Another slap at Calvinism; this time to the doctrine of "Unconditional Election." Jesus called a man to Him who had [called on Him first.]{.class_3055} A Calvinist will go to Ephesians 1:4 to prove that election was ["before the foundation of the world"]{.class_3054} and skip right over that ["IN HIM"]{.class_3054} in the verse. [No one was in Christ before Genesis 1:1.]{.class_3055} A sinner doesn't get in Christ until the Holy Spirit puts him there at [salvation]{.class_3055} (1 Cor. 12:13), and a sinner doesn't get saved until he [trusts Christ]{.class_3055} (Rom. 10:9--10; Eph. 2:8--9). Election is [conditioned]{.class_3055} on foreknowledge, and that foreknowledge is based on the Holy Spirit sanctifying you when you obeyed the Gospel and had Christ's blood atonement applied to you (1 Pet. 1:2).

Bartimaeus came to Jesus ["casting away his garment"]{.class_3054} (vs. 50). That's a picture of a sinner forsaking his own righteousness (Isa. 64:6) to receive ["the righteousness of God . . . by faith of Jesus Christ"]{.class_3054} (Rom. 3:22). Notice in verse 51 that Bartimaeus acknowledges Jesus as ["Lord."]{.class_3054} There was his ["faith"]{.class_3054} (vs. 52). As we have pointed out before, it is not faith itself that saves a man; it is the One in whom the faith is placed. It was not enough for Bartimaeus to believe he could receive his sight; he had to [trust Christ]{.class_3055} for the healing. It is not enough to believe in God to be saved (James 2:19); you have to believe in the One God sent to save you (John 3:16).

["And IMMEDIATELY he received his sight"]{.class_3054} (vs. 52). Instantaneous healing. No organ music to get you "in the mood," no theatrics like popping you on the forehead with the heel of the hand, no holding a conductor's watch against the bone behind the ear so []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1732.xhtml_page_287}the deaf person can feel the vibrations and think he's been healed, no staring up at thousand-watt stage lights so the blind person will claim to be healed because he can tell a bright light is shining in his eyes, no "misfires" or "short rounds"; just [full]{.class_3055} and [immediate]{.class_3055} healing. Bartimaeus got healed ["immediately."]{.class_3054} Salvation is not a "process." You get saved the moment you trust Christ. Sanctification may take a lifetime, but salvation is [immediate.]{.class_3055}

["And followed Jesus in the way."]{.class_3054} Bartimaeus was able to do what the rich, young ruler was not (vss. 21--22). Why was that? Because all Bartimaeus had to get rid of was a dirty, old beggar's garment. That rich, young ruler had more stuff than was good for him; all his possessions ended up [damning him.]{.class_3055}

When I got saved, I knew a good thing when I saw it. All I gave up was a drunkard's life that was sending me to an early grave, and a life of adultery and fornication that was busting up my family. There was nothing in the bars, honky-tonks, dives, and juke joints where I played drums in a dance band that was worth hanging onto. And the Catholic Church where I was taking convert courses sure wasn't helping. A moral life, an education, making good money, a nice house and car, and the "American dream" can be great hindrances to coming to Christ.

[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1907.xhtml}

# Chapter 11 {.heading_97}

11:1 And when they came nigh to Jerusalem, unto Bethphage and Bethany, at the mount of Olives, he sendeth forth two of his disciples,

2 And saith unto them, Go your way into the village over against you: and as soon as ye be entered into it, ye shall find a colt tied, whereon never man sat; loose him, and bring him.

3 And if any man say unto you, Why do ye this? say ye that the Lord hath need of him; and straightway he will send him hither.

4 And they went their way, and found the colt tied by the door without in a place where two ways met; and they loose him.

5 And certain of them that stood there said unto them, What do ye, loosing the colt?

6 And they said unto them even as Jesus had commanded: and they let them go.

7 And they brought the colt to Jesus, and cast their garments on him; and he sat upon him.

This is known as the "Triumphal Entry" of Jesus Christ. He is entering Jerusalem as the Jewish King. This is a fulfillment of a Messianic prophecy on the Second Advent---Zechariah 9:9 (see Matt. 21:5; John 12:14--15). Again, we point out to you that the First and Second Advents are right together in the Gospels; they aren't revealed as separate until after Acts 7.

Look at Genesis 49:11. This is a prophecy given 1,170 years before Zechariah 9:9 and 1,722 years before Mark 11.

["Binding his foal unto the vine, and his ass's colt unto the choice vine . . . ."]{.class_3054} There's that ["colt the foal of an ass"]{.class_3054} of Zechariah 9:9; that's a prophecy of Christ's Triumphal Entry. But look at the rest of the verse.

[". . . he washed his garments in wine, and his clothes in the blood of grapes."]{.class_3054} There's the Second Advent of Christ, where His garments are stained with the blood of His enemies like []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1907.xhtml_page_289}["him that treadeth in the winefat"]{.class_3054} (Isa. 63:1--3).

Both Advents right together, separated by only a semicolon. If the Jews had accepted their Messiah, both the leaders as well as the common folk, the Second Advent could have taken place within seven years of the Triumphal Entry.

Now Zechariah 9:9 describes Jesus as He described Himself in Matthew 11:29---["lowly."]{.class_3054} Although by the time of Christ donkeys and mules were considered lowly beasts of burden, that was not the case in the Old Testament. Asses and mules were the "rides" of judges and Kings in Israel (Judg. 10:4, 12:14; 2 Sam. 13:29; 1 Kings 1:33, 38, 44). So the Lord's choice of transportation was symbolic: it fulfilled Scripture and let the people know the King of Israel was coming to offer the Kingdom.

["Bethphage and Bethany"]{.class_3054} (vs. 1) were villages on the Mount of Olives east of Jerusalem. They were at the limit of the distance a Jew was allowed to travel on the Sabbath (Luke 24:50 cf. Acts 1:12). Bethany was the hometown of Mary, Martha, and Lazarus (John 11:1). ["The mount of Olives"]{.class_3054} should alert the reader that the passage has a Second Coming application, for that is where Jesus comes to liberate Jerusalem from the armies of the nations at His return (Zech. 14:1--4).

In keeping with the typology of the Second Coming, notice ["he sendeth forth two of his disciples"]{.class_3054} (vs. 1). Here, [in type,]{.class_3055} are the two witnesses of Revelation 11, who showed up at Christ's Transfiguration in chapter 9.

Notice, in verses 1--2, Jesus exercising the attribute of His Deity known as "omniscience" (all knowing). J. Vernon McGee, while acknowledging this could have been a display of Christ's omniscience, immediately turns around and casts doubt in his listeners' minds by telling them the whole thing could have been "prearranged." "It doesn't seem necessary to read a miracle in here when the natural explanation is in order." Where did he get that "natural explanation"? Why, [nowhere in Scripture!]{.class_3055} He came up with it out of his own noggin.

Why, after reading 8:31 and 9:31, would anybody even entertain a "natural explanation" of Christ's knowledge unless he was as []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1907.xhtml_page_290}Liberal as the translators from the National Council of Churches who put out the [RSV?]{.class_3055} "Are you saying J. Vernon McGee was a Liberal, Ruckman?" Not at all. I am saying J. Vernon McGee was a saved, "good," "godly," "dedicated" Conservative--Fundamentalist who, from time to time, [talked like an unsaved Liberal]{.class_3055}---just like Stewart Custer (see our remarks on 6:14--15).

"It doesn't seem necessary to read a miracle in here"! To that I have the same thing to say as I did to one of my professors at Bob Jones University who said something to the effect that ["the books"]{.class_3054} at the While Throne Judgment aren't necessarily real books (Rev. 20:12). I said, "It doesn't mean they necessarily can't be either."

Have you ever noticed how often, when given a choice of exalting God, Christ, and the Book, or degrading them, the scholars choose the latter? When the Greek word [pais]{.class_3055} in Acts 4:27, 30 can mean "son" ([KJV]{.class_3055}---["child"]{.class_3054}) or "servant," the translators will choose the reading that detracts from the doctrine of the Incarnation of Christ. When the rules of Hebrew grammar demand the translation of ["the Son of God"]{.class_3054} in Daniel 3:25, the translators will opt for ["a son of the gods"]{.class_3055} based on their own opinion of what Nebuchadnezzar knew (see our comments on Dan. 3:25 in that Commentary). When the Evangelicals who put out the [NIV]{.class_3055} and the Fundamentalists who put out the [NASV]{.class_3055} had a choice on how to translate Isaiah 14:12, they chose a reading that equated [Satan with Jesus Christ]{.class_3055} (cf. Rev. 22:16). When J. Vernon McGee had a choice of teaching on Christ's Deity or His humanity, he chose the Lord's humanity based on nothing but [his own opinion.]{.class_3055}

Now what you are reading in this passage is a great picture of the Lord saving a lost sinner and then using him. The first thing you need to note about this ass colt was that he was [untamed:]{.class_3055}[ "whereon never man sat"]{.class_3054} (vs. 2). So this ass colt is wild; it hasn't been tamed yet. That Bible says an unsaved man is ["born like a wild ass's colt"]{.class_3054} (Job 11:12). So this donkey is a picture of an unsaved man: he hasn't been ridden, which means he hadn't been tamed.

I had a farmer in Wing, Alabama tell me that wild asses are the most stubborn animals in the world. They like to run around in the woods at night, and some of their mothers are wild. They can't be tamed until they've been ridden, and they don't understand a []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1907.xhtml_page_291}whipping until they've been broken in. Unsaved men are stubborn (Rom. 1:32; 2 Thess. 2:12). They love ["darkness rather than light"]{.class_3054} because ["their deeds ]{.class_3054}\[are\][ evil"]{.class_3054} (John 3:19). They are "wild" because their mothers are "wild" (Psa. 51:5, 58:3; Job 15:14; John 3:6). That unsaved man can't be "tamed" until he's been "ridden" by the Lord; in other words, the Lord has to come in there and take control (Eph. 2:12--19). And no unsaved man can understand the Lord's chastisement because he's not a son (Heb. 12:5--8).

["Loose him"]{.class_3054} (vs. 2). The colt is ["tied"]{.class_3054} and needed to be [loosed]{.class_3055} before Christ could use it. The unsaved man, Jesus said, ["is the servant of sin"]{.class_3054} (John 8:36); he's in ["bondage"]{.class_3054} to it (cf. Rom. 8:15). He can only be freed from it by ["the law of the Spirit of life in Christ Jesus"]{.class_3054} (Rom. 8:2), and ["If the Son therefore shall make you free, ye shall be free indeed"]{.class_3054} (John 8:36).

["Bring him."]{.class_3054} Matthew 21:2 adds, ["unto me."]{.class_3054} The unsaved man has to be brought to Jesus Christ, just like that palsied fellow back in chapter 2. That's how Peter got saved: his brother Andrew ["brought him to Jesus"]{.class_3054} (John 1:42). Our job as saved people is to bring sinners to Christ: ["ye shall be witnesses unto me"]{.class_3054} (Acts 1:8). When that lord held that ["great supper"]{.class_3054} in Luke 14:16, he commanded his servants, ["Go out quickly into the streets and lanes of the city, and BRING IN HITHER the poor, and the maimed, and the halt, and the blind . . . . Go out into the highways and hedges, and COMPEL them to come in, that my house may be filled"]{.class_3054} (Luke 14:21, 23).

Back to Mark 11: ["And if any man say unto you, Why do ye this? say ye that THE LORD HATH NEED OF HIM" ]{.class_3054}(vs. 3). Now isn't that something? Why would God have ["need"]{.class_3054} of a critter that is stupid and stubborn after he's tamed, much less before he's been "broken in"?

["The Lord hath NEED of him."]{.class_3054} One of the great attributes of God is His self-sufficiency. You couldn't get along one moment without God, but He can get along just fine without you. If the Lord blew the whole "kit and caboodle" to a "fare thee well" tomorrow, it wouldn't phase Him one bit; He would go on being God.

That being the case, why would He ever "need" a godless []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1907.xhtml_page_292}reprobate like you or me? I mean, He told those Jews back there in the Old Testament, ["If I were hungry, I would not tell thee: for the world is mine, and the fulness thereof"]{.class_3054} (Psa. 50:12). Well, theologically, the Lord doesn't really "need" us, but the Lord condescends to use stubborn, stupid people like us. That's [grace]{.class_3055}, brother. It sure has nothing to do with our own merits or intelligence or strength or "connections" or anything else.

Isn't it charitable and thoughtful of God to think of us (Psa. 8:4)? He could have left us alone in the world without hope and without God, aliens from the covenants and promises (Eph. 2:12), but instead, He reached down by His Holy Spirit and touched our hearts and said, ["You:]{.class_3055} I want [you."]{.class_3055} I must admit, I don't understand it, but I sure do praise God for it.

The Lord is able to take that lowly beast of burden and turn it into a creature of [war]{.class_3055} and [judgment.]{.class_3055} Over in Song of Solomon 6:13, the Lord describes His followers at His Second Coming: ["As it were the company of TWO ARMIES."]{.class_3054} One of those armies is Christ's Bride on ["white horses"]{.class_3054} (Rev. 19:14), and the other is the Old Testament saints ["on white ASSES"]{.class_3054} (Judg. 5:10).

Beloved, that Bible says, ["For the earnest expectation of the creature waiteth for the MANIFESTATION OF THE SONS OF GOD"]{.class_3054} (Rom. 8:19). You may not look like much now, but one day, you are going to be [just like your Saviour]{.class_3055} (1 John 3:2; Rom. 8:29). The "jackass" is going to be a [warhorse]{.class_3055} someday.

Notice in verse 4 that that colt was tied ["by the door in a place where two ways met."]{.class_3054} Isn't that instructive? Jesus said there is a ["broad"]{.class_3054} way that leads ["to destruction, and MANY there be which go in thereat"]{.class_3054} (Matt. 7:13). The Old Testament says of that way: ["There is a way that seemeth right unto a man, but the end thereof are the ways of death"]{.class_3054} (Prov. 16:25). That's one way; it's the way on which the [majority]{.class_3055} of people are. It's broad enough to include atheism, Buddhism, Catholicism, Hinduism, Greek Orthodoxy, Protestantism, evolution, Mohammedanism, moralists, reprobates, middle class, high class, no class, educated, illiterate---[anybody]{.class_3055} who is depending on [his own self-righteousness]{.class_3055} to get him by.

Then there's the second way. It leads to eternal life, but it is a ["narrow"]{.class_3054} way that ["few"]{.class_3054}[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1907.xhtml_page_293} find (Matt. 7:14). The reason so few find it is because they have to forsake their own righteousness for the righteousness of Another (Rom. 10:3--4; Tit. 3:5). The way is so narrow that it is by one man and one man only (1 Tim. 2:5). You won't make it through Mary, Mohammed, the Pope, St. Peter, the Dalai Lama, Mother Teresa, Zoroaster, Buddha, or any other "god." You will come to God by way of His Son, or [you won't come]{.class_3055} (John 14:6).

Those two ways meet by a ["door."]{.class_3054} Christ said, ["I am the door: by me if any man enter in, he shall be saved"]{.class_3054} (John 10:9); and if you come in any other way, you're ["a thief and a robber"]{.class_3054} (John 10:1).

Back in the "march on Selma," some blackrobed "hoods" from the Catholic Church came down to try to take advantage of the colored folk. One of those Priests came up to a godly old black man who had been a deacon in a Baptist church for years and years, and asked him, "What church do you belong to?"

The black deacon said, "I's a Baptist."

That Catholic Priest said, "You belong to the wrong church. Jesus gave the keys to Simon Peter; you need to join the church that has the keys."

That old colored Christian said, "Shucks, boss, I don't need dem keys; I got de doah!"

I don't need any "keys" from the Catholic Church. I've got the whole door. I'll have gone ["in and out"]{.class_3054} and have found ["pasture"]{.class_3054} (John 10:9) while that Priest is still fumbling around for his keys.

["And they brought the colt to Jesus . . . and he sat upon him"]{.class_3054} (vs. 7). That's the "goal": Jesus is to be in charge. He is to have the reins and direct us to where He wants us to go. Hugh Pyle used to say, "The Resident should be President." If you have ["Christ in you, the hope of glory"]{.class_3054} (Col. 1:27), He should be the One on the throne of your heart, not you. Hugh Pyle illustrated his point by saying, "In your heart is a throne and a cross. When you are on the throne, Christ is on the cross, and when Christ is on the throne, you're on the cross." [You]{.class_3055} are to be on the [cross]{.class_3055} (see comments on 8:34); Jesus Christ is to be in the "driver's seat."

8 And many spread their garments in the way: and others cut down branches off the trees, and strawed them in the way.

9 And they that went before, and they that followed, cried, saying, Hosanna; Blessed is he that cometh in the name of the Lord:

10 Blessed be the kingdom of our father David, that cometh in the name of the Lord: Hosanna in the highest.

Now that spreading of the ["garments in the way"]{.class_3054} in verse 8 is indicative of two things. The first is the welcoming of a dignitary. This is called the "red carpet" treatment today. It is a recognition of a person's high rank or social standing. These Jews recognized immediately the King of Zechariah 9:9 entering Jerusalem.

At this point, most of the expositors and commentators "jump ship" and assume that since Christ didn't set up His kingdom, that the Jews were mistaken as to Christ's intention at the Triumphal Entry. The Pre­millennialist will usually limit this to His First Coming, while the Amillennialists and Postmillennialists will extend it to both Advents. That last proposition denies three-quarters of your Bible. The theme of the Old Testament is the King and the kingdom. For every prophecy Jesus fulfilled at His First Advent, there are at least four more awaiting to be fulfilled at His Second Advent. To spiritualize such prophecies away by applying them to the Church is to wrest the Scriptures to one's own destruction (2 Pet. 3:16).

As I have taught you many times before, up until Acts 7, everything is set up for both Advents to be fulfilled together; everything is "in flux." The offer of the kingdom by the King was legitimate. The key is what the Jews would do with their King (Matt. 23:37; Luke 13:34; John 19:14--15; Matt. 27:22). When the Jews reject Him in the Gospels, Christ asks the Father to forgive their ignorance (Luke 23:34). So they are given a second chance in Acts 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, and 7. When the Sanhedrin killed Stephen, the offer of the kingdom was withdrawn until a later date. One of the ways you know that is so is that ["the mystery of Christ,"]{.class_3054} that Jews and Gentiles who trusted Christ for salvation would be in the same spiritual body, was revealed [after]{.class_3055} Acts 7 (Eph. 3:3--6).

That is why you won't find the message of the death, burial, and resurrection preached by Peter and the other apostles at Pentecost connected to ["our sins"]{.class_3054} (cf. 1 Cor. 15:3--4). That is why the sins of the [nation of Israel]{.class_3055} were said to ["be blotted out"]{.class_3054} at [the return of Christ]{.class_3055} (Acts 3:19--21), and not at Calvary or upon salvation (Col. 2:11--14). That is why the first time the blood atonement [for sins]{.class_3055} is preached, it is preached [after]{.class_3055} Acts 7 (Acts 8:28--35 cf. Isa. 53:10).

The second thing the ["garments in the way"]{.class_3054} symbolized was [subjection to the King.]{.class_3055} The Jews are taking off what provided them protection from the elements and casting them on the ground for the King to ride over. It's a symbol of their [humiliation.]{.class_3055}

But not only did the Jews cast their garments on the road before Christ, they ["cut down branches off the trees, and strawed them in the way"]{.class_3054} (vs. 8). The word ["strawed"]{.class_3054} means they scattered the branches over the road until they covered it up; a more modern spelling would be [strewed]{.class_3055}. John 12:13 tells you the ["branches"]{.class_3054} were the ["branches of palm trees"]{.class_3054}; hence, the title "Palm Sunday" for the Sunday before "Easter." The palm branches speak of [victory]{.class_3055} (Rev. 7:9) and are associated with the Second Advent (Lev. 23:40 cf. Zech. 14:16--18). So the Jews are welcoming Christ into Jerusalem as a [conquering King.]{.class_3055}

Verses 9--10 are a quote from Psalm 118:25--26. ["Hosanna"]{.class_3054} is the ["save now"]{.class_3054} of Psalm 118:25. The Jews recognized the passage as a Messianic one. It is obvious to the people that Christ is offering the kingdom (["Blessed be the kingdom of our father David that cometh in the name of the Lord,"]{.class_3054} vs. 10; cf. Luke 1:32).

But notice what is missing from the quote. Psalm 118:25---["I beseech thee, send now prosperity."]{.class_3054} Psalm 118:26---["we have blessed you out of the house of the LORD."]{.class_3054} The ["prosperity"]{.class_3054} of Psalm 118:25 is what Christ called, ["the things which belong unto thy peace"]{.class_3054} in Luke 19:42. Jesus said, ["they are hid from thine eyes"]{.class_3054} (Luke 19:42). Why? Because despite all of the[ "hullabaloo"]{.class_3055} at the Triumphal Entry, the Jews, [as a people,]{.class_3055} refused to be gathered under Christ's ["wings"]{.class_3054} (Matt. 23:27). They don't bless Christ ["out of the house of the LORD."]{.class_3054} The priests and scribes throw Him out after He throws out their dove sellers and moneychangers (vs. 15--18). The Temple goes from being [His house]{.class_3055} (vs. 17) to [their house]{.class_3055}[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1907.xhtml_page_296} (Matt. 23:38), and even though the people had blessed Him that came ["in the name of the Lord,"]{.class_3054} the kingdom He would have brought if they had believed on Him was put off. They wouldn't see Him again [as a nation]{.class_3055} until they said, ["Blessed is he that cometh in the name of the Lord"]{.class_3054} at His Second Advent (Matt. 23:39).

11 And Jesus entered into Jerusalem, and into the temple: and when he had looked round about upon all things, and now the eventide was come, he went out unto Bethany with the twelve.

12 And on the morrow, when they were come from Bethany, he was hungry:

13 And seeing a fig tree afar off having leaves, he came, if haply he might find any thing thereon: and when he came to it, he found nothing but leaves; for the time of figs was not yet.

14 And Jesus answered and said unto it, No man eat fruit of thee hereafter for ever. And his disciples heard it.

Verses 11--12 show you the importance of each Gospel. A lot of the material in Mark is also covered in Matthew, but if you didn't have Mark, you wouldn't have the exact time element of Christ's "Passion Week." No other Gospel tells you what happened on each day of that week leading up to Christ's crucifixion, except Mark. Mark shows you that "Good Friday" is a [horselaugh.]{.class_3055}

1\. Christ enters into Jerusalem [Sunday.]{.class_3055} He goes to the Temple, looks around, and leaves to spend the night at Bethany (vs. 11).

2\. Christ comes back to Jerusalem [Monday]{.class_3055} (vs. 12). On the way, He curses the fig tree (vs. 14). While in Jerusalem, He cleanses the Temple (vs. 15). He leaves Jerusalem Monday evening (vs. 19).

3\. He returns to Jerusalem [Tuesday morning]{.class_3055} (vs. 20). All of the rest of chapter 11, as well as chapters 12 and 13, take place Tuesday. Chapter 14 opens up with: ["After two days was the feast of the passover" ]{.class_3054}(14:1). That made Wednesday ["the preparation"]{.class_3054} (15:42) when the Passover lamb was slain (14:12). ["The sabbath"]{.class_3054} of 15:42, therefore, would not be the regular Sabbath but a "high []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1907.xhtml_page_297}Sabbath" (John 19:31 cf. Lev. 23:5--7). "Good Friday" would actually be "Bad Wednesday."

Note in verses 11 and 19 that Jesus [leaves]{.class_3055} Jerusalem at evening. He either spends the night at Bethany with Mary, Martha, and Lazarus (vs. 11 cf. John 12:1), or He would sleep outdoors on the Mount of Olives (Luke 21:37). There is no record in the Gospels of Jesus spending the night in Jerusalem. That doesn't take place until the Millennial kingdom (Isa. 12:6, 23; Jer. 3:17; Joel 3:21; Zech. 2:10), when the city is called ["Jehovah-shammah"]{.class_3055}---["The LORD is there"]{.class_3054} (Ezek. 48:35).

In verse 13, we have what I call the "fig leaf factor." It is [profession without possession.]{.class_3055} The word ["haply"]{.class_3054} means "perchance." As a [man]{.class_3055} not exercising His divine omniscience, Jesus comes to the fig tree, hoping for fruit to satisfy His hunger, even though ["the time of figs was not yet."]{.class_3054} Even though it was not time for the Summer figs, sometimes in Palestine, the unripe figs of Fall will survive the Winter and ripen in the Spring. The other possibility is that this could have been the last of the Winter figs, which were usually harvested in March. Either way, the presence of the leaves presupposed fruit, for as Bullinger points out, figs appear [before]{.class_3055} or [with]{.class_3055} the leaves. Jesus, therefore, had a right to expect fruit from the tree.

The spiritual application is made by Jesus Christ Himself in Luke 13:6--9. You see, all the way back in the Garden of Eden, fig leaves represented [self-righteousness]{.class_3055} (see Gen. 3:7 and comments in that Commentary). Israel had a [profession]{.class_3055} of righteousness [without possessing it]{.class_3055} (Rom. 10:3). The Lord sought for righteousness in His people and found none. When ["the righteousness of God"]{.class_3054} showed up (Rom. 10:3--4), they had Him crucified. Therefore, the Lord cursed the nation so that it couldn't bear fruit for another [2,000 years.]{.class_3055} It won't bear fruit as a nation until the Lord returns at the Second Advent and gives it a [resurrection]{.class_3055} (Song of Sol. 2:10--13 cf. Ezek. 37:1--14).

15 And they come to Jerusalem: and Jesus went into the temple, and began to cast out them that sold and bought in the temple, and overthrew the tables of the []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1907.xhtml_page_298}moneychangers, and the seats of them that sold doves;

16 And would not suffer that any man should carry any vessel through the temple.

17 And he taught, saying unto them, Is it not written, My house shall be called of all nations the house of prayer? but ye have made it a den of thieves.

18 And the scribes and chief priests heard it, and sought how they might destroy him: for they feared him, because all the people was astonished at his doctrine.

19 And when even was come, he went out of the city.

This is the [second]{.class_3055} cleansing of the Temple. The first one took place in John 2:13--16. So there are two cleansings of the Temple, representing Christ's two Advents. Jesus connected the first cleansing to His First Advent (John 2:19--21), and it is obvious from the context here in Mark 11 that this second cleansing is connected to His Second Advent. Look at what Malachi prophesied of Christ bringing in His kingdom.

["But who may abide the day of his coming]{.class_3054} \[Second Advent\][? and who shall stand when he appeareth? for he is like a refiner's fire, and like fullers' soap ]{.class_3054}\[someone's about to get [cleansed]{.class_3055}\][: And he shall sit as a refiner and purifier of silver: and he shall purify the sons of Levi ]{.class_3054}\[the priests who serve in the Temple\][, and purge them as gold and silver, that they may offer unto the LORD an offering in righteousness]{.class_3054}\[the Old Testament sacrifices are coming back---Col. 2:16--17 cf. Ezek. 40--46\][. Then shall the offering of Judah and Jerusalem be pleasant unto the LORD, as in the days of old, and as in former years"]{.class_3054} (Mal. 3:2--4).

Now verse 15 is your "gentle Jesus meek and mild." He's taking those merchants in the Temple and giving them the "bum's rush" like a bouncer in a bar. He's overturning ["the tables of the moneychangers"]{.class_3054} and kicking the chairs out from under the dove sellers. And if you bought something in the Temple and were trying to get it back out to your cart to haul it home, that was just too bad for you that day: ["And would not suffer that any man should carry any vessel through the temple"]{.class_3054} (vs. 16). Don't you want []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1907.xhtml_page_299}to be more "Christ-like" and let others "see Christ in you"?

One time I had taught on the radio twelve things the fear of the Lord would do for you, as given in the Scriptures. And so help me, Hannah, on the program that came on after me (the "Lutheran Hour," I believe it was) if the speaker didn't say [three times]{.class_3055} in the first five minutes that there was no fear connected with salvation or serving Christ. The Devil sure does have a long reach, doesn't he?

Now look at that passage. Is that the Christ most Americans recognize? What would you think if the visiting evangelist came into your church, bodily threw out all the deacons, turned over the communion table, and kicked the pianist and organist out of their seats? Would you have him back in for the next "revival" and recommend his "ministry" to other churches? Do you understand now why ["the scribes and chief priests . . . sought how they might destroy him"]{.class_3054} (vs. 18)? Jesus Christ wasn't following Dale Carnegie's ["How to Win Friends and Influence People"]{.class_3055}; He was more concerned about His Father's integrity and the integrity of His word, the Scriptures. He didn't care about anybody's "feelings" at all.

In verse 17, Jesus quotes Isaiah 56:7. The context of that prophecy is the [Millennium.]{.class_3055} That's when the nations ["go up to the mountain of the LORD, to the house of the God of Jacob"]{.class_3054} (Isa. 2:3) and when prayer will be offered up to the Lord from all the Gentile peoples (Mal. 1:11).

The ["den of thieves"]{.class_3054} of verse 17 is also a quote from the Old Testament: Jeremiah 7:11. Comparing the context of Mark 11 with that of Jeremiah 7 shows us that, in God's eyes, exalting our righteousness over that of God's is as bad as stealing, murder, adultery, cussing, and idolatry (see Jer. 7:9).

Those scribes and chief priests had set up a "flea market" in the Temple to make money off God's people. That was covetousness, and the Holy Spirit said ["covetousness . . . is idolatry"]{.class_3054} (Col. 3:5). The idol wasn't Baal; it was [mammon]{.class_3055} (Matt. 6:24; Luke 16:13).

Verse 18: ["And the scribes and chief priests heard it, and sought how they might destroy him."]{.class_3054} That's what they had been trying to do since 3:6 (which see). Why? ["For they feared him."]{.class_3054} This is the Owner's Son come to take charge of the ["vineyard"]{.class_3054} [away from them]{.class_3055} (12:1--12).

["Because all the people was astonished at his doctrine."]{.class_3054} Again, that was the case since the beginning of His ministry (see 1:22 and our comments). That's because Christ had an authority those chief priests and scribes didn't have: [a final authority from God.]{.class_3055} Those scribes, Pharisees, and Sadducees had made God's words of no effect by adding their own words and traditions to them; they had no authority higher than their own opinions (see our comments on 7:6--13). Ditto modern Christian scholarship.

20 And in the morning, as they passed by, they saw the fig tree dried up from the roots.

21 And Peter calling to remembrance saith unto him, Master, behold, the fig tree which thou cursedst is withered away.

22 And Jesus answering saith unto them, Have faith in God.

23 For verily I say unto you, That whosoever shall say unto this mountain, Be thou removed, and be thou cast into the sea; and shall not doubt in his heart, but shall believe that those things which he saith shall come to pass; he shall have whatsoever he saith.

24 Therefore I say unto you, What things soever ye desire, when ye pray, believe that ye receive them, and ye shall have them.

As Christ and the disciples return to Jerusalem Tuesday morning, they notice the fig tree Jesus had cursed the day before was now ["withered away," "dried up from the roots" ]{.class_3054}(vss. 20--21). That is the nation of Israel (see our remarks on vs. 14). Christ fixes that nation God chose for His own people so that if they didn't believe on Him after His resurrection, [they couldn't bear fruit.]{.class_3055} He has Titus come in in A.D. 70 and destroy the only place where they could make sacrifices to Jehovah (Deut. 12:5--14). God took away the Jews' King in 586 B.C., and He took away their sacrifice in A.D. 70 (Hos. 3:4). They don't get either one back until the One they rejected in the Gospels returns in power. Until then, [as a people,]{.class_3055} they are ["withered away," "dried up from the roots"]{.class_3054} (cf. Ezek. 17:2, 9--[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1907.xhtml_page_301}10).

Now instead of making the prophetic application to Israel in this age, Jesus chooses, rather, to give the disciples a practical lesson on faith and prayer. Of course, one will have a hard time explaining the passage. The Charismatics come the closest to the correct view, but then get all fouled up in applying it.

As the verses stand, they are a "blank check" from God: ["he shall have whatsoever he saith"]{.class_3054} (vs. 23); ["What things soever ye desire . . . ye shall have them"]{.class_3054} (vs. 24). The stipulation in verses 23--24 is ["believe"]{.class_3054} and ["not doubt."]{.class_3054} So the Charismatics put on a pretense and profession of faith and have a "name it and claim it" theology; it is also known as "positive confession" and the "Word-Faith movement." "Have the faith of God and speak it into existence"---that kind of mess.

Now look at the example in verse 23 of what is possible through faith and prayer: ["That whosoever shall say unto this mountain, Be thou removed, and be thou cast into the sea."]{.class_3054} That takes place at Christ's Second Advent (Isa. 40:3--4; Zech. 4:7; Rev. 6:14). If Jesus is referring to the mountain from which they had just come---the Mount of Olives (Luke 21:37)---that takes place literally at the Second Advent too (Zech. 14:4). So the literal fulfillment could be to the ministry of the apostles during the Tribulation (see Matt. 10:23 and comments in that Commentary).

Of course, anybody can make application spiritually and devotionally. You can say Jesus was using hyperbole and speaking metaphorically of the difficult things in life. But if you think even then that you are going to get a "yes" answer to every prayer you pray simply because you have enough faith, you have "another think" coming. Not even the greatest Christian in the New Testament got everything for which he asked in prayer (2 Cor. 12:7--9). The Christian who does not take into account God's purpose and plan for him is in for a big disappointment.

There is a children's story about Amy Carmichael, the missionary to the girls in India who were offered as sex slaves to the priests in the Hindu temples. When she was a little girl, she had brown eyes, but wanted blue eyes. So in child-like faith, she asked the Lord for blue eyes. Of course, she didn't get them. She didn't []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1907.xhtml_page_302}understand at the time why God did not answer her prayer, but she did as a missionary in India. As a Westerner, she couldn't get into the Hindu temples to find out what happened to the little girls who were sold by their parents to the priests. So she stained her face with coffee to look like an Indian woman. If she had had blue eyes, her "cover" would have been "blown." God could see years down the road what she could not.

Now I don't want to discourage anyone from claiming the promise of verses 23--24. If the Lord gives you the faith to believe something from Him that is seemingly impossible from a human standpoint, go for it. Sometimes God does say yes. After World War II, a British missionary lady named "Miss Irene" set up an orphanage for the abandoned children of American soldiers and Japanese women. She led those little girls to Christ and taught them the Bible. There was one little girl there who was sick and bedridden. After hearing a lesson on this passage, she prayed God would move a mountain that blocked her view of the sea. When the other little girls got to go to the beach, she couldn't go because of her illness, so she prayed God would get rid of the mountain so she could at least see the ocean.

Well, "Miss Irene" tried to explain to that girl that Jesus wasn't talking about literal mountains, but that little sick girl kept on praying. She was so persistent in her prayers that all the other girls in the orphanage joined her. One day after that missionary lady returned from furlough in England, she saw all those little girls beside that sick girl's window. To her amazement, the mountain was gone, and that sick girl had a clear view of the sea. "What happened to the mountain?" she asked. "Men came with bulldozers and moved it away," they said. What had happened was the coastline was being eroded away, so the town used that mountain for fill dirt on the coast. Mountains are moved all the time by earthquakes, avalanches, dynamite, bulldozers, etc. Who is to say those weren't answers to prayers, just like the prayers of those orphans there in Japan. If the Lord gives you that kind of faith, and you are going to use the answer for His glory (don't forget [that!]{.class_3055}), then pray.

25 And when ye stand praying, forgive, if ye have ought []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1907.xhtml_page_303}against any: that your Father also which is in heaven may forgive you your trespasses.

26 But if ye do not forgive, neither will your Father which is in heaven forgive your trespasses.

Now answer me something: does that sound like Pauline theology to you? Sure, the principle of forgiving others as Christ forgave you is there (Eph. 4:32; Col. 3:13), but is that how you get your sins forgiven? Don't you get your sins forgiven by faith in Christ and His blood atonement (Acts 13:38; Col. 1:14; 1 John 1:7, 2:12)? Well, how many of your sins were forgiven when you trusted Christ? Weren't they [all]{.class_3055} forgiven? Well, what do you do with verses 25--26 here?

Do you see the mess you can make of that Book when you don't rightly divide it (2 Tim. 2:15)? God deals with different people differently in different dispensations; there's no getting around it. Forgiving others as Christ has forgiven you may be necessary for your [fellowship with God]{.class_3055} as a believer, but it has nothing to do [now]{.class_3055} with your sins being forgiven at salvation. [It did before the Crucifixion and Resurrection]{.class_3055} (see Matt. 18:23--25).

Of course, you certainly can make a [practical application to prayer]{.class_3055} (vs. 25). Having a bitter, vengeful, unforgiving spirit can keep you from getting your prayers answered; but it [won't]{.class_3055} send you to Hell if you are saved. Brethren, your soul is saved when you trust Christ, but your body [isn't]{.class_3055} and [won't be]{.class_3055} till the Rapture. You can reap in your body for the sins you sow in your body (Gal. 6:7--8); that includes being unforgiving towards others who have wronged you.

27 And they come again to Jerusalem: and as he was walking in the temple, there come to him the chief priests, and the scribes, and the elders,

28 And say unto him, By what authority doest thou these things? and who gave thee this authority to do these things?

29 And Jesus answered and said unto them, I will also ask of you one question, and answer me, and I will tell you []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1907.xhtml_page_304}by what authority I do these things.

30 The baptism of John, was it from heaven, or of men? answer me.

31 And they reasoned with themselves, saying, If we shall say, From heaven; he will say, Why then did ye not believe him?

32 But if we shall say, Of men; they feared the people: for all men counted John, that he was a prophet indeed.

33 And they answered and said unto Jesus, We cannot tell. And Jesus answering saith unto them, Neither do I tell you by what authority I do these things.

Jesus and the disciples have now come to the Temple on Tuesday morning, and they are approached by ["the chief priests, and the scribes, and the elders" ]{.class_3054}(vs. 27). So far, those men have proven themselves to be the enemies of God and of the truth. Now there was nothing wrong with any of those positions. God instituted the Aaronic priesthood back there in Leviticus. He had Moses pick out seventy of the elders of the people so they could receive God's Spirit, just like Moses, in order to help Moses bear the burden of leading the Israelites (Num. 11:16--17). Ezra ["was a ready scribe in the law of Moses,"]{.class_3054} and God's hand was on him (Ezra 7:6).

The problem wasn't the positions; it was the men in those positions. Who you are doesn't change with your location or status. When you come into a church building, you are the same person who fussed with your spouse in the car all the way to church. Your character doesn't change just because you teach a Sunday school class. These priests, scribes, and elders were more wicked on the inside than the publicans and harlots they despised (Matt. 23:25--28 cf. Luke 18:10--14).

["By what authority doest thou these things? and who gave thee this authority to do these things?"]{.class_3054} (vs. 28). Those questions may not seem like much, but they are the summation of everything in that Bible from Genesis 1 to Revelation 22. Those are the [universal]{.class_3055} questions; they are summed up by the question: "Who (or what) is in charge?" Since Lucifer tried to exalt himself in Isaiah 14:13--14, the history of the universe has been the struggle to see []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1907.xhtml_page_305}who runs whom. Who's in charge of the home, the husband or the wife? Who's in charge of the church? Who's in charge of the government? Who's in charge of the nations? Who'll get the Stanley Cup? Who'll win the Super Bowl? Who'll win the World Series? Who'll control the nuclear stockpiles? Which religion will rule the world? Who's the "top dog"; who's the "King of the mountain"?

Some of the brethren believe I am the leader of a "cult." Well, a cult exalts something or someone else as the final authority over the word of God. [I am not the final authority for anyone in my church or any of our graduates.]{.class_3055} No graduate of mine has to write back to the church or the school to get direction on anything. When a young man graduates from the Pensacola Bible Institute, he leaves with his authority in his hands---a [King James Authorized Version of the Holy Bible. That's]{.class_3055} the final authority for anybody who follows my teachings; [not me.]{.class_3055}

Now if you want a "cult," I can show you one. This is a universal cult in Christianity: it cuts across all denominational or theological lines. It exalts an altered text over the text to which the Holy Spirit bore witness throughout church history. It exalts the opinions of Christian "scholarship" over [any text]{.class_3055} of [any Bible]{.class_3055} in [any language.]{.class_3055} I call it the "Alexandrian Cult" because it sprang out of the first Christian school in Alexandria, Egypt. It controls the faculties and staffs of every major Christian institution of higher learning in America, Europe, and the mission fields. Its acolytes occupy the pastorates of a good 95% (if not more) of the pulpits of local churches, be they Fundamental, Conservative, Evangelical, "Neo-evangelical," Liberal, Modernist, "Emergent" (Neo-Liberals), or Charismatic. It teaches that a man can change that Book or correct that Book at his own discretion, or at the discretion of someone else. That Book is subject to [him]{.class_3055} and not him to the Book. That's as cultic as anything in the Mormons, Jehovah's Witnesses, or Seventh-day Adventists.

Verse 29: ["And Jesus answered and said unto them, I will also ask of you one question, and answer me."]{.class_3054} As we are about to see in chapter 12, Jesus can successfully answer any question put to Him, but when He turns the tables on those questioning Him, they can't answer His questions. That is a dreadful []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1907.xhtml_page_306}forecast of the judgment. When you die, you will hit the final authority. When you do, you can ask Him all the questions you want, and He will answer every one of them with Scripture (Rom. 3:2--4). When you are done, though, it will be His turn to question you, and you are going to be left speechless (Rom. 3:19). That shows you Who the authority is.

Now if you want to see the difference between an honest man and these birds here, compare how hypocrites answer Jesus here in verses 31--33. When Jesus asked Peter, ["What thinkest thou, Simon? of whom do the kings of the earth take custom or tribute? of their own children, or of strangers?"]{.class_3054} (Matt. 17:25), Peter didn't have to stand there, thinking to himself, "Well, if I say this, then He'll say this; but if I say this, He'll say this." He answered Jesus immediately and honestly: ["Of strangers"]{.class_3054} (Matt. 17:26). Because of that, he got a blessing from Christ: his taxes paid from a fish's mouth (Matt. 17:27).

But these hypocrites here in Mark 11 have to get in a huddle and try to come up with an answer that presents themselves in the best light possible. These guys were so crooked they had to screw their socks on in the morning.

["The baptism of John, was it from heaven, or of men?"]{.class_3054} (vs. 30). Say what you will about Simon Peter; at least he would have given an honest response. It wouldn't have taken him two seconds to answer that question. He would have said ["from heaven"]{.class_3054} without hesitation. And even if he were wrong, as he sometimes was (see our comments on 8:32--33), he would have said ["of men"]{.class_3054} just as quickly. Why? Because he wasn't a conniving little weasel trying to save his own neck.

Those chief priests and scribes didn't believe John's baptism was ["from heaven"]{.class_3054} (Matt. 21:32), but they were afraid to say what they really thought because ["they feared the people: for all men counted John, that he was a prophet indeed"]{.class_3054} (vs. 32).
