That Bible says, ["The fear of man bringeth a snare"]{.class_3054} (Prov. 29:25), and these guys are so entangled in their fear of what others think that it is going to damn them.

["And they answered and said unto Jesus, We cannot tell"]{.class_3054} (vs. 33). LIARS! They could so tell. They didn't believe John []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw1907.xhtml_page_307}was a prophet, or else they would have submitted to his baptism (vs. 31). [They're lying because they're afraid of men.]{.class_3055} The rule is, if a man fears God, he won't fear men, and if a man fears men, he won't fear God. A man is what he [fears]{.class_3055} and what he [loves: that]{.class_3055} is the measure of a man. His intellect, income, and social status have nothing to do with it.

In His answer to those religious hypocrites, Jesus says, "Well, if you can't tell me, I can't tell you. Bye bye!" Those priests and scribes lost revelation based on their deceitful conniving. The moral is, if you want revelation from God, be honest with Him, even if you don't agree with Him. In their bitterness against God, both Job and Jonah got more revelation from the Lord than these birds did. That's because they were open and honest with the Lord. You aren't going to hide anything from Him anyway (John 2:24--25; Heb. 4:13), so you might as well lay all 52 cards face up on the table.

[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2025.xhtml}

# [Chapter]{.class_3082}[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2025.xhtml_page_308}[ 12]{.class_3083} {.heading_2031}

12:1 And he began to speak unto them by parables. A certain man planted a vineyard, and set an hedge about it, and digged a place for the winefat, and built a tower, and let it out to husbandmen, and went into a far country.

2 And at the season he sent to the husbandmen a servant, that he might receive from the husbandmen of the fruit of the vineyard.

3 And they caught him, and beat him, and sent him away empty.

4 And again he sent unto them another servant; and at him they cast stones, and wounded him in the head, and sent him away shamefully handled.

5 And again he sent another; and him they killed, and many others; beating some, and killing some.

6 Having yet therefore one son, his wellbeloved, he sent him also last unto them, saying, They will reverence my son.

7 But those husbandmen said among themselves, This is the heir; come, let us kill him, and the inheritance shall be ours.

8 And they took him, and killed him, and cast him out of the vineyard.

9 What shall therefore the lord of the vineyard do? he will come and destroy the husbandmen, and will give the vineyard unto others.

10 And have ye not read this scripture; The stone which the builders rejected is become the head of the corner:

11 This was the Lord's doing, and it is marvellous in our eyes?

12 And they sought to lay hold on him, but feared the people: for they knew that he had spoken the parable against them: and they left him, and went their way.

This is the Parable of the Vineyard. It is found in all three []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2025.xhtml_page_309}"synoptic" Gospels (here; Matt. 21:33--46; Luke 20:9--19), but in no account is this parable said to be a parable of either the Kingdom of Heaven or the Kingdom of God. It is a parable that involves the whole world, both Jews and Gentiles (John 1:10--11). It is the basic problem of all mankind since Genesis 3: it is the attempt of every government, religion, and educational system to [steal God's creation from Him and run it themselves.]{.class_3066}

This parable is illustrated in two places in the Old Testament. It is illustrated, first of all, in [Joseph,]{.class_3066} a type of Christ in 153 particulars. Joseph was Jacob's favorite son. Jacob gave charge of his flock in Shechem to Joseph's brothers and then sent Joseph to check up on them (Gen. 37:12--14), just like the householder does here in the parable. And just as in the parable, the brothers want to kill the favored son to prevent him from being in charge (Gen. 37:18--20). They would have done it too if it hadn't been for Reuben and Judah (Gen. 37:21--27).

The second illustration of this parable is even more to the point. In 1 Kings 21:1--16, Ahab wants Naboth's vineyard, and Naboth won't give it to him. So through his wife Jezebel, Ahab has Naboth convicted of blasphemy through the testimony of false witnesses (cf. Mark 14:57--64) and executed. Ahab thinks the vineyard is his, but [it isn't]{.class_3066} (1 Kings 21:19--22). So the parable is a dramatic commentary on the rebellion of man.

Verse 1: ["A certain man planted a vineyard, and set an hedge about it, and digged a place for the winefat, and built a tower."]{.class_3064-0} The scribes at whom this parable was aimed (vs. 12) would have been familiar with the imagery, for Jesus takes it from Isaiah 5:1--7. There, the ["vineyard"]{.class_3064-0} is defined as ["the house of Israel,"]{.class_3064-0} which would make the ["certain man"]{.class_3064-0} who planted it ["the LORD of hosts"]{.class_3064-0} (Isa. 5:7).

The ["husbandmen"]{.class_3064-0} of the parable would be the religious leaders (vs. 12). Their job was not only to copy the Law, but to teach it as messengers of the Lord (Mal. 2:7). But these chief priests, scribes, and Pharisees had ["departed out of the way"]{.class_3064-0} and ["caused many to stumble at the law"]{.class_3064-0} (Mal. 2:8) by adding the ["traditions"]{.class_3064-0} and ["commandments of men"]{.class_3064-0} to what God said in the Old Testament (Mark 7:6--13). God Almighty said He would ["cut off . . . the MASTER and the SCHOLAR"]{.class_3064-0}[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2025.xhtml_page_310 .class-0} who did that, along with ["him that offereth an offering unto the LORD of hosts"]{.class_3064-0}---[the priests]{.class_3066} (Mal. 2:12)! That is what happens to these husbandmen in the parable (vs. 9).

Now, the man who planted the vineyard ["went into a far country."]{.class_3064-0} As in the Parable of the Pounds in Luke 19:12, that ["far country"]{.class_3064-0} is Heaven. The Lord sets up Israel, puts the priests and Levites in charge of keeping and teaching His Law, and then look what happens.

Verse 2: ["And at the season he sent to the husbandmen a servant, that he might receive from the husbandmen of the fruit of the vineyard."]{.class_3064-0} The fellow planted the vineyard, and he had a right to expect fruit from it. But those husbandmen didn't want to turn the fruit over to the owner, so they beat the servant and sent him away empty-handed.

That's a picture of how Israel treated the prophets and messengers God sent to them in the Old Testament. They beat some, they stoned some, they handled some shamefully, and they killed some. Jesus said to those Pharisees and scribes over in Matthew 23:

["O Jerusalem, Jerusalem, thou that KILLEST THE PROPHETS, AND STONEST THEM WHICH ARE SENT UNTO THEE, how often would I have gathered thy children together, even as a hen gathereth her chickens under her wings, and YE WOULD NOT!"]{.class_3064-0} (Matt. 23:37).

Having had his servants rejected by the husbandmen, the owner finally sent his ["one son, his wellbeloved"]{.class_3064-0} (vs. 6). There is absolutely no doubt about who that is (1:11, 9:7). Jesus Christ was sent ["last"]{.class_3064-0} (Heb. 1:1--2).

["They will reverence my son"]{.class_3064-0} (vs. 6). Well, they ["should"]{.class_3064-0} have (John 5:23), but they [didn't ]{.class_3066}(John 1:11, 8:40--42, 15:18--25).

Verse 7:[ "This is the heir; come, let us kill him, and the inheritance shall be ours."]{.class_3064-0} That's the Kings and rulers of this earth conspiring to rebel against the Lord's anointed (Psa. 2:2--3 cf. Acts 4:26--28). They think that by getting rid of the Heir, the title deed to the vineyard automatically falls to them. It didn't work out for those husbandmen in the parable (vs. 9), and it didn't work out for the Jews in real life either. [The Heir came back up!]{.class_3066} He didn't stay dead. He went away into ["a far country"]{.class_3064-0}[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2025.xhtml_page_311 .class-0} just like His Father and sent servants of His own to collect the fruit of the vineyard, and had them treated the same way (Matt. 23:34). He's coming back, some day, to "clean house" in the "vineyard" (Psa. 110; Zech. 14; Rev. 19).

["And cast him out of the vineyard"]{.class_3064-0} (vs. 8). That's Jesus Christ suffering ["without the gate"]{.class_3064-0} (Heb. 13:12), being crucified outside of Jerusalem. Jesus is telling those religious leaders exactly what they're going to do to Him, and they do it. They fulfill Christ's words without intending to, and verify that He was who He said He was: God's beloved Son. You are not going to get around the words of that Book no matter how hard you try.

Verse 9:[ "he will come and destroy the husbandmen, and will give the vineyard unto others."]{.class_3064-0} God said the land, which is called "Palestine" today, was [His land]{.class_3066} (2 Chron. 7:20; Isa. 14:25; Jer. 2:7, 16:18; Ezek. 36:5, 38:16; Joel 1:6, 3:2), and the Landlord's coming [in person]{.class_3066} someday to collect His rent and evict the squatters. When He does, it's going to be "Hail Columbia!" (2 Thess. 1:7--9).

That's the day to which I'm looking forward; that's the day I want to see. I want to see my Saviour get what's coming to Him, what He rightly [deserves.]{.class_3066} It won't be a whip, a crown of thorns, a cross, and a spear next time. It'll be bow down and kiss His feet, or else (Psa. 2:12, 72:9)! It'll be ["many crowns"]{.class_3064-0} (Rev. 19:12), ["a rod of iron"]{.class_3064-0} (Psa. 2:9; Rev. 2:27, 12:5, 19:5), ["the throne of his father David"]{.class_3064-0} (Luke 1:32), and ["the kingdoms of this world are become the kingdoms of our Lord, and of his Christ"]{.class_3064-0} (Rev. 11:15).

Verse 10: ["And have ye not read this scripture . . . ,"]{.class_3064-0} and then He proceeds to quote Psalm 118:22--23 in verses 10--11. Now that passage may not look like much, but it gives you the nature of the universe and the correct view of future events.

A cornerstone isn't ["the head"]{.class_3064-0} of a building; it's the first stone laid around which the building is built. Not in this case. This is a stone ["rejected"]{.class_3064-0} by ["the builders,"]{.class_3064-0} so it becomes the [last]{.class_3066} stone laid. So this stone caps off the building so that the structure is built from the top down instead of the bottom up. That's why verse 11 says, ["This was the Lord's doing, and it is marvellous in our eyes."]{.class_3064-0}

Now there is only one structure man builds that has a cornerstone as the headstone, and that's a [pyramid.]{.class_3066} So do you know what you find on your dollar bill? You find a pyramid with the headstone separate from the rest of the structure. Whoever came up with that picture (Illuminati or not) knew that top cornerstone was a [living person]{.class_3066} because it has an [eye]{.class_3066} on it (see Zech. 3:9 and our comments on that verse in the [Bible Believer's Commentary on the Minor Prophets,]{.class_3066} Vol. II). You won't get away from that Book no matter [who]{.class_3066} you are; it will control the atheist, agnostic, and Satanist just as it will any Baptist, Methodist, or Presbyterian.

Now what does that have to do with the nature of the universe? Well, in Ephesians 3:18, Paul wants every saint ["to comprehend . . . what is the breadth, and length, and depth, and height."]{.class_3064-0} Four dimensions. That's not talking about ["the love of Christ"]{.class_3064-0} in Ephesians 3:19; that's something separate he wants you to know. Breadth, length, depth, and height are [physical]{.class_3066} dimensions of a [physical]{.class_3066} structure. That structure is a [pyramid.]{.class_3066} The universe is laid out as a pyramid, with the top, where God is, separated from the rest of the structure by ["a sea of glass"]{.class_3064-0} (Rev. 4:6).

The ["chief corner stone"]{.class_3064-0} (1 Pet. 2:6--7) of verse 10 is a [supernatural stone]{.class_3066} made ["without hands"]{.class_3064-0}; it is the Smiting Stone of Daniel 2:34 (cf. Matt. 21:44). It comes down from Heaven and strikes the image of the Gentile kingdoms in the [feet]{.class_3066} (the kingdom of the Antichrist), not the iron legs of Rome at Christ's First Advent. So Amillennialism (the only kingdom is a spiritual kingdom that began at Calvary and is slowly taking over the world) is [out!]{.class_3066} That Smiting Stone sets up God's kingdom on earth from [the top down]{.class_3066} (Dan. 2:35, 44--45), so Postmillennialism is [out!]{.class_3066} No church, religion, politician, or government brings in a thousand years of peace before Jesus Christ comes back. The only Biblical view of the future is the [Premillennial ]{.class_3066}view: Jesus Christ returns [before]{.class_3066} the thousand-year kingdom on this earth. [That]{.class_3066} is what is behind verses 10--11.

Having heard the parable, those scribes and chief priests knew exactly at whom Christ was aiming---THEM (vs. 12)! Once more, you are told those religious leaders were ruled by the fear of man (see our comments on 11:32). Since they fear man, they [don't fear God.]{.class_3066} That is why they seek to kill the Heir.

The passage ends on an ominous note:[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2025.xhtml_page_313 .class-0}[ "they left him, and went their way."]{.class_3064-0} We understand, of course, from what follows, that that is referring to them going off to figure out how they can catch Christ in His words. But consider the spiritual implications of what you just read. Here Christ has told them what they were going to do and the consequences of it. Do they come to Him in repentance, believing on Him to escape that fate? Of course not. They leave Him and go [their own way]{.class_3066}---the broad way that leads to destruction (Matt. 7:13). Jesus said, ["He that is not with me is against me; and he that gathereth not with me scattereth abroad"]{.class_3064-0} (Matt. 12:30). When you leave Jesus, you don't remain neutral. It will either be [His way]{.class_3066} or [your way,]{.class_3066} but never no way. You will either take His way to eternal life or your own way to eternal damnation; there is no other way.

13 And they send unto him certain of the Pharisees and of the Herodians, to catch him in his words.

14 And when they were come, they say unto him, Master, we know that thou art true, and carest for no man: for thou regardest not the person of men, but teachest the way of God in truth: Is it lawful to give tribute to Caesar, or not?

15 Shall we give, or shall we not give? But he, knowing their hypocrisy, said unto them, Why tempt ye me? bring me a penny, that I may see it.

16 And they brought it. And he saith unto them, Whose is this image and superscription? And they said unto him, Caesar's.

17 And Jesus answering said unto them, Render to Caesar the things that are Caesar's, and to God the things that are God's. And they marvelled at him.

We have already commented on this alliance between the Pharisees and Herodians under 3:6 (which see). Here they are trying to get Jesus in a "catch 22." They are trying to get Christ into the dilemma of choosing between obeying the Law of Moses in Deuteronomy 17:14--15 (where the Jews weren't to have any King []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2025.xhtml_page_314}over them but a Jew chosen by God) or being guilty of sedition against Rome because He was a tax evader.

These Pharisees and Herodians come to Jesus and try to give Him "the gas" before "the operation": they flatter Him to "work ruin" (Prov. 26:28), but in so doing, they speak truths they don't even believe (John 8:45).

["Master, we know that thou art true . . . but teachest the way of God in truth"]{.class_3064-0} (vs. 14). Notice how this mirrors what Nicodemus said in John 3:2. The difference between Nicodemus and these birds is that Nicodemus was a sincere inquirer; these religious and political hypocrites are trying to ["catch"]{.class_3064-0} Christ ["in his words"]{.class_3064-0} (vs. 13).

Here they are addressing truth incarnate (John 14:6), whose very words are truth (John 17:17, 18:37). It was absolutely true that He didn't care for any man in the sense of not having respect of persons when it came to teaching and preaching the truth (Deut. 16:19; 2 Sam. 14:14; 2 Chron. 19:7; Prov. 24:23, 28:21). Do they believe what they just said? Of course not (John 8:44--47)!

["Is it lawful to give tribute to Caesar, or not? Shall we give, or shall we not give?"]{.class_3064-0} (vss. 14--15). The Pharisees are hoping He will say ["give"]{.class_3064-0} so the people will turn on Him for following a pagan idolater with delusions of grandeur. The Herodians are hoping He will say ["not give"]{.class_3064-0} so they can have Him arrested by the Romans for sedition.

But you aren't going to put one over on Jesus Christ. He knows what's in the heart of man (John 2:24--25): ["But he, KNOWING THEIR HYPOCRISY"]{.class_3064-0} (vs. 15). And if you know your Bible, you know what follows this later in the book of Matthew: ["Woe unto you, scribes and Pharisees, hypocrites!"]{.class_3064-0} (Matt. 23:13--15, 23, 25, 27, 29). The Lord reserved His most scathing remarks for [religious pretenders]{.class_3066} ([hypocrites]{.class_3066}---see our remarks under Luke 20:20 in that Commentary).

["Why tempt ye me?"]{.class_3064-0} Because they are following in the footsteps of their "daddy" (John 8:44 cf. Matt. 4:3).

["Bring me a penny."]{.class_3064-0} Now that ["penny"]{.class_3064-0} was not like our "penny" today. Your penny is just about worthless by itself, except when somebody wants to hold a promotion for his business ("only a []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2025.xhtml_page_315}PENNY for your down payment!"---that sort of thing). Back in Christ's day, though, that ["penny"]{.class_3064-0} was a day's wage (cf. Matt. 20:2). So right away you know those Jews had no problem using Roman currency to buy their groceries and pay their bills. They were already in subjection to Caesar whether they liked it or not.

The truth is, those Jews were in bondage to Rome because [God had sold them into slavery for their sin]{.class_3066} (Deut. 32:30). And when given a choice between God's Christ and Caesar, [they chose Caesar]{.class_3066} (Matt. 27:25 cf. John 19:15). So they really had no room to talk or complain about the tribute.

["Whose is this image and superscription? And they said unto him, Caesar's"]{.class_3064-0} (vs. 16). So Jesus' answer was basically: "All right, then, if it has Caesar's image and Caesar's superscription on it, it must belong to Caesar; and if Caesar wants his money back, give it to him."

["Render to Caesar the things that are Caesar's, and to God the things that are God's"]{.class_3064-0} (vs. 17). While those Pharisees and Herodians were prepared for a ["give"]{.class_3064-0} or a ["give not"]{.class_3064-0} answer, they certainly weren't prepared for an answer like that. ["And they marvelled at him."]{.class_3064-0} They had been ready to pounce on His words, and when they heard that, they stood there before that crowd of people who were waiting to see what they would do, and there was nothing they could do. "Give Caesar what belongs to him, and give God what belongs to Him." There was nothing they could do with that but quietly excuse themselves and get lost in the crowd.

What was the problem?[ They weren't rendering unto God what belonged to Him.]{.class_3085}

["Woe unto you, scribes and Pharisees, hypocrites! for ye pay tithe of mint and anise and cummin, and have OMITTED THE WEIGHTIER MATTERS OF THE LAW, JUDGMENT, MERCY, AND FAITH: these ought ye to have done, and not to leave the other undone"]{.class_3064-0} (Matt. 23:23).

They passed over ["judgment and the love of God"]{.class_3064-0} (Luke 11:42). They got out of there as quickly as they could (Matt. 22:22) because they didn't want the conversation to change from paying taxes to Caesar to [what they weren't paying to God.]{.class_3066}

Now before moving on, one should consider Christ's words in []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2025.xhtml_page_316}regards to the modern anti-tax movement. You can complain about income tax being unconstitutional all you want, but if you refuse to pay your taxes, in the manner prescribed by the government, all it will do is wind you up in the slammer. Every few years, some Christian gets on this anti-tax "soapbox," and eventually, he ends up arrested, fined, and in prison. I've seen it over and over again. Good Christians, with fine works, will [ruin their ministries]{.class_3066} over this issue. And in the end, all they have done is violate the words of Scripture as given by Jesus Christ and the Apostle Paul.

["Render therefore to all their dues: tribute to whom tribute is due; custom to whom custom; fear to whom fear; honour to whom honour"]{.class_3064-0} (Rom. 13:7).

One time, the Roman tax collectors came to Simon Peter and asked, ["Doth not your master pay tribute?"]{.class_3064-0} (Matt. 17:24). Without hesitation, Peter said, ["Yes"]{.class_3064-0} (Matt. 17:25). If Jesus Christ could pay taxes to a devil like Tiberius Caesar, you shouldn't have any problem paying them to Clinton, Bush, Obama, or whoever's next in the White House.

I'll be right frank with you, I don't enjoy paying taxes anymore than anybody else. I can think of a dozen ministries off the top of my head that could put the money to better use than the President or the Congress. I don't pay my taxes because I'm patriotic or even because I'm a "good Christian." I pay my taxes because I don't want to end up in the "pokey." I pay my taxes for the same reason I'd give my wallet to a mugger if he pointed a gun at me and said, "Your money or your life." If you don't want the IRS breaking down your door with a SWAT team, you had better pay your taxes.

18 Then come unto him the Sadducees, which say there is no resurrection; and they asked him, saying,

19 Master, Moses wrote unto us, If a man's brother die, and leave his wife behind him, and leave no children, that his brother should take his wife, and raise up seed unto his brother.

20 Now there were seven brethren: and the first took a wife, and dying left no seed.

21 And the second took her, and died, neither left he any []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2025.xhtml_page_317}seed: and the third likewise.

22 And the seven had her, and left no seed: last of all the woman died also.

23 In the resurrection therefore, when they shall rise, whose wife shall she be of them? for the seven had her to wife.

24 And Jesus answering said unto them, Do ye not therefore err, because ye know not the scriptures, neither the power of God?

25 For when they shall rise from the dead, they neither marry, nor are given in marriage; but are as the angels which are in heaven.

26 And as touching the dead, that they rise: have ye not read in the book of Moses, how in the bush God spake unto him, saying, I am the God of Abraham, and the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob?

27 He is not the God of the dead, but the God of the living: ye therefore do greatly err.

The religious Conservatives and the political liberals had their shot at Christ; now it's the religious Modernists' turn. These are the Sadducees. They try to trap Christ on the doctrine of the resurrection because they don't believe in any resurrection (vs. 18). Nor do they believe in spirits or angels (Acts 23:8). They are [against]{.class_3066} the very Book they are quoting (Lev. 19:31, 20:6, 27; Deut. 18:11; 21:17, 22:11, 15, 24:7, 40, 31:11, 48:16; Exod. 3:2, 14:19, 23:20, 23, 32:34, 33:2; Num. 22:22--27, 31--32, 34--35). When it came to the resurrection, the Sadducee rejected what orthodox Judaism believed (John 11:24). That Old Testament teaches the resurrection of the dead in Job 19:25--17; Isaiah 26:19; Ezekiel 37:12; and Daniel 12:2.

Now the law quoted in verse 19 is Deuteronomy 25:5, and it is illustrated in the book of Ruth. From there, they invent an implausible situation based on that law where a woman runs through seven brothers without having a baby. The question posed to entrap Christ is: ["In the resurrection therefore, when they shall rise, whose wife shall she be of them?"]{.class_3064-0} (vs. 23). You talk about []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2025.xhtml_page_318}more than one living husband (see our comments on 10:5--12)! Do you see now why we included death as a scriptural ground for "divorce"? Death dissolves the marriage bond, and this was understood (Rom. 7:2--3). The question is ridiculous, to say the least.

In answer to these religious ignoramuses, Jesus once more demonstrates the "sweet spirit of Christ," where there was "never a particle of bitterness or unkindness in the words of the Lord Jesus Christ" (see our remarks on the words of Stewart Custer under 6:15). ["Do ye not therefore err, because ye know not the scriptures, neither the power of God?"]{.class_3057-0} (vs. 24); in verse 27, He adds, ["ye therefore do GREATLY err."]{.class_3057-0}

Those "brilliant intellectuals" were a bunch of [stupid blockheads]{.class_3066} when it came to [God's words]{.class_3066} and [God's power.]{.class_3066} Nothing has changed between A.D. 33 and now (2016, as of this writing). Modern scholars are just as dumb as a box of rocks when it comes to that Book. That's why they change it: to bring it down to their level of ignorance. Those are the faculties, staffs, and administrators of every major religious College, University and Seminary in America and Europe.

["For when they shall rise from the dead, they neither marry, nor are given in marriage, but are as the angels which are in heaven"]{.class_3064-0} (vs. 25). Jesus knocks the slats out from under these Sadducees with His answer on two of the doctrinal points they denied: the resurrection and angels. Number one: ["WHEN they shall rise from the dead,"]{.class_3064-0} not "if"; the resurrection is a sure thing. Number 2: ["but are as the angels which are in heaven"]{.class_3064-0}; angels exist and are real. From that, you can conclude that spirits, too, are real (Job 1:6 cf. 1 Kings 22:19--22).

Note in verse 25 that Jesus deals with the resurrection ["from the dead."]{.class_3064-0} This is not the general resurrection, but the resurrection of the saved dead [before]{.class_3066} the Millennial kingdom (see our remarks under 9:9--10), what the book of Revelation calls ["the first resurrection"]{.class_3064-0} (Rev. 20:5--6). One of the ways you can know that's so is because no unsaved person can ["RISE from the dead."]{.class_3064-0} When they are hauled up to the White Throne Judgment, [there is no earth from which to rise]{.class_3066} (Rev. 20:11--12).

Now Scofield picks up verse 25 and says that angels are [sexless]{.class_3066} because ["they neither marry, nor are given in marriage."]{.class_3064-0}[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2025.xhtml_page_319 .class-0} So when he gets over to those ["sons of God"]{.class_3064-0} in Genesis 6:1--4, he makes them "the godly line of Seth" marrying the "ungodly line of Cain," because angels are sexless and don't marry. You never got a bigger load of bologna sausage out of the deep freeze than that.

1\. The Holy Spirit tells you who those ["sons of God"]{.class_3064-0} were back in the Old Testament, and they weren't any "godly line of Seth" (whatever that is). Those ["sons of God"]{.class_3064-0} were around before God ever made Adam (Job 38:4--7), and they could come with Satan ["to present themselves before the LORD"]{.class_3064-0} (Job 1:6, 2:1). Quite some company that "godly line" keeps, wouldn't you say?

2\. [No]{.class_3066} angel in that Bible is "sexless." They are [all male]{.class_3066} (Gen. 32:25; Josh. 5:13--14; Judg. 13:3, 6, 8--11; Dan. 8:16, 9:21; Luke 1:9; Rev. 21:17).

3\. Those ["sons of God"]{.class_3064-0} who married those ["daughters of men"]{.class_3064-0} in Genesis 6 [weren't]{.class_3066}[ "angels of God in heaven"]{.class_3064-0} (vs. 25 cf. Matt. 22:30). They were the ["angels that sinned"]{.class_3064-0} (2 Pet. 2:4) and ["kept not their first estate, but left their own habitation"]{.class_3064-0} (Jude 6).

Now do you know why no ["angel . . . in heaven"]{.class_3064-0} or any saint who comes up in the resurrection marries? [Because they're all male.]{.class_3066} God NEVER recognizes "same-sex marriage," the Supreme Court judgment notwithstanding. The angels who stayed in Heaven don't marry because there are no female angels; all the angels are ["sons of God"]{.class_3064-0} (see points 1 and 2 above). That's the same term for New Testament believers (John 1:12; 1 John 3:1). (Note ["the power of God"]{.class_3064-0} from vs. 25 showing up in John 1:12 to make you a son of God.) When you come up in the resurrection, you are ["conformed to the image"]{.class_3064-0} of THE Son of God (Rom. 8:29) and are made just ["like him"]{.class_3064-0} at His resurrection (1 John 3:2; Phil. 3:21). Well, what was your Saviour when He came up out of the grave? He was a [33½-year-old male.]{.class_3066} That's what you will be in the resurrection, and not one scholar who corrects that Book ever got it. Why not? Because they ["know not the scriptures, neither the power of God."]{.class_3064-0}

Back to Mark 12: ["And as touching the dead, that they rise: have ye not read in the book of Moses . . ."]{.class_3064-0} (vs. 26). Those Sadducees try to trap Jesus by using what Moses wrote, so the Lord []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2025.xhtml_page_320}quotes Moses right back at them. The passage He uses is Exodus 3:2--6. There God says, ["I AM]{.class_3064-0} \[present tense\][ the God of Abraham, and the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob."]{.class_3064-0} Those three patriarchs had been dead for centuries, but the Lord talked about them as if they were still around.[ They were]{.class_3066} (Luke 16:22--31).

So when Christ dies on the cross, He tells that thief who asked Him to remember him when the Lord came into His kingdom, ["To day shalt thou be with me in paradise]{.class_3064-0} \[NOT Heaven\]["]{.class_3064-0} (Luke 23:42--43). That saved thief lived on after He died, but he went [down,]{.class_3066} not up. He went where Jesus went when He died: ["into the lower parts of the earth"]{.class_3064-0} (Eph. 4:9). And when Jesus came out of the grave to be ["the firstfruits"]{.class_3064-0} of the resurrection (1 Cor. 15:20, 23), ["he led captivity captive"]{.class_3064-0} (Eph. 4:8) and took everyone in Paradise in the heart of the earth up to Heaven (2 Cor. 12:4; Rev. 2:7). As a testimony to those Jews in Jerusalem that He had done that, a resurrection took place there in Jerusalem after Christ came up (Matt. 27:52--53). Some of those Old Testament saints came up over 3,000 years before any "general resurrection." The souls of all those Old Testament saints were alive and well in "Abraham's Bosom" awaiting a resurrection [before]{.class_3066} that of the unsaved dead.

["He is not the God of the dead, but the God of the living"]{.class_3064-0} (vs. 27). For the New Testament believer, that is made possible through the resurrection of his Saviour: he is ["alive unto God through Jesus Christ"]{.class_3064-0} (Rom. 6:11). His ["life is hid with Christ in God"]{.class_3064-0} (Col. 3:3), and the promise is: ["because I live]{.class_3064-0} \[i.e., Jesus Christ\][, ye shall live also"]{.class_3064-0} (John 14:19).

28 And one of the scribes came, and having heard them reasoning together, and perceiving that he had answered them well, asked him, Which is the first commandment of all?

29 And Jesus answered him, The first of all the commandments is, Hear, O Israel; The Lord our God is one Lord:

30 And thou shalt love the Lord thy God with all thy heart, and with all thy soul, and with all thy mind, and with all thy strength: this is the first commandment.

31 And the second is like, namely this, Thou shalt love thy neighbour as thyself. There is none other commandment greater than these.

32 And the scribe said unto him, Well, Master, thou hast said the truth: for there is one God; and there is none other but he:

33 And to love him with all the heart, and with all the understanding, and with all the soul, and with all the strength, and to love his neighbour as himself, is more than all whole burnt offerings and sacrifices.

34 And when Jesus saw that he answered discreetly, he said unto him, Thou art not far from the kingdom of God. And no man after that durst ask him any question.

So far, Jesus has been tested by the religious Conservatives (the Pharisees---vs. 13), the political liberals (the Herodians---vs. 13), and the religious Liberals (the Sadducees---vs. 18). Of that last group, Jesus had said, ["ye know not the scripture"]{.class_3064-0} (vs. 24). Well, here comes a fellow who does know the Scripture because he belongs to the group that copies the Scripture for a living: he is a [scribe.]{.class_3066} While Mark makes it sound like an incidental thing that this scribe poses his question to Jesus (["And one of the scribes came, and having heard"]{.class_3064-0}---vs. 28), we gather from the parallel passage in Matthew that he was "sicced on" the Lord (Matt. 22:34--35). But this scribe was different from the Pharisees, Herodians, and Sadducees who preceded him. There was an honesty, sincerity, and lack of hypocrisy which the others didn't have and which Jesus acknowledged (vs. 34).

Having seen how well Jesus answered the questions meant to entrap Him, the scribe asks an honest question to test the Lord: ["Which is the first commandment of all?"]{.class_3064-0} (vs. 28). Now consider for a moment, if you will, that if salvation in the Old Testament were by grace through faith, what is all this emphasis on the commandments? While there are many examples of faith found in the Old Testament, do you know how many times the word actually appears in the text? [Twice]{.class_3066}---Deuteronomy 32:20 (where somebody has ["NO faith"]{.class_3064-0}) and Habakkuk 2:4. Compare that to 245 []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2025.xhtml_page_322}times the word appears in the New Testament. Quite an "imbalance" there, wouldn't you say? If salvation in the Old Testament were by grace through faith, there certainly wasn't enough there to convince those who knew more about the Old Testament than any professor at any Christian College or Seminary.

According to Rabbinical Judaism, there are 613 commandments in the Torah (the five books of Moses); these include the "Ten." The scribe's question is, out of those 613 commandments, which is "number one," which is the most important, which one "tops" all the others? Unlike the Pharisees and scribes who added ["the commandments of men"]{.class_3064-0} to ["the commandment of God"]{.class_3064-0} (see 7:7--13 and our comments on the passage), Jesus answers with the Scriptures alone: He quotes Deuteronomy 6:4--5 and Leviticus 19:18.

What you have in verses 30 and 31 here in Mark 12 is a summation not only of the Decalogue, but of the entire Old Testament (see Matt. 22:40). Now, the Ten Commandments are divided into two parts: the first four deal with man's relationship to God, and the last six deal with man's relationship to man. Here in Mark 12, verse 30 sums up the first four commandments, and verse 31 sums up the last six.

It is important to note---absolutely essential!---that ["The first of all the commandments"]{.class_3064-0} (vs. 29) deals with [God,]{.class_3066} NOT man. The first commandment is [not]{.class_3066} "loving your fellow man, and if you love your fellow man, you love God." That is a little, goody two-shoes Liberal trying to work his way to Heaven. The mark of every unsaved Liberal and kingdom builder on this earth is that they put the second commandment before the first. All of them (Catholics, Communists, the ACLU, "community organizers," the National Council of "Christian" Churches, etc.) legislate against, litigate against, or [eliminate]{.class_3066} anybody who puts obedience to God over care for his fellow man, because if you love God as you ought, there will be times when your love for your fellow man is interpreted by a lost world as [hatred]{.class_3066} (Luke 14:26 cf. Matt. 10:37). Judas Iscariot resented money being spent on Christ (God) instead of the poor (man), not because he had any particular love for poor people, but because he was "skimming off the top" from the "United Drive" (John 12:3--6). In []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2025.xhtml_page_323}other words, you had better watch out for anybody who puts man before God; for all the talk about "love, love, love," all he might love is [himself.]{.class_3066}

Lost folks use the second commandment for all kinds of "hell on wheels." If you don't believe me, just ask them. They're all trying to "make the world a better place to live in." Do you know why Hitler killed six million Jews? Why, he loved "mankind" so much he was trying to purify the race. Why did Stalin kill fifty million of his own people? As one author put it: "he had a vision for Russia and the world." Of course he had a good motive for starving and murdering fifty million folks; nothing but the best. According to Hillary Clinton, "sex perverts' rights are human rights," so the government should shut down the free speech and freedom of religion of anybody who thinks two queers or two dykes shacking up ["is abomination"]{.class_3064-0} (Lev. 18:22), and slap those people with excessive fines and jail time. Why? Because love for the sex perverts supersedes love for God's holy word, that's why. It's the second commandment before the first or the second commandment in place of the first.

According to Jesus Christ, the ["great commandment"]{.class_3064-0} is [not]{.class_3066} loving your neighbor as yourself; it is loving God [first]{.class_3066} with all your heart, soul, strength, and mind (Matt. 22:37--38). Loving your neighbor takes a backseat to loving God. Where loving your neighbor conflicts with loving God, [as it sometimes will,]{.class_3066} you pick loving God, Christ, and the Book over your neighbor, your spouse, your kids, your parents, your relatives, your friends, your community, your country, and [yourself]{.class_3066} (Luke 14:26).

Now note: these are commandments---that's [works.]{.class_3085} That means, in this age, verses 30--31 are NOT the plan of salvation for a sinner (Eph. 2:8--9; Tit. 3:5). In this age, it is not your love for God that saves you, but [God's love for you.]{.class_3085}

["But God commendeth HIS LOVE TOWARD US, in that, while we were yet sinners, CHRIST DIED FOR US"]{.class_3064-0} (Rom. 5:8).

["In this was manifested the LOVE OF GOD TOWARD US, because that God sent his only begotten Son into the world, that we might live through him. Herein is love, NOT THAT WE LOVED GOD, but that HE LOVED US, and sent his Son to be the PROPITIATION FOR OUR SINS"]{.class_3064-1}[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2025.xhtml_page_324}[ (1 John 4:9--10).]{.class_3065}

If you love God at all, it is because He loved you first (1 John 4:19), and if you don't receive the Person through whom He showed that love, [you don't love God.]{.class_3066} Don't tell me you love God and reject His love gift to you (John 3:16). Don't tell me you love God when you turn down the righteousness of His Son for your own righteousness.

Now it should be evident that no one keeps that first commandment as he should. You are commanded to love God with all your [heart]{.class_3066}---your affections. Well, aren't you supposed to love your spouse? Aren't you supposed to love the brethren? Well, then, how could you possibly love God with ALL your heart?

You are to love God with all your [soul]{.class_3066} and [strength]{.class_3066}---that's your very being and every effort you put out. Can you honestly say you've done that? There's no little part of you that you've reserved for yourself, is there? Everything you do is ["to the glory of God,"]{.class_3064-0} is it (1 Cor. 10:31)?

You should love God with all your [mind]{.class_3066}---that's everything about which you think. Aw, com'on, Lord, how is anybody to do that? You mean to tell me you think about the Lord every time you shave, brush your teeth, use the bathroom, wash the dishes, do the laundry, change the oil in the car, pay the bills, go shopping, get the kids ready for school, fix supper, get dressed, etc. I know you think on Him throughout the day, but every single thought? I mean, if you have to remember where you put the car keys, that's not thinking on the Lord.

You see, people don't think. They figure what little thought and effort they give the Lord is good enough, and [it ain't.]{.class_3066} Jesus Christ was the only person on this earth who kept that first commandment. He was able to say ["I do always those things that please him ]{.class_3064-0}\[God the Father\]["]{.class_3064-0} (John 8:29) and have God verify, [audibly,]{.class_3066} that it was so (Mark 1:11; Matt. 17:5). The proof that Jesus kept that commandment is that [He didn't stay dead]{.class_3066} (Acts 17:31, 2:24; Rom. 1:4).

Verse 31:[ "And the second is LIKE."]{.class_3064-0} Notice: it is ["like"]{.class_3064-0}; it is NOT the [same.]{.class_3066} While loving your neighbor is certainly indicative of the fact that you love God [as a believer]{.class_3066} (let's not forget that; anyone can be kind, nice, and sweet [without being saved]{.class_3066})---James 2:8--17 cf. []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2025.xhtml_page_325}1 John 3:17--19---it is not, in and of itself, loving God. Love of neighbor [follows]{.class_3066} love of God. It does not [precede]{.class_3066} it, and it certainly does not [supersede]{.class_3066} it. And love of neighbor is subject to the Book, not the other way around. You don't violate the first commandment to keep the second.

Verse 32: ["And the scribe, said unto him, Well, Master, thou hast said the truth."]{.class_3064-0} And then that scribe says something neither Jesus nor Moses said: ["there is one God."]{.class_3064-0} That's all well and good, but it's [not]{.class_3066} what Jesus quoted from Deuteronomy 6:4. Here's what the verse actually says:

["Hear, O Israel: The LORD our God is one LORD"]{.class_3064-0} (Deut. 6:4).

You say, "What's the big deal, Ruckman?" The big deal is that the verse isn't just teaching "monotheism," like Mohammed believed. Brethren, it's not just belief in ["one God"]{.class_3064-0} (vs. 32); the devils believe that (James 2:19). It's belief in ["the only true God"]{.class_3064-0} and His Son who is identified with Him (John 17:8 cf. 1 John 5:20).

Deuteronomy 6:4 doesn't just talk about ["one God"]{.class_3064-0}; it identifies the ["God."]{.class_3064-0} It's [Jehovah]{.class_3066} (["LORD"]{.class_3064-0}), [not Allah.]{.class_3066} Mohammed's "monotheism" is so much HORSEFEATHERS. If your ["one God"]{.class_3064-0} isn't the [right God,]{.class_3066} then you are as lost as a goose in a horse race. No amount of love for God or sincerity or piety will do you any good if that "God" is not ["the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ"]{.class_3064-0} who has ["begotten"]{.class_3064-0} you ["unto a lively hope by the resurrection of Jesus Christ"]{.class_3064-0} (1 Pet. 1:3).

I will say this for the scribe: he had a great deal more sense than the translators and revisers on the boards of the [RSV, NRSV, ESV]{.class_3066} (same garbage as put out by "Conservatives"), [ASV, NASV,]{.class_3066} and the Southern Baptists' [Holman Christian Standard Bible.]{.class_3066} All those versions drop the word ["God"]{.class_3064-0} and substitute the New Age ["One"]{.class_3066} in its place (see Riplinger's work [New Age Bible Versions]{.class_3066}).

Nestle is so crooked that he makes the change in his text [without giving any manuscript evidence for it.]{.class_3066} All you get from Vincent in his [Word Studies]{.class_3066} is the cryptic statement: "All the BEST texts omit God," without ever telling you what "texts" he considers "best."

As it turns out, the "best texts" are [Alexandrinus, Vaticanus,]{.class_3066} and [Sinaiticus]{.class_3066} (along with uncials K, L, 036, and 038). [Vaticanus]{.class_3066} and [Sinaiticus]{.class_3066}[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2025.xhtml_page_326 .class-0} are such corrupt manuscripts that they differ 3,000 times in the Gospels alone, and they differ from the Received Text in 13,281 places. You call those "the [best]{.class_3066} texts"?!

Siding with [Vaticanus]{.class_3066} and [Sinaiticus]{.class_3066} against the [KJV]{.class_3066} reading (["God"]{.class_3064-0}) are the [Peshitta, ]{.class_3066}the [Vulgate,]{.class_3066} and the Persian and Ethiopian translations. Siding with the majority of Greek manuscripts that have the reading (that is what the term [Received Text]{.class_3066} means---the text commonly received by Christians throughout the centuries) are uncials D and E, along with the Old Latin, the Sinaitic, Curetonian, and Palestinian Syriac manuscripts, the Sahidic and Boharic translations from Egypt, and the Armenian and Georgian translations. The manuscript evidence for the [KJV]{.class_3066} reading is at least as good as, if not better than, the evidence against it, which is probably why Nestle conveniently omitted it. These birds are just as crooked as a dog's hind leg.

["And no man after that durst ask him any question."]{.class_3064-0} That word ["durst"]{.class_3064-0} is an archaic form of the word [dare;]{.class_3066} it is still used in the backwoods of Arkansas. After trying to catch the Lord in His words (both dishonestly and honestly), and having had Jesus answer all their questions well, to the point of leaving them speechless (Matt. 22:22, 34), those scribes, Pharisees, Sadducees, and Herodians didn't dare ask Him any more questions to tempt Him. We are about to see Jesus Christ ask them a question which they won't be able to answer.

That is a picture of what will happen at the White Throne Judgment. At the White Throne Judgment, men are going to try to justify themselves by asking God questions they think He won't be able to answer. The Lord will answer every one of them with [Scripture.]{.class_3066} Then it will be the Lord's turn: He will ask the questions, and those lost sinners will stand there and not be able to answer. You say, "Ruckman, where do you get such things?" I get them from Romans 3.

Romans 3:4---["God forbid: yea, let God be true, but every man a liar; as it is written, That thou ]{.class_3064-0}\[God\][ mightest be justified in thy sayings]{.class_3064-0} \[["the oracles of God"]{.class_3064-0}---Rom. 3:2\][, and mightest overcome when THOU ART JUDGED."]{.class_3064-0} Someone is trying to judge and condemn God, and is being rebutted with []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2025.xhtml_page_327}Scripture. But look at Romans 3:19.

["Now we know that what things soever the law saith, it saith to them who are under the law: that every mouth may be STOPPED, and all the world may become guilty before God."]{.class_3064-0} God is shutting sinners up with the Scriptures, and that's what Jesus is about to do in verses 35--36.

35 And Jesus answered and said, while he taught in the temple, How say the scribes that Christ is the Son of David?

36 For David himself said by the Holy Ghost, The LORD said to my Lord, Sit thou on my right hand, till I make thine enemies thy footstool.

37 David therefore himself calleth him Lord; and whence is he then his son? And the common people heard him gladly.

The tables are now turned. The religious leaders tried catching Him in His words with tricky questions and failed. It's Christ's turn, now, and it only takes one question to throw the monkey wrench into the machinery. Although the question is broken into two parts, it is still one question: "Why do the scribes say ['Christ is the Son of David' ]{.class_3064-0}since David called him ['Lord']{.class_3064-0} and a father would never call his son ['Lord']{.class_3064-0}?" The key, though, was in the presentation.

Verse 35: ["How say the scribes that Christ is the Son of David?"]{.class_3064-0} It's a rhetorical question. The scribes taught that because the Old Testament said that (2 Sam. 7:16; 1 Chron. 17:14; Psa. 89:3--4, 29, 35--37; Isa. 9:7; Jer. 23:5--6, 33:17--26). The proposition was intended to be accepted as true, yet contrasted with the next part of the argument: verse 36.

Verse 36 is a quote from Psalm 110:1. Right away one cannot help but notice those ["enemies"]{.class_3064-0} being made Christ's ["footstool."]{.class_3064-0} This is a reference to the Second Advent, and that is the theme of the entire psalm.

The second thing to notice are the two "Lords" in the verse. Unlike verse 29, the translators have chosen, here, to present the verse exactly as it appears in the psalm in regards to the capitalization of [Lord]{.class_3066}; that is the case in every instance where the []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2025.xhtml_page_328}verse is quoted in the New Testament (Matt. 22:44; here; Luke 20:42; Acts 2:34). Why is that? Because Psalm 110:1 is a clear declaration of the [Deity of Christ.]{.class_3066}

Verse 36: ["The LORD . . . ."]{.class_3064-0} Any Jehovah's Witness will tell you that's the word [Jehovah.]{.class_3066} That's exactly right, but then, he will try to make the second [Lord]{.class_3066} a "lesser God" (see the JW reading of two Gods---a begotten one and an unbegotten one---in the [NASV]{.class_3066} reading of John 1:18).

["The LORD said unto my Lord . . . ."]{.class_3064-0} That second [Lord]{.class_3066} is the Hebrew word [Adon,]{.class_3066} a shortened form of [Adonai.]{.class_3066} It's obviously a reference to Jesus Christ by what follows: ["Sit thou on my right hand"]{.class_3064-0} (cf. Mark 16:19; Acts 2:33; Rom. 8:34; Eph. 1:20; Col. 3:1; Heb. 1:3, 13, 10:12, 12:2; 1 Pet. 3:22). The JWs try to make a distinction between [Jehovah]{.class_3066} and [Adonai]{.class_3066} that is not found in Scripture. In fact, it is the exact opposite: [Jehovah]{.class_3066} and [Adonai]{.class_3066} are referred to as the [same God.]{.class_3066}

Psalm 8:1---["O LORD our Lord . . . ."]{.class_3064-0}

Psalm 8:9---["O LORD our Lord . . . ."]{.class_3064-0}

Deuteronomy 10:17---["For the LORD your God is God of gods, and Lord of lords, a great God . . . ."]{.class_3064-0}

Psalm 136:1--3---["O give thanks unto the LORD . . . O give thanks unto the God of gods . . . O give thanks to the Lord of lords . . . ."]{.class_3064-0}

Genesis 15:2---["And Abram said, Lord ]{.class_3064-0}\[[Adonai]{.class_3066}\][ GOD]{.class_3064-0} \[[Jehovah]{.class_3066}\][ ]{.class_3066}[. . . ."]{.class_3092}

Genesis 18:26--27---["And the LORD said . . . And Abraham answered . . . I have taken upon me to speak unto the Lord . . . ."]{.class_3064-0}

Exodus 4:11, 13---["And the LORD said unto him . . . And he said, O my Lord . . . ."]{.class_3064-0}

So obviously what you have in Psalm 110:1 is one member of the Trinity addressing another member of the Trinity. See the same thing in Psalm 45:6--7 (cf. Heb. 1:8). The teaching of the Old Testament, which the Rabbis have tried to deny since the Jews' rejection of Christ, is that the Messiah was to be God in the flesh (e.g., see Isa. 7:14, 9:6; Mic. 5:2).

So the first proposition was that Christ was to be ["the Son of David."]{.class_3064-0} The second proposition was that David called Christ []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2025.xhtml_page_329 .class-0}["my Lord."]{.class_3064-0} The clincher was how could Christ be David's Son if David called Him ["Lord"]{.class_3064-0} (vs. 37)?

Now to get that last thing, you have to understand the mindset of the Oriental. Orientals are not Occidentals; they are [different.]{.class_3066} I know some of you consider that "racist," but that's because you don't have good sense. You've been spending too much time with the "glass toilet" (the [TV]{.class_3066}). If a man doesn't recognize the differences in the races, he is loonier than a toon; he is ready for the rubber room.

Those Jews were Shemites---Orientals. Orientals reverence their elders and ancestors, sometimes to the point of idolatry (like Japan). No Oriental would even think of calling his child "Lord"; if anybody was to be called "Lord," it would be the [father,]{.class_3066} not the [son.]{.class_3066} If David is addressing his Son as ["Lord,"]{.class_3064-0} it is because that Son has an ancestry that goes back farther than David; in fact, it went back all the way before Genesis 1:1 (Mic. 5:2; John 1:1--3).

What the Jewish leaders didn't want to admit was that Jesus was the Christ, because if they did, that meant the Landlord had come to take back the vineyard, and they weren't prepared to turn it over (vss. 6--8). Those Jews understood perfectly whom Jesus claimed to be, and they didn't like it one bit (John 10:30--33). If your pastor, your church, your denomination, your cult, or your religion doesn't have the sense of a bunch of lost, Hell-bound Jews, that's their problem. Jesus Christ claimed to be God in the flesh!

["And the common people heard him gladly"]{.class_3064-0} (vs. 37). The problem never was with the average, everyday, ordinary Jew; he was eager to hear Christ's words (Luke 19:48) because they held an authority the words of the Pharisees, Sadducees, and scribes didn't have (Matt. 7:28--29).

38 And he said unto them in his doctrine, Beware of the scribes, which love to go in long clothing, and love salutations in the marketplaces,

39 And the chief seats in the synagogues, and the uppermost rooms at feasts:

40 Which devour widows' houses, and for a pretence make long prayers: these shall receive greater damnation.

The passage is an extremely abbreviated version of Christ's warnings in Matthew 23 against the religious leaders. Here, as in Luke 20:45--47, the Lord's words are aimed at the scribes, but in Matthew, the Pharisees are included (Matt. 23:1). These verses follow the last statement of verse 37, which means Jesus is addressing His remarks to ["the common people."]{.class_3064-0}

Now Christ's words here are just as pertinent today as they were in A.D. 33. You had better beware any Priest or "Rector" parading around ["in long clothing"]{.class_3064-0} (vs. 38) like a black "robe" (Luke 20:46). You had better watch out for any religious leader who loves to be "saluted" as "Father" (vs. 38 cf. Matt. 23:9). You had better avoid like the plague any church that charges a widow for a "high Mass" where ["long prayers"]{.class_3064-0} are made to get her dead husband out of a place that doesn't exist (Purgatory). You see, the Pharisees and scribes didn't die out in the First Century; they simply switched religions. They're still around today as Roman Catholics and Episcopalians.

For remarks on verse 39, we refer you to our comments on Luke 14:8--11 in that Commentary. The moral is [be humble or you'll stumble.]{.class_3066}

["These shall receive greater damnation"]{.class_3064-0} (vs. 40). The verse clearly teaches [degrees of punishment in Hell.]{.class_3066} You had better go to Hell as a pimp, prostitute, drug dealer, mafioso, hit man, drunkard, cat burglar, or corrupt politician than go to Hell as a Catholic Priest, Bishop, Cardinal, or Pope. Those type of men have damned millions of souls with their false doctrines and practices. By the way, that goes for [any]{.class_3066} false religious leader. You would be better off going to Hell as Adolf Hitler, Genghis Khan, or Jack the Ripper than you would as some of these "televangelists" or "emergents."

41 And Jesus sat over against the treasury, and beheld how the people cast money into the treasury: and many that were rich cast in much.

42 And there came a certain poor widow, and she threw in two mites, which make a farthing.

43 And he called unto him his disciples, and saith unto them, Verily I say unto you, That this poor widow hath []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2025.xhtml_page_331}cast more in, than all they which have cast into the treasury:

44 For all they did cast in of their abundance; but she of her want did cast in all that she had, even all her living.

You just read back in verse 40 of religious leaders devouring ["widows' houses,"]{.class_3064-0} and in this passage, the Holy Spirit gives you a "visual aid" of what Jesus said. Here is a widow woman giving her last two coins to fund the adornments of a building God had deserted (13:1; Luke 21:5 cf. Matt. 23:38; Luke 13:25).

Now what you read in verse 41 started back in 2 Kings 12:9. In that chapter, the Temple had fallen into disrepair because of two Baal worshipers: Ahaziah and Athaliah (2 Kings 10--11). When Jehoash came to the throne, he got rid of Baal worship and started to repair the Temple. To help raise money to do that, Jehoiada the high priest set a chest with a hole in the top at the entrance of the Temple. Whenever the Jews would come to worship, they could put money in that chest to make the repairs.

That is what is going on here, except that the money wasn't needed any longer to make repairs. Instead, it was used to hang up the chandeliers, build the million-dollar performing stage, set up a Belgian bell tower, install an Olympic-size swimming pool, put in a skating rink and bowling alley---you know what I mean, jelly bean? The Temple ["was adorned with goodly stones and gifts"]{.class_3064-0} (Luke 21:5).

Rich folks were coming in there and putting what would amount today as hundreds and thousands of dollars. In the middle of all those "high rollers" was a poor widow woman. ["She threw in two mites, which make a farthing"]{.class_3064-0} (vs. 42). In that economy, she could have bought ["two sparrows"]{.class_3064-0} for that amount (Matt. 10:29). In today's money, her "two mites" would not even equal a penny; a farthing was worth either a quarter of a cent or a half a cent, depending on what source you reference. She didn't give those ["two mites"]{.class_3064-0} because she was cheap; she gave that amount because [that was all she had]{.class_3066} (vs. 44). It wasn't even worth a full day's wage (Matt. 20:2). When she got through giving, she had [nothing]{.class_3066} left over to pay her bills because she had given ["all her living."]{.class_3064-0}

Now notice the wording in verse 41: []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2025.xhtml_page_332 .class-0}["And Jesus sat over against the treasury, and beheld HOW the people cast money into the treasury." ]{.class_3064-0}The Lord isn't concerned with [how much]{.class_3066} you give, but [how]{.class_3066} you give what you give. Don't misunderstand what I am about to say. I believe in tithing (see our comments on Mal. 3:8 in the [Bible Believer's Commentary on the Minor Prophets,]{.class_3066} Vol. II), but there is no New Testament command for the Christian to give a tenth of his income. The rule for New Testament giving is found in 2 Corinthians 9:7, and it deals with the [heart attitude]{.class_3066} of the giver---[how]{.class_3066} you give.

"Every man according as he purposeth in his heart, so let him give; not grudgingly, or of necessity: for God loveth a cheerful giver."

Jesus takes note of this widow woman and commends her because she gave [sacrificially. ]{.class_3066}She didn't give of her ["abundance"]{.class_3064-0} like the rich (vs. 44); she gave out of her deep poverty (["penury"]{.class_3064-0}---Luke 21:4). It would be like you putting your whole paycheck into the offering plate when you couldn't afford to. That's what I call "courageous giving."

Now I thank God for any Christian, to whom God has given a lot of money, who gives generously. Christian works need big givers, and I don't mean for "adornment." There are all kinds of missionaries that need support, tracts that need to be printed, Bibles to be distributed, good radio programs to be broadcasted, etc. Those things take money---a [lot]{.class_3066} of money. But it's not the amount you give that gets you rewards in Heaven. There will be many a widow or elderly saint on a fixed income who "cashes in" at the Judgment Seat of Christ, while a bunch of these multimillionaire Christian celebrities stand there with little to nothing. That's because those "senior citizens" give sacrificially, out of concern for the Gospel. The Lord takes note of that.

John Wesley said, "[Earn]{.class_3066} all you can, [save]{.class_3066} all you can, and GIVE all you can." That's good advice for any Christian of any economic status. Give what you can, while you can; and do it with a cheerful, generous heart.

[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2196.xhtml}

# Chapter 13 {.heading_97}

13:1 And as he went out of the temple, one of his disciples saith unto him, Master, see what manner of stones and what buildings are here!

2 And Jesus answering said unto him, Seest thou these great buildings? there shall not be left one stone upon another, that shall not be thrown down.

This is man trying to impress the Creator (John 1:1--3) with what man had made. That Temple was built off a pattern God showed to Moses on Mt. Sinai (Heb. 8:5). Do you think God was going to be impressed with what man built after creating and  seeing the real thing up in Heaven? Why, it would be like trying to impress the architect who built the Empire State Building with some little Lego or "tinker toy" model you made on your kitchen table.

Back there in the Old Testament, God said to those Jews, ["The heaven is my throne, and the earth is my footstool: where is the house that ye build unto me? and where is the place of my rest?"]{.class_3054} (Isa. 66:1). That's the verse Stephen quoted when he preached to the Sanhedrin; he followed it up with: ["Hath not my hand made all these things?"]{.class_3054} (Acts 7:50).

Here is a disciple talking to God in the flesh and saying, "Just look at this marble, Lord. Have you ever seen anything like it? And look at how everything is overlaid with gold. And look at those gems there." And in my mind's eye I can see Jesus yawn and say to Himself, "Yeah, yeah; I remember when I made all of that stuff." The One that fellow was trying to impress made all the building materials and made all the men who put that edifice together.

The disciple of verse 1 is undoubtedly Judas Iscariot. He was the covetous one (John 12:6), and he was the disciple who habitually called Jesus ["Master."]{.class_3054} At the Last Supper, Jesus said to the twelve, ["Ye call me Master and Lord"]{.class_3054} (John 13:13). Well, who called Him ["Master"]{.class_3054} and who called Him ["Lord"]{.class_3054} there at the table? When Jesus told them that one of them would betray Him, eleven of them called Jesus ["Lord"]{.class_3054} (Matt. 26:22; John 13:25), but Judas called Him ["Master"]{.class_3054}[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2196.xhtml_page_334} (Matt. 26:25). That's exactly what Judas called Jesus when he betrayed Him (Matt. 26:29; Mark 14:45). Jesus is about to give a discourse on the great prophetic events preceding His return, and He purposefully waits until Judas is [gone]{.class_3055} before discussing them (vs. 3).

Verse 2 is a prophecy of the destruction of the Temple under the Roman General Titus in A.D. 70. This must have astounded the disciples, for that Temple had been a 46-year "building program" of Rome to pacify the Jews (John 2:20). Moreover, according to Josephus, when Titus finally did take the city, he actually intended to spare the Temple itself. But by the time his troops fought their way to the Temple, they were in an uncontrollable rage against the Zealots holed up in the sanctuary. Against the orders of Titus, they set fire to the Temple. So after the city was conquered, Titus gave the order to raze the city to the ground, leaving only three towers standing and the retaining wall on the western side of the Temple Mount. In their eagerness to find "buried treasure," the Romans dismantled every stone of the Temple and plowed the ground underneath (see Jer. 26:18) until no one could tell a Temple had ever been there.

What Jesus Christ said, took place exactly as He said it would. The thing that sets the Bible apart from any other religious book, and any of the 23 million books in the Library of Congress, is [fulfilled prophecy.]{.class_3055} When that Book says something will come to pass, you can bet your bottom dollar it will take place---[no matter how unlikely it seems.]{.class_3055}

Now what was true of that Temple in Jerusalem is true of any building you see. One day, these twenty-million dollar "launching pads for ladyfingers," known as "campuses" of Christian Colleges and Universities and "mega-churches," will [burn,]{.class_3055} and all that investment will go up in smoke (2 Pet. 3:10--12). The libraries, bell towers, swimming pools, dormitories, auditoriums, crystal chandeliers, planetariums, art museums, dining commons, sports arenas, and performing stages will be [gone]{.class_3055} as if they never existed. Only what you invested in the [souls of men]{.class_3055} will last.

3 And as he sat upon the mount of Olives over against []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2196.xhtml_page_335}the temple, Peter and James and John and Andrew asked him privately,

4 Tell us, when shall these things be? and what shall be the sign when all these things shall be fulfilled?

This is the introduction to what is known as the "Olivet Discourse." It is Christ's teaching on His Second Advent. Jesus has left the city to go to the Mount of Olives. He is on the side of the hill facing the Temple (["over against the temple"]{.class_3054}---vs. 3). It is given on the Mount of Olives because when He returns in power, [that]{.class_3055} is where He comes (Zech. 14:4 cf. Acts 1:11--12).

The discourse is not given to the Twelve generally. It is given ["privately"]{.class_3054} to His "inner circle"---Peter, James, and John; with the addition, this time, of Simon Peter's brother, Andrew. The disciple who drew Jesus' attention to the Temple buildings and their adornments is missing---Judas Iscariot (see our comments on vs. 1). The Son of Perdition (John 17:12 cf. 2 Thess. 2:3) doesn't get the revelation.

Now, the disciples ask two questions: 1) ["when shall these things be?"]{.class_3054} and 2) ["what shall be the sign when all these things shall be fulfilled?"]{.class_3054} (vs. 4). The preterists, who teach that the prophecies of Christ's Second Advent were fulfilled at the destruction of Jerusalem in A.D. 70, latch onto the fact that the first question deals with Christ's words in verse 2, so they make the second question deal with the same thing. But once again, you have a situation where Mark doesn't give you everything that was said. Sometimes Mark will go into detail (as in the case of the maniac of Gadara and blind Bartimaeus), and sometimes he won't (such as in 8:12 and 10:2). You have to compare Scripture with Scripture to get the full account.

["The sign"]{.class_3054} of verse 4 has nothing to do with the destruction of Jerusalem. Peter, James, John, and Andrew were asking for a sign of Christ's ["coming, and of the end of the world"]{.class_3054} (Matt. 24:3). So while there may be some things in the Olivet Discourse that can be applied [historically]{.class_3055} to Titus in A.D. 70, the [main]{.class_3055} application---i.e., the [doctrinal]{.class_3055} application---will be [prophetic,]{.class_3055} dealing with [the end times]{.class_3055} (vss. 7, 13). We will point out the [double applications]{.class_3055} as we go []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2196.xhtml_page_336}through the chapter.

Of course, the disciples asked for a sign because ["the Jews require a sign"]{.class_3054} (1 Cor. 1:22). Their nation began with signs (Exod. 4). As long as God deals with and through those Jews, the Lord gives them signs (Mark 16:17--20; Acts 2:22; 2 Cor. 12:12). When God stops dealing with those Jews as a nation, the signs cease (e.g., see 2 Tim. 4:20 and our comments on that verse in the [Bible Believer's Commentary on the Pastoral Epistles]{.class_3055}). But in the Tribulation, after the Church has been called out of this earth, the Lord starts dealing with Israel again, so the signs come back (see Rev. 11:5--6).

The signs given here are signs of Christ's [Second Advent,]{.class_3055} not the Rapture. But even if the signs aren't meant for you, you can still read them. Jim White used to give an illustration about driving up I-75 to his home in Milford, Ohio. He would see signs for Dayton being so many miles, but he wasn't headed for Dayton. He was headed for Milford. But he knew the closer he got to Dayton, the closer he got to Milford, because Milford was something like 35 miles before Dayton. So if you see a sign for the Second Advent, you know the Rapture must be all that much closer, because the Rapture of the Church takes place before the Tribulation, and the Second Advent is at the end of the Tribulation. So even though the signs are not for you as a Christian, they can still be an encouragement to you.

5 And Jesus answering them began to say, Take heed lest any man deceive you:

6 For many shall come in my name, saying, I am Christ; and shall deceive many.

7 And when ye shall hear of wars and rumours of wars, be ye not troubled: for such things must needs be; but the end shall not be yet.

8 For nation shall rise against nation, and kingdom against kingdom: and there shall be earthquakes in divers places, and there shall be famines and troubles: these are the beginnings of sorrows.

["Take heed lest any man deceive you"]{.class_3054} (vs. 5). The command is reiterated in the epistles---Ephesians 5:6; 2 []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2196.xhtml_page_337}Thessalonians 2:3; and 1 John 3:7. It is accompanied by the commands to ["Be not deceived"]{.class_3054} (Gal. 6:7; 1 Cor. 6:9, 15:53) and ["Let no man deceive himself"]{.class_3054} (1 Cor. 3:18). Why is it necessary to ["Take heed"]{.class_3054} you aren't deceived? Because in the days preceding Christ's return, deceivers will be coming out of the woodwork by the bushel-basket load (2 Tim. 3:13). What you have to watch out for is those ["good words and fair speeches"]{.class_3054} they use to ["deceive the hearts of the simple"]{.class_3054} (Rom. 16:18). They are like those false prophets back there in the days of Jeremiah who were all positive and no negative (Jer. 23:16--17); that was a [perversion]{.class_3055} of the word of God (Jer. 23:36). So when you hear some preacher who won't preach on Hell or sin, talking about "your best life now" and the "power of positive thinking" and "possibility thinking," you had better watch out! That's a deceiver who has deceived himself and is trying to deceive you too.

Now when Satan shows up in the flesh, he ["deceiveth the whole earth"]{.class_3054} (Rev. 12:9). He does it through ["miracles"]{.class_3054} (Rev. 13:14) and ["lying wonders"]{.class_3054} (2 Thess. 2:9--10), accomplished by a church that practices black magic in its sacraments (Rev. 18:23). So Christ's warning is particularly applicable to Jews going through the Tribulation. If they don't ["take heed,"]{.class_3054} there is a possibility of being deceived (Matt. 24:24) and being damned.

Having warned the disciples to be careful of deception, Jesus follows it up with [what]{.class_3055} will ["deceive many"]{.class_3054}: it is [false Christs]{.class_3055} (vs. 6). They are accompanied by [false prophets]{.class_3055} (vs. 22). So in the Tribulation, there is not only the Antichrist and False Prophet, there are a lot of false Christs and false prophets.

These false Christs and false prophets start popping up after the resurrection of Christ. The book of Acts talks about ["Theudas"]{.class_3054} and an ["Egyptian,"]{.class_3054} whom Josephus identifies as false prophets who insighted rebellion against Rome to deliver the Jews (Acts 5:36, 21:38). During the destruction of Jerusalem, after Titus offered to spare the Temple and spare any Jew who laid down his arms and came out to the Roman army, a false prophet promised the Jews that God would miraculously deliver them if they came to the Temple and fought the Romans from there. And of course, you know how that turned out (see vs. 2).

But the real "deluge" of false Christs started [after]{.class_3055}[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2196.xhtml_page_338} the destruction of the Temple and Jerusalem. At the second Jewish uprising in A.D. 132, there was Simon bar Kokhba. The [Jewish Encyclopedia]{.class_3055} (1986) lists Moses of Crete (who promised to lead the Jews dryshod through the Mediterranean back to the Promised Land), Ishak ben Yakub, Yudghan, Serene, one in France, one in Cordova (Spain), one in Fez (Morocco), David Alroy, one in Yemen, Abraham Abulfia, Moses Botarel, Asher Lemmlein, David Reubeni, Solomon Molko, Isaac Luria, Hayyim Vital Calabrese, Abraham Shalom, one at Coromandel (India), Shabbethai Zebi, Jacob Querido and his son Berechiah, Miguel Cardoso, Mordecai Mokiah, Lobele Prossnitz, Isaiah Hasid, Jonathan Eybeschutz, Jacob Frank, and Moses Luzzatto. It didn't stop in the Eighteenth Century, either. There was Shukr Kuhayl I and Judah ben Shalom in the Nineteenth Century, and Moses Guibbory and Menachem Schneerson in the Twentieth Century.

That is what the Jews got for rejecting Jesus Christ. It would be funny if it weren't so tragic. Some of those fellows were trying to claim the Old Testament promises and got themselves into some first-rate "fixes." The Mohammedans arrested one, and he asked them to cut off his head so he could prove his claims. The idea was he would come back to life after they decapitated him.[ They did; he didn't.]{.class_3055} Moses Botarel challenged those Spanish Catholics to throw him in a "fiery furnace," and he would survive just like Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego.[ He ended up a "crispy critter."]{.class_3055} Old Shabbethai Zebi, to save his own life, converted to Islam; his followers justified what he did by saying he was fulfilling Isaiah 53:12 (["he was numbered with the transgressors"]{.class_3054}). [Likely story.]{.class_3055}

But the most tragic thing of all was that through twenty centuries of this nonsense, the Jews continued to reject the one Claimant who could actually back up His claim to being the Messiah. He actually fulfilled Isaiah 7:12 and Micah 5:2 in His birth. God actually sent a forerunner before Him in fulfillment of Malachi 3:1 and Isaiah 40:3. He didn't just talk about miracles; He performed them on a regular basis. He healed the sick, restored sight, made the deaf to hear, delivered the possessed from the Devil with just His word, and raised the dead. And when He was finally executed out of envy, He actually did come up from the dead and had it verified by over 500 []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2196.xhtml_page_339}eyewitnesses at one time (1 Cor. 15:6). The Jews rejected the One who would have taken them under His wings in compassion and love for a bunch of demented, deranged religious lunatics with delusions of grandeur.

But that is not the half of it. The "best" is yet to come. Jesus said, ["I am come in my Father's name, and ye receive me not: if another shall come in his own name, him ye will receive"]{.class_3054} (John 5:43). Not only will ["many"]{.class_3054} of the Jews be deceived by false Christs and false prophets in the Tribulation, many of them will accept the [Devil incarnate]{.class_3055} as the Messiah. They rejected the One who performed miracles from God (John 5:36 cf. John 15:24), so the Lord allows the Devil's Christ to come along ["with all power and signs and lying wonders . . . with all deceivableness of unrighteousness"]{.class_3054} (2 Thess. 2:9--10). The Lord reinforces those ["lying wonders"]{.class_3054} and deceiving miracles with ["strong delusion, that they should believe a lie"]{.class_3054} because they [rejected His Son]{.class_3055} (2 Thess. 2:11--12). And whereas those false Messiahs throughout the Middle Ages and on into the modern era merely led their followers to destruction and death, ["the prince that shall come"]{.class_3054} will destroy Jerusalem and the rebuilt temple (Dan. 9:26), and attempt to exterminate the very people who claimed him as their Messiah (Rev. 12:13--15; Dan. 8:24). If it were not for God sending down Moses and Elijah to preach to them and get them right (Mal. 4:4--6 cf. Rev. 11:3--7), and if it were not for the Lord Himself fighting for them (Zech. 12, 14), there would be no Jew left alive at the end of Daniel's Seventieth Week.

The next thing that leads up to Christ's return is ["wars and rumours of wars"]{.class_3054} (vs. 7). Isn't that what you are reading in your morning newspaper and seeing on the evening newscast? Isn't it North Korea getting ready for war, ISIS waging war to create its own caliphate, Syria warring against its own people, Russia waging war against Ukraine, Iran developing a nuclear weapon to go to war with Israel, the Taliban carrying on a guerilla war in Pakistan and Afghanistan, America trying to be the "policeman" of the world by sending its troops everywhere to fight undeclared wars, etc., etc., etc.? It's ["nation . . . against nation, and kingdom against kingdom"]{.class_3054} (vs. 8) without a let-up.

You say, "There've always been wars, Ruckman." Not like the last 120 years, no. Before 1900, the bloodiest war America had was the Civil War: a total of 191,963 casualties on both sides. After 1900, we had four wars in a row that totalled more casualties than [all]{.class_3055} the wars America had fought up to that point: World War I---53,402; World War II---291,557; Korea---33,741; Vietnam---47,424. We have been fighting a constant war against Moslems since 1991 (Persian Gulf, Afghanistan, Iraq, Libya, Syria, Al Qaeda, ISIS, and very soon Iran) with no permanent, satisfactory outcome.

There were more people killed in wars during the Twentieth Century than between A.D. 90 and 1900. Since the founding of the United Nations in 1945 to usher in the "greatest era of peace ever to come," there have been 177 armed conflicts throughout the world (as of 2008), with 70 million being killed by their own governments in the Twentieth Century (genocide).

But as Christ said, ["the end shall not be yet."]{.class_3054} The biggest and best wars are yet to come (Rev. 6, 19, 20). In Revelation 9:15--18, a third of the population of the earth is slaughtered by one army. In today's statistics, that would be something like two billion, 400 million people; that doesn't include another one billion, 750 million killed in Revelation 6:8. Before the Advent, well over half the population of the earth is killed off in wars.

Verse 8: ["and there shall be earthquakes in divers places."]{.class_3054} The [KJV]{.class_3055} word ["divers,"]{.class_3054} of course, has nothing to do with SCUBA divers or deep-sea divers. It is the old spelling of [diverse.]{.class_3055} It means a "variety" or "very different." It simply means there will be earthquakes in all kinds of places.

Again, it is not merely a matter of earthquakes always happening or even better ways of recording earthquakes. According to Tom Parsons and Eric Geist of the U.S. Geological Survey, major earthquakes of 7.0 and above on the Richter Scale have [increased]{.class_3055} since 1979. Moreover, they aren't linked to one another in a certain region, like smaller quakes which cluster together. They are scattered around in [diverse places.]{.class_3055}

But the "big one," when it comes to earthquakes, is not the one that will sink half of California into the Pacific Ocean. In three places in the book of Revelation, the Holy Spirit tells you about ["a great earthquake"]{.class_3054}[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2196.xhtml_page_341} that takes place during the Tribulation (Rev. 6:12, 11:13, 16:18--20).
