He seemingly has a human father (John 12:4, 13:26), but the ["perdition"]{.class_3057-0} of ["son of perdition"]{.class_3057-0} (John 17:12) is not merely a state of being; it is a [person]{.class_3058-0} (see our remarks on ["Apollyon"]{.class_3057-0} and ["perdition"]{.class_3057-0} up under vs. 4). Judas is in the same position as [Cain]{.class_3058-0} (a type of the Antichrist), ["who was OF THAT WICKED ONE"]{.class_3057-0} and responsible for the death of his brother Abel (1 John 3:12)---[a type of Christ]{.class_3058-0} (Heb. 12:24). Judas is a "hybrid," just like the spawn of those ["sons of God"]{.class_3057-0} back in Genesis 6:4. Some of his "brethren" will be showing up here in the near future (see our comments on Dan. 2:43 in that Commentary).

Notice, in verse 10, how Judas is identified---["one of the twelve."]{.class_3057-0} That observation is made several times by the Gospel writers (Matt. 26:14, 47; Mark 14:43; Luke 22:3, 47; John 6:71). That means he was out with his buddy (6:7) casting out devils and healing the sick (6:13) and preaching repentance (6:12). Was he saved? Not unless ["a devil"]{.class_3057-0} can be saved, no (John 6:70--71). Was he performing miracles by the power of the Holy Ghost? Well, no more than ["Jannes and Jambres"]{.class_3057-0} were when they ["withstood Moses"]{.class_3057-0} (2 Tim. 3:8 cf. Exod. 7:9--12, 19--22, 8:5--7). Like he will when he shows up the second time, ["the son of perdition"]{.class_3057-0} performed ["all power and signs and lying wonders" "after the working of Satan"]{.class_3057-0} (2 Thess. 2:3--9). People, just because some Charismatic fakir confesses "Jesus is Lord!" and can prophesy, cast out demons, and cause gold dust to fall from the ceiling doesn't mean he is any more saved than your Aunt Sally's house cat (Matt. 7:22--23).

Judas poses quite a "sticky wicket" for the Baptist Brider, especially if he believes in Eternal Security. If the only church in the New Testament is the local church where members enter Christ's "Body" by water baptism (which is how all Baptist Briders interpret 1 Cor. 12:13), and the Body is Christ's Bride (as it most certainly is---Eph. 5:23--32), that means one of the members of Christ's Bride [went []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2374.xhtml_page_375}to Hell.]{.class_3058-0}

This, of course, is a fulfillment of prophecy (see Psa. 41:9, 55:12--14), as is the amount of money the chief priest promised to give Judas (vs. 11 cf. Matt. 26:15, 27:9; Zech. 11:12). Who knows how many times, while growing up, Judas heard those prophecies from Psalms and Zechariah read without realizing [they were about him.]{.class_3058-0} Once again, we are faced with the ghastly truth that a man can know exactly what the Lord plans to do to him if he does wrong, and [he will go through with it anyway]{.class_3058-0} (see our remarks on 12:12).

If a sinner rejects the light God gives him, he loses the ability to find, understand, and act on the warnings given to him. The saying is: "Light rejected becomes lightning." How else do you account for the United Nations rejecting over 400 years of revelation about them (see our comments on Zeph. 3:8 in that Commentary) and taking up arms against a Man from outer space who needs no space suit, starship, saucer, or reentry capsule to travel over 47 billion light years in a matter of minutes (Rev. 19:11). The United Nations will fulfill their God-ordained destiny, and they will do so with no relation at all to "Total Depravity," "Reprobation," or "Limited Atonement." They will do so based on their [rejection of God's Son]{.class_3058-0} (2 Thess. 1:8, 2:10--13).

Having been deprived of whatever money he could have stolen from the ["more than three hundred pence"]{.class_3057-0} from the sale of the ointment (vs. 5), Judas decides to turn a profit from the [sale of his Master]{.class_3058-0} (see our remarks under 13:1). Judas sold out the ["King of the Jews"]{.class_3057-0} (Matt. 2:2 cf. Mark 15:26) for the price of a [slave]{.class_3058-0} (Exod. 21:32) or the ["estimation"]{.class_3057-0} of ["a female"]{.class_3057-0} between twenty and sixty years old for ["a singular vow"]{.class_3057-0} (Lev. 27:1--4).

["The love of money is the root of all evil"]{.class_3057-0} (1 Tim. 6:10). I know the new "Bibles" don't say that, but the Conservatives, Fundamentalists, Liberals, and Modernists on those translation and revision committees don't have good sense half the time anyway. [In this age]{.class_3058-0} (the statement was given as present-tense truth for the Church Age; not back in Isa. 14:12--14), ["the love of money is the root of ALL evil,"]{.class_3057-0} not just ["all kinds"]{.class_3058-0} like the corrupt perversions of Scripture read. The envy of the Jews and the cowardice of Pilate towards Jesus Christ would have found no fruition without the greed []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2374.xhtml_page_376}of Judas.

["And when they heard it, they were glad"]{.class_3057-0} (vs. 11). Look at that. It is repeated in Luke 22:5, but it is the only time in the life of Christ those Pharisees and Sadducees were ["glad"]{.class_3057-0} about anything in Christ's ministry. They called Him a blasphemer for forgiving sins (2:7). They complained of Him receiving sinners to repentance (2:16--17 cf. Luke 15:2). They accused Him of being demon-possessed when He delivered people from devils (3:22), and would rather have their means of income instead of a peaceful, safe town because Jesus had delivered a maniac in their midst (5:14--17). ["They laughed him to scorn"]{.class_3057-0} when He raised a dead girl (5:40--42). They were offended at His teaching (6:1--3). They exalted their own traditions and commandments over His words (7:1--13). They tempted Him (8:11, 10:2) and tried to entrap Him in His words (12:13). But they never rejoiced or were glad over any good thing He did. In fact, they wanted to kill Lazarus after Jesus had raised him from the dead because many of the people believed on Jesus because of that miracle (John 12:10--11).

The only thing in the recorded account that "made their day" was one of Christ's closest disciples betraying Him to death. They were such hypocrites that they wouldn't enter a Gentile building so that they would be "clean" enough to eat the Passover (John 18:28), but they had no problem conspiring to [murder]{.class_3058-0} Jesus Christ.

["And he sought how he might CONVENIENTLY betray him."]{.class_3057-0} The word ["convenient"]{.class_3057-0} appears nine times in the Bible, and once in its adverbial form here in verse 11. The words have two basic definitions: 1) "fit, suitable, proper" and 2) "adapted to [use]{.class_3058-0} or [wants."]{.class_3058-0} One can see that, when it comes to [morals,]{.class_3058-0} the first set of definitions are to be judged by an [absolute standard]{.class_3058-0} of right and wrong (e.g., Rom. 1:28; Eph. 5:4), while the second is [pragmatism,]{.class_3058-0} based on one's whims. That is the use in Mark 6:21 (Herod); Acts 24:25 (Felix); and here in verse 11 (Judas). It is what is right, proper, and suitable [for you.]{.class_3058-0} The modern philosophy is: "Truth is whatever is true [for you;]{.class_3058-0} it may not be true for anyone else." Of course, in Darwin's evolutionary jungle of "survival of the fittest" and "natural selection," it works out that truth is truth for the ones who have the most politicians, legislators, judges, police, "G-men," bullets, guns, []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2374.xhtml_page_377}tanks, missiles, RPGs, mobs, etc. "Might makes right." They will enforce their "truth" on you whether it is so or not, whether it works or not, and whether you agree with it or not. That kind of "truth" will [damn]{.class_3058-0} you every time (cf. John 14:6).

12 And the first day of unleavened bread, when they killed the passover, his disciples said unto him, Where wilt thou that we go and prepare that thou mayest eat the passover?

13 And he sendeth forth two of his disciples, and saith unto them, Go ye into the city, and there shall meet you a man bearing a pitcher of water: follow him.

14 And wheresoever he shall go in, say ye to the goodman of the house, The Master saith, Where is the guestchamber, where I shall eat the passover with my disciples?

15 And he will shew you a large upper room furnished and prepared: there make ready for us.

16 And his disciples went forth, and came into the city, and found as he had said unto them: and they made ready the passover.

Verse 12 refers to Tuesday evening, after six o'clock. That is when the Jewish Wednesday would begin. The passage is the late afternoon leading up to that evening.

Wednesday was the day immediately before the Passover which started on Wednesday evening at 6 p.m. (beginning of the Jewish Thursday). It was ["the preparation"]{.class_3057-0} (15:42) because that is when the unleavened bread for the Passover supper was baked and the Passover lamb slain (vs. 12). It couldn't be done on the Passover itself, because Passover was a special sabbath (John 19:31) in which no work was to be done (Lev. 23:5--7). There is no trouble at all figuring out when Christ was crucified if one will believe what he reads in the Book instead of Catholic mythology.

Verse 13 is Jesus exercising his divine attribute of omniscience, just as He did back in 11:2--3 (see our comments on that passage). This time, J. Vernon McGee is divided on his interpretation of the []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2374.xhtml_page_378}passage. He will make verse 13 a display of Christ's omniscience because there is no getting around that. Jerusalem was filled at the Passover with nearly three million people. That means the odds of meeting the right man with a pitcher of water, who could lead the two disciples to the right house, was at least one in three million. Jesus knew exactly where and to whom to lead the disciples.

But McGee will make verses 14--15 a prearranged thing between Jesus and ["the goodman of the house."]{.class_3057-0} It makes wonderful filler for a radio program, but it misses the point. Jesus was about to partake of the Passover a full 24 hours before everyone else in Jerusalem did. The ["goodman"]{.class_3057-0} of verse 14 had the guest chamber fully prepared for a Passover supper on Tuesday evening, right when Christ and the disciples needed it; not on Wednesday evening when everyone else did. That was God moving that guy to do that without him knowing the reason he did it.

Now everything Jesus says comes to pass (vs. 16). Jesus and the disciples are eating the Passover early because, of course, the next day, [He will be the Passover lamb]{.class_3058-0} (1 Cor. 5:7).

17 And in the evening he cometh with the twelve.

18 And as they sat and did eat, Jesus said, Verily I say unto you, One of you which eateth with me shall betray me.

19 And they began to be sorrowful, and to say unto him one by one, Is it I? and another said, Is it I?

20 And he answered and said unto them, It is one of the twelve, that dippeth with me in the dish.

21 The Son of man indeed goeth, as it is written of him: but woe to that man by whom the Son of man is betrayed! good were it for that man if he had never been born.

This is what is known as the "Last Supper," and it has been memorialized by Leonardo Da Vinci in his famous painting by the same name. Of course, Da Vinci didn't have the sense of a brass monkey when it came to illustrating the Bible. He has Jesus and the disciples all sitting at one long table, all facing the same direction (which makes conversation quite a problem), with an Italian []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2374.xhtml_page_379}landscape out the window in the background. None of that was the case. If there were any windows in that guest chamber, it would have been the bustling city of [Jerusalem]{.class_3058-0} in their view. Nobody sat bolt upright on chairs at a table, much less everyone facing the same direction. They would be on their sides on ["beds"]{.class_3057-0} (Esth. 1:5--6, 7:8; Ezek. 23:41; Amos 6:4)---low-lying reclining couches, around a table (a board) near the ground. You know that to be the case at the Last Supper because John is lying there with his head on Christ's chest (John 13:25).

As they are eating, Jesus breaks the news of His betrayal to them. Of course, that throws a wet blanket over the feast, and ["they began to be sorrowful"]{.class_3057-0} (vs. 19). Then ["one by one,"]{.class_3057-0} they begin to ask Him, ["Is it I?"]{.class_3057-0} (vs. 19). They have a guilty conscience and know, under the right circumstances, they could do it. All of them, [except one,]{.class_3058-0} that is. Only one man there at the table had a clear conscience in the matter. It was [John.]{.class_3058-0} He doesn't ask, ["Is it I?"]{.class_3057-0} He asks, ["Lord, who is it?"]{.class_3057-0} (John 13:25).

Something left out by Mark, which Matthew records, is Judas' response to this news. Ten of the disciples not only ask ["Is it I?"]{.class_3057-0}, they address Christ as ["Lord"]{.class_3057-0} (Matt. 26:22). John, too, addresses Him as ["Lord"]{.class_3057-0} when he asks, ["Lord, who is it?"]{.class_3057-0} (John 13:25). But Judas is the [last]{.class_3058-0} to ask; he waits until after Christ's words in verses 20--21 here in Mark 14 (cf. Matt. 26:23--24). [Then]{.class_3058-0} he asks Jesus, ["MASTER]{.class_3057-0} \[not Lord\][, is it I?"]{.class_3057-0} (Matt. 26:25).

Christ identifies the traitor to the disciples as ["one of the twelve, that dippeth with me in the dish"]{.class_3057-0} (vs. 20). This was a normal practice for the Jews, even at regular meals (see Ruth 2:14 and our comments on the verse in the [Bible Believer's Commentary on Judges and Ruth]{.class_3058-0}). It certainly did not "narrow the field," though, for the disciples, for none of them at the table knew why Jesus told Judas, ["That thou doest, do quickly"]{.class_3057-0} (John 13:27--28). And when Judas left to betray the Lord, the assumption of the other disciples was that Judas, as the treasurer, had gone to ["give something to the poor"]{.class_3057-0} (John 13:29). So most, if not all, of the disciples must have dipped with Jesus ["in the dish"]{.class_3057-0} at some point during the supper. A more specific revelation was given to John alone about the presentation of the sop (John 13:25--26).

["The Son of man indeed goeth, as it is written of him"]{.class_3057-0} (vs. 21)---[fulfilled prophecy.]{.class_3058-0} After His resurrection, Jesus took those two disciples on the road to Emmaus and gave them a "survey course" through the Old Testament on His ["sufferings . . . and the glory that should follow"]{.class_3057-0} (Luke 24:26--27 cf. 1 Pet. 1:10--11). If it's in that Book, it is going to come to pass surer than day follows night (it [won't]{.class_3058-0} one day---see our remarks under 13:24).

Now Christian, let that be an encouragement to you. You have, [in writing,]{.class_3058-0} the promise that if you have trusted Christ as your Saviour, [nothing]{.class_3058-0} can separate you from God's love (Rom. 8:38--39), that the Holy Spirit has sealed you until the day your Lord returns to redeem your body (Eph. 4:30), that your standing in Christ is ["blameless"]{.class_3057-0} to the day He comes to take you home to Heaven (1 Cor. 1:7--8)---you can be absolutely ["confident"]{.class_3057-0}[ ]{.class_3081}of that (Phil. 1:6)---and that should you die before the Rapture, you will be ["present with the Lord"]{.class_3057-0} even though you are ["absent from the body"]{.class_3057-0} (2 Cor. 5:8). You have the full authority of infallible Scripture behind your salvation, Christian. According to your Saviour, that ["scripture cannot be broken"]{.class_3057-0} (John 10:35). Your salvation is as sure as the fact that Jesus would have to die for your sins and rise again. You [will]{.class_3058-0} make it to Heaven, Christian, whether you believe it or not---that's a [fact.]{.class_3058-0} If you want the [feeling]{.class_3058-0} of security that comes from assurance of salvation, you are going to have to [believe]{.class_3058-0} the promise in the Book (1 John 5:13).

["But woe to that man by whom the Son of man is betrayed"]{.class_3057-0}---a warning to Judas. That warning had been preceded by the warning of Christ back in Matthew 18:7.

"Woe unto the world because of offences! for it must needs be that offences come; but woe to that man by whom the offence cometh."

There is the "sovereign will of God" working conjunctively with the "free will of man." God has "sovereignly decreed" that offenses come, but you don't have to be the one who commits them. As a [man]{.class_3058-0} (and I understand that is hard to reconcile with Judas being ["a devil"]{.class_3057-0} \[John 6:70--71\] and ["the son of perdition"]{.class_3057-0} \[John 17:12\]), Judas had as much of a choice [not]{.class_3058-0} to betray Christ as ["the man Christ Jesus"]{.class_3057-0} (1 Tim. 2:5) had to [yield to temptation]{.class_3058-0} in Matthew 4. As Christ, Judas had [two natures:]{.class_3058-0} that of a [man]{.class_3058-0} and that of a []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2374.xhtml_page_381 .class-0}[devil.]{.class_3058-0}

["Good were it for that man if he had never been born."]{.class_3057-0} That was Job's wish in his troubles (Job 3), and Jeremiah's lament in Jeremiah 20:14--15. Jesus said that, in the Tribulation, it would be better if the Jewish women never bore any children (Luke 23:29).

22 And as they did eat, Jesus took bread, and blessed, and brake it, and gave to them, and said, Take, eat: this is my body.

23 And he took the cup, and when he had given thanks, he gave it to them: and they all drank of it.

24 And he said unto them, This is my blood of the new testament, which is shed for many.

25 Verily I say unto you, I will drink no more of the fruit of the vine, until that day that I drink it new in the kingdom of God.

26 And when they had sung an hymn, they went out into the mount of Olives.

["And as they did eat"]{.class_3057-0} (vs. 22), and what follows is the Lord's Supper, communion. The Baptist Briders, in order to support their doctrine that each local church is the Body of Christ, practice what is known as "closed communion"; that is, only members of that particular congregation can partake of the Lord's Supper. To eliminate the problem of Judas Iscariot being a baptized member of the local church (see our remarks under vs. 10), they have Judas leaving before communion is instituted.

But the Lord's Supper was instituted ["AS they did eat."]{.class_3057-0} Judas didn't leave till after the supper was over (John 13:2 cf. John 13:26--30). Judas was right there at the table as Jesus gave the first communion (Luke 22:20--22): he partook of the elements.

The Catholic Church has obviously never considered the implication of this in regards to what it teaches about the "Mass." If the elements are the actual body and blood of Jesus Christ (see its perversion of John 6:55--56, while ignoring John 6:57), then what you have is the Son of Perdition eating Christ's flesh and drinking Christ's blood, and getting in Christ (John 6:56) and having ["eternal life"]{.class_3057-0}[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2374.xhtml_page_382 .class-0} (John 6:54). The teaching of the Pope and the Bishops results in a [saved]{.class_3058-0} ["devil"]{.class_3057-0} (John 6:70--71).

Now what about this business of "transubstantiation"? After all, Jesus did say of the bread, ["This is my body."]{.class_3057-0} Well, is it His [literal]{.class_3058-0} body? The Catholic Church says, "Yes." So in the Roman Catholic Mass, what you have is "holy" [cannibalism]{.class_3058-0} and "holy" [vampirism]{.class_3058-0} (the drinking of literal blood). (There's no need to be squeamish; that's what it is if the elements are Christ's literal body and blood.)

(By the way, the magic words [hocus pocus]{.class_3058-0} come from the Latin expression used in the Mass for ["this is my body"]{.class_3057-0}: ["Hoc est corpus meum."]{.class_3058-0} So what you have in the Roman Catholic Mass is an act of [black magic]{.class_3058-0} performed by a [witch doctor.]{.class_3058-0})

The answer is, it [can't]{.class_3058-0} be literal. You say, "Why not?" Because His literal, physical body is right there at the table, breaking the bread. His blood is still in the veins of that body: He hadn't shed one drop of it yet. Moreover, it can't be His [spiritual]{.class_3058-0} Body, either, because that doesn't show up until His death on the cross (Eph. 2:16). Since it [can't]{.class_3058-0} be His physical body or His spiritual body, then it must be a [figure]{.class_3058-0} of His body. The Lord is using a [similitude]{.class_3058-0} to show you something.

One of the basic rules of Biblical interpretation is if a passage of Scripture can possibly be taken literally, then it is to be taken literally; if it can't, then it is to be taken figuratively. For example, as a pastor, I am commanded to ["Feed the flock of God"]{.class_3057-0} (1 Pet. 5:2). Well, my congregation isn't a herd of wooly, four-footed critters that I set out to pasture. The ["flock"]{.class_3057-0} is a figurative term.

If there is any doubt the Lord is using figurative language, look at the next couple of verses: ["And he took the cup . . . And he said unto them, This is my blood"]{.class_3057-0} (vss. 23--24). What? The cup? Don't be stupid. They didn't drink the cup; they drank the [contents]{.class_3058-0} of the cup. That's a figure of speech known as "synecdoche," where the contents are called by their container (among other things).

[Now when it comes to a knowledge of that Book, just to show you how stupid Americans are, including professing Christians (and especially Catholics), hardly anybody looks up what the Bible says about the matter to find out why Christ couldn't possibly be talking about His literal blood in verse 24. Three times in that Bible, the Holy Spirit tells you it is a ]{.class_3056}[sin]{.class_3058-1}[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2374.xhtml_page_383}[ to drink blood: before the Law, during the Law, and in the Church Age, under "grace."]{.class_3056}

Before the Law---["But flesh with the life thereof, which is the BLOOD thereof, SHALL YE NOT EAT"]{.class_3057-0} (Gen. 9:4).

Under the Law---["YE SHALL NOT EAT ANY THING WITH THE BLOOD: neither shall ye use enchantment ]{.class_3057-0}\[see the comments on [hocus pocus]{.class_3058-0} above\][, nor observe times"]{.class_3057-0} (Lev. 19:26). (See also Lev. 3:17, 7:26, 17:10--14; Deut. 12:16, 23, 15:23.)

Under "grace" in the New Testament---["But that we write unto them, that they ABSTAIN from pollutions of idols ]{.class_3057-0}\[like Madonnas, crucifixes, St. Christopher medallions, pietas, images of the saints, etc.\][, and from fornication, and from things strangled, AND FROM BLOOD"]{.class_3057-0} (Acts 15:20).

That last one is to saved, born-again Christians in the Body of Christ. If Jesus Christ commanded His disciples to drink His literal blood, then He violated the commandments of God under three "testaments" and would be a [sinner.]{.class_3058-0} If that fermented hootch in the Mass is literal blood (in an "unbloody" form, of course, of course), you are headed for [Hell]{.class_3058-0}.

The practical implications of this "forged fable and dangerous deceit" (to quote Article 30 of the Thirty-Nine Articles of the Church of England) are just too funny for words. If an insect or spider were found in the communion wine, the Priest either had to swallow it with the wine, or remove it, wash it off over the communion chalice, and then burn it. If a rat ran off with the "host" (the communion wafer), the Priest had to do forty days of penance for letting the rat carry off "Christ." Back in the Dark Ages, if the Priest vomited up the host, he was to eat the puke in its entirety. Now it is allowed to be picked up in a linen cloth, dissolved in water, and poured down a "sacred sink" (called the ["sacrities sacrarium"]{.class_3058-0}---no kidding) so that it drains down into the earth instead of the sewer. Why being dumped in the sewer should be such a problem is anybody's guess, since, according to Jesus Christ, ["all meats" "goeth out into the draught"]{.class_3057-0} anyway (see 7:19 and comments).

Occasionally, someone would [poison]{.class_3058-0} the communion wine just to see how "Precious" the "Blood" really was: it killed whoever drank it deader than a doornail. If some of the "Precious Blood" were []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2374.xhtml_page_384}spilled from the chalice onto the floor, the communicant was to get down and lap it up with his tongue; nowadays, the Priest is allowed to mop it up with a linen cloth, which is then soaked in water and the water dumped down the "sacred sink."

If "Jesus" got stuck in a communicant's dentures, he was to scrape "Christ" off with a knife or fingernail, and the scrapings put in water and drunk. If "Jesus" is dropped on the floor, the communicant can observe the "five-second rule" and pick "Him" up and eat "Him" immediately, making sure no crumbs of "Jesus" are left behind.

One time, Alex Dunlap, who ran the Catholic Conversion Center up in Pennsylvania, was speaking to some Priests at some sort of ecumenical get-together. He always carried a box of Roman Catholic communion wafers on him which he had bought at the religious supply store. He took one of them out as he spoke, dropped it on the floor, and said, "Excuse me, sir." Those Priests sat there just fuming. That's that bunch of superstitious, pagan idolaters.

At another meeting---a Christian businessman's luncheon this time---a Catholic Priest was talking about his "Charismatic" experience (this was when the Catholics were beginning to infiltrate the Charismatic movement). Alex Dunlap stood up and asked that Priest to give his testimony of salvation. That Priest cleared his throat nervously and said, "I don't think anybody here is interested in that." Some drunk salesman who had come out to the meeting for the free lunch yelled out, "I think that would be a good idea."

Once more, Dunlap took out a communion wafer, held it up before that Priest's face, and said, "Tell the truth to these men. Isn't [that]{.class_3058-0} your God?" It sure was (and [is]{.class_3058-0}): in the Mass, the Priest holds that wafer up and calls it, ["My Lord, and MY GOD"]{.class_3057-0} (see John 20:28).

The Apostle John described the church that practices that blasphemy as a religious [whore]{.class_3058-0} ["full of names of BLASPHEMY"]{.class_3057-0} (Rev. 17:3) and ["having a golden cup in her hand FULL OF ABOMINATIONS"]{.class_3057-0} (Rev. 17:4). That's what the Roman Catholic Mass is---a [blasphemous abomination,]{.class_3058-0} a [perversion]{.class_3058-0} of what Christ set up here in verses 22--24. Paul called it ["the cup of DEVILS"]{.class_3057-0} and ["the table of DEVILS"]{.class_3057-0} (1 Cor. 10:21).

The Roman Catholic Mass is said to be a literal, bloody sacrifice (offered in an "unbloody manner," of course, of course); Paul called it a ["sacrifice to DEVILS"]{.class_3057-0} (1 Cor. 10:20). No Christian offers up a literal, physical sacrifice. The only ["sacrifices"]{.class_3057-0} offered up by any Christian are ["spiritual"]{.class_3057-0} ones---that is an [\
]{.class-0}[ex cathedra"]{.class_3058-0} statement of the "first Pope" in 1 Peter 2:5.

Listen to what the Apostle to the Gentiles said about Christian communion: ["The cup of blessing which we bless, is it not the COMMUNION of the blood of Christ?"]{.class_3077} (1 Cor. 10:16). Was it ["the blood of Christ"]{.class_3077} itself? No! It is ["the communion"]{.class_3077} of that blood. Communion is a close relationship between people; with Christians, it is between Christians who have the [same beliefs.]{.class_3078} In this case, it is Christians who are trusting the blood Christ shed to [redeem]{.class_3078} them (Col. 1:14), to [bring them nigh to God]{.class_3078} (Eph. 2:13), to [justify]{.class_3078} them (Rom. 5:9), to be a [propitiation]{.class_3078} (an appeasement to satisfy God, a satisfaction) for them before God (Rom. 3:25), to [purge]{.class_3078} them (Heb. 9:14), and to [cleanse]{.class_3078} them ["from ALL sin"]{.class_3077} (1 John 1:7). No Catholic who takes the Mass has confidence that the shinny in the chalice has done any of those things for him.

What about Christ's ["body"]{.class_3057-0}---the bread. ["The bread which we break, is it not the COMMUNION of the body of Christ?"]{.class_3057-0} (1 Cor. 10:16). Again, it is not Christ's literal flesh; it is the ["communion"]{.class_3057-0} of Christ's ["body."]{.class_3057-0} What is that ["body"]{.class_3057-0}? Well, it sure isn't Christ's literal flesh in the form of bread. ["For WE]{.class_3057-0} \[the Christians eating the bread\][ being many are ONE BREAD, and ONE BODY"]{.class_3057-0} (1 Cor. 10:17). So the bread isn't Christ's literal, physical body at all; it's the spiritual Body of Christ made up of Jews and Gentiles (Eph. 2:11--16, 4:4). Are Catholics eating each other? Don't be ridiculous.

The bread and "wine" (we'll discuss that in a minute) aren't some literal sacrifice of Christ's literal body and blood; they are a [memorial.]{.class_3058-0} ["Take eat . . . this do in REMEMBRANCE of me"]{.class_3057-0} (1 Cor. 11:24); ["This cup . . . as oft as ye drink it, in REMEMBRANCE of me"]{.class_3057-0} (1 Cor. 11:25). Nobody is sacrificing anything.

Communion is a bunch of Christians using the bread and "wine" to remember the sacrifice of Christ on the cross that saved them and []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2374.xhtml_page_386}made them one with their Saviour. They "ate" Christ's body and blood in the same sense by which Jesus Christ lived by His Father (John 6:56--57). Well, Jesus wasn't "chawing" on God the Father; it was a [spiritual]{.class_3058-0} relationship. [Four times]{.class_3058-0} before saying that eating His flesh and drinking His blood would give a sinner eternal life (John 6:54), Jesus told those Jews that [believing]{.class_3058-0} in Him would give them eternal life (John 6:29, 35, 40, 47). So when a bunch of them left in a huff over what Christ said about eating His flesh and drinking His blood, do you know what Jesus told His disciples?

["It is the spirit that quickeneth; THE FLESH PROFITETH NOTHING: the WORDS that I speak unto you, THEY are SPIRIT ]{.class_3057-0}\[not literal, physical\][, and THEY are LIFE"]{.class_3057-0} (John 6:63).

And just to make sure you "get it," Christ follows that up with: ["But there are some of you that BELIEVE NOT"]{.class_3057-0} (John 6:64).

So no real Christian is depending on the Lord's Supper for forgiveness of sins or eternal life. He got those the moment he personally trusted Jesus Christ [by faith.]{.class_3058-0} All that is going on in the Lord's Supper is remembering Christ's death ["till he come"]{.class_3057-0} (1 Cor. 11:26). That is something else that is not in the Roman Catholic Mass, but we don't have time to go into that here. For further study, see our comments on 1 Corinthians 11:26 in the [Bible Believer's Commentary on 1 & 2 Corinthians.]{.class_3058-0} What you need to get here is that there is [nothing]{.class_3058-0} Scriptural about the Roman Catholic Mass from start to finish.

Take, for instance, that fermented hootch served by the Priest. After warning you against alcoholic wine over and over again in the Old Testament (e.g., Prov. 20:1, 23:29--33, 31:4; Hab. 2:15--16), do you really think the One who gave those verses is going to have you drink what He just told you not to drink or even look at, and cursed the one who gave it to you? Up until Vatican II, only the Priest was allowed to drink the communion wine. Do you know what the result of that was? [Drunk Priests.]{.class_3058-0} From time to time, a fund-raising letter comes across my desk for ["rehab" centers for alcoholic Roman Catholic Priests.]{.class_3058-0} They got that way from getting hooked on the liquor in the Mass. You call that "of God," do you?

Now in that Bible there are two types of wine. There is ["strong drink"]{.class_3057-0}---fermented liquor---and there is ["new wine."]{.class_3057-0}[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2374.xhtml_page_387 .class-0} That was the wine used in Temple worship (Neh. 10:39, 13:5, 12). According to the Holy Ghost who wrote the Book, ["new wine is found IN THE CLUSTER"]{.class_3057-0} (Isa. 65:8). It is ["the pure blood of the grape"]{.class_3057-0} (Deut. 32:14); it hasn't been corrupted by the fermentation process. This type of wine is squeezed from the grapes directly into the cup (Gen. 40:11). This is what Christ serves when He institutes the Lord's Supper: He calls it ["the fruit of the vine"]{.class_3057-0} and talks about drinking ["it new"]{.class_3057-0} with the disciples ["in the kingdom of God"]{.class_3057-0} (vs. 25). That is why we Bible-believing Baptists serve [grape juice]{.class_3058-0} in communion.

The Catholic Church has been 100% wrong for at least sixteen centuries. According to that Book (who cares what any "infallible teaching Church" says), that wine from Christian Brothers Distillery is ["the vine of Sodom," "grapes of gall," ]{.class_3057-0}bitter clusters, ["the poison of DRAGONS, and the cruel venom of ASPS"]{.class_3057-0} (Deut. 32:32--33). [It's of the Devil]{.class_3058-0} (Rev. 12:9). If you take the Pope seriously on the "Eucharist," you will be damned just as sure as the world. That's the spiritual condition of over 900 million Roman Catholics on this earth today.

Back to verse 25: ["until that day that I drink it new in the kingdom of God."]{.class_3057-0}[ Right now,]{.class_3058-0}[ ]{.class_3081}["the kingdom of God is not meat and drink; but righteousness, and peace, and joy in the Holy Ghost"]{.class_3057-0} (Rom. 14:17). But when Jesus Christ comes to reign, He reigns as King over both the Kingdom of God (spiritual) and the Kingdom of Heaven (physical). Hence Christ's statement to Nicodemus about [seeing]{.class_3058-0} the Kingdom of God (John 3:3). And when the King is there on the throne, He eats and drinks with His people just as He did when He rose from the dead (Luke 24:41--43). So the kingdom in which He eats and drinks is not only the Kingdom of Heaven (Matt. 19:28), it is the Kingdom of God as well (Luke 22:16--18, 29--30), because the King of that kingdom is right there bodily.

Verse 26: ["And when they had sung an hymn."]{.class_3057-0} Music now shows up in the life of Christ at the very end. Of course, this was undoubtedly not the first time He ever sung a song while growing up, but it is the first time it is mentioned. He talks about children piping in the marketplace for people to dance in Matthew 11:17 and Luke []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2374.xhtml_page_388}7:32, but that is a similitude He uses for how the Jews responded to John the Baptist's and His preaching.

Now the largest book in the Bible is a [song]{.class_3058-0} book---Psalms. Music is one of the closest things on this earth to the [spirit;]{.class_3058-0} it's not physical. Music originates [inside]{.class_3058-0} a man; the instruments, scores, and sounds are what he uses on the outside to manifest the music inside him. Music was around before man was ever created (Job 38:4--7; Ezek. 28:13), and when you get to Heaven, you will run into music (Rev. 5:8--9, 14:2--3, 15:2--3).

Here, you have a chorus of twelve men (Judas has left---John 13:20), singing before Christ's ordeal in Gethsemane and His agony on the cross. Having been through the Book over 150 times, I have never come across what that hymn was which they sang. The commentators say it "was probably Psalms 113--118, which the Jews term the great Hallel, or praise, and always sing at the paschal festivity" ([Treasury of Scripture Knowledge,]{.class_3058-0} comment on Mark 14:26). Even though all those psalms are relatively short (compared with many of the others in the book), I am not sure [six]{.class_3058-0} psalms (plural) qualify as ["AN hymn"]{.class_3057-0} (singular). Certainly a song like Psalm 116 or Psalm 118 has something to do with what He is about to go through, so it might have been [one]{.class_3058-0} of the psalms from that section.

27 And Jesus saith unto them, All ye shall be offended because of me this night: for it is written, I will smite the shepherd, and the sheep shall be scattered.

28 But after that I am risen, I will go before you into Galilee.

29 But Peter said unto him, Although all shall be offended, yet will not I.

30 And Jesus saith unto him, Verily I say unto thee, That this day, even in this night, before the cock crow twice, thou shalt deny me thrice.

31 But he spake the more vehemently, If I should die with thee, I will not deny thee in any wise. Likewise also said they all.

The Lord tells the disciples they are all about to desert Him, and He quotes a prophecy written 520 years before---Zechariah 13:7 (written in 487 B.C.). Understand, He just gave them something which ["cannot be broken"]{.class_3057-0} (John 10:35), so there is no way of getting around it. But Jesus follows that up with a promise: ["But after that I am risen, I will go before you into Galilee"]{.class_3057-0} (vs. 28). In other words, "You may forsake me, but I won't forsake you" (see Heb. 13:5).

Once more, Jesus tells the disciples of His death (the smiting of vs. 27) and His resurrection (vs. 28). They still don't "get it" any more than they did back in 8:31--32, 9:9--10, or 9:31--32 (which see, along with the comments on those passages). They knew it was definitely a possibility that the Jews could kill Him (John 11:8), and they were even willing to die with their Lord (vs. 31 here and John 11:16). Of course, as we are about to see, at least Peter doesn't plan on going down without a fight. But they still hadn't a clue about the Resurrection (see John 20:9).

Nevertheless, in contradiction to the words of infallible Scripture and an infallible Saviour, Peter says, ["Although all shall be offended, yet will not I"]{.class_3057-0} (vs. 29). "I know the Bible says, ['the sheep shall be scattered,']{.class_3057-0} Lord, but what it really says in the originals is all the sheep [except me."]{.class_3058-0}

That's how we are: the Scriptures apply to everyone else [except us.]{.class_3058-0} No, it means [you too.]{.class_3058-0} ["All have sinned"]{.class_3057-0} (Rom. 3:23)---that includes [you.]{.class_3058-0} ["No man cometh unto the Father, but by me"]{.class_3057-0} (John 14:6)---you're not exempt. ["Ye must be born again"]{.class_3057-0} (John 3:7)---no exceptions, not even [you.]{.class_3058-0}[ "The wicked shall be turned into hell"]{.class_3057-0} (Psa. 9:17)---that's [you,]{.class_3058-0} buddy. ["And whosoever was not found written in the book of life was cast into the lake of fire"]{.class_3057-0} (Rev. 20:15)---that's your mother, your father, your children, your grandparents, your Pope, your Priest, your President, your friends, your enemies, your pastor, your Rabbi, your Imam, your brother, your sister, your wife, AND YOU. You won't get around that Book. It will tell you what will happen one way or another, and it will happen. There will be no ducking or dodging it, or doing an "end run" around it.

Peter says, "These other fellows might be offended of you, Lord, []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2374.xhtml_page_390}but not me." Jesus says, "Peter, not only will you be offended of me, but before that rooster gets a chance to sound off a second time, you will have denied me three times" (vs. 30).

And of course, after Christ rises from the dead, Peter had to "make up" for denying the Lord three times. Over in John 21:15, the resurrected Lord asks Peter, ["lovest thou me more than these,"]{.class_3057-0} referring to the other disciples. Before the Crucifixion, Peter bragged he wouldn't be offended of the Lord even when all his buddies were. So Jesus makes Peter prove it after the Resurrection by [confessing]{.class_3058-0} Him three times for Peter's three denials (John 21:15--17).

There was a fellow from Baptist Bible College, one time, named M. H. Clark, who put out a tape against "Ruckman." He said you really couldn't understand the meaning of Jesus' three questions and Peter's replies in John 21:15--17 unless you understood the "original Greek." (He meant the use of [phileo]{.class_3058-0} and [agapao]{.class_3058-0} in the passage, but he was trying to play coy so you would be impressed with his "superior intellect.") I'll tell you what Clark missed messing around with the "original Greek": he missed (completely) the fact that Peter, as a public display of his love for Christ, had to confess the Lord for each time he denied Him. And if you miss [that]{.class_3058-0} (as Clark did), you really have missed the meaning of the passage.

Now we shouldn't get onto Peter too much. He was just the one who opened his big mouth first (as usual). Look at the end of verse 31: ["Likewise also said they ALL."]{.class_3057-0} They all insisted they wouldn't be offended of the Lord; they all said they would be willing to die for Him, alone if necessary. But Peter "took point" and ["spake more vehemently ]{.class_3057-0}\[i.e., forcefully, intently, passionately\]["]{.class_3057-0} than the rest, so he had to be dealt with in a stronger way than they were.

The thing to remember in what you are about to read in regards to Peter's denials, is that Peter never stopped loving the Lord. Peter didn't deny Christ out of a lack of love; he denied Him because he was [mad with the Lord.]{.class_3058-0} Simon Peter was prepared to [fight]{.class_3058-0} to save Christ's life, and the Lord wouldn't let him. Instead of being this great hero making his "last stand" for his Lord, he had to run away like a sniveling coward because the Lord wouldn't let him fight. Simon resented being put in that position by Jesus, so he got mad; and in his anger, he denied the Lord.

The reason some dedicated Christians get into the bad messes they do morally is because they get upset with the Lord over something. He let something bad happen, or He didn't answer some important prayer request the way or in the time they thought He should have, or He did something they think He should have done a different way, so they get "miffed" with the Lord. Then they do something wrong to "get back" with Him; it's kind of an act of personal vengeance. The problem is, you'll just have to make up for it later, and then you'll be more embarrassed than if you had just let the thing be. That's what's going to happen to Peter.

32 And they came to a place which was named Gethsemane: and he saith to his disciples, Sit ye here, while I shall pray.

33 And he taketh with him Peter and James and John, and began to be sore amazed, and to be very heavy;

34 And saith unto them, My soul is exceeding sorrowful unto death: tarry ye here, and watch.

35 And he went forward a little, and fell on the ground, and prayed that, if it were possible, the hour might pass from him.

36 And he said, Abba, Father, all things are possible unto thee; take away this cup from me: nevertheless not what I will, but what thou wilt.

37 And he cometh, and findeth them sleeping, and saith unto Peter, Simon, sleepest thou? couldest not thou watch one hour?

38 Watch ye and pray, lest ye enter into temptation. The spirit truly is ready, but the flesh is weak.

39 And again he went away, and prayed, and spake the same words.

40 And when he returned, he found them asleep again, (for their eyes were heavy,) neither wist they what to answer him.

41 And he cometh the third time, and saith unto them, Sleep on now, and take your rest: it is enough, the hour is come; behold, the Son of man is betrayed into the hands of []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2374.xhtml_page_392}sinners.

42 Rise up, let us go; lo, he that betrayeth me is at hand.

When Jesus and the disciples left the upper room where they had eaten the Last Supper, they went to the Mount of Olives (vs. 26). At the foot of the hill was ["a place which was named Gethsemane"]{.class_3057-0} (vs. 32). The name means "oil press." Gethsemane was a garden (John 18:1) where olive trees were grown. In the garden was a "press" where the olive berries had the oil squeezed out of them. The process was not like the wine vats, where people would stomp out the juice. Any good Bible dictionary can describe the process of pressing the olives, if you want to know. The meaning is very appropriate for what is about to happen: Christ is about to be "pressed" beyond despair; His sorrow of soul will be "pressed" ["unto death"]{.class_3057-0} (vs. 34). He gets praying, and He is "pressed" so much that ["his sweat was as it were great drops of blood falling down to the ground"]{.class_3057-0} (Luke 22:44).

Christ is in a [garden,]{.class_3058-0} as Adam was. He had a problem with [blood,]{.class_3058-0} like Adam. He was dealing with the Devil, like Adam (Isa. 50:7--8). That is why Paul calls the Lord Jesus ["the last Adam"]{.class_3057-0} in 1 Corinthians 15:45; Romans 5:12--13 is a comparison between Adam and Christ.

So in the Bible, there are two genealogies of the human race: one is ["in Adam"]{.class_3057-0} in which ["all die"]{.class_3057-0} (1 Cor. 15:22 cf. Gen. 5); the other is ["in Christ"]{.class_3057-0} in which [no one dies.]{.class_3058-0} Read Christ's genealogy in Matthew 1 and Luke 3; death doesn't show up in either one of them. Of course, we know all those people in His lineage died, but it is not recorded there by the Holy Spirit because ["in Christ shall all be made alive"]{.class_3057-0} (1 Cor. 15:22).

Back to verse 32: ["Sit ye here, while I shall pray."]{.class_3057-0} This is addressed to eight of the twelve. Judas had departed, back in the upper room, to betray Christ; Jesus takes His "inner circle" (Peter, James, and John) with Him to pray (vs. 33). The admonition to [all]{.class_3058-0} of the disciples by the Lord was: ["Pray that ye enter not into temptation"]{.class_3057-0} (Luke 22:40). The admonition to Peter, James, and John was to [watch and pray]{.class_3058-0} (vss. 33--34, 37--38).

The Lord tells those eight disciples to [sit]{.class_3058-0} while they pray. He goes and [kneels]{.class_3058-0} while He prays. In the Old Testament, people [stand]{.class_3058-0}[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2374.xhtml_page_393 .class-0} as they pray (1 Kings 8:22) and pray [flat on their faces]{.class_3058-0} (Gen. 17:3; Num. 16:22 cf. Matt. 26:39). So in the Bible, there is no set "position" for prayer. The important thing is to approach God in [humility]{.class_3058-0} and [faith]{.class_3058-0} (1 Pet. 5:6--7; James 1:6).

Verse 33: ["and began to be sore amazed, and to be very heavy."]{.class_3057-0} It is amazing, sometimes, since the American federal school system dumped the Bible, to see how stupid the populous has become in understanding its own language. ["Sore amazed"]{.class_3057-0} and ["very heavy"]{.class_3057-0} are not "archaic expressions," at least not in the context of Mark 14:33 and according to a [dictionary.]{.class_3058-0}

["Sore"]{.class_3057-0} is used here as an adjective. It means "suffering mental pain; grieved, distressed, or sorrowful," as in ["sore at heart."]{.class_3058-0} So you know what you read in the next verse?[ "My soul is EXCEEDING SORROWFUL unto death"]{.class_3057-0} (vs. 34).

The "Greek boys" try to make a big deal of the word ["amazed"]{.class_3057-0} as well, making you think the [King James']{.class_3058-0} wording is "odd" and incomprehensible without going back to the "original Greek." God forbid they should compare Scripture with Scripture to find out what the Scriptures have to say about the Scriptures. In the [Bible]{.class_3058-0} (who cares how the word has been perverted in the modern parlance of the "dumbed-down" graduates of the federal school system, who actually think [gay]{.class_3058-0} has something to do with sex perversion instead of being [happy]{.class_3058-0}), amazement is connected to [astonishment]{.class_3058-0} (1:22, 27), [trembling]{.class_3058-0} and [fear]{.class_3058-0} (16:8; Exod. 15:15; Ezek. 32:10), and [pain]{.class_3058-0} (Isa. 13:8). Here, Christ is ["in an AGONY"]{.class_3057-0} (Luke 22:44).

And, of course, the ["very heavy"]{.class_3057-0} is [heaviness of heart,]{.class_3058-0} as you read in Proverbs 12:25. A ["heavy heart"]{.class_3057-0} (Prov. 25:20) is a sad, miserable state of, in Christ's case, the ["soul"]{.class_3057-0} (vs. 34); hence the expression "WEIGHED DOWN with grief." Did you really need the "original Greek" to understand that?

Now why is Jesus so ["sorrowful,"]{.class_3057-0} and why is He praying so ["earnestly"]{.class_3057-0} (Luke 22:44)? He's praying that ["the hour might pass from him"]{.class_3057-0} (vs. 35). What ["hour"]{.class_3057-0}? The ["hour"]{.class_3057-0} of His [death]{.class_3058-0} (John 2:4, 7:6, 30, 8:20, 12:23, 27, 13:1). What death? Well, I'll tell you one thing, it wasn't dying in Gethsemane. It wasn't even physical death on the cross. He endured that with joy (Heb. 12:2).

John R. Rice will run you over to Hebrews 5:7 and make the ["death"]{.class_3057-0} the [physical]{.class_3058-0} death of Christ's [body]{.class_3058-0} in the garden. Rice has Christ about to die in Gethsemane, and He prays to survive so He can die on the cross the following day. According to "dear Dr. John," the Father ["heard"]{.class_3057-0} Christ's prayer and answered in the affirmative. [Private interpretation from start to finish;]{.class_3058-0} private interpretation from a "good, godly, dedicated fundamental" tract evangelist who thought he had enough sense to teach the Bible just because Bob Jones College gave him an honorary doctorate for selling his newspaper ([The Sword of the Lord]{.class_3058-0}).

God the Holy Spirit defined that ["cup"]{.class_3057-0} Christ wanted the ["Father"]{.class_3057-0} to ["take away"]{.class_3057-0} (vs. 36), and it had nothing to do with [physical ]{.class_3058-0}death. It was the [cup of God's wrath against sin]{.class_3058-0} (Job 21:20; Psa. 11:6; Isa 51:17, 22; Jer. 25:15; Rev. 14:9--10, 16:19). Christ prayed for the Father to save Him from the hour when He would drink that cup long before He got to Gethsemane (John 12:27). Moreover, [He did drink the cup]{.class_3058-0} (John 18:11), so the answer to Christ's prayer was ["no."]{.class_3058-0}

Christ didn't just ["bare our sins"]{.class_3057-0} (1 Pet. 2:24), He was ["made . . . to be sin for us"]{.class_3057-0} (2 Cor. 5:21). When Christ died on the cross, He became the [personification of the Devil himself]{.class_3058-0} (John 3:14 cf. Rev. 12:0; see also Psa. 22:6). That had nothing to do with His bodily death by crucifixion; it was---["My SOUL is exceeding sorrowful unto death"]{.class_3057-0} (vs. 34). Look at the end of Isaiah 53.

Isaiah 53:10---["Yet it pleased the LORD to bruise him; he hath put him to grief: when thou shalt make HIS SOUL AN OFFERING FOR SIN . . . ."]{.class_3057-0}

Isaiah 53:11---["He shall see of the travail of HIS SOUL, and shall be satisfied . . . ."]{.class_3057-0}

Isaiah 53:12---["he hath poured out HIS SOUL UNTO DEATH: and he was numbered with the transgressors; and he bare the sin of many, and made intercession for the transgressors."]{.class_3057-0}

Three times in a row the prophet tells you the death about which Christ prayed was the death of the [soul.]{.class_3058-0} It is called ["the second death"]{.class_3057-0} in Revelation 20:14. So as far as the Father taking away the cup of His wrath against sin, yes, Christ's prayer was indeed heard, but it was answered in the []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2374.xhtml_page_395 .class-0}[negative.]{.class_3058-0}

But there is more to Hebrews 5:7 than that. You see, the verse didn't say Christ prayed for the Father to save Him from [dying;]{.class_3058-0} it said, ["unto him that was able to save him from DEATH."]{.class_3057-0} Let me ask you something: how long is ["the second death"]{.class_3057-0}? You already got the answer back in Mark 9:44, 46, and 48.

"Where their worm DIETH NOT, and the fire is NOT QUENCHED."

If you go to Hell, there will be no "annihilation of the soul" like the Seventh-day Adventists teach. It will be [forever]{.class_3058-0} (Rev. 14:9--10). You will dwell in the ["consuming fire"]{.class_3057-0} of God's wrath (Heb. 12:29) for as long as God lives.

Well, what about Christ? Did He have to burn forever for your sins? Of course not. Why not? [Because a dead Saviour burning in Hell for your sins can't give you eternal life.]{.class_3058-0} For the Lord Jesus Christ to give you ["eternal life"]{.class_3057-0} (Rom. 6:23), [He had to have eternal life Himself]{.class_3058-0} (1 John 5:11, 20). So before Genesis 1:1, when faced with taking the sins of the world on Himself and paying for them in Hell (1 Pet. 1:19--20; Rev. 13:8), the promise was made to God the Son that if He did that, the Father would give Him ["eternal life"]{.class_3057-0} (see Tit. 1:2 and our comments on the verse in the [Bible Believer's Commentary on the Pastoral Epistles]{.class_3058-0}). So the promise was that Christ's ["soul"]{.class_3057-0} would not be left ["in hell"]{.class_3057-0} (Acts 2:27); that's the Resurrection (Acts 2:31).

So Christ ["was heard"]{.class_3057-0} in Gethsemane, and God the Father did ["save him from death,"]{.class_3057-0} but it had nothing to do with the Devil killing Him in Gethsemane or the Romans nailing Him to the cross. The Father saved Him from eternal death in Hell (Rev. 1:18) and restored the bodily shape of His soul to that of a man (Psa. 22:6 cf. our comments on 8:36 and 9:44, 46, 48 in this Commentary).

Notice, in verse 36, the Aramaic term by which Christ addresses His Father: ["Abba."]{.class_3057-0} The term is used three times in the New Testament, and each time it is followed by the word ["Father"]{.class_3057-0} (Mark 14:36; Rom. 8:15; Gal. 4:6). The standard exposition of the word says that since it is a form of [intimate]{.class_3058-0} address between a child and his father, that it is the Hebrew equivalent of our word [daddy.]{.class_3058-0} I have read just as many Hebrew scholars that say, "No, the Greek word that follows ['Abba']{.class_3057-0} is [o pater]{.class_3058-0}[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2374.xhtml_page_396 .class-0}---'the father' or 'my father.'" So what you have, they say, is an Aramaic/Hebrew word, followed by the Greek word defining it.

Of course, some of us Southerners call our daddy "Daddy" all the way up into our nineties (like me), and modern Hebrew speakers do indeed use the word [abba]{.class_3058-0} as [daddy.]{.class_3058-0} Regardless of its exact meaning, what can be said about the use of the word in the New Testament is this:

1\. It was a form of [personal, intimate]{.class_3058-0} address between the Son of God and His Father (vs. 36).

2\. You have the same right to address your Heavenly Father that way because you have been adopted into God's family (Rom. 8:15) by the Spirit of Christ indwelling you (Gal. 4:6). It's not a [corporate]{.class_3058-0} relationship between you and the Father as it was with a Jew in the nation of Israel (note that ["OUR Father"]{.class_3057-0} in Matthew 6:9); it is a [personal]{.class_3058-0} relationship (John 14:20, 20:17).

["All things are possible unto thee."]{.class_3057-0} You have to be careful when applying statements like this and 10:27 to [yourself.]{.class_3058-0} You see, there [are]{.class_3058-0} some things that are impossible for God to do. It is ["impossible for God to lie"]{.class_3057-0} (Heb. 6:18; Num. 23:19; Tit. 1:2). Evidently, it is impossible for God to take away sin apart from the sacrifice of His Son (vs. 36 here). I'll tell you something else---it's impossible for God to deny you as a son once you are [in Christ]{.class_3058-0} (2 Tim. 2:13).

["Nevertheless not what I will, but what thou wilt."]{.class_3057-0} Here is another reason Christ said He came (see our comments on 1:38), except this time, He said it [before He was born]{.class_3058-0} (Psa. 40:8 cf. Heb. 10:7--10). This is [part]{.class_3058-0} of Jesus learning ["obedience by the things which he suffered"]{.class_3057-0} (Heb. 3:8). He ["became obedient unto death, even the death of the cross"]{.class_3057-0} (Phil. 2:8), and because He did, He was ["made perfect"]{.class_3057-0} (not sinless, but perfect, a [completed]{.class_3058-0} salvation) to become ["the author of eternal salvation"]{.class_3057-0} (Heb. 5:9) so we could be sanctified through that [one-time]{.class_3058-0} sacrifice (Heb. 10:10).

Having prayed once, Jesus returns to Peter, James, and John to find them asleep. And while it is late at night after a big supper, the gravity of what Christ told them in verse 34 should have motivated []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2374.xhtml_page_397}them to stay awake and pray. But Simon Peter never did have any problem sleeping when death was imminent. Over in Acts 12, the night before his own execution, he was asleep in chains between his two guards even while the church was having an all-night prayer meeting for him (Acts 12:5--6).

["Simon, sleepest thou? couldest not thou watch one hour?"]{.class_3057-0} (vs. 37). Evidently not. This is the disciple who bragged he was ready to die with Christ (vs. 31), and he couldn't stay awake one hour on his watch. Back when I was in the Army, the penalty for falling asleep on watch could be as severe as death in time of war. That's because if the sentry is asleep on watch, the enemy can come into the camp and create havoc. Here in Mark 14, the enemy was not Judas or the company that came to take Jesus (vs. 43); it was the [flesh.]{.class_3058-0}

["Watch ye and pray, lest ye enter into temptation. The spirit truly is ready, but THE FLESH IS WEAK"]{.class_3057-0} (vs. 38).

Salvation doesn't change your flesh: it's still the same body of sin and corruption that it was before the New Birth, and it will stay that way until it is ["redeemed"]{.class_3057-0} at the Rapture (Rom. 8:23). Brethren, the greatest Christian in that Bible still had problems with the flesh. He said in Romans 7:18, ["For I know that in me (that is, IN MY FLESH,) dwelleth NO GOOD THING . . . ."]{.class_3057-0} The Holy Spirit may live in your body, Christian (1 Cor. 6:19), but that doesn't mean He has anything to do with your sinful flesh. That's why He circumcised your soul from it when He came to reside (Col. 2:11--12).

[". . . for to will is present with me . . . ."]{.class_3057-0} There's that willing, ready spirit---the new man in Christ (2 Cor. 5:17; Eph. 4:24; Col. 3:10). But look what happens: [". . . but how to perform that which is good I find not. For the good that I would I do not: but the evil which I would not, that I do"]{.class_3057-0} (Rom. 7:18--19). Oh, come on, Paul, not you. Yeah, [him]{.class_3058-0}---and [you too]{.class_3058-0} (and [me too]{.class_3058-0}). What's the problem? It's that stinking [flesh.]{.class_3058-0}

["Now if I do that I would not, it is no more I that do it ]{.class_3057-0}\[his new man in Christ---Rom. 7:22\][, but sin that dwelleth in me"]{.class_3057-0} (Rom. 7:20).

There is no "eradication of the old nature"; you will be at [war]{.class_3058-0} with your flesh (Rom. 7:23) till the day you die or the day you're []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2374.xhtml_page_398}caught up (1 Cor. 15:50--57). In the meanwhile, you are to keep a watch out for the attacks of your flesh and "waste" its deeds (Rom. 8:13; Col. 3:5).

Back to Mark 14: ["And again he went away, and prayed, and spake the same words"]{.class_3057-0} (vs. 39). This is not the ["vain repetitions"]{.class_3057-0} of ["the heathen"]{.class_3057-0} (Matt. 6:7), twiddling their prayer beads or twirling their prayer wheels while repeating meaningless prayers over and over again by rote. This is real, earnest praying where the burden is so great that you don't know how to express it any other way than what you have. All you can do is repeat your request to the Lord and wait on His answer.

Once more, Christ returns to find the disciples sleeping (vs. 40), and although the text doesn't record the Lord's rebuke as it did back in verses 37--38, it is clear from the statement at the end of verse 40 that Jesus did say something to them: ["neither wist they what to answer him."]{.class_3057-0} They were in the same predicament as they were back in 9:6 (see our comments on that passage). My dad taught me there were only three acceptable answers in the military: "Yes, Sir!" "No, Sir!" "No excuse, Sir!" The disciples' eyes may have been heavy with sleep, but even they realized that was [no excuse]{.class_3058-0} for not staying awake and praying as their Lord had asked.

The word ["wist"]{.class_3057-0} in verse 40 is one of those archaic English words in the [KJV;]{.class_3058-0} it means "knew." But it also shows the influence of Martin Luther's [Heilige Schrift]{.class_3058-0} ("Holy Scripture") on the [AV]{.class_3058-0} translators. They used the word ["wist"]{.class_3057-0} here because it was related, linguistically, to the word ["wussten"]{.class_3058-0} in Luther's Bible in this same passage. This illustrates the Holy Spirit's line of "apostolic succession" in regards to the God-honored text of the Reformation. That's one reason why you don't want to do like the modern versions and "update" those archaic words: they are there for a reason.

Mark 14:41---["And he cometh the third time,"]{.class_3057-0} implying Christ had gone and prayed the same prayer a third time and had gotten the same answer from His Father (see comments on vs. 35). That "three times" appears several times in Scriptures associated with prayer. Elijah prayed for the dead son of that widow woman three times before the Lord gave life back to the boy (1 Kings 17:21--22); the same thing is implied of Elisha raising the son of the great []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2374.xhtml_page_399}woman of Shunem (2 Kings 4:33--35). Those Jews prayed three times a day (Psa. 55:17; Dan. 6:10). Peter was praying on the housetop when he received the vision of the great sheet of beasts three times (Acts 10:9--16, 11:10). Paul prayed three times for the Lord to take away his ["thorn in the flesh,"]{.class_3057-0} and he got the same answer Christ did in Gethsemane all three times---NO (2 Cor. 12:7--9).

Now I don't want to set any limits on importunity in prayer (see our comments on 7:24-26), but if you have prayed seriously about something, and the Lord has given you a definite, emphatic "NO" three times, then I would say it is time to accept your lot and move on. If you keep harping on the matter, you are liable to do something foolish and take matters into your own hands, even though you know the Lord's will in the matter. That's what happened to Paul in Acts 21: the Holy Spirit warned him three times not to go up to Jerusalem (Acts 21:4, 11, 12), and he went anyway and lost two years of his ministry.

Back to Mark 14. There is obviously an interval of time between the ["Sleep on now"]{.class_3057-0} of verse 41 and the ["Rise up, let us go"]{.class_3057-0} of verse 42. How long it is, the Scriptures do not say, but we may infer from verse 41 that it is within the hour of His betrayal. It is sometime after midnight, between 1 a.m. and 3 a.m.

Jesus, having resigned Himself to the Father's will, now sits, watching over His sleeping disciples, awaiting the time of His betrayal ["into the hands of sinners"]{.class_3057-0} (vs. 41). The ["sinners,"]{.class_3057-0} in this case, are the [religious]{.class_3058-0} crowd: ["the chief priests and the scribes and the elders"]{.class_3057-0} (vs. 43). These are men who wouldn't think of defiling themselves so they could eat the Passover, all the while doing their "dead levelest" to bring about the [murder]{.class_3058-0} of the Son of God (John 18:28). They live on in their modern-day counterparts: Roman Catholic Popes, Cardinals, and Archbishops, Buddhist monks, and Hindu pandits. If you are a faithful witness for your Saviour, they will [kill you ]{.class_3058-0}just like the religious crowd [killed Him]{.class_3058-0} (John 15:20; 1 Thess. 2:15), if they can possibly get away with it.

43 And immediately, while he yet spake, cometh Judas, one of the twelve, and with him a great multitude with swords and staves, from the chief priests and the scribes []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2374.xhtml_page_400}and the elders.

44 And he that betrayed him had given them a token, saying, Whomsoever I shall kiss, that same is he; take him, and lead him away safely.

45 And as soon as he was come, he goeth straightway to him, and saith, Master, master; and kissed him.

46 And they laid their hands on him, and took him.

47 And one of them that stood by drew a sword, and smote a servant of the high priest, and cut off his ear.

48 And Jesus answered and said unto them, Are ye come out, as against a thief, with swords and with staves to take me?

49 I was daily with you in the temple teaching, and ye took me not: but the scriptures must be fulfilled.

50 And they all forsook him, and fled.

51 And there followed him a certain young man, having a linen cloth cast about his naked body; and the young men laid hold on him:

52 And he left the linen cloth, and fled from them naked.

["A great multitude with swords and staves"]{.class_3057-0} (vs. 43). This is not just the Marshall and his Deputy, or the police officer with his "backup," come to arrest Jesus; this is a whole "SWAT" team (fifty men or more). They are armed ["with swords and staves ]{.class_3057-0}\[bo and quarter staffs\][."]{.class_3057-0} Now you understand why Peter wants to fight: he sees it as the only way of getting himself and the Lord out alive.

Now why the ["great multitude"]{.class_3057-0} for taking just one man? It's not just that Jesus had an entourage of followers wherever he went; it's because Jesus had no problem escaping a mob if He so wished (see Luke 4:29--30; John 8:59, 10:39). They weren't going to let Him slip away again.

Notice the Mafia "kiss of death" in verse 44; it means the recipient is [marked]{.class_3058-0} for death. This kiss is ["a TOKEN"]{.class_3057-0}; one synonym for the word [token]{.class_3058-0} is a [mark.]{.class_3058-0} Judas is a type of the Antichrist (John 17:12 cf. 2 Thess. 2:3), as was Absalom (2 Sam. 18:18 cf. Acts 1:25), who betrayed his father (David---a type of Christ) with [kisses]{.class_3058-0} (2 Sam. 15:5--6). The Antichrist is pictured as a [leopard]{.class_3058-0}[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2374.xhtml_page_401 .class-0} (Rev. 13:2). A leopard is a beast covered with [black spots]{.class_3058-0} (Jer. 13:23) that look like a pair of [black lips]{.class_3058-0} pursed in a [kiss.]{.class_3058-0} That kiss in the Old Testament was connected with [idolatry ]{.class_3058-0}(1 Kings 19:18; Hos. 13:2). So in Revelation 13:17, that Antichrist has a [name]{.class_3058-0} (Judas), he has a [number]{.class_3058-0} (666---Rev. 13:18), and he has a [mark.]{.class_3058-0} That mark is a [kiss]{.class_3058-0} conferred on a person who worships the Antichrist and his image.

To truly appreciate what follows in verse 45 here in Mark 14, you need to pick up the parallel account in John 18. Before Judas steps up to destroy Jesus with a kiss, Jesus steps forward and asks the mob that comes to take Him, ["Whom seek ye?"]{.class_3057-0} (John 18:4). They answer back, ["Jesus of Nazareth"]{.class_3057-0} (John 18:15).

What happens next is a brief display of the Deity of Christ to His enemies. Christ replies, ["I am he"]{.class_3057-0} (John 18:5), but you will notice in the text of the [AV]{.class_3058-0} that ["he"]{.class_3057-0}[ ]{.class_3081}is in italics, meaning it has been added by the translators for clarity. There was nothing wrong in doing that, but what Jesus says in the language He spoke, as recorded by John, was ["I am."]{.class_3057-0} That's the name God gave Moses back in Exodus 3:14---["I AM THAT I AM."]{.class_3057-0} He told Moses to tell those Jews in Egypt, ["I AM hath sent me unto you."]{.class_3057-0} In Hebrew, that's known as the "Tetragrammaton"---the four Hebrew letters in the name ["JEHOVAH"]{.class_3057-0} (Exod. 6:3).

Jesus unleashed the power of His name as God, and it wasn't "Allah." Allah can't do any more for you than that ["UNKNOWN GOD"]{.class_3057-0} back there in Athens (Acts 17:23). Until Paul connected that unknown God to the God of Genesis 1:1 (Acts 17:24), it was simply one ["theos"]{.class_3058-0} (Gk. for [god]{.class_3058-0}) among many (Acts 17:16). That's all Allah was and is. He started out as a moon god out of the 360 gods in the Ka'aba in Mecca. Since he was the chief god of Mohammed's Quraysh tribe, when Mohammed finally conquered Mecca, he elevated his moon god to the position of "the God," which is all [Allah]{.class_3058-0} means. He is no more connected with the God of the Bible than Mickey Mouse or Bugs Bunny.

For one thing, the God of the Bible had a [name]{.class_3058-0}---Jehovah in the Old Testament and Jesus in the New (1 John 5:20). Poor, old Allah is a god with no name. "The god" is no more a name for a deity than "the man" is for a human. ["The man"]{.class_3057-0} of Genesis 2 had a [name:]{.class_3058-0} it was [Adam]{.class_3058-0}[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2374.xhtml_page_402 .class-0} (Gen. 5:1--2). If you have a name (like Ralph, George, Bill, Donald, Harold, etc.), you have something Mohammed's god doesn't have.

Jesus Christ unleashed the power of His divine name, and that whole crowd, including Judas, fell backward on the ground. Judas had to pick himself up from flat on his back in order to step forward and kiss Jesus. Judas had to step over both the Scripture (see vs. 21 and comments) and the power of God in order to go to Hell. But that is par for the course for any sinner (12:24; Rom. 1:16 cf. 1 Pet. 1:25).

["Master, master"]{.class_3057-0} (vs. 45)---Judas never does call Jesus "Lord" (see our comments on 13:1). Imagine that, after being knocked flat on his back by Christ's divine name.

Having received the token of betrayal, the mob takes Jesus. That's when "all hell breaks loose," as the saying goes. The ["one"]{.class_3057-0} who draws his sword and cuts off the ear of the high priest's servant is [Peter]{.class_3058-0} (John 18:10--11). If it were not for the "odd" Gospel, you would never know who it was, for neither Matthew, Mark, nor Luke identify the sword wielder (see also Matt. 26:51; Luke 22:50). In fact, it is John, and John alone, who names that ["servant of the high priest"]{.class_3057-0}---it was ["Malchus"]{.class_3057-0} (John 18:10). The Liberal idea that John is not an historical Gospel because it has a theological "agenda" (see John 20:31) is just so much "Texas cow pies."

Like the so-called "Synoptics," John will give you historical details the other Gospels won't. For instance, Matthew and Mark simply tell you an ear of the servant got cut off. It is Luke and John who tell you it was ["his right ear"]{.class_3057-0} (Luke 22:50; John 18:10). Luke tells you Jesus healed the man's ear (Luke 22:51), which Matthew, Mark, and John do not. To get the entire revelation, you have to believe [everything]{.class_3058-0} God said about it. You don't have the right to "vote" on what Jesus actually said, like the Liberals on the "Jesus Seminar" do, when you have the [eyewitness accounts]{.class_3058-0} of people who were [right there]{.class_3058-0} (Luke 1:2; 1 John 1:1).

We have already commented on Jesus likening Himself to a thief (vs. 48) under our comments on 3:27 (which see). Here, Christ goes on to chide those who were come to take Him with: ["I was daily with you in the temple teaching, and ye took me not"]{.class_3057-0} (vs. 49). The immediate reference is to His teaching in the Temple on []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2374.xhtml_page_403}Sunday, Monday, and Tuesday of "Passion Week"; but the statement was also true of [any time]{.class_3058-0} Jesus was in Jerusalem and taught in the Temple. Over in John 7, long before Christ's last Passover, the text says that as Christ taught ["in the temple"]{.class_3057-0} (John 7:28):

["Then they sought to take him: but no man laid hands on him, because his hour was not yet come"]{.class_3057-0} (John 7:30).

No one could kill Christ until He freely laid down His life (John 10:18). But the reason they were able to take Him here was because ["the scriptures must be fulfilled"]{.class_3057-0} (vs. 49). Starting with Gethsemane to the time He was placed in the tomb, at least thirty prophecies were fulfilled (see a list in the Chick tract "The Passover Plot?"). The ["hour"]{.class_3057-0} spoken of throughout the Gospel of John (i.e., the time He would take our sins on Himself and make the payment for them---see John 2:4, 7:6, 30, 8:20, 12:23, 13:1) had finally come.

Verse 50---["And they all forsook him, and fled."]{.class_3057-0} Christ's words back in verse 27, prophesied by Zechariah in 487 B.C. (Zech. 13:7), came to pass just as He said. You have to feel for Peter at this point. The reason he had drawn his sword was because he was one of only two disciples who had a sword (Luke 22:38). What happened to his buddy with the other sword, we aren't told.

He was trying to defend his Lord, and Christ tells him to put the weapon away (John 18:11). Jesus then heals the man Peter injured and turns Himself over to His captors, after asking them to let His disciples go. What was Peter supposed to do? I suppose he dropped his sword, or sheathed it, and then he ran away like all the other disciples. He had done exactly what he said he'd never do (vss. 29, 31).

I can tell you why he denies Christ here in a few verses: he's been humiliated, and he's mad with the Lord. It wasn't that he was a coward, as is evident by the fact that he was willing to take on that crowd of armed men, singlehanded if necessary. But what Christ did left Peter feeling like a fool all around, so he denies Christ and cusses just to "show" the Lord. It was a petty way of getting back with the Lord, and it has been repeated literally millions of times since then in the lives of Christians who get "miffed" with their Saviour.

And now we come to one of the most peculiar scenes in the garden that night. In verses 51--52, we are told of ["a certain young man,"]{.class_3057-0} clothed in just ["a linen cloth."]{.class_3057-0}[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2374.xhtml_page_404 .class-0} Outside of Mark, no Gospel writer mentions the Temple "posse" trying to take anyone but Jesus; except here. The armed men try to take this young fellow, but like Joseph back in Genesis 39:12, he leaves that linen cloth in the hands of his would-be captors and runs away ["naked."]{.class_3057-0} You aren't told anything about this kid except the fact that he followed Christ. You don't know who he was or if he were even one of the apostles.

You say, "Who was that, Ruckman?" After going through that Book over 150 times, I can state emphatically that [I haven't an idea in the world.]{.class_3058-0} If you find out, you tell me, and then we'll both know.

Of course, there is all kind of speculation. Some say it's [Lazarus,]{.class_3058-0} and they'll run you over to John 12:10--11 where the priests wanted to kill Lazarus because a lot of the Jews believed on Christ after seeing Him raise Lazarus from the dead. Others say it's Mark referring to himself in the third person as John often does in his Gospel. Some say it was John because John was supposedly the youngest of the apostles. Some have even tried to make it the angel of 16:5 who announced the Resurrection to the women who came to the tomb (although what an angel would be doing running around naked in a garden, trying to get away from men he could kill with nothing but a touch, is anybody's guess). The wildest interpretation I have seen is that this naked kid was wrapped in linen because [he had been dead]{.class_3058-0} until Jesus used His divine name to knock over the soldiers. The power of His name (["I am"]{.class_3057-0}) raised that dead young man, and he was following the One who had raised him from the dead. Of course, that's nowhere in the Scriptures; it's someone trying to stretch the "original text" beyond all limits.

Without indulging in such fantastic speculation, I will just say, "There it is." Having read it, we will now bravely move on to the next verse (which is my way of saying, ["Ich weiss nicht"]{.class_3058-0}---I DON'T KNOW!).

53 And they led Jesus away to the high priest: and with him were assembled all the chief priests and the elders and the scribes.

54 And Peter followed him afar off, even into the palace of the high priest: and he sat with the servants, and []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2374.xhtml_page_405}warmed himself at the fire.

55 And the chief priests and all the council sought for witness against Jesus to put him to death; and found none.

56 For many bare false witness against him, but their witness agreed not together.

57 And there arose certain, and bare false witness against him, saying,

58 We heard him say, I will destroy this temple that is made with hands, and within three days I will build another made without hands.

59 But neither so did their witness agree together.

60 And the high priest stood up in the midst, and asked Jesus, saying, Answerest thou nothing? what is it which these witness against thee?

61 But he held his peace, and answered nothing. Again the high priest asked him, and said unto him, Art thou the Christ, the Son of the Blessed?

62 And Jesus said, I am: and ye shall see the Son of man sitting on the right hand of power, and coming in the clouds of heaven.

63 Then the high priest rent his clothes, and saith, What need we any further witnesses?

64 Ye have heard the blasphemy: what think ye? And they all condemned him to be guilty of death.

65 And some began to spit on him, and to cover his face, and to buffet him, and to say unto him, Prophesy: and the servants did strike him with the palms of their hands.

Here in verse 53 are the ["sinners"]{.class_3057-0} of whom Christ spoke in verse 21. If we were to make practical application to our time, we would be talking about [Catholic Priests, Campbellite and Mormon "Elders,"]{.class_3058-0} and the [translators and revisers of the corrupt, apostate modern versions.]{.class_3058-0} They would be any religious leaders who are leading people to Hell (see Matt. 23:15) by setting up false systems of salvation by works and by corrupting the written words of God (2 Cor. 2:17).

We will comment on verse 54 under our remarks on verses 66--[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2374.xhtml_page_406}72.

Watch out for that ["council"]{.class_3057-0} in verse 55. That word is used throughout the Gospels and Acts for a group of men gathered together in [opposition to the propagation of the word of God]{.class_3058-0} (Matt. 12:14; Mark 15:1; Luke 22:66; John 11:47; Acts 4:15, 5:21, 6:12, 22:30, 25:12). They are a gathering of Christ-rejecting, God-defying, blaspheming, religious, pious [hypocrites]{.class_3058-0} who are in charge of preventing the truth from being spoken. You had better beware any Vatican [Council,]{.class_3058-0} ecumenical [council,]{.class_3058-0} National [Council]{.class_3058-0} of Christian Churches, or World [Council]{.class_3058-0} of Churches.

Now the trial of Jesus Christ before the religious leaders is what is known as a "kangaroo court" (or a "mustang court" back in the old West). It is a court of biased jurists and jurors that disregards the law to render a hasty, preconceived verdict against the defendant without allowing any evidence for his defense.
