Jesus Christ could [not]{.class_3055} save Himself, but not because He didn't have the power to do so. He told Peter there in the garden, ["Thinkest thou that I cannot now pray to my Father, and he shall presently give me more than twelve legions of angels?"]{.class_3054} (Matt. 26:53). But He couldn't do it because:

1\. The Scriptures had to be fulfilled (Matt. 26:53).

2\. He had submitted Himself to the Father's will (Mark 14:36) to drink the cup of God's wrath on sin (John 18:11).

3\. If He had come down from the cross, []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2670.xhtml_page_441}[you and I could never be saved]{.class_3055} (1 Pet. 3:18).

That last one is a subtle temptation of Satan for Christ to save Himself, but if He had, that would be our [damnation.]{.class_3055} The Lesbians, queers, and Socialists mock the vicarious atonement of the Son of God by calling it "child abuse" on the part of God the Father. All that shows is a "disconnect" with reality. History is replete with accounts of a man (or woman) sacrificing himself for the greater good.

Back in World War II, somebody had to go fight the Germans and the Japanese before they came over here and took over this country. The draft board would say, "You and you." Was that "child abuse"? Those were [young]{.class_3055} men, not middle aged or senior citizens. The old saying is: "Old men start wars; young men fight them." But not all of them were draftees; you had fellows who were "4-F," like Audie Murphy, eating bananas to get enough weight to join the army; some kids as young as fifteen lied about their age to go fight.

Jesus Christ wasn't "made" to go to Calvary. He[ volunteered.]{.class_3055} He saw what needed to be done, and He "stepped up to the plate." He [rose]{.class_3055} (literally) to the occasion. If He could not save Himself, it was because of a conscious decision on His part not to. Thank God He didn't, or I (and you too!) would be just as good as in Hell with the door shut.

Verse 32: ["Let Christ the King of Israel descend now from the cross, that we may see and believe."]{.class_3054} Suppose He had? Do you really think they would have believed?

Don't kid yourself. They had seen Him heal the sick, cast out devils, give sight to the blind, raise dead people, and feed thousands of people (twice!) from practically nothing. Had any of that caused them to believe? Of course not!

When that rich man in Luke 16 died and went to Hell, he asked ["Father Abraham"]{.class_3054} to send Lazarus back from the dead to preach to his five brothers so they wouldn't end up in Hell (Luke 16:27--28). Do you know what Abraham told that old reprobate? ["If they hear not Moses and the prophets]{.class_3054} \[[the Scriptures!]{.class_3055}\][, neither will they be persuaded, though one rose from the dead"]{.class_3054} (Luke 16:31).

[That's exactly what happened.]{.class_3055} A ["Lazarus"]{.class_3054} was raised from the []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2670.xhtml_page_442}dead, and those priests wanted to kill him so no one would believe on Jesus (John 12:10--11). Christ Himself rose from the dead, and those priests labored day and night to shut up the apostles in the book of Acts. If Christ had come down from the cross, the whole lot of them would have tried to find a way to hang Him back up.

33 And when the sixth hour was come, there was darkness over the whole land until the ninth hour.

34 And at the ninth hour Jesus cried with a loud voice, saying, Eloi, Eloi, lama sabachthani? which is, being interpreted, My God, my God, why hast thou forsaken me?

35 And some of them that stood by, when they heard it, said, Behold, he calleth Elias.

36 And one ran and filled a spunge full of vinegar, and put it on a reed, and gave him to drink, saying, Let alone; let us see whether Elias will come to take him down.

["The sixth hour"]{.class_3054} of verse 33 is "high noon"; ["the ninth hour"]{.class_3054} is three o'clock in the afternoon, the beginning of the Jewish "evening." There is a supernatural ["darkness"]{.class_3054} for three hours which is usually explained as God forsaking the Man Jesus Christ (vs. 34) because He was becoming ["sin for us"]{.class_3054} (2 Cor. 5:21). It certainly had nothing to do with a solar eclipse (see our remarks on the subject under Matt. 27:45 and Luke 23:45 in those Commentaries). There can be no solar eclipse during a full moon, and Passover always takes place during a full moon.

Three o'clock winds things up. It is then Jesus cries, ["Eloi, Eloi, lama sabachthani?"]{.class_3054} That's an Aramaism (see comments on 5:41) for the quote from Psalm 22:1---["My God, my God, why hast thou forsaken me?"]{.class_3054} Matthew gives the quote from Hebrew; Mark from Aramaic.

This has nothing to do with "the Christ" leaving "Jesus" at His death (see our remarks on the penitent thief above). Notice He doesn't say, "My Father"; He says, ["My God."]{.class_3054} This is not the Son of God speaking to His Father; this is the Son of Man crying out to His God. This is the human nature of Jesus Christ bearing the full wrath of God upon sin.

Now that is interpreted by some standing there watching as: ["Behold, he calleth Elias"]{.class_3054} (vs. 35). It is unclear whether this is said in sarcasm or not (it certainly is in the next verse). Whether they confused ["Eloi"]{.class_3055} with ["Helios"]{.class_3055} or whether they are just sarcastically making a play on the words, they certainly had reason to expect the coming of Elijah (["Elias"]{.class_3054} is the Greek word for Elijah) at the coming of the Messiah (Mal. 4:5).

Once more, in verse 36, the apostate translators and revisers on the committees of the [NASV]{.class_3055} and the [ESV]{.class_3055} try to pour their Roman Catholic hootch down the throat of the Saviour (see our comments on vs. 23). Both corrupt translations make the [oxos]{.class_3055} of verse 36 ["sour wine."]{.class_3055} Anything to make a pure, holy Saviour a wino like an egotistical blasphemer like the Pope.

37 And Jesus cried with a loud voice, and gave up the ghost.

38 And the veil of the temple was rent in twain from the top to the bottom.

39 And when the centurion, which stood over against him, saw that he so cried out, and gave up the ghost, he said, Truly this man was the Son of God.

When Jesus cries out in verse 37, Mark doesn't tell you what He says, but John and Luke do. It consists of the last two of Christ's "Seven Sayings on the Cross." I have already listed and discussed these under my remarks and comments on John 19:25--30 and Luke 23:34 in those Commentaries (which see), so I will not take the time to list them again here. The last two things Jesus Christ says before He gives ["up the ghost"]{.class_3054} are:

1\. The word of VICTORY: ["It is finished"]{.class_3054} (John 19:30). In preparation for this, Jesus prayed in His high-priestly prayer (the [real]{.class_3055} Lord's prayer): ["I have finished the work which thou ]{.class_3054}\[the Father\][ gavest me to do"]{.class_3054} (John 17:4). He had told His disciples, ["the Son of man came . . . to give his life a ransom for many"]{.class_3054} (10:45), and now that had been completed.

["It is finished."]{.class_3054} The atonement had been completed. It doesn't need to be "reenacted" every Sunday morning at a Roman Catholic Mass in an "unbloody manner." []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2670.xhtml_page_444}["The offering of the body of Jesus Christ"]{.class_3054} was ["once for all"]{.class_3054} (Heb. 10:10, which explains why the Vatican manuscript is missing everything in Hebrews after Hebrews 9:14---[no final sacrifice for our sins]{.class_3055}). Getting rid of the italicized words (["for all"]{.class_3054}) doesn't do Karl Keating and the Roman Catholic priesthood any good at all. The sacrifice of Christ's literal body (not some thin, starchy wafer) is still only ["ONCE,"]{.class_3054} not continual.

2\. The word of CONTENTMENT or RESIGNATION: ["Father, into thy hands I commend my spirit"]{.class_3054} (Luke 23:46). Jesus Christ doesn't die until He is ready to die. If He hadn't laid down His life voluntarily, He would still be hanging on the cross today (John 10:18).

Back to verse 37: ["gave up the ghost."]{.class_3054} That's a "metaphorical" expression meaning "to expire or die." It is [ekpneo]{.class_3055} in Greek and [gava]{.class_3055} in Hebrew. The expression is found here in Tyndale, but it is one of those "popular expressions" that everyone (including the secular linguists) attributes to the [King James]{.class_3055} Bible.

The corrupt modern versions, like the [ESV,]{.class_3055} try to get rid of the expression with ["expired"]{.class_3055} or ["breathed out his last,"]{.class_3055} but that obscures the definition of man's essential nature. In the Holy Bible ([AV]{.class_3055} 1611, not some "wannabee" like the [ESV]{.class_3055}), man is a body, a soul, and a spirit (1 Thess. 5:23). When that man dies, his body goes to the grave and rots until it is resurrected either at the Rapture (the saved) or the White Throne Judgment (the lost). His soul goes to either Heaven or Hell (Luke 16:22--23). But the spirit of a man returns to ["God who gave it"]{.class_3054} (Eccl. 12:7).

Because Christ had both a God nature and a human nature, He not only was one with the Father (John 10:30, 14:9) and the only begotten Son of God (John 1:18 in a [King James]{.class_3055} Bible; don't bother with the [NASV,]{.class_3055} the [NIV,]{.class_3055} or the [ESV]{.class_3055} because you'll end up with [two separate Gods:]{.class_3055} one begotten and one unbegotten), being the fullness of the Holy Spirit of God bodily (Col. 2:9); He also had a [human]{.class_3055} body, a [human]{.class_3055} soul, and a [human ]{.class_3055}spirit. That human [body]{.class_3055} went into the tomb, as we are about to see in verse 46. That human [soul]{.class_3055} went down (Eph. 4:9) to Paradise (Luke 23:43) and Hell (Acts 2:27). But His human [spirit, ]{.class_3055}His ["ghost,"]{.class_3054} went back to the Father.

Modern perversions of the Scriptures don't like that word ["ghost,"]{.class_3054} either in regards to a man's spirit or God's Spirit. The word, as many others in the [King James,]{.class_3055} comes from the German (see our remarks about ["wist"]{.class_3054} under 14:40): ["geist."]{.class_3055} It means "a spirit," but it can be [any]{.class_3055} spirit: good or bad, or man, devil, or angel. That is why the highly accurate and scientific [Authorized Version]{.class_3055} says ["God is A Spirit"]{.class_3054} in John 4:24, instead of just ["God is spirit"]{.class_3055} like such silly, inaccurate versions like the [ESV,]{.class_3055} which obscure "the original intent of the author." There are all kinds of spirits: the Devil is a spirit; you have a spirit; even dogs, cats, bats, etc. have spirits. Do you think they are all "God"? Don't be stupid.

But as we have come to understand the word [ghost,]{.class_3055} it is not merely a man's spirit; it is the manifestation of that man's spirit [when he is dead.]{.class_3055} Hence, the distinction made in Luke 23:46---["Father, into thy hands I commend my SPIRIT]{.class_3054} \[He's still alive when he says it\][: and having said this, he gave up the GHOST]{.class_3054} \[He died\][."]{.class_3054}

So do you know what the most common name for the Author of Scripture (2 Pet. 1:20--21) is, chosen by [Himself?]{.class_3055} It is [Holy Ghost,]{.class_3055} appearing [ninety]{.class_3055} times in the [AV,]{.class_3055} as opposed to [seven]{.class_3055} times for the [Holy Spirit.]{.class_3055} Why is that? Because ["the Spirit of God"]{.class_3054} is ["the Spirit of Christ"]{.class_3054} (Rom. 8:9; 1 Pet. 1:11), and Christ is a man who [died.]{.class_3055}

The rending of ["the veil of the temple"]{.class_3054} is recorded here in Mark 15:38, as well as in Matthew 27:51--53 and Luke 23:45, but only Matthew gives you all the details of what happened. There was an earthquake that occurred when that veil ["rent."]{.class_3054} Not only was the veil ["rent,"]{.class_3054} so were ["the rocks"]{.class_3054} (Matt. 27:51). When that happened, ["the graves were opened"]{.class_3054} (Matt. 27:52), and they stayed ["opened"]{.class_3054} until Jesus Christ arose from His grave (Matt. 27:52--53) on Saturday night after 6:00 p.m. When He got up ("Up from the grave He arose!"), so did a bunch of those Old Testament saints who had been buried outside of Jerusalem, and they ["went into the holy city, and appeared unto many"]{.class_3054} (Matt. 27:53).

Now [not all]{.class_3055} of the Old Testament saints arose; just ["many."]{.class_3054} King David stayed ["dead and buried"]{.class_3054} in ["his sepulchre"]{.class_3054} (Acts 2:29). Since the ones who arose ["appeared unto many"]{.class_3054} in Jerusalem, it is reasonable to assume they were saints who had recently died and []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2670.xhtml_page_446}would be readily recognized. But if they were saints who went all the way back to Old Testament times, then God would have given supernatural recognition of them to the inhabitants of Jerusalem the same as He did with Moses and Elijah to Peter, James, and John on the Mount of Transfiguration. Can you imagine a good high priest like Jehoiada (2 Kings 11) going and giving Caiaphas a "piece of his mind" on what had transpired a few days earlier?

Verse 39 records the conversion of the Centurion at the death of Christ. A Centurion commanded a hundred men ([cent]{.class_3055}---Latin for 100, as in [century:]{.class_3055} a hundred years). A crucifixion usually consisted of four Roman soldiers overseen by a Centurion, so this fellow was the "C.O." (Commanding Officer). This Centurion probably had supervised many crucifixions, but none of them were like the crucifixion of this Man.

Being in charge there at the cross of Jesus, he had heard and seen everything we have been discussing since verse 22. He saw the conversion of the penitent thief. He heard the jeering taunts of the chief priests. He saw the sun go out as the Man on the middle cross cried out to God. He heard that Man as He took the time in the midst of His sufferings to arrange for the care of His mother (John 19:27). He saw Him turn down drink that would have given some relief to His sufferings. He heard Him forgive His tormenters (Luke 23:34), and he heard His final cries of victory and contentment before giving ["up the ghost."]{.class_3054} All that led that Centurion to say, ["Certainly this was a righteous man"]{.class_3054} (Luke 23:47). But that earthquake shook him up, boy. He knew this ["KING OF THE JEWS"]{.class_3054} was more than just a man: ["Truly this man was THE SON OF GOD"]{.class_3054} (vs. 39 cf. Matt. 27:54). That Centurion knew more than those chief priests, scribes, and Jewish elders did (cf. Matt. 27:41--43).

40 There were also women looking on afar off: among whom was Mary Magdalene, and Mary the mother of James the less and of Joses, and Salome;

41 (Who also, when he was in Galilee, followed him, and ministered unto him;) and many other women which came up with him unto Jerusalem.

The women of verse 40 are the women who would come to anoint His body Sunday morning (see 16:1). Of course, by then, it would be too late: there would be no body there to anoint.

We are familiar with Mary Magdalene. Before she got saved, she had ["seven devils"]{.class_3054} in her (Luke 8:2). She was the first person to see the risen Lord (John 20:14--17 and comments on the passage in that Commentary).

The second ["Mary"]{.class_3054} of verse 40 is said to be the sister of Mary, the mother of Jesus (John 19:25). Her husband's name was ["Cleophas"]{.class_3054} (John 19:25), the Hebrew form of the name [Alphaeus.]{.class_3055} So ["James the less"]{.class_3054} is ["James the son of Alphaeus"]{.class_3054} (see our comments under 3:18).

["Salome"]{.class_3054} was "Mrs. Zebedee" and the mother of James and John (Matt. 27:56). These women here in verse 40 were part of a larger cadre of women who "bankrolled" the ministry of Jesus (vs. 41 cf. Luke 8:2--3 and comments on that passage in that Commentary).

Unlike Islam, the Christian faith gives great honor to women. Only one of the apostles witnessed the Lord's death (John---John 19:26); the rest abandoned Him (Judas killed himself---Matt. 27:1--5). But the women who followed Him were all present at His death, His burial (vs. 47), and His resurrection (Matt. 28:1--9). In fact, they were the first to "preach" the resurrection of Jesus Christ (Matt. 28:7--10); the apostles, the leaders of the church, had to get their information from these women first (Luke 24:22--23). The only use for women in Islam is as sex objects and "breeders." Why these left-wing feminists side with Moslems can only be explained by their hatred of Jesus Christ (see comments on Luke 23:12 in that Commentary), because there is certainly nothing in Mohammed's religion that could be appealing to such a philosophy. Jesus Christ gave true liberty and dignity to women.

42 And now when the even was come, because it was the preparation, that is, the day before the sabbath,

43 Joseph of Arimathaea, an honourable counsellor, which also waited for the kingdom of God, came, and went in boldly unto Pilate, and craved the body of Jesus.

44 And Pilate marvelled if he were already dead: and []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2670.xhtml_page_448}calling unto him the centurion, he asked him whether he had been any while dead.

45 And when he knew it of the centurion, he gave the body to Joseph.

46 And he bought fine linen, and took him down, and wrapped him in the linen, and laid him in a sepulchre which was hewn out of a rock, and rolled a stone unto the door of the sepulchre.

47 And Mary Magdalene and Mary the mother of Joses beheld where he was laid.

We have already discussed ["the sabbath"]{.class_3054} of verse 42 on a couple of occasions now. It was not the regular Sabbath; it was a special one (John 19:31). Passover was connected with the Feast of Unleavened Bread in which no work was to be done on the first day of the feast (Exod. 12:16; Lev. 23:7). One more time with feeling---"Good Friday" is a Roman Catholic [fairy tale.]{.class_3055} It didn't exist. It was invented by Biblical ignoramuses who no more took their Bible studies seriously than they have climbed to the moon on their hands. They wouldn't have enough sense to come in out of the rain on a dark night.

Verse 43 gives you one of the two "dissenters" to the Jewish council of verse 1 that condemned Jesus. Joseph of Arimathea was called ["an honourable counsellor"]{.class_3054}; he was honorable in that he hadn't ["consented to the counsel and deed"]{.class_3054} of Caiaphas and his cronies (Luke 23:50--51). The reason was, he was a "secret disciple," along with his buddy Nicodemus (John 7:50--51, 19:38--39). Now both of them come forward publicly, with Joseph taking the lead. He goes ["in boldly unto Pilate, and craved the body of Jesus"]{.class_3054}; then Nicodemus joins him to prepare the body for burial (John 19:39--40).

Notice in verses 44--45 that Pilate confirmed the [death]{.class_3055} of Jesus with the Centurion in charge of the execution. You say, "So what, Ruckman?" So, "Allah" told Mohammed in Sura 4:157--158 of the "Noble Koran" that Jesus Christ didn't die on the cross. The two are joined by "Christians" such as the Neo-orthodox Emil Brunner, who said, "The atonement is [not]{.class_3055} history" ([The Mediator,]{.class_3055} pg. 505), and []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2670.xhtml_page_449}the Liberals who subscribe to the "Swoon Theory" (Jesus only swooned on the cross and revived in the "cool freshness of the tomb"). I don't know what other points on which they may agree, but they are "peas in a pod" with the Mohammedans when it comes to that.

But the only way to come to the conclusion that Jesus didn't die on the cross is to ignore the four [contemporary, eyewitness]{.class_3055} testimonies to that [fact.]{.class_3055}

1\. The officiating officer at Christ's execution (the Centurion) affirmed Jesus was dead (vs. 45). After Christ's scourging and His crucifixion, one of the soldiers under the Centurion's command pierced the heart with a spear to make sure Jesus was dead (John 19:34). One does not "revive" from a wound like that, no matter how "cool" and "fresh" a tomb may be.

2\. An official Governor of the Roman Empire (Pilate) was convinced Jesus was dead (vss. 44--45).

3\. A registered physician of that day pronounced Jesus dead (Dr. Luke: Col. 4:14 cf. Luke 23:46).

4\. Christ's best friend (John---John 21:20--24) recorded Jesus' death after observing it firsthand (John 19:35).

And you are going to take the word of an [illiterate,]{.class_3055} epileptic, murderous pederast, who lived nearly [600 years after the events took place?]{.class_3055} You are going to listen to a bunch of pseudo-intellectual stuffed shirts sitting around in air-conditioned offices, making up things off the top of their heads in order to [avoid accountability to their Creator]{.class_3055}? Well, then, I have some prime acres of "shore-line property" here in Florida I can sell you dirt cheap (a swamp). No legitimate court in the world (except a Moslem Sharia court) would accept such testimony over that of the eyewitnesses. Your heart must be blacker than a coal cellar at midnight during a blackout to believe someone like Mohammed or Brunner or the author of [The Passover Plot]{.class_3055} (Hugh Schonfield).

The Catholics try to get their spooky "Shroud of Turin" out of verse 46. But anyone familiar with the so-called "Shroud" knows that it supposedly covered a body from head to foot. [That's not how bodies were wrapped for burial in the Gospels and Acts.]{.class_3055} The linen burial clothes were wound around the body (Acts 5:6) in such a way []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2670.xhtml_page_450}that one could not loose himself from them (John 11:44). In fact, look at that last reference: Lazarus was ["bound hand and foot with graveclothes."]{.class_3054} That's not the "Shroud." Any body wrapped like the Shroud of Turin could have easily fallen out of either side of the Shroud, since there was no cloth on the side holding the body in. Whatever the Shroud of Turin may have been, it certainly was [not]{.class_3055} the burial clothes of Jesus Christ.

Back to verse 46: ["and rolled a stone unto the door of the sepulchre."]{.class_3054} Remember, it is not just Joseph of Arimathea who does this; Nicodemus is there helping him (John 19:39--40). This shows you how ridiculous silly little theories like the "Swoon Theory" are. Consider, if you will, the following things:

1\. Could anyone survive the blood loss Jesus experienced on the cross? No, if you have any sense at all.

2\. If we granted that Jesus did "swoon" on the cross (which He didn't, but let's just go along for the sake of argument for the moment), would He "revive in the cool freshness of the tomb"? Any paramedic or EMT would tell you, "No!" His body would go into shock and require [heat,]{.class_3055} not "cool freshness." If He weren't dead on the cross, He would be after a day in the tomb, in shock, with no food or water.

3\. And if we finally grant the last premise---that He survived the beating, the crucifixion, and three days and nights in the tomb---would anyone in Christ's condition be able to roll away a stone so huge it took at least two grown men in good health to move it in the first place? Would He be able to convince 500 people at one time (1 Cor. 15:6) that He was the victorious Lord of life?

But all of that is too much for one century. Such wild conjecture is not the mark of intellectual skepticism; it is the musing of a mad man desperate to come up with [anything]{.class_3055} to deny the resurrection of Jesus Christ.

Now Mark omits something in regards to the burial of Jesus that only Matthew records. Ever present in the minds of ["the chief priests and Pharisees"]{.class_3054} (Matt. 27:62) was Christ's prophecy of His own resurrection (Matt. 27:63). You see, they knew exactly what Jesus meant in John 2:19, even though they had lied about it at His trial and crucifixion (Mark 14:58, 15:29).

Of course, they did not believe it was going to happen, at least not supernaturally. But they did think ["his disciples"]{.class_3054} might ["come by night, and steal him away"]{.class_3054} (Matt. 27:64). So they asked Pilate for a guard on the tomb (Matt. 27:64).

But as we saw back under verse 37 here in Mark 15, Pilate has a notion that Jesus is more than some deluded religious fanatic. He grants the priests and Pharisees their guard, but he says, ["make it AS SURE AS YE CAN"]{.class_3054} (Matt. 27:65). He's not convinced Jesus Christ won't be coming up, and no Roman guard will stop Him.

This leads us to the earliest of the theories against Christ's resurrection, and it was the chief priests themselves who came up with it: the disciples stole the body of Jesus while the guards slept (Matt. 28:11--15). This theory fares no better than the Swoon Theory.

1\. How could twelve untrained disciples with only two swords between them (Luke 22:38) overcome four (and probably [more]{.class_3055}---Acts 12:4) seasoned Roman legionnaires who fought and killed for a living? These disciples sure weren't that organized in Gethsemane (Mark 14:47--50).

2\. Would any amount of money or persuasion have saved those Roman guards from execution by Pilate if they had actually slept on duty and failed in their charge (see Acts 12:19)? Something's fishier than a pile of dead shrimp from last month.

3\. Why would any "commando" team take the time to unwrap the body and fold the napkin covering the face (John 20:7)?

4\. And evidently the conspiracy wasn't that secret if Matthew knew enough about it to record it in detail. I suppose you could say the Holy Spirit gave it all to him by inspiration (which He did), but from a human point of view (see Luke 1:2), one or more of those Roman soldiers probably let his lips be "loosened" on the matter at a local saloon as he spent part of the money the priests gave him.

We have already commented on verse 47 under verse 41.

[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2892.xhtml}

# Chapter 16 {.heading_97}

16:1 And when the sabbath was past, Mary Magdalene, and Mary the mother of James, and Salome, had bought sweet spices, that they might come and anoint him.

2 And very early in the morning the first day of the week, they came unto the sepulchre at the rising of the sun.

3 And they said among themselves, Who shall roll us away the stone from the door of the sepulchre?

4 And when they looked, they saw that the stone was rolled away: for it was very great.

We have already discussed the women of verse 1 in our comments on 15:40 (which see). Luke 24:10 says there were ["other women . . . with them,"]{.class_3054} one of whom was named ["Joanna."]{.class_3054}

["The sabbath,"]{.class_3054} this time, [is]{.class_3055} the regular weekly Sabbath, for it is immediately followed by ["the first day of the week"]{.class_3054} (vs. 2). This is what throws all the commentators and expositors who believe in the Roman Catholic myth of a "Good Friday" crucifixion. John 19:31 makes it clear that there are [two]{.class_3055} [separate]{.class_3055} Sabbaths in Mark 15 and 16.

The ["three days and three nights"]{.class_3054} of Christ's burial ended at 6 p.m. That's when Jesus arose from the dead. At some point between that time and before the women got there "at the crack of dawn" Sunday morning, there was a ["great earthquake,"]{.class_3054} and ["the angel of the Lord"]{.class_3054} came down and rolled the stone away from the tomb (Matt. 28:2). The Roman guard fainted dead away (Matt. 28:4), but by the time the women arrive, the soldiers are up and gone, for there is no mention that the women even saw them. So the question of verse 3 is a reasonable one on the women's part. No guard had yet been posted when they watched the tomb being sealed (Matt. 27:60--66). Having not been back there since that time, as far as they knew, they were on their own to get the stone rolled away so they could anoint the body.

Note that ["the stone . . . was VERY GREAT"]{.class_3054} (vs. 4); so great []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2892.xhtml_page_453}that at least six women (Luke 24:10) knew they wouldn't be able to move it on their own. What makes a Liberal or a Modernist think one man, suffering from multiple lacerations, trauma, and a massive loss of blood---not to mention no food or water in 72 hours---would have been able to move it on His own? They are just as nutty as a pecan pie.

5 And entering into the sepulchre, they saw a young man sitting on the right side, clothed in a long white garment; and they were affrighted.

6 And he saith unto them, Be not affrighted: Ye seek Jesus of Nazareth, which was crucified: he is risen; he is not here: behold the place where they laid him.

7 But go your way, tell his disciples and Peter that he goeth before you into Galilee: there shall ye see him, as he said unto you.

8 And they went out quickly, and fled from the sepulchre; for they trembled and were amazed: neither said they any thing to any man; for they were afraid.

Of course, when the women arrived at the tomb, they didn't have to worry about the stone; it was already moved. The thing to remember about that stone being moved is that [it wasn't moved to let Jesus out of the tomb.]{.class_3055}

He had already traveled through the crust and mantle of the earth to its center (Eph. 4:9). What's a few feet of stone to a Man who could travel through nearly 4,000 miles of solid rock and molten magma? In His resurrected state, Jesus Christ could pass through locked doors (John 20:19). You think He was worried about a big rock holding Him in that tomb?

Notice again that an angel appears as ["a young man"]{.class_3054} (see our comments on 12:25). This is either a different angel from the one in Matthew 28:2, or that angel has changed locations. The angel in Matthew sat outside the tomb on the stone after he had rolled it away from the door of the sepulcher; the one here in Mark is seated inside the sepulcher ["on the right side"]{.class_3054} of where Jesus had lain (vs. 5).

Moreover, this angel wasn't the only one they saw. Later on, two more appeared standing beside them (Luke 24:4). After Mary Magdalene has gone and gotten Peter and John, and has brought them to the tomb, she sees two other angels (or perhaps the same two as earlier) in the sepulcher, sitting at the head and foot of where Jesus had been, whom Peter and John never saw when they went into the tomb (John 20:6--12). So the empty tomb was all "a bustle" that first Resurrection morning with angelic visitation.

["And they were affrightened . . . Be not affrightened"]{.class_3054} (vss. 5 --6). In the Bible, fear was the typical response to seeing an angel of God (Dan. 8:17, 10:7--9; Luke 1:12, 29--30, 2:9--10; etc.). There is none of this "love," "warmth," "comfort," "beauty," and all that "positive-thinking" garbage. That's an evil spirit masquerading as ["an angel of light"]{.class_3054} to get someone to reject the Book (2 Cor. 11:14). When someone in the Bible saw an angel in its unveiled glory (not ["unawares"]{.class_3054} as the ones in Heb. 13:2), it scared the fire out of him or her.

["Ye seek Jesus of Nazareth . . . he is risen; he is not here"]{.class_3054} (vs. 6). The best news this sin-cursed world ever got, it got from a graveyard.

["He is not here: for he is risen, as he said"]{.class_3054} (Matt. 28:6). Because of that, we can be [saved]{.class_3055} (Rom. 5:10). Because of that, we can have assurance that we will [get out of the grave someday]{.class_3055} (1 Cor. 15:20--22). Because of that, we can have [victory over sin]{.class_3055} (Rom. 6:4--10). Because of that, we can have [eternal life]{.class_3055} (John 14:19). And because of Christ's resurrection, we have a ["blessed hope"]{.class_3054} (Col. 3:4 cf. Tit. 2:13).

["He is risen; he is not here."]{.class_3054} That's what sets [Biblical]{.class_3055} Christianity (not Roman Catholic paganism) apart from every other religion on earth. Mohammed is stone-cold dead on the market, and his grave has a mosque built over it in Medina (the "Green Dome"). Buddha is deader than a mackerel on the beach last week, and pieces of his body have been divided into 84,000 "relic temples" called "stupas." Sometimes those pieces of the Buddha are hauled out and paraded around for his followers to venerate. Zoroaster is [dead!]{.class_3055} Confucius is [dead!]{.class_3055} Lao Tzu is [dead!]{.class_3055} The "Bab" is [dead!]{.class_3055} Joseph Smith is [dead!]{.class_3055} Charles Taze Russell is [dead!]{.class_3055} Mary Baker Eddy is [dead!]{.class_3055} Jesus Christ is [alive]{.class_3055} and His tomb is []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2892.xhtml_page_455}[empty!]{.class_3055}

Roman Catholicism, which claims to follow this risen Saviour, couldn't resist the temptation to elevate its demon goddess (["the queen of heaven"]{.class_3054}---Jer. 7:18, 44:17--19, 25) to a level of equal (and [greater]{.class_3071}) authority than that of the Lord Jesus Christ. So in 1950, Pope Pius XII exercised his "papal infallibility" and declared that Mary was "assumed" into Heaven 1,900 years before the rest of the Body of Christ. A little late, don't you think, to declare an infallible dogma incumbent on all "Christians" to believe, under pain of excommunication, after several hundred million of your church members lived and died believing no such thing? Of course, "Hitler's Pope" (see the work by John Cornwell, Viking Press, 1999) didn't get his infallible ["ex cathedra"]{.class_3071} decree from the Bible; he got it from apocryphal literature from the Fourth Century A.D., 300 years after the last eyewitness died (the Apostle John---John 19:26--27). You'd get more correct information out of "Peanuts" or "Hagar the Horrible."

["But go your way, tell his disciples AND PETER"]{.class_3054} (vs. 7). Peter has been singled out of the remaining eleven disciples because he lost his discipleship when he denied Christ. Notice I said Peter lost his [discipleship,]{.class_3055} not his [salvation.]{.class_3055} You will notice that Peter got the Holy Spirit (John 20:22) [before]{.class_3055} he was publicly reinstated as a disciple (John 21:15--19). Technically speaking, a person can be [saved]{.class_3055} without being a [Christian,]{.class_3055} because a Christian isn't merely a sinner who has trusted Christ for salvation. He is a [disciple:]{.class_3055} a saved person who is following Jesus Christ (Acts 11:26). Those rules for discipleship (not salvation) are laid down by Jesus in such places as Luke 9:57--62, 14:26--27.

["He goeth before you into Galilee."]{.class_3054} That took place after at least a week in Jerusalem, for Jesus appears there to the disciples plus Thomas eight days after His resurrection (Thomas had been missing at the first appearance in the upper room---John 20:24--26). You will find two of those Galilee meetings in John 21 and Matthew 28:16--20.

Now, verse 8 says the women ["went out quickly, and fled from the sepulchre."]{.class_3054} They run away from the tomb trembling in amazement at what they have seen and heard. Then we have a problem: []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2892.xhtml_page_456}["neither said they any thing to any man; for they were afraid."]{.class_3054}

Matthew 28:8 says, ["And they departed quickly from the sepulchre with fear and great joy; and did run to bring his disciples word."]{.class_3054}

Luke 24:9 says,[ "And returned from the sepulchre, and told all these things unto the eleven, and to all the rest."]{.class_3054}

Well, did the women tell anybody what happened or not? Surely this is one of the "contradictions" the Liberals, infidels, and skeptics (along with professing Conservatives and Evangelicals nowadays) are so fond of bringing up. But everything is easily cleared up when the "serious student" of the Scriptures (to borrow a cliché from the apostates) takes all of the post-Resurrection accounts together. This was laid out in [Bibliotheca Sacra]{.class_3055} all the way back in February of 1845 (pp. 187--188), and cited by Haley in his work [An Examination of the Alleged Discrepancies of the Bible]{.class_3055} in 1874 (pp. 328--330). An abbreviated explanation would be that the women made more than one trip to the tomb, and they split into at least two parties after their first trip. Mark refers to a group of them who said nothing (at least after the first trip), while Matthew and Luke records what the other group did. The thing that distinguishes a Bible believer from a religious infidel is that the Bible believer gives the Holy Spirit the benefit of the doubt that He's telling the truth, while the infidel (from Fundamental, Conservative institutions foremost) jumps to the conclusion that man (whether in the "originals" or in the copies and translations) has somehow managed to circumvent God's promise to preserve His words (Psa. 12:6--7).

Now before moving on to an exposition of another passage here in Mark 16, we must first deal with the apostates who have corrupted God's words (see 2 Cor. 2:17). You see, versions like the [NASV]{.class_3055} and the [ESV]{.class_3055} will put verses 9--20 here in Mark 16 in double brackets. The average Christian, having never studied the critical apparatus of Nestle's Greek text, does not realize what this means, so the [NASV]{.class_3055} and the [NIV]{.class_3055} will add a small footnote to this effect: "later manuscripts [add]{.class_3055} verses 9--20" ([NASV]{.class_3055}) or "The earliest manuscripts and some other ancient witnesses do not have verses 9--20" ([NIV]{.class_3055}).

You are to conclude that Mark 16:9--20 [do not belong in your Bible.]{.class_3055} Of course, because the [King James]{.class_3055}[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2892.xhtml_page_457} Bible runs the world, the lousy hypocrites put the passage in their English translations because the [AV]{.class_3055} has the passage in the text. The Jehovah's Witnesses are more honest than the "godly" Conservatives and Fundamentalists: they drop the passage altogether.

John MacArthur, being the "godly Conservative" that he is, joins the JWs in ending his teaching through Mark at verse 8. His last sermon in Mark is entitled: "The Fitting End to Mark's Gospel (Mark 16:9--20)." According to MacArthur, Mark is left "speechless" by the angelic announcement. It was "good enough" for Mark that the women [didn't do what they were told to do]{.class_3055} (not saying anything about the Resurrection to anyone out of [fear]{.class_3055}). What do you need to add past verse 8, according to MacArthur? Why nothing, of course, because you have "the wonder of eyewitnesses." Eyewitnesses to what?! [Not the resurrected Christ.]{.class_3055}

Every other Gospel records the eyewitness accounts of those who actually saw the [living Saviour]{.class_3055} (1 Cor. 15:5--8). The evidence for the Resurrection is that [Christ]{.class_3055}[ "was seen,"]{.class_3054} not just some [angel.]{.class_3055} If the words of the angels had been good enough, Christ would not have had to spend the next forty days with the disciples. In fact, the disciples didn't believe the women who did come and tell them what the angels said (Luke 24:11). Someone is exalting his Seminary education over the words of Scripture, and it's not Mark or the women. Without a record of the actual, physical resurrected Christ, you are left with an [incomplete Gospel,]{.class_3055} as defined by the apostle to the Gentile churches (Rom. 11:13, 15:16; 1 Tim. 2:7) whom you were told to follow (1 Cor. 11:1).

Let's actually go over the manuscript evidence John MacArthur refused to give his congregation when he was initiating them into the "mysteries" of the Alexandrian Cult. In a sleight of hand worthy of Houdini or Blackstone, MacArthur deftly leads his congregation to believe (without actually saying it) that all the Latin evidence and all the Syriac evidence agree with Hort's "darlings," [Vaticanus]{.class_3055} (B) and [Sinaiticus]{.class_3055} ([Aleph]{.class_3055}). To the contrary, only [one out of the twelve]{.class_3055} Old Latin manuscripts that have Mark side with [Aleph]{.class_3055} and B; even the [Vulgate]{.class_3055} contains the longer ending on Mark. As for the Syriac evidence, only the Sinaitic Syriac has the shorter ending, against the []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2892.xhtml_page_458}Curetonian Syriac, the Harclean Syriac, the Palestinian Syriac, and the [majority]{.class_3055} of the Old Syriac evidence (the [Peshitta]{.class_3055}).

The Sahidic and Boharic evidence, which usually stands with [Aleph]{.class_3055} and B, this time contains the [KJV]{.class_3055} reading at the end of Mark (as does the Fayyumic Coptic translation). With the exception of a few Armenian manuscripts, all the rest of the Armenian evidence supports the [AV]{.class_3055} reading, along with the Gothic and Ethiopic versions. It is also "extant" in (i.e., found somewhere in) five other manuscripts (047, 055, 0211, 0233, and 0257).

Irenaeus cites the passage in A.D. 202, nearly 150 years before [Sinaiticus]{.class_3055} and over a hundred years before [Vaticanus.]{.class_3055} Ambrose quotes the passage in A.D. 397, and Augustine quotes it in A.D. 430. So when MacArthur boasts that we could construct the text of the New Testament from the quotations of the Church Fathers alone, [Mark 16:9--20 would be part of that text.]{.class_3055}

The truth is, the last twelve verses of Mark were dropped on the authority of [two]{.class_3055} uncial Greek manuscripts ([Vaticanus]{.class_3055} and [Sin­­­aiticus]{.class_3055}), in opposition to the [majority]{.class_3055} of Greek manuscript evidence that contains the passage. That is based on the conjectures and theories of [one man:]{.class_3055} Fenton John Anthony Hort. In his "neutral view" of the New Testament text (disproven over and over again by Dean John Burgon, Prebendary Scrivener, Hoskier, Miller, and Pickering), the precedence, when deciding a reading in the New Testament, is to be given to the [Vatican manuscript.]{.class_3055} This is based solely on the fact of its [age]{.class_3055} (which is why [Sinaiticus]{.class_3055} is included with it), without any consideration for the [character]{.class_3055} of the document.

Dean John Burgon (who collated more manuscripts than Hort, and cataloged every New Testament Scripture quotation of the Church Fathers) described [Vaticanus]{.class_3055} and [Sinaiticus]{.class_3055} as "the foulest in existence" (David Otis Fuller, [Which Bible?]{.class_3055}, pg. 127), "the most scandalously corrupt copies extant," "the most shamefully mutilated texts," and "the depositories of the largest amount of fabricated readings, ancient blunders, and [intentional perversion of Truth"]{.class_3055} (John Burgon, [The Revision Revised,]{.class_3055} pp. 15--16). Those weren't just "prejudiced rants"; he had good reason for describing them as such.

According to Herman Hoskier ([Codex B and its Allies,]{.class_3055} 1914), there are 3,036 variations in the Gospels alone between [Aleph]{.class_3055}[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2892.xhtml_page_459} and B. What MacArthur won't tell you is that [Sinaiticus]{.class_3055} has a space at the end large enough to contain the last twelve verses of Mark's Gospel. That is, the scribe knew the verses should be there, he was probably told to leave them out (which he did), but he left a space for them "just in case."

MacArthur also won't tell you that although [Sinaiticus]{.class_3055} left out the last twelve verses of Mark, it did manage to add the Apocryphal books [as part of the Old Testament text]{.class_3055} (not between the Testaments as "recommended reading" but [not Scripture]{.class_3055} as Luther and the [KJV]{.class_3055} translators did) and "The Shepherd of Hermas" and "The Epistle of Barnabas" [as part of the New Testament text.]{.class_3055} Moreover, if Mark 16:9--20 is to be left out of the Bible based on its omission from [Vaticanus]{.class_3055} and [Sinaiticus,]{.class_3055} what are Hebrews 9:14--13:25, Titus, Philemon, and Revelation (not to mention Genesis 1--46 and Psalm 105--137) doing in an [NIV,]{.class_3055} an [NASV,]{.class_3055} or an [ESV?]{.class_3055} [They are all out of the Vatican manuscript!]{.class_3055} That is, [no modern version prints the manuscripts it claims to follow;]{.class_3055} they all have to follow the [KJV]{.class_3055} in order to get a readership (note the verse numberings back in 9:43--48). There is no one as dishonest or crooked as the revisers or translators of a modern version; alongside such "good, godly men," the Mafia are fine folk.

Now we will go from the ridiculous to the sublime. We will leave unbelieving scholarship to stew in its own apostasy down to a fine poison ([NASV, NIV,]{.class_3055} and [ESV]{.class_3055}), while we feast upon the pure bread of Heaven found in the Holy Scriptures ([AV]{.class_3055} 1611). We will accept and believe the last twelve verses of this chapter to be the very words of God in English as found in our [King James]{.class_3055} Bible and reject the [NASV,]{.class_3055} the [NIV,]{.class_3055} and the [ESV]{.class_3055} (and similar corrupt, perverted translations) that follow the Alexandrian text of [Vaticanus]{.class_3055} and [Sinaiticus.]{.class_3055}

9 Now when Jesus was risen early the first day of the week, he appeared first to Mary Magdalene, out of whom he had cast seven devils.

10 And she went and told them that had been with him, as they mourned and wept.

11 And they, when they had heard that he was alive, and []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2892.xhtml_page_460}had been seen of her, believed not.

The verses give you the first exception to verse 8. What changed Mary Magdalene from a scared, frightened bunny rabbit who wouldn't say anything to anyone was [seeing the resurrected Christ.]{.class_3055} "What more needs to be said," indeed (MacArthur's justification for ending the chapter at vs. 8)! Plenty more needed to be said, as is evidenced from those women keeping "mum" there in verse 8. Why, if that had been the ending to the Gospel, there would have been [no Great Commission in verse 15.]{.class_3055} The rest of the chapter deals with people [going and telling]{.class_3055} about the risen Saviour, not holed up somewhere afraid to open their yaps. You think any Roman would have been impressed with a Gospel ending as it did in verse 8? (See our remarks about the "target audience" for Mark's Gospel in the Introduction.)

Verses 9--11 are a summary of what John gives in detail in chapter 20 of his Gospel. ["Them that had been with him"]{.class_3054} (vs. 10) certainly was a reference to ["the eleven,"]{.class_3054} for they are singled out for "upbraiding" by Jesus in verse 14. But it also included [more than the eleven.]{.class_3055} Look at Luke 24:33.

"And they rose up the same hour, and returned to Jerusalem, and found the eleven gathered together, AND THEM THAT WERE WITH THEM."

You say, "So what, Ruckman?" So when Jesus appeared to those disciples in the upper room and gave them the power to remit and retain sins (John 20:23), that was directed at more than just the eleven apostles; it was to [all]{.class_3055}[ "the disciples"]{.class_3054} gathered there (John 20:19). The power to remit and retain sins is not relegated to some special priesthood of alcoholic bachelors; it is given to every disciple of Jesus Christ. You have the right to tell any sinner who trusts the finished atonement of Jesus Christ that his sins are remitted, and you have the right to tell any sinner who rejects that once-for-all sacrifice for his own self-righteousness that his sins are retained.

Those disciples have a hard time believing the testimonies of their Lord's resurrection because ["as yet they knew not the scripture, that he must rise again from the dead"]{.class_3054} (John []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2892.xhtml_page_461}20:9). If Christ's hand-picked apostles and those who followed Him for three-and-a-half years didn't even understand the Resurrection when it happened, what makes you think Abraham got it in 1900 B.C. with less revelation than that. Abraham may have seen Christ's "day" in type (John 8:56), but he sure didn't believe the same gospel you did to get saved (Gal. 3:8). Your salvation is [similar]{.class_3055} to Abraham's, but it is NOT[ identical.]{.class_3055}

12 After that he appeared in another form unto two of them, as they walked, and went into the country.

13 And they went and told it unto the residue: neither believed they them.

This passage is an abbreviated account of the two men on the road to Emmaus to whom Jesus appeared, as recorded in Luke 24:13--32. Notice how this longer ending verifies Luke's words in chapter 1, verse 1, of his Gospel. ["Many"]{.class_3054} wrote a biography of Christ's ministry here on earth. Mark was one of them---one the Holy Spirit chose to be in the Canon of the New Testament. Mark's long ending gives the first of the two witnesses the Holy Ghost wants to verify a thing (Deut. 19:15).

Notice, once again, the disciples have a problem with [belief.]{.class_3055} Verse 14 here in Mark 16 makes it clear that the ["unbelief"]{.class_3054} is due to ["hardness of heart."]{.class_3054} Jesus told those two men on the road to Emmaus, ["O fools, and SLOW OF HEART to BELIEVE ALL that the PROPHETS HAVE SPOKEN"]{.class_3054} (Luke 24:25), and what follows is a "Bible conference" on "Christ in the Old Testament" (Luke 24:27). What ["the prophets have spoken"]{.class_3054} (2 Pet. 1:21) is recorded in the ["prophecy of SCRIPTURE"]{.class_3054} (2 Pet. 1:20), and ["ALL SCRIPTURE is given by inspiration of God"]{.class_3054} (2 Tim. 3:16).

Is your Bible the Scripture? You say, "No, Ruckman, because it is not the 'originals.'" Then you are in a first-rate mess. You are a [fool,]{.class_3055} according to your Saviour, because you don't ["believe ALL that the prophets have spoken"]{.class_3054} in that Book.

Don't kid me; I sat under men like Charles Brokenshire and Barton Payne. I read everything your professor, or the man who []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2892.xhtml_page_462}taught him, ever read about "Biblical Introduction," transmission of the text, manuscript evidence, and the "original text." None of them believed they had a perfect Bible in any translation or any "original text." When pinned right down to the mat, they will tell you [every]{.class_3055} version, [every]{.class_3055} translation, and [every]{.class_3055} Greek or Hebrew text has mistakes in it, and they are smart enough to tell you where those mistakes are. When all is said and done, the only thing apostates like that have is confidence in [their own]{.class_3055} brilliance, [their own]{.class_3055} intellect,[ their own ]{.class_3055}scholarship, and [their own]{.class_3055} opinions and preferences; not any edition or any version or any text of any Bible in any language. [They are their own gods.]{.class_3055}

Not me! I am just stupid enough to believe just like the Apostle Paul. I believe ["ALL things which are written in the law and in the prophets,"]{.class_3054} and that is why I am considered a [heretic]{.class_3055} (Acts 24:14). If I would just cast doubt on [one word]{.class_3055} in that Book, I would be forgiven and welcomed into the Scholars' Union, but [I won't do it.]{.class_3055} I will go to my grave believing every word of that Book ([AV]{.class_3055} 1611), and scholarship can go take a flying leap into the Lake of Fire. (Or as Billy Sunday used to say: "Scholarship can go plumb to the [Devil]{.class_3055}.")

14 Afterward he appeared unto the eleven as they sat at meat, and upbraided them with their unbelief and hardness of heart, because they believed not them which had seen him after he was risen.

Verse 14 takes place immediately on the heels of verse 13, as given in Luke 24:33--36, with another supplementary account given in John 20:19--23. The lessons we learn from those passages are:

1\. [The resurrection body can pass through solid objects.]{.class_3055}[ "The doors were shut"]{.class_3054} when Jesus came and ["stood in the midst of them"]{.class_3054} (John 20:19; Luke 24:36).

2\. [The resurrection body is not a ghost or spirit.]{.class_3055} It has ["flesh and bones"]{.class_3054} (Luke 24:39)---NOT[ "flesh and blood"]{.class_3054} (1 Cor. 15:50). Christ shed all His blood on the cross for your redemption. You won't have any blood in you when you go up because [your blood is no good.]{.class_3055} You will leave every last drop of it down here at the Rapture.

3\. [The resurrection body can eat just to enjoy food,]{.class_3055}[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2892.xhtml_page_463} without the current methods of digestion and defecation (Luke 24:41--43).

Now if some infidel were on his toes (and what infidel actually reads the Scriptures for himself? We will help him out here), he would have noticed that Jesus appeared ["unto the eleven"]{.class_3068-0} that first Resurrection evening; this is confirmed in Luke 24:33. How can that be? I thought Thomas wasn't with them (John 20:24). Surely here is one of those contradictions in the Bible the infidels are so desperate to find. Don't bet on it.

You see, there was a man at the table besides the ten apostles (remember that ["them that were with them"]{.class_3054} there in Luke 24:33?) whom Paul lumped in with ["the twelve"]{.class_3054} (1 Cor. 15:5) before the fellow ever was ["numbered with the eleven apostles"]{.class_3054} (Acts 1:26). Matthias was with those apostles from the time of John's baptism all the way to Christ's ascension, and was an eyewitness of the Resurrection when Jesus appeared to the apostles (Acts 1:22). Writing after the events like Paul did, Mark and Luke both count Matthias among the apostles who were there, even when Thomas was missing.

Learn it and learn it well: that [King James]{.class_3055} Bible is [right,]{.class_3055} and you and your teacher and your professor and your momma and your daddy and your grandparents and your uncles and your aunts and your brothers and your sisters and your friends and your coworkers and your fellow church members and your pastor and your elder and your Priest and your Pope and your Rabbi and your Imam, et al., are WRONG when they go against that Book. ["Let God be true, but EVERY MAN a liar"]{.class_3054} (Rom. 3:4).

15 And he said unto them, Go ye into all the world, and preach the gospel to every creature.

And here we have a problem, which the Hyperdispensationalists "make hay of." This is Mark's version of the so-called "Great Commission." At this point in the Bible, what exactly would ["the gospel"]{.class_3054} be that is to be preached? Well, it still isn't Paul's ["gospel of the grace of God"]{.class_3054} (Acts 20:24) as he defined it in 1 Corinthians 15:1--4. How do you know? Because after forty days of further []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2892.xhtml_page_464}training by Jesus Christ Himself (Acts 1:3), Peter doesn't connect Christ's death with dying for anyone's sins when he gets up and preaches at Pentecost. Go ahead---read Acts 2 and see if you find anything about Christ dying for sins. It isn't there. You will find Peter talking about Jesus' death on the cross, what happened those three days and nights in the tomb, and Jesus coming up from the dead; but you won't find anything about Christ's death being a payment for sin. Folks, there's a gap wider than the Grand Canyon between what the "good, godly, dedicated" Conservatives and Fundamentalists teach and [what that Book says.]{.class_3055}

Because of that, the "Dry Cleaners" will say the Great Commission isn't for anybody in the New Testament Church. That is, you aren't suppose to go ["preach the gospel to every creature."]{.class_3054} Brethren, if the Devil can't shove you one way, he'll shove you the other.

Now Luke's version of the Great Commission comes awfully close to 1 Corinthians 15:3--4.

["And said unto them, Thus it is written, and thus it behoved Christ to suffer, and to rise from the dead the third day: And that repentance and remission of sins should be preached in his name among all nations, beginning at Jerusalem"]{.class_3054} (Luke 24:46--47).

Like I said, that's awfully close, but did you notice it never actually said Christ died for sins. You had to "connect the dots" between verse 46 and verse 47 based on what you know to be so from the Pauline Epistles. Like John, Luke is writing with the full Pauline revelation before him (Mark probably isn't). As such, from time to time, he records something the Holy Ghost has given him that is "reminiscent" of Paul's preaching. Historically, though, that revelation wasn't given until after Acts 9.

Having said all that, I don't want you to think you can't preach Mark 16:15 as applicable to the Body of Christ in this age. As I told you back under my comments on 1:1, ["the gospel"]{.class_3054} was revealed to the apostles [progressively,]{.class_3055} with the final revelation of what that Gospel is being given to the Apostle Paul (Gal. 1:12). Whatever revelation of "the gospel" was preached in Acts 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, and 7, once the Holy Ghost connected Christ's death to sins in Acts 8:30--[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2892.xhtml_page_465}35 (see our comments on the passage in that Commentary), the final message was confirmed to [all]{.class_3055} the apostles and [agreed]{.class_3055} upon: salvation by grace through faith in the finished atonement of Christ for sins (Acts 15:11), apart from any works at all---especially [water baptism]{.class_3055} (Acts 2:38 cf. Acts 10:43--48). So yes, you can apply Mark 16:15 [practically ]{.class_3055}and[ spiritually]{.class_3055} to the present worldwide missionary movement. The apostles began to fulfill the Great Commission from Acts 2 onward (vs. 20; Acts 1:4--8; Gal. 2:7--10). That means it is still binding on us today, baptism and all (Matt. 28:19).

Missions are [fundamental, ]{.class_3055}not secondary. Missions are not optional; they are [mandatory. ]{.class_3055}Do you know why the "Dry Cleaners" want to get rid of the Great Commission? Because it relieves them of the responsibility to go and preach the Gospel to the lost. They can stay put in their little "fellowships" and split dispensational hairs while a lost world goes to Hell.

Missions are [essential,]{.class_3055} not superfluous. General Douglas MacArthur said a country will have war with any nation it doesn't evangelize. Do you know why you have been fighting the Mohammedans for the past forty years (since Carter)? Because Christians haven't been diligent in reaching them with the Gospel.

Finally, foreign missions are [providential,]{.class_3055} not circumstantial. Someone asked Adoniram Judson one time when he was at a low point in his ministry to Burma, "How does the future look now?" Adoniram replied, "The future is as bright as the promises of God."

The promise is that if you go and preach the good news, the Lord will be with you (Matt. 28:20). That's more than just the indwelling Holy Spirit. The Great Commission in Matthew starts out with ["All power is given unto me in heaven and in earth"]{.class_3054} (Matt. 28:28). The promise in Acts 1:8 is: ["But ye shall receive power, after that the Holy Ghost is come upon you."]{.class_3054} The promise is, if you go and preach, the Holy Ghost of God will give you whatever power you need to accomplish whatever He has called you to do.

A Christian should lift up his hands and [give]{.class_3055} (Phil. 4:15--18). He should lift up his eyes and [look]{.class_3055} (John 4:35). He should lift up his heart and [pray]{.class_3055} (Matt. 9:38). And he should lift up his feet and [go]{.class_3055} (Mark 16:15 cf. Rom. 10:15).

16 He that believeth and is baptized shall be saved; but he that believeth not shall be damned.

This verse is one of the most important in the whole chapter, because it deals with the plan of [salvation.]{.class_3055} It is the "proof text," in Mark, for the Catholic and Campbellite heresy of [baptismal regeneration;]{.class_3055} that is, baptism is essential for [salvation,]{.class_3055} not merely discipleship. They get that by only quoting the first part of the verse:[ "He that believeth and is baptized shall be saved . . . ."]{.class_3054}

Now isn't that a wonderful verse on which to go to Hell? And by the Lord Himself, no less. How could you get much clearer than that?

You say, "Well, if He didn't mean it, Ruckman, why did He say it?" Oh, He meant it, all right. But the Lord puts verses like that in the Bible to see if you'll handle His words [honestly,]{.class_3055} without divorcing a statement from its context. He wants to see if you'll obey His command to [rightly divide]{.class_3055}[ "the word of truth"]{.class_3054} (2 Tim. 2:15), or if you'll only ["rightly handle"]{.class_3055} it like in the [ASV]{.class_3055} and [ESV]{.class_3055} (and similar corrupt translations that remove the only command to [study]{.class_3055} the Book, and [how]{.class_3055} to do it, from the Bible).

Notice in the rest of the verse, that what damns a man is [unbelief,]{.class_3055} not refusing or neglecting water baptism. Judas was baptized. Where did he go? Well, it sure wasn't Heaven (Acts 1:25 cf. Rev. 9:11).

We can understand why Peter connected water baptism with salvation in Acts 2:38. He is still practicing the baptism of John where water baptism was an outward symbol of an inner repentance. But did you notice that the further away from Acts 2 Peter gets, the less he talks about water baptism? In Acts 3:19, he doesn't tell those Jews who saw the lame man healed to ["Repent, and be baptized"]{.class_3054} (Acts 2:38); he tells them, ["Repent ye therefore, and be converted."]{.class_3054} But Acts 10 is the "clincher." In Acts 10:43--44, about the time he gets ready to tell Cornelius to be baptized to get his sins remitted, the Holy Ghost falls on those Gentiles the moment they believe, before they even have a chance to get wet. They receive the Holy Ghost [before]{.class_3055} they get baptized in water (Acts 10:47), so Peter knows it isn't essential for salvation.

By the time Peter has met with Paul (Gal. 1:18) and "hashed things out" at the so-called "Council of Jerusalem," he is in []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2892.xhtml_page_467}agreement that salvation is by grace alone, through faith alone in the finished atonement of Jesus Christ, [with no water baptism necessary]{.class_3055} (Acts 15:11). So when Peter makes his famous statement about baptism saving us, he calls it a ["FIGURE"]{.class_3054} (1 Pet. 3:21); that is, NOT THE ACTUAL THING. The "Water Dogs" do have a time with sixth-grade English, don't they?

So let's get everything straight. Mark 16:16 is given before the Pauline revelation of the Gospel. It is given at a time when God is still dealing with the Jews as a [nation,]{.class_3055} and that continues all the way up until Acts 7. In the Gospels and Acts, water baptism signified the "candidate" had repented of his sins (the sin in Acts 2 being the crucifixion of the Messiah) in preparation to receive the Messiah. Once Israel rejected the Messiah in Acts 7, the return of the King is postponed 1,900-plus years, God saves and calls the Apostle to the Gentiles and reveals to him ["the gospel of the grace of God,"]{.class_3054} the Lord shows Peter that baptism has been disconnected from salvation, and Peter and Paul agree on [one Gospel---minus water baptism.]{.class_3055} Now water baptism is only a [figure]{.class_3055} of death, burial, and resurrection; and it shows that a sinner has [already]{.class_3055} been saved and has already received the Holy Ghost.

If you are going to apply Mark 16:16 to today, you have to say that baptism shows the sinner has already believed, but it is that belief that saves the sinner, not the baptism. Look here, he that buys the ticket and gets on the plane to fly to New York, makes it to New York. But if you don't buy that ticket, you won't be flying anywhere.

17 And these signs shall follow them that believe; In my name shall they cast out devils; they shall speak with new tongues;

18 They shall take up serpents; and if they drink any deadly thing, it shall not hurt them; they shall lay hands on the sick, and they shall recover.

Another proof text for foisting a heresy on the Body of Christ. This time, it is the Charismatic, Pentecostal, and Holiness heresy of tongues, healing, signs, and wonders for the Body of Christ [right now.]{.class_3055}

Right off the bat, the word ["signs"]{.class_3054}[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2892.xhtml_page_468} (vs. 17) should raise a "red flag" for the reader. The ["signs"]{.class_3054} are for the nation of [Israel]{.class_3055} (1 Cor. 1:22); not the Gentiles or the Body of Christ (1 Cor. 10:32).

Well, to whom is Jesus speaking in verse 17? It is to [Jewish]{.class_3055} apostles. There are no Gentiles present, and not one of the men He is addressing is in the Body of Christ yet (1 Cor. 12:13 cf. Acts 1:5). These are [apostolic]{.class_3055} signs (2 Cor. 12:12) given to [Jewish]{.class_3055} apostles and their converts (["these signs shall follow them that believe"]{.class_3054}). Once the Jewish apostles are gone and their converts die off, the signs disappear (1 Tim. 5:23; 2 Tim. 4:20). So if you want to be a [Scriptural]{.class_3055} Charismatic (and what Charismatic ever followed the Scriptures in regards to tongues and healing---1 Cor. 12, 14?), then you would have to be converted under the ministry of an [apostle.]{.class_3055}

That is why these Assemblies of God, Churches of God, Pentecostal, Holiness, and Charismatics are so eager to bring back the office of an apostle. But unless their "apostles" have seen the Lord ([literally]{.class_3055}---1 Cor. 9:1), they are ["false apostles, deceitful workers"]{.class_3054} (2 Cor. 11:13). Any Charismatic claiming to have the apostolic signs and wonders is nothing but a [liar]{.class_3055} (Rev. 2:2).

Now let's go through these ["signs"]{.class_3054} and see if any of them apply to you.

["In my name shall they cast out devils"]{.class_3054} (vs. 17). Did you ever notice that for all the casting out of devils Paul does in the book of Acts (e.g., Acts 16:18, 18:12), he never commands it as a practice to the Gentile churches, not even in a passage like 1 Corinthians 12 or 14 that deals with the sign gifts? A Christian may wrestle against demonic spirits (Eph. 6:12), but I would strongly advise [against]{.class_3055} making a ministry of casting out devils.

Of course, there may be times when you recognize the work of an unclean spirit and have to rebuke it in the name of Jesus Christ, but if you want to cast out devils from someone, [cast them out of yourself.]{.class_3055} I don't see how any Christian can go through a day, in the age in which we live, exposed to everything to which he is exposed, without picking up three or four devils a day. It would be more profitable to you to look yourself in the mirror every night and say, "You unclean spirits, come out of me in the name of Jesus Christ!" than it would to go "picking a fight" with some devil in someone else.

["They shall speak with new tongues."]{.class_3054}[]{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2892.xhtml_page_469} What are ["tongues"]{.class_3054}? Well, I'll tell you what they aren't---they're not some inane babble or incomprehensible "language of Heaven" (as it turns out, the language of Heaven is [Hebrew]{.class_3055}---see our comments on Rev. 19:1--4 in that Commentary).

The first time ["tongues"]{.class_3054} are practiced in the New Testament, they are ["other tongues"]{.class_3054} (Acts 2:4), and those tongues are [known languages]{.class_3055} (Acts 2:6), and the languages are listed (Acts 2:8--11). When Paul talks about ["an unknown tongue"]{.class_3054} (1 Cor. 14:2, 4, 13, 14, 19, 27), he's not talking about a language unknown to anyone on earth. He's telling those Corinthians not to speak in a language no one in the congregation can understand unless they have an interpreter present (1 Cor. 14:27--28).

When Jesus talks about ["new tongues,"]{.class_3054} He's not talking about some made-up language no one has ever heard before. He's talking about a tongue ["new"]{.class_3054} to the apostle speaking it. It is a language not naturally spoken by the apostle or his convert which is given to him supernaturally by the Holy Spirit to edify someone who hears the language and understands what is being said.

Tongues are a sign given to the Jews, as prophesied in the Old Testament (1 Cor. 14:21 cf. Isa. 28:11). They are a sign to a Jew who doesn't believe something he is supposed to believe (1 Cor. 14:22), and that is how tongues operate in the book of Acts the three times they show up. In Acts 2, you have a bunch of Jews who had just crucified their Messiah. They needed to believe Jesus was the Christ and that He had died and risen from the grave in fulfillment of Old Testament prophecy. Those Jewish apostles spoke in ["other tongues"]{.class_3054} so that Jews from other lands could hear the message and believe.

In Acts 10, the saved Jews who accompanied Peter to Cornelius' house didn't believe a Gentile could be saved and receive the Holy Ghost unless he became a Jewish proselyte and submitted to water baptism. The Holy Ghost showed them otherwise by allowing those Gentiles to speak in tongues the moment they trusted Christ and received the Holy Ghost---without water baptism.

In Acts 19, there were some Jews who had submitted to John's baptism, but had never heard about Jesus Christ or the Holy Ghost. []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2892.xhtml_page_470}So after they were baptized in Jesus' name and had Paul lay his hands on them to receive the Holy Ghost, they spoke in tongues to confirm what they had believed was so.

In every case, tongues were for [Jews,]{.class_3055} even when Gentiles spoke in them. If all you are doing is sprinkling a few nonsense words in the middle of a sermon (as Pentecostal preachers are wont to do) in order to prove you are Spirit filled, [you are not exercising the Biblical gift of tongues.]{.class_3055} Just sit down and shut up and give your congregation a break.

["They shall take up serpents"]{.class_3054} (vs. 18). This only took place [once]{.class_3055} in the book of Acts, and it wasn't on purpose (read Acts 28:1--6). Nevertheless, that doesn't stop the Holiness folks in the backwoods of Kentucky, Tennessee, North Carolina, Georgia, Alabama, etc., from practicing "snake handling."

Of course, the snakes are made use to being handled by humans before ever going into a service. They are usually in poor health and not fed or watered properly so their venom will be weak. Plus the "handlers" sometimes slowly condition themselves to the venom so the occasional bite won't affect them. But that doesn't stop some of the snake-handling brethren from getting a newly-captured snake in good health with a pit-full of strong venom. Then it's either go to the hospital or [die]{.class_3055} (which happens more than they like to admit). I guess the ones who die weren't "filled with the Spirit" when they handled their last snake; maybe they had lost their salvation.

I'll tell you what, if you really want to prove you "have the gift," don't go to a snake-handling service. Go out in the African bush and find you a black mamba with which to play (the deadliest snake in the world). Go over to Southeast Asia and pick up one of those "one-steppers," "two-steppers," or "three-steppers" (how many steps you could take before keeling over dead after you had been bitten), which our boys encountered when they were fighting in Vietnam, and see how long you last. That will be the real test of how "Spirit filled" you are; not some anemic rattler on death's door.

["And if they drink any deadly thing, it shall not hurt them."]{.class_3054} That didn't work so well for some of a couple of the Popes in the Middle Ages. At least two of them, along with several \"saints,\" died from being poisoned. I guess they didn't have "apostolic []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2892.xhtml_page_471}succession" from "blessed Simon Peter."

Some Moslem over in South Africa used to debate these "prosperity gospel" Charismatic preachers to prove "there is no God but Allah and Mohammed is his apostle." If he got some preacher who claimed he could cast out devils, speak in tongues, and hold healing crusades, he would bring out a glass of ammonia cleaning solution and tell him to drink up. Why did he do that? Because it was in the same passage used to prove they could do all the other stuff. Too bad none of them knew enough Bible to shut the mouth of a Christ-rejecting blasphemer.

(Mohammed himself had no right to brag about being Allah's apostle. He died from a Jewish woman, whose husband he had killed, poisoning him. As Allah's "apostle," he was about as effective as being Allah's "prophet." He never exercised any of the apostolic signs, and he never prophesied anything outside of a lucky guess or something he stole from the Old Testament.)

Again, because there is no case in Scripture of this happening, all you can say about it is that it was a sign given to the apostles should anyone try to poison them. Like the "snake handling," it was not to be done on purpose to impress anyone with how "Spirit filled" they were. God protected the Apostle Paul through many trials and perils in his ministry that the Lord wouldn't get you through if you got into them just to "show off" (see 2 Cor. 11:23--27).

["They shall lay hands on the sick, and they shall recover."]{.class_3054} Who would that first ["they"]{.class_3054} be? Oral Roberts, Kathryn Kuhlman, Benny Hinn, Robert Tilton? Why, if you had that power, what would you be doing with a healing line and bouncers, in a tent or auditorium, taking up as many as four offerings during a service?

Why, Peter would heal sick folk in the streets, spontaneously, with no organ to "set the mood" (Acts 3:6--8, 5:15--16). Moreover, he ["healed every one"]{.class_3054} (Acts 5:16): no "duds," "short rounds," or "misfires." No one "lost his healing" or "didn't have enough faith." He would touch them and they were healed. Paul would heal folks without ever taking up an offering or having them "plant their seed faith" (Acts 19:12, 28:8--9).

If you could touch someone and heal the person, what would you be doing in a tent or a "civic center" anyway? Just go down to []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2892.xhtml_page_472}the cancer ward, the burn clinic, and the children's hospital and empty them out. I guarantee you that you would never need to take up an offering again. Some of those rich Yankees, who go down to Tampa, Orlando, Miami, and St. Petersburg to die, would give you their life savings to be free from cancer or Alzheimer's or heart disease. You would be "set for life" before you had been down there a week.

You don't know any of these fakers who can do that. They couldn't heal a hang nail. A friend of mine over in Bay Minette, Alabama went to a healing meeting and saw some woman, to whom he was kin, hobbling through the healing line on crutches. She got up there to the faith healer and threw down her crutches. That man "cornered" her afterwards and asked, "What were you doing in that line? You're not a cripple." She said, "They gave me ten dollars; it was the easiest ten bucks I ever made." "How could you do that and deceive those people?" he said. To which she replied, ["It was to help them have faith."]{.class_3055}

Bribery and fraud! Bribery and fraud for the glory of God, of course, of course. Lying to someone to give him a false faith---[and getting paid to do it.]{.class_3055}

Some TV reporter had an interview with Benny Hinn once and asked him how many people were really healed in his crusades. In a fit of honesty (knowing he could be [sued]{.class_3055} for lying publicly and being recorded as he did it), Hinn had to admit there were [no ]{.class_3055}verifiable healings under his ministry. He only "builds up faith" for people to be healed. A "screener" for the Hinn crusades said no truly sick person was allowed on the platform.

I remember going to one of these healing meetings with a buddy of mine named Jon Hall. He had been wounded in Korea in combat and was confined to a wheelchair for the rest of his life. The first couple of nights he was in a good humor over all the foolishness going on. But the third night he wouldn't go. He said to me, "Brother Ruckman, I know that faith healer is a liar, and I enjoyed the comedy. But honest to God, when that choir gets to singing 'Only Believe,' I want to come out of this wheelchair so badly that I break out in a sweat."

These faith healers are some of the meanest, most low-down []{#11hUKl1KToiErvrMCxN7Aw2892.xhtml_page_473}devils on the face of this earth. They blame their failures on their victims after stripping them of their money, given in hopes of God seeing how much faith they had to be healed. I don't know of any more contemptible, lying cowards than that.

Do you know where Kathryn Kuhlman went when she got sick (and finally died from it)? She went to a hospital, that's where. She was a couple of miles from T. T. Osborne in one direction and Oral Roberts in another direction, two of the greatest self-proclaimed "faith healers" this country had, at the time. Besides that, Kuhlman herself claimed to have the gift. Why didn't she heal herself? [She couldn't,]{.class_3055} which is why she went to the hospital.

During the so-called "Brownsville Revival," the pastor of the Brownsville Assembly of God (John Kilpatrick), where the "revival" was being held, was building a [three-million dollar house]{.class_3055} over in Seminole, Alabama (from gifts from the Brownsville Revival, of course). He fell off the roof and broke some bones. After claiming to have healed so many people (and raised the dead!) at the "revival," do you think he called for the evangelist (Steve Hill) or the "worship leader" (Lindell Cooley) or his "Bible teacher" (Dr. Michael Brown) or any member of his church to come lay hands on him and heal him? Of course not. [He went to the hospital.]{.class_3055} How "Spirit filled" can one get?!

Listen, I believe God still heals, but the [gift of healing]{.class_3055} went out with the Jewish apostles. By the end of his ministry, the Apostle Paul couldn't heal one of his buddies in the ministry; he left Trophimus ["at Miletum sick"]{.class_3054} (2 Tim 4:20). He told his protegé in the ministry (Timothy) to ["use a little wine"]{.class_3054} as a medicine for his ["often infirmities"]{.class_3054} (1 Tim. 5:23). Nobody laid hands on Timothy to get rid of his health problems. You had better learn to rightly divide that Book, or you will make the biggest mess you ever saw.

19 So then after the Lord had spoken unto them, he was received up into heaven, and sat on the right hand of God.

20 And they went forth, and preached every where, the Lord working with them, and confirming the word with signs following. Amen.

There is a gap of time somewhere either between verses 14 and 15, or between verses 18 and 19. I take it to be between verses 14 and 15 based on where the Great Commission falls in Luke 24:46--51 and Acts 1:8--11. It is given just before Christ's ascension.

Verse 19 is the doctrine of the "Session" of Christ; that is, Christ, in His present state, is seated at God the Father's right hand.
